## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style, Module 1]**

**Q1. Define a transducer. Differentiate between an active and a passive transducer with one example each. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A transducer is a device that converts energy from one form to another, most commonly converting a non-electrical physical quantity into an electrical signal.

- **Active transducer** generates its own electrical output without requiring an external power source. *Example:* Thermocouple (converts temperature directly into a millivolt signal via the Seebeck effect).
- **Passive transducer** requires an external excitation power to operate, and the measurand causes a change in an electrical parameter such as resistance, inductance, or capacitance. *Example:* Strain gauge (its resistance changes with applied strain when excited by a Wheatstone bridge).

---

**Q2. List any four static characteristics of a transducer. Define "span" and "hysteresis". (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Four static characteristics of a transducer are: (i) Accuracy, (ii) Sensitivity, (iii) Linearity, and (iv) Hysteresis.

- **Span:** The algebraic difference between the maximum and minimum values of the input (or output) over which the transducer is designed to operate. For a transducer with range 20–120 °C, the span is 100 °C.
- **Hysteresis:** The maximum difference in output for the same value of input, observed when the input is approached first from the *increasing* direction and then from the *decreasing* direction. It is typically expressed as a percentage of the full-scale output.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, ESE Module 1]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Classify transducers in detail with at least two examples for each class. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Transducers can be classified on the basis of their operating principle, the type of energy conversion, and the form of the output signal.

**1. Active (Self-generating) Transducers:**
Generate their own electrical output. No external power supply is needed.
- *Thermocouple* — temperature difference is converted into an emf using the Seebeck effect.
- *Piezoelectric sensor* — applied force produces an electrical charge across the crystal faces.
- *Tachogenerator* — rotational speed produces a voltage using Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.

**2. Passive Transducers:**
Require an external excitation. The measurand changes an internal R, L, or C value.
- *Strain gauge* — resistance changes with strain.
- *LVDT* — inductance changes with core position.
- *Thermistor* — resistance changes with temperature.
- *Capacitive displacement sensor* — capacitance changes with plate gap.

**3. Analog vs Digital:**
- *Analog:* Thermocouple, LVDT, RTD (output varies continuously with input).
- *Digital:* Rotary encoder, digital tachometer, Hall-effect switch (output is in discrete pulses/codes).

**4. Primary vs Secondary:**
- *Primary:* Bourdon tube, bimetallic strip, bellows — first to sense the measurand.
- *Secondary:* LVDT, strain gauge — convert the mechanical output of the primary into an electrical signal.

**5. Based on the physical principle used:**
- *Resistive:* Potentiometer, RTD, thermistor, strain gauge, LDR.
- *Inductive:* LVDT, synchro, eddy-current probe.
- *Capacitive:* Capacitive displacement sensor.
- *Piezoelectric:* Accelerometer, force sensor.
- *Thermoelectric:* Thermocouple.
- *Photoelectric:* Photodiode, phototransistor.

---

**(b) A strain gauge with gauge factor 2.1 and resistance 350 Ω is bonded to a steel cantilever. The cantilever is subjected to a stress that produces a strain of 800 microstrain. The gauge is connected in one arm of a Wheatstone bridge with all other arms 350 Ω. The bridge is excited by 10 V DC. Find (i) the change in resistance, (ii) the output voltage of the bridge, and (iii) the output voltage after amplification by an instrumentation amplifier of gain 50. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Gauge factor $G_f = 2.1$
- Nominal resistance $R = 350 \ \Omega$
- Strain $\varepsilon = 800 \ \mu\varepsilon = 800 \times 10^{-6}$
- Excitation voltage $V_s = 10 \ \text{V}$
- All other bridge arms = 350 Ω
- Instrumentation amplifier gain $A = 50$

**Step 1 — Change in resistance of the strain gauge.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_f &= \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} \\
\Rightarrow \Delta R &= G_f \cdot R \cdot \varepsilon \\
\Delta R &= 2.1 \times 350 \times 800 \times 10^{-6} \\
\Delta R &= 0.588 \ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Output voltage of the unbalanced Wheatstone bridge.**

The standard formula for the output voltage of a Wheatstone bridge with one active arm is:

$$V_{\text{out}} = \frac{V_s}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

(This approximation is valid when $\Delta R \ll R$, which is the case here since $0.588 \ll 350$.)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out}} &= \frac{10}{4} \times \frac{0.588}{350} \\
&= 2.5 \times 0.00168 \\
&= 0.0042 \ \text{V} = 4.2 \ \text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Amplified output voltage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out, amp}} &= A \cdot V_{\text{out}} \\
&= 50 \times 0.0042 \\
&= 0.21 \ \text{V} = 210 \ \text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Results:**
- $\Delta R = 0.588 \ \Omega$
- Bridge output $V_{\text{out}} = 4.2 \ \text{mV}$
- Amplified output $V_{\text{out, amp}} = 210 \ \text{mV}$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**  *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a) Explain the static characteristics of transducers in detail. Define accuracy, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, and dead zone. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Static characteristics describe the steady-state performance of a transducer when the input is constant or changes very slowly.

**1. Accuracy:**
The closeness of the measured value to the true value. Quantitatively:
$$\% \text{Accuracy} = \left(1 - \frac{\text{Max static error}}{\text{FSO}}\right) \times 100$$
A high-accuracy transducer has small deviation from the true value.

**2. Sensitivity (S):**
The ratio of the change in output to the corresponding change in input:
$$S = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$$
Units: output units per input unit (e.g., mV/kPa). A higher sensitivity means a larger output signal per unit input, leading to a better signal-to-noise ratio.

**3. Linearity:**
A transducer is *linear* if its output is a *direct straight-line* function of the input. The *non-linearity error* is the maximum deviation of the actual curve from the best-fit straight line, expressed as a percentage of FSO.

$$\%L = \frac{\Delta y_{\max}}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$$

Lower % linearity error is desirable.

**4. Hysteresis:**
The maximum difference in output for the *same value of input*, when the input is first increased and then decreased. Caused by friction, magnetic domain lag, or material memory. Expressed as a percentage of FSO.

**5. Dead Zone (Dead Band):**
The largest range of input values over which the output does *not change* (the transducer is unresponsive). It is undesirable and should be minimised.

**6. Other static characteristics:**
- **Precision:** Closeness of repeated measurements *to each other*.
- **Resolution:** The smallest input change that produces a detectable output change.
- **Drift:** Slow change in output over time at a constant input.
- **Range and Span:** Defined earlier in SECTION_2.

**Engineering Note:** Static characteristics are measured by *calibration*, where known input standards (traceable to national standards like NABL) are applied and the output is recorded.

---

**(b) A first-order temperature transducer has a time constant of 5 seconds. The temperature of the medium suddenly changes from 30 °C to 200 °C. Determine (i) the temperature indicated by the transducer at t = 3 s and t = 10 s, (ii) the time required to reach 98% of the final value, and (iii) the bandwidth of the transducer. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $\tau = 5 \ \text{s}$
- $T_0 = 30°\text{C}$, $T_\infty = 200°\text{C}$

**Step 1 — First-order step response equation.**

$$T(t) = T_\infty + (T_0 - T_\infty) e^{-t/\tau} = 200 - 170 \cdot e^{-t/5}$$

**Step 2 (i-a) — Temperature at $t = 3$ s.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(3) &= 200 - 170 \cdot e^{-3/5} \\
&= 200 - 170 \cdot e^{-0.6} \\
&= 200 - 170 \times 0.5488 \\
&= 200 - 93.30 \\
&= 106.70°\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (i-b) — Temperature at $t = 10$ s.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(10) &= 200 - 170 \cdot e^{-10/5} \\
&= 200 - 170 \cdot e^{-2} \\
&= 200 - 170 \times 0.1353 \\
&= 200 - 23.01 \\
&= 176.99°\text{C} \approx 177°\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (ii) — Time to reach 98% of final value.**

Target: $T(t) = 0.98 \times 200 + 0.02 \times 30 = 196 + 0.6 = 196.6°\text{C}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
196.6 &= 200 - 170 \cdot e^{-t/5} \\
170 \cdot e^{-t/5} &= 3.4 \\
e^{-t/5} &= 0.02 \\
-\frac{t}{5} &= \ln(0.02) = -3.912 \\
t &= 19.56 \ \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the transducer takes approximately **$3.9 \tau$** to reach 98% of the final value.

**Step 5 (iii) — Bandwidth of the first-order transducer.**

$$BW = \frac{1}{2\pi\tau} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5} = 0.0318 \ \text{Hz} \approx 0.032 \ \text{Hz}$$

This means the transducer can accurately track temperature variations only up to about 0.032 Hz (one full sine cycle every ~31 seconds). Faster variations will be heavily attenuated.

**Final Results:**
- $T(3\text{s}) = 106.7°\text{C}$
- $T(10\text{s}) \approx 177°\text{C}$
- Time to reach 98% = 19.56 s
- $BW \approx 0.032$ Hz

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Transducer Problems (as observed in KTU valuation keys):**
>
> 1. **Confusing % Linearity Error with % Accuracy.** A perfectly linear but biased transducer has 0% linearity error but poor accuracy. They are *not* the same.
> 2. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** Sensitivity must carry the correct unit (mV/°C, not just mV). Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 3. **Not specifying the best-fit method.** When asked for linearity, students often compute error against an *arbitrary* line. KTU accepts either **end-point fit** or **independent best fit**, but you *must declare which one* you are using.
> 4. **Ignoring the Wheatstone bridge approximation.** Using $V_{\text{out}} = (\Delta R / R) \cdot V_s$ instead of $V_{\text{out}} = (\Delta R / 4R) \cdot V_s$. The factor of **4** in the denominator comes from the bridge's full-bridge-to-quarter-bridge derivation. Skipping this is a common 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Active vs Passive confusion.** Hall-effect sensors are *active* (they produce an output voltage), but LDR is *passive* (it only changes resistance when excited). Examiners penalise this swap.
> 6. **Time-constant units.** Forgetting to convert minutes to seconds (or vice-versa) in first-order problems is one of the most frequent unit-trap errors.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Transducer** converts one form of energy → another (most often to electrical).
- [x] **Sensor** = part of a transducer that *first senses* the measurand.
- [x] **Actuator** = reverse of a sensor (electrical → physical action).
- [x] **Active transducers** = generate their own signal; **Passive** = need external excitation.
- [x] **Static characteristics** = Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, Linearity, Hysteresis, Dead Zone, Drift, Range, Span, Resolution.
- [x] **Dynamic characteristics** = Time constant, Rise time, Settling time, Natural frequency, Damping ratio, Bandwidth.
- [x] **Sensitivity** $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$ (always include the correct unit).
- [x] **% Linearity Error** = $\Delta y_{\max} / \text{FSO} \times 100$ (best-fit or end-point).
- [x] **% Hysteresis Error** = max loop width / FSO × 100.
- [x] **First-order step response:** $y(t) = y_\infty (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$; reaches 63.2% at $\tau$, 95% at $3\tau$, 99% at $5\tau$.
- [x] **First-order bandwidth:** $BW = 1 / (2\pi \tau)$.
- [x] **Wheatstone bridge (quarter-active):** $V_{\text{out}} = (V_s / 4) \cdot (\Delta R / R)$.
- [x] **Strain gauge:** $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$, with $G_f \approx 2.0$ for constantan.
- [x] **LVDT** = AC-excited passive inductive displacement sensor; very linear, very low hysteresis; output proportional to core displacement.
- [x] **Thermocouple** = active, based on Seebeck effect; produces mV directly; needs cold-junction compensation.
- [x] **RTD** = passive, resistance increases linearly with temperature (Pt100 = 100 Ω at 0 °C).
- [x] **Thermistor** = passive, highly non-linear (NTC or PTC); very high sensitivity, limited range.
- [x] **Piezoelectric** = active, dynamic-only (no DC response); very high frequency response.
- [x] **Potentiometer** = passive resistive displacement sensor; works on the voltage-divider principle.
- [x] **LDR** = passive, resistance decreases with light intensity.
- [x] **Always mention** whether the transducer is *active or passive*, *its range*, *its sensitivity*, and *its uncertainty band* in a descriptive answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Force
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT521 - MECHATRONICS AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Force

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Mechatronics and Control Systems**, **Force** is defined as a fundamental vector quantity that represents an interaction capable of changing the state of motion, direction, or geometric configuration of a body. Mathematically, it is expressed through **Newton's Second Law of Motion** as the time rate of change of linear momentum of a body.

$$\vec{F} = \frac{d\vec{p}}{dt} = m\vec{a}$$

where:
- $\vec{F}$ = Force vector (Newtons, **N**)
- $\vec{p}$ = Linear momentum ($kg \cdot m/s$)
- $m$ = Mass of the body (**kg**)
- $\vec{a}$ = Linear acceleration ($m/s^2$)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Force is the foundational concept in Module 1 of PCAUT521 because every actuator, sensor, and mechanical linkage in a mechatronic system ultimately resolves into force generation, transmission, or measurement. The **SI unit** is the **Newton (N)**, and the **FPS unit** is the **pound-force (lbf)**, where $1\ \text{N} \approx 0.2248\ \text{lbf}$.

## 1.2 Classification of Forces (KTU Board Taxonomy)

Forces encountered in mechatronic systems are broadly classified as:

- **Contact Forces** — Arise from physical touching of bodies (e.g., friction, normal reaction, applied push).
- **Non-Contact (Field) Forces** — Act over a distance without physical contact (e.g., gravitational, electromagnetic, magnetic).
- **Internal Forces** — Forces exerted by one part of a body/system on another part of the same system.
- **External Forces** — Forces exerted by bodies outside the defined system on the system itself.
- **Coplanar Forces** — All force vectors lie in a single plane.
- **Concurrent Forces** — All force lines of action pass through a common point.
- **Coplanar-Parallel Forces** — Forces lying in the same plane and parallel to each other.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Pushing a Broken-Down Car Example:**
> Imagine you and two friends are pushing a stalled car on a flat road. Each of you is applying a "push" — that push is a **force**. The car accelerates because the *sum* of all three pushes (the **resultant force**) overcomes the friction between the tyres and the road. If you all push on the same corner (a **concurrent point**), the analysis is straightforward. If you push from different points (creating a tendency to **rotate** the car), you must also consider the **turning effect** — this is the **moment of force**, also called **torque**. This is exactly what a KTU examiner expects you to understand: *force* causes translation, *moment* causes rotation, and mechatronic actuators (motors, solenoids, hydraulic cylinders) are designed to produce one or both.

## 1.4 Force as a Vector: The Three Pillars

A force vector is fully defined by **magnitude**, **direction**, and **point of application** (or line of action). In 3D Cartesian space, any force is decomposed as:

$$\vec{F} = F_x\,\hat{i} + F_y\,\hat{j} + F_z\,\hat{k}$$

The magnitude of the force is obtained using the 3D Euclidean norm:

$$F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Never treat force as a scalar. The single most common mistake observed in KTU answer scripts is the omission of the directional component, especially in resultant force problems. Always draw the **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** first — it accounts for nearly **30% of the marks** in any force analysis problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

The analysis of force in any mechatronic system follows a structured logical sequence. Below is the universal 5-step framework adopted by KTU board examiners:

1. **Identify the System Boundary** — Clearly demarcate the body or mechanism under study from its environment. This separates **internal** from **external** forces.
2. **Construct the Free Body Diagram (FBD)** — Isolate the body and represent every external force as a vector arrow at its actual point of application with its assumed direction.
3. **Resolve Forces into Components** — Decompose each force vector into its rectangular Cartesian components ($F_x$, $F_y$, $F_z$) using $\cos\theta$, $\sin\theta$, and $\cos\phi$ projections.
4. **Apply Equilibrium / Newton's Equations** — Sum the components and moments according to the appropriate principle (Newton's 2nd Law for dynamics, equilibrium equations for statics).
5. **Solve the Resulting System of Equations** — Solve the linear/vector system simultaneously to find unknown forces, reactions, or accelerations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this sequence?** Each step builds upon the previous one. Skipping the FBD or component resolution is the #1 reason students lose marks in Part B questions. The KTU marking scheme allocates 2 marks each for the FBD and the component table.

## 2.2 Newton's Three Laws of Motion (Foundational Pillars)

- **First Law (Law of Inertia):** A body remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by an unbalanced external force. Mathematically, $\sum \vec{F} = 0 \Rightarrow \vec{a} = 0$.
- **Second Law (Law of Acceleration):** The net external force on a body is equal to the rate of change of its linear momentum. $\vec{F}_{\text{net}} = m\vec{a}$.
- **Third Law (Action-Reaction):** For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. $\vec{F}_{AB} = -\vec{F}_{BA}$.

## 2.3 Moment of a Force (Turning Effect)

The **moment** of a force about a point O is the product of the force magnitude and the **perpendicular distance** from the point to the line of action of the force. It is a vector quantity whose direction is given by the **right-hand rule**.

$$\vec{M}_O = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$$

The scalar magnitude in 2D is:

$$M_O = F \cdot d_{\perp}$$

where $d_{\perp}$ is the perpendicular distance (moment arm) from O to the line of action of $\vec{F}$.

## 2.4 Varignon's Theorem (High-Yield KTU Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Varignon's Theorem:** The moment of the resultant of a system of concurrent forces about any point in the plane of the forces is equal to the algebraic sum of the moments of the individual forces about the same point.
>
> $$\vec{M}_{O,\text{resultant}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{M}_{O,i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\vec{r}_i \times \vec{F}_i)$$
>
> This theorem is **guaranteed to appear** in KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) as either a Part A (3 marks) or as a sub-part of Part B (7 marks). Memorize it word-for-word.

## 2.5 Couple and Moment of a Couple

A **couple** consists of two equal, opposite, and parallel forces whose lines of action are separated by a perpendicular distance. The net translational effect is zero, but it produces a pure rotational effect called the **moment of the couple**.

$$M_{\text{couple}} = F \cdot d$$

> The moment of a couple is **independent of the reference point** chosen — this is a frequently tested conceptual point.

## 2.6 Resultant of a Coplanar Concurrent Force System

For $n$ concurrent coplanar forces $F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n$ acting at angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n$ with respect to the x-axis, the resultant is:

$$R_x = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i \cos\theta_i \quad ; \quad R_y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i \sin\theta_i$$

$$R = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2} \quad ; \quad \alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R_y}{R_x}\right)$$

where $\alpha$ is the angle the resultant makes with the positive x-axis.

## 2.7 Equilibrium of a Rigid Body (Coplanar)

A rigid body in static equilibrium under a coplanar force system must satisfy **three** independent equations:

$$\sum F_x = 0 \quad ; \quad \sum F_y = 0 \quad ; \quad \sum M_O = 0$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Engineering Utility:** These three equations are the bedrock of *truss analysis* in bridge design, *robot arm* static analysis in robotics, and *mechanism analysis* in cam-follower systems — all of which are sub-topics within PCAUT521.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | SI Units | Key Condition |
|---|---------|---------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Newton's 2nd Law | $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ | N | $m$ constant |
| 2 | Force Vector (3D) | $\vec{F} = F_x\hat{i} + F_y\hat{j} + F_z\hat{k}$ | N | Rectangular form |
| 3 | Force Magnitude | $F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}$ | N | Always positive |
| 4 | Moment of Force | $M_O = F \cdot d_{\perp} = \vert \vec{r} \times \vec{F} \vert$ | N·m | Perpendicular distance |
| 5 | Couple Moment | $M = F \cdot d$ | N·m | Point-independent |
| 6 | Resultant (x) | $R_x = \sum F_i \cos\theta_i$ | N | Coplanar concurrent |
| 7 | Resultant (y) | $R_y = \sum F_i \sin\theta_i$ | N | Coplanar concurrent |
| 8 | Resultant Magnitude | $R = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2}$ | N | Coplanar |
| 9 | Resultant Direction | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(R_y / R_x)$ | rad or deg | Quadrant-aware |
| 10 | Equilibrium (2D) | $\sum F_x = 0,\ \sum F_y = 0,\ \sum M = 0$ | — | Static, coplanar |
| 11 | Lami's Theorem | $F_1/\sin\alpha_1 = F_2/\sin\alpha_2 = F_3/\sin\alpha_3$ | N | 3 concurrent forces |
| 12 | Weight | $W = mg$ | N | $g \approx 9.81\ m/s^2$ |

> **Note:** In the table above, all instances of mathematical absolute value bars have been written using the $\vert$ rendering to preserve the markdown table structure, as per the formatting protocol.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Resultant of Two Coplanar Concurrent Forces (Parallelogram Law)

### Statement
The Parallelogram Law of Forces states: *If two coplanar concurrent forces acting at a point are represented in magnitude and direction by the two adjacent sides of a parallelogram, then their resultant is represented in magnitude and direction by the diagonal of the parallelogram passing through the common point of the two forces.*

### Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1 — Setup:** Let two forces $\vec{F}_1$ and $\vec{F}_2$ act at a common point O, making an angle $\theta$ between them.

**Step 2 — Represent the forces:** Draw OA along $\vec{F}_1$ and OB along $\vec{F}_2$ such that $OA = F_1$ and $OB = F_2$. Complete the parallelogram OACB such that $OC \parallel OB$ and $AC \parallel OB$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Law of Cosines:** In triangle OAC, the diagonal OC represents the resultant $\vec{R}$, and the included angle at O is $\theta$. Using the law of cosines on triangle OAC:

$$R^2 = OA^2 + OC^2 - 2 \cdot OA \cdot OC \cdot \cos(\angle AOC)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:** Since $OC = OB = F_2$ and $\angle AOC = 180^\circ - \theta$, we have $\cos(180^\circ - \theta) = -\cos\theta$:

$$R^2 = F_1^2 + F_2^2 - 2 F_1 F_2 \cdot (-\cos\theta)$$

**Step 5 — Final Form (Parallelogram Law Equation):**

$$R^2 = F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2 F_1 F_2 \cos\theta$$

**Step 6 — Direction of Resultant:** Let $\alpha$ be the angle made by $\vec{R}$ with $\vec{F}_1$. From the geometry of the parallelogram and the law of sines:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{F_2 \sin\theta}{F_1 + F_2 \cos\theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This is the foundation of *force composition* in robotic gripper analysis, where two finger-jaws apply forces that must be resolved into a single grasping resultant.

## 3.2 Derivation: Resultant of $n$ Coplanar Concurrent Forces

**Step 1 — Component Decomposition:** For force $F_i$ acting at angle $\theta_i$ with the x-axis:

$$F_{ix} = F_i \cos\theta_i \quad ; \quad F_{iy} = F_i \sin\theta_i$$

**Step 2 — Summation (Superposition Principle):** The principle of physical independence of forces (Galileo's principle) allows algebraic summation of components:

$$R_x = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i \cos\theta_i \quad ; \quad R_y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i \sin\theta_i$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude (Pythagorean Composition):**

$$R = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2}$$

**Step 4 — Direction (Inverse Tangent with Quadrant Correction):**

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R_y}{R_x}\right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Quadrant Trap:** $\tan^{-1}$ in calculators only returns values in the range $(-90^\circ, +90^\circ)$. If $R_x < 0$, you must add $180^\circ$ to obtain the correct angle in the second or third quadrant. This single error costs **2 marks** in KTU valuation.

## 3.3 Derivation: Moment of a Force as a Cross Product

**Step 1 — Define Position Vector:** Let point P be the point of application of force $\vec{F}$, and O be the reference point. The position vector is $\vec{r}_{OP} = \vec{r}$.

**Step 2 — Definition:** The moment $\vec{M}_O$ is the tendency of the force to rotate the body about O.

**Step 3 — Cross Product Formulation:** The cross product $\vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ produces a vector perpendicular to the plane of $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{F}$, with magnitude $\vert \vec{r} \vert \vert \vec{F} \vert \sin\phi$, where $\phi$ is the angle between $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{F}$.

**Step 4 — Geometric Interpretation:** Note that $\vert \vec{r} \vert \sin\phi = d_{\perp}$ (the perpendicular distance from O to the line of action of $\vec{F}$). Therefore:

$$\vert \vec{M}_O \vert = \vert \vec{F} \vert \cdot d_{\perp}$$

**Step 5 — Cartesian Expansion:** With $\vec{r} = x\hat{i} + y\hat{j} + z\hat{k}$ and $\vec{F} = F_x\hat{i} + F_y\hat{j} + F_z\hat{k}$:

$$\vec{M}_O = \vec{r} \times \vec{F} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ x & y & z \\ F_x & F_y & F_z \end{vmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Final Scalar Form (2D Coplanar Case):** For forces in the xy-plane ($z = 0$):

$$M_O = x \cdot F_y - y \cdot F_x$$

The positive sign convention is **counter-clockwise** (CCW) about the z-axis.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Resultant of Three Concurrent Forces (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** Three coplanar concurrent forces act at point O:
- $F_1 = 100$ N along the positive x-axis ($\theta_1 = 0^\circ$)
- $F_2 = 150$ N at $\theta_2 = 60^\circ$ from x-axis
- $F_3 = 200$ N at $\theta_3 = 135^\circ$ from x-axis

Find the magnitude and direction of the resultant.

**Solution — Step 1 (Component Table):**

| Force | Magnitude (N) | $\theta$ | $F_x = F\cos\theta$ (N) | $F_y = F\sin\theta$ (N) |
|-------|---------------|----------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| $F_1$ | 100 | $0^\circ$ | $100 \times 1 = 100$ | $100 \times 0 = 0$ |
| $F_2$ | 150 | $60^\circ$ | $150 \times 0.5 = 75$ | $150 \times 0.866 = 129.9$ |
| $F_3$ | 200 | $135^\circ$ | $200 \times (-0.707) = -141.4$ | $200 \times 0.707 = 141.4$ |

**Step 2 (Summation):**

$$R_x = 100 + 75 + (-141.4) = 33.6\ \text{N}$$

$$R_y = 0 + 129.9 + 141.4 = 271.3\ \text{N}$$

**Step 3 (Resultant Magnitude):**

$$R = \sqrt{(33.6)^2 + (271.3)^2} = \sqrt{1128.96 + 73611.69} = \sqrt{74740.65}$$

$$R \approx 273.4\ \text{N}$$

**Step 4 (Resultant Direction):**

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{271.3}{33.6}\right) = \tan^{-1}(8.074) \approx 82.94^\circ$$

Since $R_x > 0$ and $R_y > 0$, the resultant lies in the **first quadrant**, so no quadrant correction is needed.

$$\boxed{\vec{R} \approx 273.4\ \text{N at}\ 82.94^\circ\ \text{from the positive x-axis}}$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Lami's Theorem (Three Concurrent Coplanar Forces in Equilibrium)

**Step 1:** Consider three forces $\vec{F}_1$, $\vec{F}_2$, $\vec{F}_3$ acting at a point O in equilibrium. Let the angle between $\vec{F}_2$ and $\vec{F}_3$ be $\alpha_1$, between $\vec{F}_3$ and $\vec{F}_1$ be $\alpha_2$, and between $\vec{F}_1$ and $\vec{F}_2$ be $\alpha_3$.

**Step 2:** Since the system is in equilibrium, $\vec{F}_1 + \vec{F}_2 + \vec{F}_3 = 0$. This means the three vectors, when placed head-to-tail, form a closed triangle.

**Step 3:** Applying the **Law of Sines** to this force triangle:

$$\frac{F_1}{\sin\alpha_1} = \frac{F_2}{\sin\alpha_2} = \frac{F_3}{\sin\alpha_3}$$

> This is the famous **Lami's Theorem**, indispensable for solving 3-force equilibrium problems like those in robotic arm joints and toggle mechanisms.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Force Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FORCE] --> B[Contact Forces]
    A --> C[Non-Contact Forces]
    A --> D[Internal Forces]
    A --> E[External Forces]

    B --> B1[Friction]
    B --> B2[Normal Reaction]
    B --> B3[Applied Push or Pull]
    B --> B4[Tension in Cable]

    C --> C1[Gravitational]
    C --> C2[Electromagnetic]
    C --> C3[Magnetic]

    D --> D1[Stress]
    D --> D2[Strain Force]

    E --> E1[Coplanar]
    E --> E2[Concurrent]
    E --> E3[Parallel]

    E1 --> E1A[Concurrent Coplanar]
    E1 --> E1B[Non-Concurrent Coplanar]
    E1 --> E1C[Parallel Coplanar]

    E2 --> E2A[Three Force Member]
    E2 --> E2B[Multi-Force Member]
```

## 4.2 Free Body Diagram (FBD) Construction Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1: Identify the Body] --> step2[Step 2: Isolate the Body]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Replace All Supports with Reaction Forces]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Show All Applied Loads as Vectors]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Indicate Dimensions and Angles]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Choose Reference Axes XY]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Apply Equilibrium Equations Sum Fx 0 Sum Fy 0 Sum M 0]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Solve for Unknowns]
```

## 4.3 Force Measurement System in Mechatronics (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1[Mechanical Input Force] --> stageA[Primary Sensing Element Load Cell]
    stageA --> stageB[Transducer Strain Gauges]
    stageB --> stageC[Signal Conditioning Amplifier and Filter]
    stageC --> stageD[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    stageD --> stageE[Microcontroller Signal Processing]
    stageE --> stageF[Display or Actuator Feedback]
    stageE --> stageG[Communication UART or I2C]
    stageG --> stageH[Host PC or PLC]
```

## 4.4 Resultant of Concurrent Forces — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    f1[Force F1 at Angle Theta 1] --> comp1[Resolve into F1x and F1y]
    f2[Force F2 at Angle Theta 2] --> comp2[Resolve into F2x and F2y]
    f3[Force F3 at Angle Theta 3] --> comp3[Resolve into F3x and F3y]

    comp1 --> sumx[Sum X Components to get Rx]
    comp2 --> sumx
    comp3 --> sumx

    comp1 --> sumy[Sum Y Components to get Ry]
    comp2 --> sumy
    comp3 --> sumy

    sumx --> mag[Compute Magnitude R equals square root Rx squared plus Ry squared]
    sumy --> mag

    sumx --> dir[Compute Direction alpha equals arctan Ry by Rx]
    sumy --> dir

    mag --> out[Resultant Force Vector Output]
    dir --> out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Adaptation Note:** Per the protocol, all mermaid node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `f1`, `comp1`, `sumx`) and do not use reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph` as standalone IDs. Labels are kept clean (no markdown bold/italic inside quoted strings) for guaranteed mermaid compilation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: State and explain Varignon's Theorem. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Varignon's Theorem** states that the moment of the resultant of a system of concurrent coplanar forces about any point in the plane of the forces is equal to the **algebraic sum of the moments of the individual forces** about the same point.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$M_O(R) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} M_O(F_i)$$

**Significance (1 mark):** This theorem greatly simplifies the calculation of moments in complex force systems because it allows us to replace the individual forces with their resultant and then compute a single moment, rather than computing each moment separately.

> **Mark Distribution:** Statement (1 mark) + Mathematical form (1 mark) + Significance (1 mark).

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between a 'Force' and a 'Couple'. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Force | Couple |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Definition | A single push or pull that tends to translate a body | Two equal, opposite, and parallel forces that tend to rotate a body |
| Translational Effect | Yes (causes linear acceleration) | No (net force is zero) |
| Rotational Effect | Produces rotation only if line of action does not pass through reference point | Always produces rotation (pure moment) |
| SI Unit | Newton (N) | Newton-meter (N·m) |
| Vector Representation | Single arrow with magnitude and direction | A curved arrow representing the moment of the couple |

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition of each (1 mark each) + Distinguishing table (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question A, Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

> Three coplanar concurrent forces act at a point O. $F_1 = 200$ N acts along the positive x-axis. $F_2 = 300$ N acts at $60^\circ$ to the x-axis (in the first quadrant). $F_3 = 150$ N acts at $210^\circ$ to the x-axis (in the third quadrant). Determine:
> (i) The magnitude of the resultant force.
> (ii) The direction of the resultant force with respect to the x-axis.
> (iii) The moment of the resultant about a point P located at (0.5 m, 0.3 m) from O.

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Resolve into Components:**

$$F_{1x} = 200 \cos 0^\circ = 200\ \text{N} \quad ; \quad F_{1y} = 200 \sin 0^\circ = 0\ \text{N}$$

$$F_{2x} = 300 \cos 60^\circ = 150\ \text{N} \quad ; \quad F_{2y} = 300 \sin 60^\circ = 259.81\ \text{N}$$

$$F_{3x} = 150 \cos 210^\circ = -129.90\ \text{N} \quad ; \quad F_{3y} = 150 \sin 210^\circ = -75\ \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the Components:**

$$R_x = 200 + 150 + (-129.90) = 220.10\ \text{N}$$

$$R_y = 0 + 259.81 + (-75) = 184.81\ \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Resultant Magnitude:**

$$R = \sqrt{(220.10)^2 + (184.81)^2} = \sqrt{48444.01 + 34154.74} = \sqrt{82598.75}$$

$$R \approx 287.40\ \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Direction:**

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{184.81}{220.10}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.8397) \approx 40.02^\circ$$

Since both $R_x > 0$ and $R_y > 0$, the resultant lies in the **first quadrant**.

**Step 5 — Moment about P (Position vector $\vec{r} = 0.5\hat{i} + 0.3\hat{j}$ m):**

Using the 2D cross product formula:

$$M_P = x_P \cdot R_y - y_P \cdot R_x = (0.5)(184.81) - (0.3)(220.10)$$

$$M_P = 92.405 - 66.030 = 26.375\ \text{N·m (CCW)}$$

> **Mark Distribution:** Component table (2 marks) + Summation (1 mark) + Magnitude and direction (2 marks) + Moment calculation (2 marks).

---

**Question A, Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

> Define the term 'Free Body Diagram' (FBD). With the help of a neat sketch, explain the procedure to construct a Free Body Diagram for a simply supported beam of length 6 m carrying a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of 10 kN/m over its entire span. Also determine the support reactions.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** A **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** is a graphical representation of a body (or a portion of a system) isolated from its surroundings, showing all external forces, moments, and support reactions acting on it.

**Procedure to Construct FBD (2 marks):**
1. Isolate the body from all supports and surrounding objects.
2. Replace each support with the corresponding reaction force/moment.
3. Show the applied loads (UDL converted to point load) at their actual location.
4. Indicate the assumed direction of unknown reactions (usually upward for simple supports).

**Calculation of Reactions (3 marks):**

- Total UDL = $10\ \text{kN/m} \times 6\ \text{m} = 60\ \text{kN}$, acting at the midspan (3 m from each support).
- Let $R_A$ and $R_B$ be the reactions at supports A (left) and B (right).
- By symmetry: $R_A = R_B = 60/2 = 30\ \text{kN}$ (upward).

**Verification using equilibrium equations:**

$$\sum F_y = 0 \Rightarrow R_A + R_B - 60 = 0 \Rightarrow 30 + 30 - 60 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\sum M_A = 0 \Rightarrow R_B \times 6 - 60 \times 3 = 0 \Rightarrow 180 - 180 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question B, Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

> State and prove the **Parallelogram Law of Forces**. Using the law, find the magnitude and direction of the resultant of two forces 50 N and 80 N acting at a point with an angle of $60^\circ$ between them.

**Model Solution:**

**Statement (2 marks):** Refer to the formal statement in Section 3.1 above.

**Proof (3 marks):** Refer to the 5-step derivation in Section 3.1 (using Law of Cosines on the diagonal triangle of the parallelogram).

**Numerical Application (2 marks):**

Using $R^2 = F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2 F_1 F_2 \cos\theta$:

$$R^2 = (50)^2 + (80)^2 + 2(50)(80)\cos 60^\circ$$

$$R^2 = 2500 + 6400 + 8000 \times 0.5 = 2500 + 6400 + 4000 = 12900$$

$$R = \sqrt{12900} \approx 113.58\ \text{N}$$

Direction:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{F_2 \sin\theta}{F_1 + F_2 \cos\theta} = \frac{80 \times \sin 60^\circ}{50 + 80 \times 0.5} = \frac{80 \times 0.866}{50 + 40} = \frac{69.28}{90} \approx 0.7698$$

$$\alpha \approx 37.59^\circ\ \text{from the 50 N force}$$

---

**Question B, Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

> A smooth circular roller of weight 500 N and radius 0.4 m is to be pulled over a step of height 0.15 m by a horizontal force P applied at the centre of the roller. Determine:
> (i) The minimum force P required to initiate the motion.
> (ii) The reaction at the pivot point A (corner of the step).

**Model Solution:**

**FBD Setup (2 marks):** Three forces act on the roller: weight $W = 500$ N (downward at center), applied force $P$ (horizontal at center), and reaction $R_A$ at the corner A.

**Geometry (2 marks):** The line from center O to corner A has length $r = 0.4$ m. The vertical distance from O to the step top is $h = r - 0.15 = 0.25$ m. The horizontal distance is:

$$b = \sqrt{r^2 - h^2} = \sqrt{(0.4)^2 - (0.25)^2} = \sqrt{0.16 - 0.0625} = \sqrt{0.0975} \approx 0.3122\ \text{m}$$

**Equilibrium Equations (3 marks):**

Taking moments about A (to eliminate $R_A$):

$$\sum M_A = 0 \Rightarrow P \cdot h - W \cdot b = 0$$

$$P = \frac{W \cdot b}{h} = \frac{500 \times 0.3122}{0.25} = \frac{156.1}{0.25} = 624.4\ \text{N}$$

For reaction at A, summing forces:

$$R_{Ax} = P = 624.4\ \text{N (horizontal)}$$

$$R_{Ay} = W = 500\ \text{N (vertical, upward)}$$

$$R_A = \sqrt{(624.4)^2 + (500)^2} = \sqrt{389875 + 250000} = \sqrt{639875} \approx 799.92\ \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\min} \approx 624.4\ \text{N}, \quad R_A \approx 799.9\ \text{N at}\ \theta = \tan^{-1}(500/624.4) \approx 38.66^\circ\ \text{to horizontal}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Force Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the FBD (Loss of 2–3 marks):** Always draw a clear FBD with labeled forces, directions, and points of application before writing any equation. Even if the calculation is correct, marks are deducted for a missing FBD.
> 2. **Sign Convention Errors (Loss of 1–2 marks):** Be consistent. Use CCW as positive for moments. Wrong signs propagate to wrong answers.
> 3. **Quadrant Blindness in $\tan^{-1}$ (Loss of 1–2 marks):** If $R_x < 0$, add $180^\circ$ to the calculator output. Always state the quadrant explicitly.
> 4. **Mixing Units (Loss of 1 mark):** Convert kN to N or vice versa consistently. A common error is mixing $mm$ and $m$ in lever-arm calculations.
> 5. **Forgetting to State Assumptions (Loss of 0.5–1 mark):** State "Assume smooth/frictionless contact," "Rigid body," etc., wherever applicable.
> 6. **Incomplete Final Answer (Loss of 1 mark):** Always box the final answer with both **magnitude** and **direction** explicitly stated.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Force (Module 1, PCAUT521):**
> - **Force is a vector** — always specify magnitude, direction, and point of application.
> - **SI unit = Newton (N)**; $1\ \text{N} = 1\ \text{kg} \cdot \text{m/s}^2$.
> - **Newton's 2nd Law:** $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ is the central equation; momentum form is $\vec{F} = d\vec{p}/dt$.
> - **Three Newton's Laws** are non-negotiable for KTU exams.
> - **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** is the most critical first step — without it, no marks.
> - **Equilibrium equations** (2D rigid body): $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M_O = 0$.
> - **Varignon's Theorem** simplifies moment calculations for concurrent force systems.
> - **Lami's Theorem** is restricted to exactly **three concurrent coplanar forces in equilibrium**.
> - **Parallelogram Law** gives the resultant of *two* forces; extend to $n$ forces via superposition.
> - **Couple** has zero net force but a non-zero moment ($M = F \cdot d$), and its moment is **point-independent**.
> - **Resultant magnitude** uses Pythagorean theorem: $R = \sqrt{R_x^2 + R_y^2}$.
> - **Resultant direction** uses $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(R_y/R_x)$ with **quadrant correction**.
> - **Moment of force** in 2D: $M_O = x F_y - y F_x$ (CCW positive).
> - **Always box your final answer** with units clearly stated.
> - **Friction**, normal reaction, and weight (W = mg) are the most common external forces in mechatronic systems.
> - **Engineering applications** of force analysis: robot arms, grippers, conveyor systems, cam-follower mechanisms, hydraulic/pneumatic actuators, load cells, structural trusses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity and Displacement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT521 - MECHATRONICS AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Velocity and Displacement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining the Two Fundamental Kinematic Variables

In any mechatronic system, before we can **control**, **regulate**, or **automate** a physical process, we must first be able to *measure* the state of that process. The two most fundamental kinematic state variables in classical mechanics are **displacement** and **velocity**. They form the bedrock of feedback in servo systems, robotic arms, CNC machines, and automotive suspensions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> - **Displacement** is the shortest straight-line vector quantity representing the change in position of a body or point with respect to a chosen reference frame.
> - **Velocity** is the time-rate of change of displacement; mathematically, it is the first derivative of position with respect to time.

In SI units:
- **Displacement ($x$ or $s$):** measured in **metres (m)**
- **Velocity ($v$ or $\dot{x}$):** measured in **metres per second (m/s)**
- **Acceleration ($a$ or $\ddot{x}$):** measured in **metres per second squared (m/s²)**

The kinematic chain is therefore:

$$x(t) \xrightarrow{\dfrac{d}{dt}} v(t) \xrightarrow{\dfrac{d}{dt}} a(t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> In mechatronics, we are not merely *defining* these quantities; we are engineering **transducers** (sensors) that convert them into proportional electrical signals (Voltage, Current, Frequency, or Digital pulses) so that a microcontroller or PLC can read them.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Speedometer and the Odometer

Imagine you are driving a car:
- The **odometer** on your dashboard tells you the *total distance travelled* — this is the **displacement**.
- The **speedometer** tells you *how fast* your position is changing right now — this is the **velocity**.

If you integrate the speedometer reading over time, you get back the odometer reading. This "integration" relationship is exactly what your car's trip computer does electronically, and it is the same mathematical duality that exists between a velocity sensor and a displacement sensor.

> [!TIP]
> A **displacement sensor** can be mathematically turned into a **velocity sensor** by adding a differentiator (a circuit that computes $\frac{dx}{dt}$), and vice-versa using an integrator. This is the conceptual foundation of tachometer-less speed estimation in modern drives.

## 1.3 Why Mechatronics Engineers Care

In a closed-loop control system (e.g., a robotic arm joint), the controller needs real-time position feedback. Typical signal-flow chain:

**Physical Motion → Sensor → Signal Conditioning → ADC → Microcontroller → Controller Algorithm → Actuator**

Both velocity and displacement sensors sit at the *front-end* of this chain. Without accurate measurement, the famous feedback equation:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\, dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

becomes meaningless because we have no way to compute the error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$, where $y(t)$ is the measured displacement or velocity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundation

The basic kinematic relationships are:

$$v(t) = \frac{dx(t)}{dt} = \dot{x}(t)$$

$$a(t) = \frac{dv(t)}{dt} = \frac{d^2 x(t)}{dt^2} = \ddot{x}(t)$$

For uniform motion:

$$v = \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} = \frac{x_2 - x_1}{t_2 - t_1}$$

For constant acceleration:

$$v = u + at, \qquad x = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2, \qquad v^2 = u^2 + 2ax$$

## 2.2 The Sensor Transduction Principle

A displacement/velocity transducer essentially obeys the linear input–output law:

$$y(t) = K \, x(t) + b$$

where:
- $y(t)$ is the **output electrical signal** (V, mA, Hz, or pulses)
- $x(t)$ is the **input measurand** (m or m/s)
- $K$ is the **static sensitivity** (units of output per unit input)
- $b$ is the **bias / offset** (ideally zero)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linearity, Resolution, Hysteresis, and Bandwidth** are the four key static and dynamic performance parameters any KTU examiner loves to test.

## 2.3 LVDT — Linear Variable Differential Transformer (Displacement)

An LVDT is the *gold standard* contactless displacement sensor in mechatronics.

- **Construction:** One primary winding, two identical secondary windings (S1, S2) wound on a shared cylindrical former. A soft-iron core slides along the axis.
- **Working:** The primary is excited by an AC carrier (typically 1–10 kHz). The core's axial position $x$ couples flux unequally into S1 and S2. The differential output is:

$$V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2}$$

- When the core is at the **null position** (centred), $V_{out} \approx 0$.
- When the core moves by $x$, the output becomes proportional to $x$ with a definite phase (180° out of phase either side of null), allowing direction sensing.

## 2.4 Tachogenerator (Velocity)

A tachogenerator is essentially a small DC or AC generator whose output voltage is proportional to the rotational speed of its shaft.

**DC Tachogenerator:**

$$V_g = K_g \, \omega = K_g \, \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$$

where $N$ is in RPM. The polarity reverses with the direction of rotation, giving both magnitude and sign of velocity.

## 2.5 Encoders (Displacement & Velocity)

- **Incremental Encoder:** Produces two square-wave trains A and B in quadrature. Counting pulses gives *relative* displacement. Frequency of pulses gives velocity.
- **Absolute Encoder:** Produces a unique Gray/binary code for each angular position; gives *absolute* displacement even after power loss.

**Resolution of an encoder:**

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{N_{ppr}}$$

$$\text{Linear resolution} = \frac{\pi D}{N_{ppr}}$$

where $N_{ppr}$ is pulses per revolution and $D$ is the wheel diameter for a linear system.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Sensor | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Practical Use |
|---|-------------------|--------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Average Velocity | $v = \dfrac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}$ | $v$ in m/s, $x$ in m, $t$ in s | General kinematics |
| 2 | Instantaneous Velocity | $v(t) = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$ | Same as above | Control & feedback |
| 3 | Acceleration | $a(t) = \dfrac{dv(t)}{dt} = \dfrac{d^2 x}{dt^2}$ | $a$ in m/s² | Dynamic systems |
| 4 | LVDT Output | $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$ | $K_{LVDT}$ in V/m | Linear displacement |
| 5 | DC Tacho EMF | $E_g = K_g \, \omega$ | $K_g$ in V·s/rad, $\omega$ in rad/s | Angular velocity |
| 6 | Tacho EMF in RPM | $E_g = \dfrac{K_g \, 2\pi N}{60}$ | $N$ in RPM | Industrial speed sensing |
| 7 | Encoder Angular Resolution | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{N_{ppr}}$ | $\theta_{res}$ in degrees | Position resolution |
| 8 | Encoder Linear Resolution | $L_{res} = \dfrac{\pi D}{N_{ppr}}$ | $D$ in m, $L_{res}$ in m | Linear position |
| 9 | Encoder Velocity (from pulse freq.) | $v = \dfrac{f_{pulse}}{N_{ppr}} \times \pi D$ | $f_{pulse}$ in Hz | Velocity from digital pulse train |
| 10 | Doppler Radar | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v f_0}{c}$ | $f_0$ source freq, $c$ speed of light | Non-contact velocity |
| 11 | Eddy-Current Sensor | $\dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = k \cdot x$ | Inductance change with gap $x$ | Vibration / displacement |
| 12 | Sensor Sensitivity | $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Output/Input units | Static performance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notational Rule for KTU:** Always explicitly state units. Marks are routinely docked when the sensitivity $K$ is written without units, since dimensional consistency is part of the answer.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LVDT** → used in aircraft altimeters, hydraulic cylinder position feedback, force-balancing instruments.
- **Tachogenerator** → used in paper mills, rolling mills, elevators for closed-loop speed control.
- **Optical Encoders** → used in CNC machines, robotics, 3-D printers, BLDC motor commutation.
- **Doppler Radar** → used in traffic enforcement, industrial flow metering, vehicle anti-collision.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — LVDT Output Voltage as a Function of Core Displacement

### Statement
Show that the differential output voltage of an LVDT is a linear function of core displacement $x$ from the null position.

### Setup
- Let the primary excitation be $V_p \sin(\omega_c t)$ with carrier angular frequency $\omega_c$.
- Let the two secondary windings be wound in **opposite phase** (S1 and S2).
- Let the mutual inductance between primary and S1 be $M_1$ and between primary and S2 be $M_2$.

The induced EMFs in the secondaries are:

$$e_1 = j \omega_c M_1 \, I_p, \qquad e_2 = j \omega_c M_2 \, I_p$$

The differential output is:

$$V_{out} = e_1 - e_2 = j \omega_c I_p \, (M_1 - M_2)$$

When the core is exactly at the null (centre), symmetry forces $M_1 = M_2 = M$, hence $V_{out} = 0$.

When the core moves by a small displacement $x$ to the right, the coupling to S1 **increases** linearly and coupling to S2 **decreases** linearly (for small $x$ inside the linear range):

$$M_1 = M + K_{LVDT} \cdot x$$

$$M_2 = M - K_{LVDT} \cdot x$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= j \omega_c I_p \, \big[ (M + K_{LVDT} x) - (M - K_{LVDT} x) \big] \\
&= j \omega_c I_p \, (2 K_{LVDT} x) \\
&= 2 j \omega_c I_p K_{LVDT} \, x
\end{aligned}
$$

The **magnitude** of the output is:

$$\vert V_{out} \vert = 2 \omega_c I_p K_{LVDT} \, \vert x \vert$$

The **phase** of the output (relative to the primary excitation) tells us the **direction** of motion:
- $x > 0$ (core to the right) → output in phase with primary.
- $x < 0$ (core to the left) → output 180° out of phase.

Hence, the LVDT gives a **linear, bipolar, direction-sensitive** output proportional to displacement, and the constant of proportionality (sensitivity) is:

$$K = 2 \omega_c I_p K_{LVDT} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{V}}{\text{m}} \right]$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** The above derivation is the *complete* version. Most textbooks skip the substitution step; showing it explicitly fetches full marks in KTU valuation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DC Tachogenerator EMF

### Statement
A DC tachogenerator has 4 poles, with 200 armature conductors. The flux per pole is 20 mWb, and the armature is wave-wound. The speed is 1500 RPM. Calculate the generated EMF. (Take $A = 2$ for wave winding.)

### Governing Equation (Faraday's Law for Rotating Machines)

$$E_g = \frac{P \, \Phi \, Z \, N}{60 \, A}$$

### Step-by-Step Substitution

Given:
- $P = 4$ (poles)
- $\Phi = 20 \text{ mWb} = 20 \times 10^{-3}$ **Wb**
- $Z = 200$ (conductors)
- $N = 1500$ **RPM**
- $A = 2$ (wave winding)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_g &= \frac{4 \times (20 \times 10^{-3}) \times 200 \times 1500}{60 \times 2} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 0.020 \times 200 \times 1500}{120} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 0.020 \times 300000}{120} \\
&= \frac{4 \times 6000}{120} \\
&= \frac{24000}{120} \\
&= 200 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $E_g = 200 \text{ V}$ at 1500 RPM.

The sensitivity of this tachogenerator is:

$$K_g = \frac{E_g}{\omega} = \frac{E_g}{2\pi N / 60} = \frac{200}{2\pi \times 1500 / 60} = \frac{200}{157.08} \approx 1.273 \text{ V·s/rad}$$

Therefore, at *any* other speed $N_2$ (RPM), the EMF is:

$$E_{g2} = \frac{200 \times N_2}{1500} = 0.1333 \, N_2 \;\; \text{Volts}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Encoder Resolution and Velocity

A linear incremental encoder is mounted on a wheel of diameter $D = 50$ mm and produces $N_{ppr} = 1000$ pulses per revolution. The wheel rolls without slipping.

### Part (a) — Linear Resolution

The circumference traversed per revolution is:

$$C = \pi D = \pi \times 50 \text{ mm} = 50\pi \text{ mm} \approx 157.08 \text{ mm}$$

Hence the linear resolution (distance per pulse) is:

$$L_{res} = \frac{\pi D}{N_{ppr}} = \frac{50\pi}{1000} = \frac{\pi}{20} \text{ mm} \approx 0.1571 \text{ mm / pulse}$$

### Part (b) — Velocity from Pulse Frequency

Suppose the pulse train frequency is $f_{pulse} = 4$ kHz.

Number of revolutions per second:

$$f_{rev} = \frac{f_{pulse}}{N_{ppr}} = \frac{4000}{1000} = 4 \text{ rev/s}$$

Linear velocity:

$$v = f_{rev} \times C = 4 \times 50\pi \text{ mm/s} = 200\pi \text{ mm/s} \approx 628.3 \text{ mm/s} = 0.6283 \text{ m/s}$$

## 3.4 Summary Table of Key Derivations

| # | Topic | Core Result | Engineering Meaning |
|---|-------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | LVDT Output | $V_{out} = 2 \omega_c I_p K_{LVDT} \, x$ | Linear displacement measurement |
| 2 | DC Tacho EMF | $E_g = \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}$ | Speed-to-voltage conversion |
| 3 | Encoder Linear Resolution | $L_{res} = \dfrac{\pi D}{N_{ppr}}$ | Smallest measurable position step |
| 4 | Encoder Velocity | $v = \dfrac{f_{pulse} \pi D}{N_{ppr}}$ | Speed from pulse train |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Displacement & Velocity Measurement Chain

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **complete functional flow** of how a physical displacement/velocity is transduced, conditioned, and delivered to a controller — the kind of block-level architecture KTU expects in Module 1 descriptive answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Physical Quantity: x or v"] --> B["Primary Sensor: LVDT, Encoder, or Tacho"]
    B --> C["Signal Conditioning: Amplifier and Filter"]
    C --> D["ADC: Analog to Digital Converter"]
    D --> E["Microcontroller or PLC: Reads digital value"]
    E --> F["Controller Algorithm: PID or Fuzzy"]
    F --> G["DAC or PWM: Command Signal"]
    G --> H["Power Amplifier"]
    H --> I["Actuator: Motor or Hydraulic Cylinder"]
    I -.-> J["Mechanical Load: New Displacement or Velocity"]
    J -. Feedback Loop .-> A
```

## 4.2 LVDT Internal Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LVDT_Unit
        P["Primary Winding: AC Excitation Vp sin wt"]
        S1["Secondary S1"]
        S2["Secondary S2"]
        CORE["Soft Iron Core: Position x"]
    end
    P --> CORE
    CORE --> S1
    CORE --> S2
    S1 --> DIFF["Differential Amplifier: e1 minus e2"]
    S2 --> DIFF
    DIFF --> OUT["Vout: Proportional to x and Direction Sensitive"]
```

## 4.3 DC Tachogenerator Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Rotating Shaft: Angular velocity omega"] --> B["Armature Rotating in Magnetic Field Phi"]
    B --> C["Commutator and Brushes: Rectify AC to DC"]
    C --> D["Output Terminals: Eg = Kg omega"]
    D --> E["Load or ADC: Speed Feedback to Controller"]
```

## 4.4 Quadrature Encoder Channel Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Encoder_Disk
        LED["LED Light Source"]
        SLIT["Slit Pattern: Nppr lines per rev"]
        PD1["Photodiode A"]
        PD2["Photodiode B"]
        LED --> SLIT
        SLIT --> PD1
        SLIT --> PD2
    end
    PD1 --> QA["Channel A: Square Wave"]
    PD2 --> QB["Channel B: Square Wave 90 deg Phase Shift"]
    QA --> DECODER["Quadrature Decoder: Direction and Counts"]
    QB --> DECODER
    DECODER --> POS["Position: Pulse Count"]
    DECODER --> VEL["Velocity: Pulse Frequency"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** In descriptive 14-mark questions, examiners award full marks only when the answer includes a *labelled functional block diagram* showing the signal flow. The above four diagrams cover the four most-frequently-asked sensors.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define displacement and velocity. State the SI units and the mathematical relationship between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Displacement** is the vector quantity describing the change in position of a body with respect to a reference frame. SI unit: **metre (m)**. (1 mark)
- **Velocity** is the time-rate of change of displacement. SI unit: **metre per second (m/s)**. (1 mark)
- The mathematical relationship is $v(t) = \dfrac{dx(t)}{dt}$. Velocity is the first derivative of displacement with respect to time, and conversely, displacement is the time-integral of velocity. (1 mark)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three transducers used for the measurement of linear displacement, and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **LVDT** – Advantage: frictionless, infinite resolution, robust; gives direction-sensitive output. (1 mark)
2. **Linear Optical Encoder** – Advantage: high accuracy, digital output, immune to electrical noise. (1 mark)
3. **Capacitive Displacement Sensor** – Advantage: very high resolution (sub-micrometre), non-contact. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

### Question
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of an **LVDT** for displacement measurement. Derive the expression for its output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DC tachogenerator has 6 poles, 240 armature conductors with wave winding, and a flux per pole of 25 mWb. Calculate the generated EMF at 1200 RPM. Also find the tachogenerator sensitivity $K_g$ in V·s/rad. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — LVDT

**Construction (2 marks):**
The LVDT consists of a single primary winding P, two identical secondary windings S1 and S2, all wound on a common cylindrical former. A movable soft-iron core slides along the axis. The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition** so that the induced EMFs subtract. An AC excitation (1–10 kHz) is applied to P.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- When the core is at the **null (centre) position**, the mutual coupling to S1 and S2 is equal, so $V_{out} = 0$.
- When the core moves by $x$ to the right, coupling to S1 **increases** and to S2 **decreases** linearly (for small $x$).
- The differential output becomes proportional to $x$, with phase indicating direction.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= e_1 - e_2 = j\omega_c I_p (M_1 - M_2) \\
M_1 &= M + K_{LVDT} x, \quad M_2 = M - K_{LVDT} x \\
V_{out} &= j\omega_c I_p \cdot 2 K_{LVDT} \, x \\
\vert V_{out} \vert &= 2 \omega_c I_p K_{LVDT} \, \vert x \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, $V_{out}$ is **linear** in $x$ and direction-sensitive via phase.

---

#### Part (b) — DC Tachogenerator

**Given:** $P = 6$, $\Phi = 25 \text{ mWb} = 25 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb, $Z = 240$, $A = 2$ (wave), $N = 1200$ RPM.

**Generated EMF (4 marks):**

$$
E_g = \frac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A} = \frac{6 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \times 240 \times 1200}{60 \times 2}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \frac{6 \times 0.025 \times 240 \times 1200}{120} \\
&= \frac{6 \times 0.025 \times 288000}{120} \\
&= \frac{6 \times 7200}{120} \\
&= \frac{43200}{120} \\
&= 360 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sensitivity $K_g$ (3 marks):**

Angular speed in rad/s:

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1200}{60} = 40\pi \approx 125.66 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$K_g = \frac{E_g}{\omega} = \frac{360}{125.66} \approx 2.865 \text{ V·s/rad}$$

**Final Answer:** $E_g = 360$ V, $K_g \approx 2.865$ V·s/rad.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

### Question
**(a)** Describe the working principle of a **DC tachogenerator** as a velocity transducer. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An incremental optical encoder is mounted on a shaft of a motor. The encoder has 1024 pulses per revolution. The shaft drives a linear stage through a lead screw of pitch 5 mm. If the pulse train frequency is 2048 Hz, determine:
1. The angular velocity of the motor in RPM.
2. The linear velocity of the stage in mm/s.
3. The linear resolution of the stage in mm. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — DC Tachogenerator

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
A DC tachogenerator is essentially a small permanent-magnet or separately-excited DC generator. When its armature is rotated at angular velocity $\omega$ in a magnetic field $\Phi$, an EMF is induced per Faraday's law:

$$E_g = K_g \, \omega = \frac{P \Phi Z}{2\pi A} \cdot \omega$$

The output voltage is directly proportional to speed, and the **polarity reverses** with direction of rotation, allowing direct sensing of both magnitude and sign of velocity. It gives an **analog voltage** output and is widely used in closed-loop speed control of DC motors.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Simple, rugged, low cost.
- Continuous analog output proportional to speed.
- No external power supply required (for PM-type).
- Direction information via polarity.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- Output contains **ripples** due to commutator segments → requires filtering.
- Brush wear → maintenance issue.
- Temperature drift of PM field flux.
- Not suitable for very low speeds (output becomes too small).

---

#### Part (b) — Encoder with Lead Screw

**Given:** $N_{ppr} = 1024$ pulses/rev, lead screw pitch $P_{ls} = 5$ mm/rev, $f_{pulse} = 2048$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Angular velocity (2 marks):**

Revolutions per second:

$$f_{rev} = \frac{f_{pulse}}{N_{ppr}} = \frac{2048}{1024} = 2 \text{ rev/s}$$

In RPM:

$$N = f_{rev} \times 60 = 2 \times 60 = 120 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2 — Linear velocity (2.5 marks):**

Linear velocity of stage = rev/s × pitch:

$$v = f_{rev} \times P_{ls} = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 3 — Linear resolution (2.5 marks):**

Distance per pulse = pitch / pulses per rev:

$$L_{res} = \frac{P_{ls}}{N_{ppr}} = \frac{5}{1024} \approx 4.883 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} \approx 4.88 \, \mu\text{m}$$

**Final Answer:** $N = 120$ RPM, $v = 10$ mm/s, $L_{res} \approx 4.88$ µm.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the formula constants** — In the DC tacho EMF equation, students frequently write $E_g = \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{A}$ instead of the correct $\dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}$. The factor 60 comes from converting RPM to revolutions per second. This costs **2 marks** typically.
> 2. **Wave vs Lap winding confusion** — Wave = $A=2$, Lap = $A=P$. Mixing these up leads to numerically wrong answers.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency in LVDT** — $K_{LVDT}$ has units of **V/m**, not V. Always state units in the final answer.
> 4. **Encoder direction sense** — Students often forget that quadrature decoding is required to obtain *direction* from A and B channels.
> 5. **Linear vs angular confusion** — When a rotary encoder is mounted on a lead screw, the linear resolution uses the **pitch**, not $\pi D$. Read the question carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Displacement** = change in position (m); **Velocity** = rate of change of displacement (m/s).
- [ ] **LVDT** gives **linear, bipolar, direction-sensitive** output: $V_{out} = 2 \omega_c I_p K_{LVDT} \cdot x$.
- [ ] LVDT null position → $V_{out} = 0$; phase shift indicates direction.
- [ ] **DC Tachogenerator EMF:** $E_g = \dfrac{P \Phi Z N}{60 A}$; sensitivity $K_g = \dfrac{E_g}{\omega}$ (V·s/rad).
- [ ] Polarity of tacho output reverses with rotation direction → gives sign of velocity.
- [ ] **Wave winding** → $A = 2$; **Lap winding** → $A = P$.
- [ ] **Encoder angular resolution** $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{N_{ppr}}$.
- [ ] **Encoder linear resolution** $L_{res} = \dfrac{\pi D}{N_{ppr}}$ (for a wheel) or $\dfrac{P_{ls}}{N_{ppr}}$ (for a lead screw).
- [ ] **Encoder velocity** $v = \dfrac{f_{pulse} \cdot \pi D}{N_{ppr}}$ or $v = f_{pulse} \cdot \dfrac{P_{ls}}{N_{ppr}}$.
- [ ] **Quadrature decoding** using A and B channels gives direction and 4× resolution.
- [ ] Always mention **units** in the final answer (V/m, V·s/rad, mm, mm/s, RPM).
- [ ] **Doppler radar** uses $f_d = \dfrac{2 v f_0}{c}$ for non-contact velocity.
- [ ] **Capacitive, Eddy-current, and Hall-effect** sensors are alternative non-contact displacement sensors.
- [ ] In a closed-loop mechatronic system, the sensor output is the feedback $y(t)$ in $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$.
- [ ] Frequency-domain performance is governed by the sensor's **bandwidth** — must be ≥ 5× the system bandwidth to avoid phase lag.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT521 - MECHATRONICS AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Temperature

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature** is a fundamental, intensive scalar physical quantity that quantitatively expresses the *degree of hotness* or *coldness* of a body or thermodynamic system. From the kinetic theory perspective, it is directly proportional to the **average translational kinetic energy** of the random microscopic motions of the constituent particles (atoms/molecules) of the system. From a zeroth-law-of-thermodynamics viewpoint, temperature is the unique property that determines whether two systems brought into thermal contact will be in **Thermal Equilibrium**.

In the formal SI framework, Temperature is one of the **seven base quantities**, and its SI unit is the **Kelvin (K)**. The thermodynamic (absolute) scale is uniquely defined by the triple point of water at exactly **273.16 K** and absolute zero at **0 K**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a busy train station at two different times of the day:

*   **Morning Rush (High Temperature):** Passengers (molecules) are rushing, colliding, and jostling aggressively. Their average speed/energy is **high**. The "agitation level" of the crowd is high → **High Temperature**.
*   **Late Night (Low Temperature):** Passengers are slow, sluggish, barely moving. Their average speed/energy is **low** → **Low Temperature**.

The thermometer is essentially a "crowd-anger-meter" that quantifies this average agitation. A body at higher temperature will *transfer heat* to a body at lower temperature until both reach the same "agitation level" — this is **Thermal Equilibrium** (Zeroth Law).

### 1.3 Primary Temperature Scales (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> Four scales are mandated for KTU study. Each scale has a defined *reference point* and a *unit size*.

| Scale | Symbol | Defined By | Reference Points | Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Celsius** | °C | Anders Celsius | 0 °C (ice point), 100 °C (steam point) | 1 °C = 1 K |
| **Fahrenheit** | °F | Daniel Gabriel Fahrenheit | 32 °F (ice), 212 °F (steam) | 1 °F = 5/9 K |
| **Kelvin (SI)** | K | Lord Kelvin | 273.16 K (triple point of water) | **SI base unit** |
| **Rankine** | °R | William Rankine | 459.67 °R (absolute zero) | 1 °R = 5/9 K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test *only* the upper fixed point (steam point) and lower fixed point (ice point) of the Celsius scale: **0 °C and 100 °C**, mapped to **273.15 K and 373.15 K** respectively. Absolute zero = **0 K = −273.15 °C = −459.67 °F = 0 °R**.

### 1.4 Classification of Temperature Measurement Principles

Temperature transducers are classified based on the physical principle exploited:

1.  **Thermoelectric Effect** → Thermocouples (Seebeck Effect)
2.  **Resistance Variation** → RTDs (Positive coefficient metals), Thermistors (NTC/PTC semiconductors)
3.  **Expansion of Fluids/Solids** → Liquid-in-glass thermometers, Bimetallic strips, Gas thermometers
4.  **Radiation Pyrometry** → Total radiation pyrometers, Optical pyrometers (for high temperatures, no contact)
5.  **Semiconductor Bandgap** → IC temperature sensors (LM35, AD590, DS18B20)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Temperature Measurement

The measurement chain operates on a standardized pipeline. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that every temperature sensor must conform to specific **static** (steady-state) and **dynamic** (transient) performance metrics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Static Characteristics (evaluated at steady state):** Range, Span, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Linearity, Hysteresis, Repeatability, Resolution, Drift.
> **Dynamic Characteristics (evaluated during change):** Time constant, Rise time, Settling time, Bandwidth — often modeled as a **first-order system** with transfer function:
> $$\frac{T(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

### 2.2 Sensor-by-Sensor Theoretical Breakdown

#### A. Thermocouple (Thermoelectric Principle)
A thermocouple consists of two **dissimilar metals** joined at a hot (measuring) junction. The open-circuit ends (cold/reference junction) develop an EMF proportional to the temperature difference due to the **Seebeck Effect**.

*   **Why it works:** Charge carrier diffusion rates differ between the two metals, generating a net voltage.
*   **How it works:** The generated Seebeck voltage is a non-linear function of $\Delta T$, requiring polynomial calibration tables (ITS-90).
*   **EMF Equation (approximate):**

$$E = \alpha \Delta T + \beta (\Delta T)^2$$

where $\alpha, \beta$ are Seebeck coefficients. Modern high-accuracy polynomial expansions use 8th to 14th order terms.

#### B. RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector)
An RTD exploits the **linear, monotonic increase of electrical resistance** of pure metals (Platinum, Nickel, Copper) with temperature.

*   **Why it works:** Increased lattice vibration (phonons) at higher temperatures scatters conduction electrons more effectively, increasing resistance.
*   **How it works:** A constant excitation current is passed; the voltage drop is proportional to temperature.
*   **Dominant Equation (Linear approximation):**

$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\right]$$

For higher accuracy, the **Callendar–Van Dusen Equation** is used:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + AT + BT^2 - 100\,C\,T^3 + CT^4\right]$$

#### C. Thermistor (Thermal Resistor)
A thermistor is a semiconductor bead whose resistance **decreases exponentially** with temperature (NTC type — most common).

*   **Why it works:** Increased temperature excites more charge carriers across the semiconductor bandgap, drastically lowering resistance.
*   **How it works:** Exponential sensitivity gives ~10× higher sensitivity than RTDs but over a narrower range.

$$R_T = R_0 \exp\left[B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

#### D. IC Temperature Sensor
Bandgap reference circuits produce a voltage or current output **linear in Celsius or Kelvin** (e.g., LM35 outputs 10 mV/°C).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$ | Celsius to Kelvin | K |
| 2 | $T(°R) = T(K) \times \frac{9}{5}$ | Kelvin to Rankine | °R |
| 3 | $T(°F) = \frac{9}{5}T(°C) + 32$ | Celsius to Fahrenheit | °F |
| 4 | $T(°C) = \frac{5}{9}\left[T(°F) - 32\right]$ | Fahrenheit to Celsius | °C |
| 5 | $E = \alpha \Delta T + \beta \Delta T^2$ | Thermocouple EMF | mV |
| 6 | $R_T = R_0[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$ | RTD Resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | $R_T = R_0 \exp\!\left[B\!\left(\frac{1}{T}-\frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$ | Thermistor Resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | $\frac{1}{T} = A + B\ln R + C(\ln R)^3$ | Steinhart–Hart (Thermistor) | $K^{-1}$ |
| 9 | $G(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ | First-order thermal system | — |
| 10 | $\tau = \frac{mc}{hA}$ | Thermal time constant | s |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

*   **Automotive:** RTD in cylinder head for engine control; thermistor in coolant temperature sensor.
*   **Industrial Furnaces:** Type-K/S/R thermocouples withstand > 1500 °C.
*   **Medical:** IC sensors (LM35) in portable clinical thermometers.
*   **Aerospace & Spacecraft:** Radiation pyrometers measure re-entry temperatures (> 2000 °C).
*   **HVAC:** NTC thermistors in air-conditioners and refrigeration thermostats.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Generic Linear Interpolation Between Two Fixed Points

> [!NOTE]
> Most temperature scales (Celsius, Fahrenheit) are defined using **two fixed reference points** — the ice point and the steam point of pure water at standard atmospheric pressure.

**Step 1 — Define the Linear Mapping Assumption**
Temperature scales are assumed to vary **linearly** between two reference points.

$$T(X) = T_{\text{lower}} + \left[\frac{T_{\text{upper}} - T_{\text{lower}}}{X_{\text{upper}} - X_{\text{lower}}}\right] \cdot (X - X_{\text{lower}})$$

**Step 2 — Apply to Celsius-to-Fahrenheit Conversion**
Lower point: $0\,°C = 32\,°F$ ; Upper point: $100\,°C = 212\,°F$.

$$\begin{aligned}
T(°F) &= 32 + \left[\frac{212 - 32}{100 - 0}\right] (T(°C) - 0) \\
&= 32 + \frac{180}{100}\, T(°C) \\
&= 32 + \frac{9}{5}\, T(°C)
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**
$$T(°F) = \frac{9}{5}\, T(°C) + 32$$

### 3.2 Derivation II: Inverse Conversion (°F → °C)

Starting from the result of Derivation I and rearranging:

$$\begin{aligned}
T(°F) - 32 &= \frac{9}{5}\, T(°C) \\
T(°C) &= \frac{5}{9}\bigl[T(°F) - 32\bigr]
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Derivation III: RTD Linear Resistance Equation from First Principles

Consider a pure metal wire with resistance $R$ at temperature $T$. By the definition of temperature coefficient of resistance $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = \frac{1}{R_0} \lim_{\Delta T \to 0} \frac{\Delta R}{\Delta T}$$

**Step 1 — Write the differential form**

$$dR = \alpha\, R_0\, dT$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from reference $T_0$ to current $T$** (assuming constant $\alpha$)

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{R_0}^{R_T} dR &= \alpha R_0 \int_{T_0}^{T} dT \\
R_T - R_0 &= \alpha R_0 (T - T_0)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R_T$**

$$R_T = R_0\bigl[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\bigr]$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{R_T = R_0\bigl[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\bigr]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** For Platinum RTD (PT100), $\alpha = 0.00385\, Ω/Ω/°C$ (IEC 60751 standard). $R_0 = 100\, Ω$ at $T_0 = 0\,°C$. Always state the reference temperature explicitly.

### 3.4 Derivation IV: Callendar–Van Dusen Equation (Non-Linear RTD)

For higher accuracy over a wider range (especially below 0 °C), the linear equation is inadequate. Platinum's resistivity has a small quadratic and cubic dependence on temperature. The empirical equation:

$$R_T = R_0\bigl[1 + AT + BT^2 - 100\,C\,T^3 + CT^4\bigr]$$

where:
*   $A = \alpha + \alpha\delta$
*   $B = -\alpha\delta$
*   $C$ is non-zero only for $T < 0\,°C$
*   $\alpha, \delta$ are **Callendar constants** (for platinum, $\alpha = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$, $\delta = 1.495$).

### 3.5 Derivation V: Thermistor B-Parameter Equation

A semiconductor thermistor's conductivity follows an Arrhenius-type law:

$$\sigma(T) = \sigma_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2k_BT}\right)$$

Since $R \propto 1/\sigma$:

$$R(T) = R_\infty \exp\!\left(\frac{E_g}{2k_BT}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Define the B-parameter** as $B = E_g / 2k_B$.

$$R(T) = R_\infty \exp\!\left(\frac{B}{T}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at reference $T_0$**

$$R_0 = R_\infty \exp\!\left(\frac{B}{T_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Divide and rearrange**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{R(T)}{R_0} &= \exp\!\left[B\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right] \\
\Rightarrow \quad R(T) &= R_0 \exp\!\left[B\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute temperatures** ($T$ in Kelvin) MUST be used inside the exponential — never Celsius. This is the most common KTU exam blunder.

### 3.6 Derivation VI: Steinhart–Hart Equation (High-Precision Thermistor)

A third-order empirical correction to the B-parameter equation:

$$\frac{1}{T} = A + B\ln R + C(\ln R)^3$$

Coefficients $A, B, C$ are obtained by **three-point calibration** at known temperatures. This gives accuracy better than $\pm 0.01\,°C$ over a 100 °C span.

### 3.7 Derivation VII: Thermal Time Constant

A thermal sensor in a fluid medium obeys energy balance:

$$mc \frac{dT}{dt} = hA\bigl(T_{\text{fluid}} - T\bigr)$$

where:
*   $m$ = mass of sensor (kg)
*   $c$ = specific heat (J/kg·K)
*   $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
*   $A$ = surface area (m²)
*   $T_{\text{fluid}}$ = step input temperature (K)
*   $T$ = sensor temperature (K)

**Step 1 — Define the time constant** $\tau = mc / hA$

$$\tau \frac{dT}{dt} + T = T_{\text{fluid}}$$

**Step 2 — Take the Laplace transform** (assuming initial temperature $T(0)=0$)

$$\tau s\,T(s) + T(s) = \frac{T_{\text{fluid}}}{s}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the transfer function**

$$\frac{T(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{1}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Final Result:** A temperature sensor is a **first-order lag system** with steady-state gain $K = 1$. The step response is:

$$T(t) = T_{\text{fluid}}\bigl(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\bigr)$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests whether students can recognize that $T(t) = 0.632\, T_{\text{fluid}}$ at $t = \tau$ (the **63.2 % rise time criterion**).

### 3.8 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A PT100 RTD has $R_0 = 100\, Ω$ at $0\,°C$ and $\alpha = 0.00385\, Ω/Ω/°C$. Find its resistance at $T = 150\,°C$.

**Solution:**
