$$R_{150} = 100\bigl[1 + 0.00385 \times (150 - 0)\bigr]$$

$$R_{150} = 100\bigl[1 + 0.5775\bigr]$$

$$R_{150} = 100 \times 1.5775 = 157.75\, Ω$$

**Conversion check using the Callendar–Van Dusen equation:**
With $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$, and using $R_{150} \approx 157.31\, Ω$ — a small non-linear correction.

---

### 3.9 Comparative Analytical Matrix of Temperature Sensors

| Property | Thermocouple | RTD (PT100) | Thermistor (NTC) | IC Sensor (LM35) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Range** | −200 to 2300 °C | −200 to 850 °C | −55 to 150 °C | −55 to 150 °C |
| **Accuracy** | ± 0.5 to 5 °C | ± 0.1 to 1 °C | ± 0.1 to 1 °C | ± 0.5 to 2 °C |
| **Sensitivity** | 10–80 µV/°C | ~0.385 Ω/°C | ~200 Ω/°C | 10 mV/°C |
| **Linearity** | Moderate (needs TC tables) | Excellent (Pt) | Poor (needs SH) | Excellent |
| **Response Time** | Fast (0.1–10 s) | Moderate (1–10 s) | Fast (ms–s) | Fast (ms) |
| **Self-heating** | Negligible | Low | High | Low |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate–High | Low | Low |
| **KTU Exam Tip** | Cold-junction comp. needed | Standard reference | B-parameter equation | Direct in mV |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Generalized Temperature Measurement System

The block-level architecture of any temperature measurement chain maps the physical quantity through transduction, conditioning, and finally into a usable electrical signal.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Process Temperature T process] --> B[Temperature Sensor]
    B --> C[Raw Electrical Signal]
    C --> D[Signal Conditioning Amplifier and Filter]
    D --> E[Cold Junction Compensation for Thermocouple only]
    E --> F[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    F --> G[Microcontroller or PLC]
    G --> H[Display Unit]
    G --> I[Actuator or Control Valve]
    G --> J[Data Logger]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
*   `A` is the *physical quantity* (e.g., coolant at 85 °C).
*   `B` transduces it to `C` (e.g., µV for thermocouple, mV for LM35, Ω change for RTD/thermistor).
*   `D` amplifies weak signals and filters EMI.
*   `E` is critical *only* for thermocouples — reference-junction compensation must be performed, otherwise the reading is **erroneous**.
*   `F`, `G`, `H/I/J` form the digital readout and closed-loop control.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Classification Tree of Temperature Sensors

This classification tree helps KTU students organize sensor choice based on physical principle.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Temperature Sensors] --> S1[Contact Type]
    S --> S2[Non Contact Type]
    S1 --> S1A[Thermoelectric]
    S1 --> S1B[Resistive]
    S1 --> S1C[Expansion Based]
    S1 --> S1D[Semiconductor]
    S2 --> S2A[Radiation Pyrometer]
    S2 --> S2B[Optical Pyrometer]
    S2 --> S2C[Infrared Thermometer]
    S1A --> S1A1[Thermocouple Type K J R S T B E N]
    S1B --> S1B1[RTD Platinum Nickel Copper]
    S1B --> S1B2[Thermistor NTC PTC]
    S1C --> S1C1[Liquid in Glass]
    S1C --> S1C2[Bimetallic Strip]
    S1D --> S1D1[IC Sensor LM35 AD590 DS18B20]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cold-Junction Compensation Flow for Thermocouples

This is a *frequently asked 7-mark question* in KTU ESE and illustrates the reference-junction compensation algorithm.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Measured EMF E measured] --> P2[Look up Table Inverse Polynomial]
    P3[Reference Junction Temperature T ref] --> P4[Cold Junction Sensor RTD or IC]
    P4 --> P5[Cold Junction EMF E cold from Polynomial]
    P5 --> P6[Add E cold to E measured]
    P2 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Total EMF E total]
    P7 --> P8[Inverse Polynomial Solve for Hot Junction T hot]
    P8 --> P9[Final Temperature Reading]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — First-Order Thermal Step Response

Conceptual flow of how a temperature sensor responds to a step change in fluid temperature.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Step Input T fluid] --> B[Thermal Resistance R th]
    B --> C[Thermal Capacitance C th]
    C --> D[Sensor Temperature T]
    D --> E[T equals T fluid times 1 minus e to power minus t over tau]
```

Where $R_{th} = 1/(hA)$ and $C_{th} = mc$, giving the time constant $\tau = R_{th} \cdot C_{th} = mc/(hA)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A *smaller* sensor (lower $mc$) or *better* convective coupling (higher $hA$) reduces $\tau$ → faster response. Industrial bare-bead thermocouples have $\tau \approx 0.1\,s$; sheathed industrial RTDs may have $\tau \approx 5\,s$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
Define temperature. List and compare the four principal temperature scales with their unit sizes and reference points.

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Temperature is an intensive thermodynamic property that determines the direction of spontaneous heat flow between two bodies in thermal contact. Quantitatively, it is proportional to the **average kinetic energy** of molecules (kinetic theory) and is uniquely defined as the property equalized at thermal equilibrium (Zeroth Law).
>
> **Four Principal Scales:**
>
> | Scale | Unit | Lower Fixed Point | Upper Fixed Point | Conversion Anchor |
> | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Celsius | °C | 0 °C (Ice point) | 100 °C (Steam point) | 273.15 K = 0 °C |
> | Fahrenheit | °F | 32 °F (Ice point) | 212 °F (Steam point) | −40 °F = −40 °C |
> | Kelvin (SI) | K | 273.15 K (Ice) | 373.15 K (Steam) | 0 K = absolute zero |
> | Rankine | °R | 491.67 °R (Ice) | 671.67 °R (Steam) | 0 °R = absolute zero |
>
> Unit size: $1\,°C = 1\,K = 9/5\,°F = 9/5\,°R$. SI unit is **Kelvin**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**  
Explain the **Seebeck Effect** and state the approximate EMF equation for a thermocouple.

**Model Answer:**

> The **Seebeck Effect** is the phenomenon whereby a continuous EMF is generated in a circuit composed of two dissimilar conductors (or semiconductors) when their junctions are maintained at different temperatures. Charge carriers diffuse across the junction at different rates, producing a net potential. The magnitude of the EMF depends on the materials used and the temperature difference between the *hot (measuring) junction* and the *cold (reference) junction*.
>
> **EMF Equation (approximate quadratic form):**
>
> $$E = \alpha\, \Delta T + \beta\, (\Delta T)^2$$
>
> where $\alpha, \beta$ are material-specific **Seebeck coefficients**; $\Delta T = T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}}$. Real thermocouples require higher-order polynomial calibrations (ITS-90 standard) for accuracy better than $\pm 1\,°C$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model 2024]

#### Part (a) — Explain the construction, principle, and characteristics of an RTD. Derive the linear resistance-temperature equation. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** A Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD) consists of a **fine wire coil** (typically pure platinum, occasionally nickel or copper) wound on a ceramic or glass mandrel, enclosed in a protective metallic sheath. Lead wires (usually 3-wire or 4-wire configuration) connect to a Wheatstone bridge or direct ohmmeter. The platinum PT100 (100 Ω at 0 °C) is the **industrial standard**.

**Principle:** The electrical resistance of pure metals increases linearly and reproducibly with temperature. Increased lattice vibrations at higher temperatures scatter conduction electrons, raising resistivity.

**Derivation of Linear R-T Equation:**
Differential definition of temperature coefficient of resistance:

$$\alpha = \frac{1}{R_0} \frac{dR}{dT}$$

Separating and integrating:

$$\int_{R_0}^{R_T} dR = \alpha R_0 \int_{T_0}^{T} dT$$

$$R_T - R_0 = \alpha R_0 (T - T_0)$$

$$\boxed{R_T = R_0\bigl[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\bigr]}$$

**Characteristics:**
*   High accuracy (± 0.1 °C) and excellent long-term stability
*   Wide range: −200 °C to 850 °C (Platinum)
*   Linear response (Pt is the most linear metal)
*   Moderate sensitivity: 0.385 Ω/°C (PT100)
*   Self-heating error must be minimized using low excitation current (typically 1 mA)
*   Lead-wire compensation (3-wire or 4-wire Kelvin connection) is essential.

**Callendar–Van Dusen correction** (for non-linearities, especially $T < 0\,°C$):

$$R_T = R_0\bigl[1 + AT + BT^2 - 100\,C\,T^3 + CT^4\bigr]$$

---

#### Part (b) — Explain the working principle of a thermocouple. Discuss the law of intermediate metals and the law of intermediate temperatures, and explain cold-junction compensation. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:** A thermocouple uses the **Seebeck Effect**. Two dissimilar metals (e.g., Chromel and Alumel in Type-K) are joined at one end — the *measuring (hot) junction*. The other ends — the *reference (cold) junction* — are kept at a known temperature. The open-circuit voltage developed is a monotonic function of $\Delta T$.

**EMF Equation:**

$$E = \alpha \Delta T + \beta (\Delta T)^2 + \gamma (\Delta T)^3 + \dots$$

**Law of Intermediate Metals:** Inserting a third metal into the thermocouple circuit does **not** alter the net EMF, provided the two junctions of the third metal are at the **same temperature**. This permits use of connecting leads, solder, and binding posts without introducing errors.

**Law of Intermediate Temperatures:** The EMF developed by a thermocouple with junctions at $T_1$ and $T_3$ equals the sum of EMFs of two equivalent thermocouples — one with junctions at $T_1, T_2$ and another with junctions at $T_2, T_3$:

$$E(T_1, T_3) = E(T_1, T_2) + E(T_2, T_3)$$

This law enables **extension wires** and software reference-junction compensation.

**Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC):**
Thermocouple tables are referenced to 0 °C at the cold junction. In practice, the cold junction is at the instrument terminals (e.g., 25 °C). Compensation is performed by:

1.  **Hardware CJC:** A separate temperature sensor (usually an IC like LM35 or RTD) measures the actual cold-junction temperature $T_{\text{ref}}$. Its equivalent EMF $E_{\text{ref}}$ is computed from the polynomial and added to the measured EMF: $E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{meas}} + E_{\text{ref}}$.
2.  **Software CJC:** The microcontroller reads $T_{\text{ref}}$, computes $E_{\text{ref}}$ using stored coefficients, and adds it digitally.

Without CJC, errors of 20–25 °C can occur in ambient variations.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* [KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model 2024]

#### Part (a) — Explain the principle, construction, and B-parameter equation of a thermistor. Compare NTC and PTC types. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** A thermistor (thermally sensitive resistor) is a semiconductor device whose resistance varies **strongly and non-linearly** with temperature. It exploits the temperature-dependent mobility and concentration of charge carriers in the semiconductor bandgap.

**Construction:** A small bead, disk, or rod of sintered metal oxides (e.g., Mn, Ni, Co, Fe oxides) with two lead wires, coated in glass or epoxy for protection. The bead may be as small as 0.5 mm for fast response.

**B-Parameter Equation Derivation:**
Semiconductor conductivity follows Arrhenius behavior:

$$\sigma(T) = \sigma_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2k_BT}\right)$$

Resistance $R \propto 1/\sigma$, so:

$$R(T) = R_\infty \exp\!\left(\frac{E_g}{2k_BT}\right) = R_\infty \exp\!\left(\frac{B}{T}\right)$$

where $B = E_g/2k_B$ is the **B-parameter** (typically 3000–5000 K).

Evaluating at reference $T_0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_0 &= R_\infty \exp\!\left(\frac{B}{T_0}\right) \\
\Rightarrow R(T) &= R_0 \exp\!\left[B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**NTC vs PTC:**

| Property | NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) | PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Resistance behavior | Decreases with $T$ | Increases with $T$ |
| Mechanism | Increased carrier generation | Structural phase change |
| Application | Inrush current limiters, thermometry | Self-regulating heaters, motor protection |
| Sensitivity | Very high (~ −4 %/°C) | Sharp transition at Curie point |

---

#### Part (b) — A temperature sensor used in a process control loop has thermal time constant $\tau = 4\,s$ and steady-state gain $K = 0.9\,mV/°C$. The fluid temperature suddenly changes from 30 °C to 80 °C. Determine (i) the transfer function, (ii) the steady-state output voltage, and (iii) the time taken for the sensor to read within ± 1 °C of true value. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Transfer Function:**
A thermal sensor behaves as a first-order system:

$$\frac{V_o(s)}{T(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} = \frac{0.9}{4s + 1} \quad \text{[mV/°C]}$$

**(ii) Steady-State Output Voltage:**
Using Final Value Theorem for a step input $\Delta T = 80 - 30 = 50\,°C$:

$$V_o(\infty) = K \cdot \Delta T = 0.9 \times 50 = 45\,mV$$

**(iii) Time to Reach Within ± 1 °C of True Value:**
The error must satisfy $|\Delta T \cdot e^{-t/\tau}| \leq 1\,°C$:

$$50 \cdot e^{-t/4} \leq 1$$

$$e^{-t/4} \leq \frac{1}{50}$$

Taking natural log:

$$-\frac{t}{4} \leq -\ln(50) = -3.912$$

$$t \geq 4 \times 3.912 \approx 15.65\,s$$

**Therefore, $t \approx 15.65\,s$** for the sensor to settle within ± 1 °C.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Unit Error in Thermistor B-Equation:** Using Celsius inside the exponential instead of **Kelvin** → full marks deducted.
> 2. **Forgetting Cold-Junction Compensation:** Any thermocouple numerical without CJC is considered incomplete.
> 3. **Confusing RTD $\alpha$:** Writing $0.00385/°C$ without the unit $\Omega/\Omega/°C$ loses a mark.
> 4. **Wrong Time-Constant Definition:** Writing $\tau = hA/mc$ instead of $\tau = mc/hA$ (the correct thermal RC) loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Missing Reference Temperature:** Stating $\alpha$ for RTD without specifying $T_0 = 0\,°C$ is incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print-This-Before-Exam 1-Page Summary:**

*   [x] **Temperature** = intensive property, proportional to mean kinetic energy, equalized at thermal equilibrium (Zeroth Law).
*   [x] **SI unit** = **Kelvin (K)**; fixed points of Celsius = **0 °C (273.15 K)** and **100 °C (373.15 K)**.
*   [x] **Absolute Zero** = **0 K = −273.15 °C = −459.67 °F = 0 °R** (lowest theoretical temperature).
*   [x] **Four Scales:** Celsius (°C), Fahrenheit (°F), Kelvin (K), Rankine (°R); conversions require exact $9/5$ and $32/5/9$ factors.
*   [x] **Thermocouple** — Seebeck Effect, EMF = $\alpha \Delta T + \beta \Delta T^2$, **must have CJC**, wide range.
*   [x] **RTD (PT100)** — $R_T = R_0[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$, $\alpha_{Pt} = 0.00385\, Ω/Ω/°C$, very linear, accurate.
*   [x] **Thermistor (NTC)** — $R_T = R_0 \exp[B(1/T - 1/T_0)]$, **T in Kelvin**, exponential sensitivity.
*   [x] **IC Sensor (LM35)** — linear **10 mV/°C** output, easy interfacing with ADC.
*   [x] **Laws of Thermocouple:** Law of Intermediate Metals & Intermediate Temperatures → enable extension wires and software CJC.
*   [x] **Thermal System** — First-order with $G(s) = K/(\tau s + 1)$, $\tau = mc/(hA)$.
*   [x] **Static Characteristics:** Range, span, accuracy, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, repeatability, resolution, drift.
*   [x] **Dynamic Characteristics:** Time constant $\tau$, rise time, settling time — all derived from the first-order transfer function.
*   [x] **Cold-Junction Compensation** is **mandatory** in any thermocouple measurement chain; never omit.
*   [x] **Self-heating error** in RTDs and thermistors must be controlled by limiting excitation current.
*   [x] **Steinhart–Hart equation** gives $\pm 0.01\,°C$ thermistor accuracy after three-point calibration.

> **End of Module 1 — Temperature Notes (PCAUT521, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Presentation Systems: Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT521 - MECHATRONICS AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Presentation Systems: Displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Presentation Systems (Displays):** A *data presentation system* in mechatronics is an **output subsystem** that converts processed electrical signals from a controller (microcontroller, PLC, or computer) into a human-perceivable visual (or tactile) format. A **display** is the physical transducer device that performs this conversion, allowing the operator to monitor system parameters such as temperature, pressure, speed, position, fault codes, and status messages in real time.

In the architecture of a mechatronic system, the display sits at the **HMI (Human-Machine Interface)** layer, forming the final stage of the signal-processing chain:

$$
\text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Signal Conditioning} \rightarrow \text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{Driver Circuit} \rightarrow \textbf{Display}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module-1 Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes **principles of operation**, **selection criteria**, **driver interfacing**, and **multiplexing techniques** of common displays used in industrial and embedded mechatronic systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded in a kitchen and someone is cooking. You cannot see the food, but you want to know how it is progressing. The cook might **call out** "the temperature is 180 °C" — that *call-out* is essentially a **display**. The display is the *voice* of the machine.

A more direct analogy:
- **Sensor** = Your eyes (gather data)
- **Controller** = Your brain (process data)
- **Display** = Your mouth (announce/present the result to others)

If the brain processes the data but the mouth never speaks, the observer (user) has no feedback — so a mechatronic system without a display is "mute" and cannot communicate.

## 1.3 Classification of Displays (High-Level Map)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Displays in Mechatronics are classified by:**
> 1. **Physical principle of light emission** — Emissive vs. Non-emissive
> 2. **Information density** — Simple (single LED) → Segmented (7-seg) → Matrix (Dot-matrix) → Graphical (LCD panels)
> 3. **Interactivity** — Read-only (passive) vs. Touch (input + output)

**Emissive displays** generate their own light (LEDs, OLEDs, CRTs, Vacuum Fluorescent Displays).
**Non-emissive displays** modulate an external light source (LCDs — they block or pass light).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Every KTU Student Must Know

> [!NOTE]
> - **Luminance:** Measured in **cd/m² (candela per square meter)** — defines brightness.
> - **Contrast Ratio:** Ratio of brightest white to darkest black.
> - **Response Time:** Time to switch between states — measured in **ms (milliseconds)**.
> - **Viewing Angle:** Maximum angle at which the display is readable — measured in **degrees (°)**.
> - **Resolution:** Number of **pixels per unit area** (PPI — pixels per inch).
> - **Refresh Rate:** Number of times the image is redrawn per second — measured in **Hz (Hertz)**.
> - **Duty Cycle (multiplexed displays):** Fraction of time a segment is actually energized.

These are universally evaluated in display datasheets and are common in KTU numerical/short-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Major Display Technologies

### 2.1.1 Light Emitting Diode (LED) — Seven-Segment Display

A **7-segment display** is a digital display device containing **seven (7)** bar-shaped LEDs arranged in a figure-of-eight pattern, plus an optional **decimal point (dp)**. Each segment is labeled **a, b, c, d, e, f, g**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **7-segment display is the most commonly tested display in KTU 2024 module-1 questions**, including logic-table derivation, decoder ICs (7447/7448), and multiplexing arithmetic.

**Two electrical configurations exist:**
- **Common Anode (CA):** All anodes tied to Vcc; segment ON when logic **0** applied to cathode.
- **Common Cathode (CC):** All cathodes tied to GND; segment ON when logic **1** applied to anode.

### 2.1.2 Dot-Matrix LED Display

A dot-matrix display uses an **m × n grid of LEDs** (e.g., 8×8, 16×32). Each LED (pixel) is addressed by its row and column. **Persistence of vision** allows the human eye to perceive a stable image when the rows are scanned rapidly (multiplexing).

### 2.1.3 Liquid Crystal Display (LCD)

LCDs are **non-emissive**. They consist of:
- Two polarizer films
- ITO (Indium Tin Oxide) transparent electrodes
- Liquid crystal fluid (nematic type — twisted 90° between crossed polarizers in OFF state)
- Backlight (modern displays)

When a voltage is applied across a segment/pixel, the liquid crystal **untwists**, blocking light — that segment appears **dark**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **16×2 character LCD (HD44780 controller)** is a KTU-favorite because it requires only a parallel 4-bit or 8-bit interface and is found in nearly all microcontroller-based projects.

### 2.1.4 Comparison of Display Technologies

| Display Type | Principle | Emissive? | Power | Response Time | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **7-Segment LED** | Electroluminescence | Yes | Moderate | < 1 ms | Numeric readouts, counters |
| **Dot-Matrix LED** | Electroluminescence | Yes | High | < 1 ms | Scrolling text, animations |
| **Character LCD** | Light modulation | No | Low | ~100 ms | HMI panels, instrumentation |
| **OLED** | Organic electroluminescence | Yes | Low | < 1 ms | Modern HMI, mobile screens |
| **Touch Screen (Resistive/Capacitive)** | Voltage/charge sensing | No (display + sensor) | Low | ~10–20 ms | Interactive HMI |
| **CRT (legacy)** | Cathode ray phosphorescence | Yes | High | < 1 ms (analog) | Lab oscilloscopes, legacy monitors |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Forward voltage of LED** | $V_F \approx 1.8\text{–}3.2 \text{ V}$ | V (depends on color) |
| 2 | **Series current-limiting resistor** | $R = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F}$ | $\Omega$ (ohms) |
| 3 | **Number of segments in 7-seg** | $N_{seg} = 7$ (or 8 with dp) | — |
| 4 | **Duty cycle of multiplexed display** | $D = \dfrac{t_{on}}{T_{frame}} \times 100\%$ | % (typically 1/N for N-digit multiplexing) |
| 5 | **Refresh frequency requirement** | $f_{refresh} \geq 50 \text{ Hz}$ (for no flicker) | Hz |
| 6 | **Number of digits multiplexed** | $N_{digits} = \dfrac{f_{refresh}}{f_{per\text{-}digit}}$ | — |
| 7 | **Baud rate (serial LCD/I2C LCD)** | $f_{baud} = \dfrac{1}{T_{bit}}$ | bits per second |
| 8 | **I2C address of PCF8574 LCD backpack** | Hex `0x27` or `0x3F` | — |
| 9 | **Contrast ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{L_{white}}{L_{black}}$ | unitless |
| 10 | **Luminance of LED** | $L_v = \dfrac{I_v}{A}$ (cd/m²) | cd/m² |
| 11 | **Total power per segment** | $P_{seg} = V_F \cdot I_F$ | W (watts) |
| 12 | **Total power for 7-seg digit (all on)** | $P_{digit} = 8 \cdot V_F \cdot I_F$ | W (incl. decimal point) |

> [!NOTE]
> **K**ey Tip: For the KTU 2024 exam, memorize formulas **#2, #4, #5, #6** — these appear in nearly every numerical/derivation question on displays.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Mechatronic Systems

- **Automotive dashboards:** Mixed 7-segment (speedometer) + graphical LCD (navigation).
- **CNC machines:** TFT touch-screen HMI showing G-code, tool path, spindle RPM.
- **Medical devices (ventilators, infusion pumps):** OLED displays for high-contrast readability in low-light operating rooms.
- **Industrial PLCs:** 16×2 or 20×4 character LCDs for parameter setting and alarms.
- **Consumer electronics:** Capacitive touch screens integrating display + input as a unified I/O device.

The display is the **only feedback channel** for the operator — its selection directly determines the *usability, safety, and reliability* of the entire mechatronic product.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Current-Limiting Resistor for a 7-Segment LED

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A common-cathode 7-segment LED is driven by a microcontroller running at $V_{CC} = 5$ V. The forward voltage of the red LED is $V_F = 2.0$ V and the desired forward current per segment is $I_F = 15$ mA. Compute the series current-limiting resistor for a single segment.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the segment loop:**

$$
V_{CC} = V_R + V_F
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the voltage drop across the resistor:**

$$
V_R = V_{CC} - V_F = 5 - 2.0 = 3.0 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Ohm's Law to compute the resistance:**

$$
R = \dfrac{V_R}{I_F} = \dfrac{3.0 \text{ V}}{15 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{3.0}{0.015}
$$

$$
R = 200 \ \Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check & practical choice:**

> [!NOTE]
> A standard **220 Ω** resistor (next E12 value up) is used to safely limit current to ~13.6 mA — protecting the microcontroller pin (max 20 mA per GPIO pin on most MCUs).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Duty Cycle of a Multiplexed N-Digit 7-Segment Display

> **Problem (KTU Module-1 type):** A mechatronic HMI multiplexes **N = 4** seven-segment digits. If each digit is energized for $t_{on} = 5$ ms, determine (a) the total frame period $T_{frame}$, (b) the duty cycle of each digit, and (c) the minimum refresh frequency to avoid flicker.

**Step 1 — Total frame period (all digits lit sequentially per cycle):**

$$
T_{frame} = N \times t_{on}
$$

$$
T_{frame} = 4 \times 5 \text{ ms} = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Duty cycle of each digit:**

$$
D = \dfrac{t_{on}}{T_{frame}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{5 \text{ ms}}{20 \text{ ms}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
D = 25\%
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum refresh frequency (flicker-free):**

For no visible flicker, the *whole frame* must be repainted at least 50 times per second:

$$
f_{refresh} = \dfrac{1}{T_{frame}} = \dfrac{1}{0.020 \text{ s}} = 50 \text{ Hz}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since 50 Hz > 50 Hz threshold, **this display will be flicker-free**. A common KTU trick: if $T_{frame}$ yields less than 50 Hz, increase $N \times t_{on}$ carefully — usually reduce $t_{on}$.

**Step 4 — Per-digit switching frequency:**

$$
f_{per\text{-}digit} = \dfrac{f_{refresh}}{N} = \dfrac{50 \text{ Hz}}{4} = 12.5 \text{ Hz}
$$

This is fine because each digit is shown 50 times per second overall (50 Hz refresh), but is *energized* only 12.5 times per second for 5 ms each.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Consumption Comparison (LED vs. LCD)

> **Problem (KTU analytical):** Compare the average power consumption of an 8-digit 7-segment multiplexed LED display vs. an 8-character 16×2 LCD for the same HMI scenario. Assume $V_F = 2.0$ V, $I_F = 10$ mA per segment, $D = 1/8$ (equal-time multiplexing), and that LCD consumes $P_{LCD} = 50$ mW total.

**Step 1 — Power per segment when ON:**

$$
P_{seg} = V_F \cdot I_F = 2.0 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} = 0.020 \text{ W} = 20 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 2 — Power per digit (8 segments all on, but multiplexed):**

$$
P_{digit\text{-}peak} = 8 \times 20 \text{ mW} = 160 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 3 — Average power across all 8 digits (multiplexing):**

$$
P_{LED\text{-}avg} = N \times P_{digit\text{-}peak} \times D
$$

$$
P_{LED\text{-}avg} = 8 \times 160 \text{ mW} \times \dfrac{1}{8} = 160 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with LCD:**

$$
P_{LCD} = 50 \text{ mW}
$$

$$
\dfrac{P_{LED\text{-}avg}}{P_{LCD}} = \dfrac{160}{50} = 3.2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The LCD consumes **3.2× less power** than the equivalent multiplexed 7-segment LED display, making LCD the preferred choice for **battery-powered mechatronic devices** (e.g., portable measurement instruments).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Traps

| # | Given | Find | Key Formula | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{CC}=5$ V, $V_F=2.2$ V, $I_F=20$ mA | Series R | $R = (V_{CC}-V_F)/I_F$ | Forgetting to convert mA → A |
| 2 | $N=6$ digits, $t_{on}=4$ ms | Refresh freq | $f=1/(N \cdot t_{on})$ | Mixing up frame time and per-digit time |
| 3 | 7-seg all segments on | Power | $P=7 \cdot V_F \cdot I_F$ (excluding dp) | Counting dp or missing one segment |
| 4 | LCD 16×2 chars | Memory bytes | $16 \times 2 = 32$ byte DDRAM | Confusing with 16×2 = 32 columns |
| 5 | Touchscreen 4-wire resistive | Touch coord | Voltage divider on two layers | Mixing X and Y measurement order |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Display System Architecture in a Mechatronic Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Physical Process] --> B[Sensor: Temp/Pressure/Position]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning: Amplify/Filter]
    C --> D[ADC: Convert Analog to Digital]
    D --> E[Microcontroller / PLC: Process Data]
    E --> F{Display Driver IC}
    F --> G1[7-Segment LED Display]
    F --> G2[16x2 Character LCD]
    F --> G3[Dot Matrix LED 8x8]
    F --> G4[OLED / TFT Touch Panel]
    H[Operator / User] -->|Read| G1
    H -->|Read| G2
    H -->|Read| G3
    H -->|Touch Input| G4
    I[Power Supply: 5V / 3.3V] -->|Vcc| F
    I -->|Vcc| E
    G1 -->|Visual Feedback| H
    G2 -->|Visual Feedback| H
    G3 -->|Visual Feedback| H
    G4 -->|Visual + Tactile Feedback| H
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Multiplexed 4-Digit 7-Segment Display Interfacing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU[Microcontroller GPIO] -->|Data Lines a-g, dp| DRV[BCD-to-7-Seg Decoder 7447]
    DRV --> SEG1[Segments a,b,c,d,e,f,g of Digit 1]
    DRV --> SEG2[Segments a,b,c,d,e,f,g of Digit 2]
    DRV --> SEG3[Segments a,b,c,d,e,f,g of Digit 3]
    DRV --> SEG4[Segments a,b,c,d,e,f,g of Digit 4]
    MCU2[Microcontroller GPIO] --> TR1[Transistor Q1: Digit 1 Common]
    MCU2 --> TR2[Transistor Q2: Digit 2 Common]
    MCU2 --> TR3[Transistor Q3: Digit 3 Common]
    MCU2 --> TR4[Transistor Q4: Digit 4 Common]
    TR1 --> D1[Digit 1: Common Anode]
    TR2 --> D2[Digit 2: Common Anode]
    TR3 --> D3[Digit 3: Common Anode]
    TR4 --> D4[Digit 4: Common Anode]
    D1 -->|ON 5ms then OFF| SEG1
    D2 -->|ON 5ms then OFF| SEG2
    D3 -->|ON 5ms then OFF| SEG3
    D4 -->|ON 5ms then OFF| SEG4
    TIMER[Software Timer / Interrupt: 5ms Tick] --> MCU2
    TIMER --> MCU
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — 16×2 LCD Pinout and Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HOST[Microcontroller] -->|D0-D7 or D4-D7| LCD[16x2 LCD Module HD44780]
    HOST -->|RS: Register Select| LCD
    HOST -->|RW: Read/Write| LCD
    HOST -->|EN: Enable Strobe| LCD
    HOST -->|V0 via Pot| LCD
    PWR[5V Supply] -->|Vcc, GND, A, K| LCD
    BACK[Backlight LED +/-] --> LCD
    LCD -->|Character Code Index| CGROM[CGROM: 192 5x8 patterns]
    LCD -->|User Defined| CGRAM[CGRAM: 8 custom chars]
    LCD -->|Display Buffer| DDRAM[DDRAM: 80 bytes]
    HOST -->|Command: 0x80 + addr| DDRAM
    HOST -->|Data: ASCII char| DDRAM
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Decision Tree for Display Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Choose Display for Mechatronic System] --> Q1{Need Graphics?}
    Q1 -->|No, numeric only| Q2{Budget Low?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, text/graphics| Q3{Need Touch Input?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| S1[7-Segment LED]
    Q2 -->|No| S2[14-Segment Alphanumeric LED]
    Q3 -->|No, read-only| S3[Character LCD 16x2 / 20x4]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Battery Powered?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| S4[OLED 0.96 inch I2C]
    Q4 -->|No, mains powered| S5[TFT 2.4-5.0 inch SPI/Parallel]
    S1 -.-> END[Final HMI]
    S2 -.-> END
    S3 -.-> END
    S4 -.-> END
    S5 -.-> END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block labelled `SEG1`, `SEG2`, etc. represents the **shared segment lines** that go to all four digits; only one transistor (Q1–Q4) is ON at any instant, selecting which digit is active — this is **time-multiplexing**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
Define a *data presentation system*. List any **four** common display devices used in mechatronic systems.

**Model Answer:**

> A **data presentation system** is an output subsystem of a mechatronic system that converts electrical signals from a controller into a **human-perceivable form** (visual, auditory, or tactile), enabling the operator to monitor and interpret system status and parameters.

**Four common displays used in mechatronics:**
1. **7-Segment LED display** — for numeric readouts.
2. **16×2 Character LCD** — for alphanumeric status messages.
3. **Dot-matrix LED (8×8 or larger)** — for scrolling text/graphics.
4. **TFT / OLED touch panel** — for interactive graphical HMI.

(Other valid: 14-segment alphanumeric LED, VFD — Vacuum Fluorescent Display, E-paper, 7-segment LCD.)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
Differentiate between **emissive** and **non-emissive** displays. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Emissive Display | Non-Emissive Display |
|---|---|---|
| Light source | **Generates** its own light | **Modulates** external/ambient light |
| Power consumption | Higher (especially dot-matrix) | Lower |
| Visibility in dark | Excellent (self-lit) | Requires backlight |
| Example | **7-Segment LED**, OLED, CRT | **LCD** (character or graphical) |

> Non-emissive displays are preferred in **battery-powered** mechatronic systems (e.g., portable digital multimeters) due to their **lower power consumption**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a multiplexed 4-digit 7-segment LED display** interfaced to a microcontroller. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A common-cathode 7-segment LED is interfaced to a 5 V microcontroller. Each segment has a forward voltage $V_F = 1.8$ V and requires a forward current $I_F = 12$ mA. If all **7 segments** of a single digit are ON simultaneously, compute: (i) the series resistance per segment, (ii) total current drawn by the digit, and (iii) total power dissipated. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

> [!NOTE]
> A **4-digit multiplexed 7-segment display** uses **time-division multiplexing (TDM)** to drive 4 digits using only **7 segment lines** + **4 digit-select lines** = 11 GPIO pins (versus 32 pins if not multiplexed).

**Architecture:**

1. **Microcontroller** generates BCD/segment data.
2. **Segment driver (e.g., 7447 BCD-to-7-seg decoder or current driver ULN2003)** energizes the 7 segment lines (a–g, dp).
3. **Digit drivers (transistor arrays)** selectively switch ON the common terminal of one digit at a time.
4. **Software timer / interrupt** runs at $T = N \times t_{on}$ so each digit is refreshed at ≥ 50 Hz.

(Refer to **Diagram 4.2** in Section 4 above.)

**Working:** The controller displays digit-1 data → activates Q1 for 5 ms → switches off Q1 → displays digit-2 data → activates Q2 for 5 ms → and so on. Due to **persistence of vision**, all four digits appear to be lit simultaneously.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**(i) Series resistance per segment:**

$$
R = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F} = \dfrac{5 - 1.8}{12 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{3.2}{0.012}
$$

$$
R = 266.67 \ \Omega \quad (\text{use standard } 270 \ \Omega)
$$

**(ii) Total current drawn by the digit (7 segments ON):**

$$
I_{total} = 7 \times I_F = 7 \times 12 \text{ mA}
$$

$$
I_{total} = 84 \text{ mA}
$$

**(iii) Total power dissipated by the digit (LEDs only, excluding resistor losses):**

$$
P_{LED} = 7 \times V_F \times I_F = 7 \times 1.8 \times 0.012
$$

$$
P_{LED} = 0.1512 \text{ W} = 151.2 \text{ mW}
$$

**Power in resistors:**

$$
P_R = 7 \times I_F^2 \times R = 7 \times (0.012)^2 \times 266.67 \approx 0.269 \text{ W}
$$

**Total power drawn from supply:**

$$
P_{total} = P_{LED} + P_R \approx 0.420 \text{ W}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a 16×2 character LCD module** based on the HD44780 controller. List the **functions of pins RS, RW, and EN**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a mechatronic HMI, **6 seven-segment digits** are multiplexed with each digit lit for **3 ms**. Calculate: (i) the frame period, (ii) the duty cycle per digit, (iii) the refresh frequency, and (iv) state whether the display will appear flicker-free. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

> [!NOTE]
> The **16×2 character LCD** has 2 rows × 16 columns = **32 character positions**. Each character is a **5×8 dot matrix** generated from the built-in **CGROM** (Character Generator ROM) of the HD44780 controller. Up to 8 **custom characters** can be stored in **CGRAM**.

**Working Principle:**
1. The **HD44780 controller** contains a **DDRAM** (Display Data RAM) of 80 bytes, a **CGROM** of 192 standard 5×8 patterns (ASCII + Japanese katakana), and a **CGRAM** of 64 bytes (8 user characters).
2. The host (microcontroller) sends a command (RS=0) or data (RS=1) on the data bus (D0–D7 or D4–D7 in 4-bit mode).
3. The **EN (Enable)** pin is pulsed HIGH-to-LOW to **latch** the data into the LCD.
4. The **R/W** pin selects read (1) or write (0).
5. Liquid crystal segments corresponding to the character pattern modulate backlight → visible character appears.

**Pin Functions:**

| Pin | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **RS** | Register Select | **0 = Command register** (instructions like clear, cursor, position); **1 = Data register** (ASCII to display) |
| **RW** | Read/Write | **0 = Write** to LCD; **1 = Read** from LCD (busy flag, address counter) |
| **EN** | Enable | A **HIGH-to-LOW transition** latches the data/command into the LCD |

#### Model Solution — (b)

**(i) Frame period:**

$$
T_{frame} = N \times t_{on} = 6 \times 3 \text{ ms} = 18 \text{ ms}
$$

**(ii) Duty cycle per digit:**

$$
D = \dfrac{t_{on}}{T_{frame}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{3}{18} \times 100\% = 16.67\%
$$

**(iii) Refresh frequency:**

$$
f_{refresh} = \dfrac{1}{T_{frame}} = \dfrac{1}{0.018 \text{ s}} \approx 55.56 \text{ Hz}
$$

**(iv) Flicker assessment:**

Since $f_{refresh} = 55.56 \text{ Hz} > 50 \text{ Hz}$, the display is **flicker-free** ✓.

> [!NOTE]
> If the calculated refresh frequency were less than 50 Hz, the display would visibly flicker and cause operator eye-strain — a common KTU scenario trap.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Display Questions:**
> 1. **Units confusion:** Forgetting to convert **mA → A** in $R = V/I$ calculations (loses 1 mark).
> 2. **Confusing $T_{frame}$ with $t_{on}$:** Frame period is $N \times t_{on}$, not just $t_{on}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the decimal point (dp):** When asked for "all segments ON," include **8 segments** (a–g + dp), not 7.
> 4. **Wrong RS/RW/EN polarity:** RS=0 is **command**, not data — KTU students often invert this.
> 5. **Power calculation:** Including only LED power and ignoring resistor power losses loses 1 mark in numericals.
> 6. **LCD addressing:** Writing data to DDRAM address `0x80 + position` — using `0x00` is a frequent mistake.
> 7. **Refresh frequency threshold:** Always state the **50 Hz flicker-free criterion** explicitly in your answer for full marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Data presentation systems** are the **HMI output layer** of any mechatronic system — they convert electrical signals into human-perceivable information.
- **Displays are classified as emissive (LED, OLED, CRT) and non-emissive (LCD).**
- The **7-segment display** is the most-tested element in KTU Module 1: know the segment labels (a–g, dp), common-anode vs. common-cathode, and the BCD-to-7-seg decoder concept.
- **Multiplexing** is essential for multi-digit displays to reduce GPIO pin count — always ensure refresh rate ≥ **50 Hz** to avoid flicker.
- **Duty cycle** in an N-digit multiplexed display is **1/N** when each digit is given equal time.
- **Current-limiting resistor:** $R = (V_{CC} - V_F)/I_F$ — always convert mA to A.
- **LCD (HD44780)** uses pins **RS, RW, EN** and operates in 4-bit or 8-bit parallel mode; **I2C backpack (PCF8574)** is a popular serial alternative.
- **Touch screens** (resistive: 4-wire; capacitive: projected) integrate display + input — used in modern HMIs.
- **Selection criteria for a display** include: information type (numeric vs. graphical), environment (lighting, temperature), power budget, viewing distance, and cost.
- **Power efficiency:** LCDs and OLEDs are far more power-efficient than large LED dot-matrix displays — crucial for battery-operated mechatronic systems.
- For KTU 2024 ESE: practice **duty cycle, refresh frequency, and resistor calculations** — these are the most common 7–14 mark question formats.
- Always draw a **neat block diagram** showing the controller → driver → display signal path (gain 1–2 marks).
- Remember to **state units** at the end of every numerical answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement Systems and Calibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT521 - MECHATRONICS AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Measurement Systems and Calibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Measurement System — The Formal KTU Definition

A **measurement system** is an assembly of physical components and functional blocks (sensors, transducers, signal conditioners, transmission lines, and display elements) organized to acquire information about a physical variable, convert it into a recognizable form, and present it in a quantitatively meaningful format. According to the **Neubert classification** adopted in the KTU 2024 Mechatronics syllabus, a generalized measurement system consists of three primary functional stages chained together:

1. **Sensing Stage** — Detects the measurand (the physical quantity to be measured).
2. **Conversion / Signal Conditioning Stage** — Converts the sensed signal into a usable electrical/displayable form.
3. **Presentation / Output Stage** — Displays, records, or transmits the result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Recall-Ready):**
> A measurement system is a *chain of elements* that receives energy/information from the measurand, processes it, and delivers it as a useful output to an observer or controller. Every link in the chain has its own **static** and **dynamic** characteristics that ultimately dictate the quality of the measurement.

## 1.2 What Exactly is Calibration?

**Calibration** is the experimentally established *comparison* of a measuring instrument (instrument under test, IUT) against a **standard of known higher accuracy** under specified, controlled conditions. The result is the determination of:

- The **deviation** of the IUT from the true value (offset, gain error, linearity error).
- A documented **calibration curve** or **correction table**.
- The **uncertainty** of measurement, expressed statistically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board Exams:**
> Calibration is *not* the same as *adjustment*. **Calibration = compare + record deviation.** **Adjustment = physically alter** the instrument to bring the deviation within acceptable limits. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests this distinction.

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Bathroom Weighing Scale"

Imagine stepping on a bathroom scale. The spring inside compresses (sensing), pushes a pointer across a dial (conversion), and you read your weight (presentation). Now, suppose your scale reads **68 kg** but a doctor's calibrated digital scale reads **70 kg**. Your scale has a **systematic error of -2 kg**. You take it to a metrology lab where the technician places **standard certified masses (e.g., 50 kg, 70 kg)** on it. The technician notes the readings and produces a *calibration certificate* stating "At 50 kg true mass, the scale reads 49.1 kg; at 70 kg, it reads 68.6 kg." This is calibration.

Now think of *traceability*: the doctor's standard is itself compared to a **National Physical Laboratory (NPL)** standard, which in turn is compared to the **International Bureau of Weights and Measures (BIPM)** in France, where the **Planck constant** defines the kilogram. This unbroken chain of comparisons is called **metrological traceability** — a concept frequently asked in KTU 14-mark questions.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Reference Constants (Bold = KTU High-Yield)

- **SI base units**: kilogram (kg), metre (m), second (s), ampere (A), kelvin (K), mole (mol), candela (cd).
- **Calibration hierarchy** (top → bottom): **International standards (BIPM) → National standards (NPL) → Secondary/Working standards → Field instruments.**
- **Resolution**: smallest increment the instrument can resolve, e.g., **0.01 mm** for a vernier caliper, **0.1 °C** for a digital thermometer.
- **Reference Standard Temperature**: **23 °C (293.15 K)** as per ISO standards for industrial calibration labs.
- **Reference Relative Humidity**: **50 %** for dimensional metrology.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Calibration Hierarchy:** *"Big Numbers Prevail Over Small"* — **B**IPM → **N**PL → **W**orking → **F**ield. (BNWF)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generalized Measurement System — Component-wise Logic

Following Neubert's Model, a measurement system is decomposed into **five functional blocks**. Understanding each block's role is mandatory for KTU board questions.

### Block 1 — Primary Sensing Element (Sensor)
- **Why**: To interact directly with the measurand. The sensor establishes physical contact (or non-contact coupling) with the process variable.
- **How**: Converts the input physical quantity into a more usable intermediate variable (e.g., strain gauge converts force → resistance change; thermocouple converts temperature → millivolts).
- **KTU Examples**: Strain gauge, LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer), RTD, thermistor, piezoelectric crystal.

### Block 2 — Variable Conversion Element (Transducer)
- **Why**: To transform the intermediate signal into a standardized electrical signal (typically voltage, current, or digital code).
- **How**: Often co-located with the sensor, this block ensures the signal is in a form that subsequent electronics can process. The terms "sensor" and "transducer" overlap heavily in industry.

### Block 3 — Variable Manipulation Element (Signal Conditioning)
- **Why**: Raw sensor output is typically low-amplitude, noisy, and contaminated with interference.
- **How**: Applies amplification, filtering, linearization, cold-junction compensation, isolation, and A/D conversion.

### Block 4 — Data Transmission Element
- **Why**: To carry the conditioned signal from the field to the controller or display unit.
- **How**: 4–20 mA current loops, 0–10 V voltage signals, fieldbuses (CAN, Profibus, Modbus), or wireless telemetry.

### Block 5 — Data Presentation Element
- **Why**: The human or machine consumer needs a meaningful output.
- **How**: Digital displays, recorders, alarms, PLC registers, SCADA dashboards, or feedback signals to a controller.

## 2.2 Static Characteristics of Measurement Systems

A measurement system's *static* behaviour is its response when the measurand is **constant or slowly varying** (i.e., steady-state, no dynamic lag).

| # | Characteristic | Engineering Definition | Why It Matters in Practice |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Accuracy** | Closeness of a reading to the *true value* | Determines systematic error budget |
| 2 | **Precision** | Closeness of *multiple readings* to each other | Indicates repeatability, scatter |
| 3 | **Sensitivity (K)** | Ratio of output change to input change | $K = \Delta y / \Delta x$ |
| 4 | **Resolution** | Smallest measurable increment | Sets the detection floor |
| 5 | **Linearity** | How closely the calibration curve approximates a straight line | Simplifies signal processing |
| 6 | **Hysteresis** | Max difference between upscale and downscale readings at the same input | Critical in magnetic/elastic sensors |
| 7 | **Repeatability** | Ability to reproduce same output under identical conditions (short term) | Reliability indicator |
| 8 | **Reproducibility** | Ability to reproduce readings under *varying* conditions (long term, different operators) | Robustness indicator |
| 9 | **Drift** | Slow change of output with time at constant input | Ageing/environmental stability |
| 10 | **Span (Range)** | Difference between max and min input (or output) | Defines the working zone |
| 11 | **Dead Zone / Threshold** | Smallest input change that produces a measurable output change | Sets sensitivity floor |
| 12 | **Static Error** | Difference between measured value and true value | Combined effect of all above |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trap:** Many students confuse **accuracy vs precision**. A dart-throw analogy helps — all darts on the *centre* = accurate + precise. All darts clustered *off-centre* = precise but not accurate. Scattered darts = neither.

## 2.3 Dynamic Characteristics of Measurement Systems

When the measurand changes rapidly, *dynamic* errors emerge. A measurement system is essentially a **filter** between the real world and the observer — it cannot respond instantaneously.

### First-Order System
The standard governing differential equation:

$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

where $\tau$ is the **time constant** (seconds) and $K$ is **static sensitivity**.

The **step response** (input $x(t) = A \cdot u(t)$) is:

$$y(t) = K A \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$

The output reaches **63.2 %** of its final value at $t = \tau$, **86.5 %** at $t = 2\tau$, and **95.0 %** at $t = 3\tau$ (the 3τ rule of thumb).

### Second-Order System
$$\frac{d^{2}y(t)}{dt^{2}} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}\frac{dy(t)}{dt} + \omega_{n}^{2} y(t) = K \omega_{n}^{2} x(t)$$

where $\omega_n$ is the **natural frequency** (rad/s) and $\zeta$ is the **damping ratio**. Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$) systems exhibit overshoot and oscillation — a critical concern in accelerometer and force-gauge selection.

## 2.4 Errors in Measurement — The Three Pillars

### (a) Systematic Errors
- **Predictable, repeatable, and directional** (always positive or always negative).
- Causes: mis-calibration, zero offset, gain error, environmental effects (temperature drift), loading effect.
- **Reducible** by calibration and correction factors.

### (b) Random Errors
- **Unpredictable scatter** about the mean due to noise, electrical interference, micro-vibrations.
- Treated **statistically** — Gaussian distribution, standard deviation, confidence intervals.
- **Irreducible** but quantifiable (uncertainty analysis).

### (c) Gross / Illegitimate Errors
- **Human blunders**: misreading scales, instrument misconnection, transcription errors, parallax in analogue meters.
- **Eliminated** by training, automated data acquisition, and redundancy.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Specific Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly asks for the *classification of errors and the sources of errors* in measurement systems. Always structure your answer under the three sub-headings above.

## 2.5 Calibration — Methods and Standards

| Calibration Type | Description | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Calibration** | Performed using absolute standards (e.g., SI units) at national labs | NPL India level |
| **Secondary Calibration** | Transfer of accuracy from primary to working standards | Industry metrology labs |
| **Static Calibration** | Input held at fixed points, output measured | Pressure gauge, thermometer |
| **Dynamic Calibration** | Input varied rapidly (step, sine sweep), transient response captured | Accelerometer, pressure transducer |
| **In-situ / Online Calibration** | Calibration done while instrument is installed in process | pH probes, level transmitters |
| **Comparison Calibration** | IUT compared directly to a reference standard of higher class | Dimensional metrology |
| **Self-Calibration** | Instrument calibrates itself using built-in references | Smart sensors with embedded standards |

### Traceability Chain
$$\text{Field instrument} \leftarrow \text{Working Standard} \leftarrow \text{Secondary Standard} \leftarrow \text{Primary Standard (NPL)} \leftarrow \text{International Standard (BIPM)}$$

Each link in the chain contributes to the **combined measurement uncertainty**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Static Sensitivity | $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Output unit per input unit |
| 2 | Overall Sensitivity (Cascaded) | $K_{total} = K_{1} \cdot K_{2} \cdot K_{3} \cdot \ldots \cdot K_{n}$ | Block-diagram product rule |
| 3 | Absolute Error | $E = M - A$ | $M$ = measured, $A$ = true |
| 4 | Relative Error | $e_{r} = \dfrac{E}{A} = \dfrac{M - A}{A}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Percentage Error | $e_{\%} = e_{r} \times 100$ | Expressed in % |
| 6 | Accuracy (as %) | $\text{Accuracy} = 100 - \vert e_{\%} \vert$ | Or $\dfrac{\vert A - M \vert}{A} \times 100$ |
| 7 | Precision (Standard Deviation) | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_{i} - \bar{x})^{2}}{n - 1}}$ | $n$ = no. of readings |
| 8 | Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{i}$ | Best estimate of true value |
| 9 | First-Order Step Response | $y(t) = KA(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | $A$ = step magnitude |
| 10 | Time to reach 63.2 % | $t = \tau$ | Definition of time constant |
| 11 | Dead Time (Delay) | $t_{d}$ | Time between input and response onset |
| 12 | Loading Error | $e_{L} = \dfrac{Z_{m}}{Z_{s}} \times 100\,\%$ | $Z_{m}$ = meter impedance, $Z_{s}$ = source impedance |
| 13 | Hysteresis Error | $e_{H} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{max}}{\text{Full Scale}} \times 100\,\%$ | Expressed as % of full scale (FS) |
| 14 | Linearity Error | $e_{L} = \dfrac{\vert y_{actual} - y_{ideal} \vert_{max}}{\text{FS}} \times 100\,\%$ | Best-fit straight line |
| 15 | Propagation of Uncertainties (Sum) | $u_{c} = \sqrt{u_{1}^{2} + u_{2}^{2} + \ldots + u_{n}^{2}}$ | RSS rule for independent sources |
| 16 | Natural Frequency (2nd order) | $\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $k$ = stiffness, $m$ = mass |
| 17 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | $c$ = damping coefficient |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall Sensitivity of a Cascaded Measurement System

**Problem Statement:** A measurement system has three stages cascaded. Stage 1 has sensitivity $K_1 = 0.5 \text{ V/kg}$, Stage 2 (amplifier) has gain $K_2 = 200$, and Stage 3 (display) has sensitivity $K_3 = 0.1 \text{ kg/V}$. Derive the overall sensitivity and the output of the system for a 10 kg input.

### Step 1 — Identify the Block Diagram
The measurand $x$ enters Stage 1, whose output is $y_1$. This feeds Stage 2 (output $y_2$), which feeds Stage 3 (output $y_3$).

$$x \;\rightarrow\; [\text{Stage 1: }K_{1}] \;\rightarrow\; y_{1} \;\rightarrow\; [\text{Stage 2: }K_{2}] \;\rightarrow\; y_{2} \;\rightarrow\; [\text{Stage 3: }K_{3}] \;\rightarrow\; y_{3}$$

### Step 2 — Write Each Stage Equation
By definition of static sensitivity, the output of each stage is its sensitivity times its input.

$$y_{1} = K_{1} \cdot x$$

$$y_{2} = K_{2} \cdot y_{1}$$

$$y_{3} = K_{3} \cdot y_{2}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Backwards
Substitute the equation for $y_1$ into the $y_2$ expression:

$$y_{2} = K_{2} \cdot (K_{1} \cdot x) = K_{1} K_{2} \cdot x$$

Now substitute this into the $y_3$ expression:

$$y_{3} = K_{3} \cdot (K_{1} K_{2} \cdot x) = K_{1} K_{2} K_{3} \cdot x$$

### Step 4 — Define Overall Sensitivity
The overall sensitivity is the product of the individual stage sensitivities.

$$K_{total} = K_{1} \cdot K_{2} \cdot K_{3}$$

### Step 5 — Plug in the Numerical Values

$$K_{total} = 0.5 \;\dfrac{V}{kg} \times 200 \times 0.1 \;\dfrac{kg}{V}$$

$$K_{total} = 10 \;\;\dfrac{\text{(dimensionless display units)}}{\text{kg}}$$

### Step 6 — Compute Output for 10 kg Input
For an input of $x = 10 \text{ kg}$:

$$y_{3} = K_{total} \cdot x = 10 \times 10 = 100 \;\;\text{display units}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This product rule is the basis of **calibration of multi-sensor systems** (e.g., force-balance accelerometers, load cells with amplifier + ADC). The KTU 2024 board examiner often tests this with a *non-trivial cascaded transducer* problem involving strain gauge + bridge + amplifier + display.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Step Response of a First-Order Measurement System

**Problem Statement:** A temperature sensor (first-order, $\tau = 5$ s, $K = 10$ mV/°C) is suddenly immersed in a bath at 100 °C from an initial ambient of 25 °C. Derive the time response and find the output at $t = 5$ s, $10$ s, and $15$ s.

### Step 1 — Governing Differential Equation
The sensor obeys the standard first-order ODE (energy balance between convective heat gain and thermal storage).

$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Step Input
A step from 25 °C to 100 °C is modelled as:

$$x(t) = A \cdot u(t) = (100 - 25) \cdot u(t) = 75 \cdot u(t)\;\;°C$$

The ODE becomes:

$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot 75 \cdot u(t)$$

### Step 3 — Solve Homogeneous Equation
The homogeneous part is $\tau y' + y = 0$, giving:

$$y_{h}(t) = C \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

### Step 4 — Find Particular (Steady-State) Solution
At steady state, $\frac{dy}{dt} = 0$, so $y_{ss} = K \cdot 75$.

### Step 5 — Combine and Apply Initial Condition
At $t = 0$, the output is $y(0) = K \cdot 25$ (the sensor was reading the initial 25 °C). The complete solution is:

$$y(t) = K \cdot 75 + C \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

Apply $y(0) = K \cdot 25$:

$$K \cdot 25 = K \cdot 75 + C \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C = -50K$$

Thus:

$$y(t) = 75K - 50K \cdot e^{-t/\tau} = 75K\left(1 - \dfrac{50}{75}e^{-t/\tau}\right) = 75K\left(1 - \dfrac{2}{3}e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

Or, in the cleaner absolute form often used in textbooks:

$$y(t) = y_{initial} + KA\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) = 25K + 75K\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Evaluate at Specified Times (with $K = 10$ mV/°C, $\tau = 5$ s)

At $t = 5$ s, $t/\tau = 1$:

$$y(5) = 25(10) + 75(10)(1 - e^{-1}) = 250 + 750(0.6321) = 250 + 474.07 = 724.07\;\text{mV} \approx 724.1\;\text{mV}$$

At $t = 10$ s, $t/\tau = 2$:

$$y(10) = 250 + 750(1 - e^{-2}) = 250 + 750(0.8647) = 250 + 648.50 = 898.50\;\text{mV}$$

At $t = 15$ s, $t/\tau = 3$:

$$y(15) = 250 + 750(1 - e^{-3}) = 250 + 750(0.9502) = 250 + 712.65 = 962.65\;\text{mV}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Shortcut:** At $t = \tau$, output reaches ~63.2 % of the way to final; at $t = 3\tau$, it reaches ~95 %. Always quote the **3τ settling rule** in derivations for full marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Standard Deviation and Uncertainty from Repeated Measurements

**Problem Statement:** A pressure transducer is read 5 times for a constant applied pressure. The readings (in kPa) are: 101.2, 100.8, 101.5, 100.9, 101.1. Compute the arithmetic mean, standard deviation, and the 95 % confidence interval (assume $t_{0.025,4} = 2.776$).

### Step 1 — Compute the Mean

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{i} = \frac{1}{5}(101.2 + 100.8 + 101.5 + 100.9 + 101.1)$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{505.5}{5} = 101.1\;\text{kPa}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Deviations Squared
For each reading $x_i$, compute $d_i = x_i - \bar{x}$ and $d_i^{2}$.

| $i$ | $x_i$ (kPa) | $d_i = x_i - 101.1$ | $d_i^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 101.2 | +0.1 | 0.01 |
| 2 | 100.8 | -0.3 | 0.09 |
| 3 | 101.5 | +0.4 | 0.16 |
| 4 | 100.9 | -0.2 | 0.04 |
| 5 | 101.1 | 0.0 | 0.00 |
| **Sum** | — | — | **0.30** |

### Step 3 — Compute the Sample Standard Deviation

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum d_{i}^{2}}{n - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.30}{5 - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.30}{4}} = \sqrt{0.075} \approx 0.2739\;\text{kPa}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Standard Error of the Mean

$$u_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{0.2739}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{0.2739}{2.2361} \approx 0.1225\;\text{kPa}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the 95 % Confidence Interval

$$U_{95} = t_{0.025,4} \cdot u_{\bar{x}} = 2.776 \times 0.1225 \approx 0.340\;\text{kPa}$$

The final measurement is reported as:

$$\bar{x} \pm U_{95} = 101.1 \pm 0.34\;\text{kPa}\;\;(\text{at }95\,\% \text{ confidence})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Calibration Report Format (ISO 17025 Standard):**
> Always state measurement as $\text{Value} \pm U \text{ kPa}$ (where $U$ is the expanded uncertainty), include the **coverage factor $k$** and the **confidence level**. KTU examiners award 2 extra marks for proper reporting in laboratory/calibration problems.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Loading Error of a Voltmeter Connected to a Source

**Problem Statement:** A voltmeter of internal resistance $R_m = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ is used to measure the EMF of a source with Thevenin resistance $R_s = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$. The true EMF is $E = 5.00$ V. Derive the loading error.

### Step 1 — True Voltage (Open Circuit)
With no load, the true source voltage equals the EMF:

$$V_{true} = E = 5.00\;\text{V}$$

### Step 2 — Voltage Divider (With Voltmeter Connected)
When the voltmeter of $R_m$ is connected across the source, the circuit becomes a simple voltage divider. The measured voltage is:

$$V_{m} = E \cdot \frac{R_{m}}{R_{s} + R_{m}} = 5.00 \cdot \frac{10}{1 + 10} = 5.00 \cdot \frac{10}{11}$$

$$V_{m} = 4.545\;\text{V}$$

### Step 3 — Absolute Loading Error

$$E_{loading} = V_{m} - V_{true} = 4.545 - 5.00 = -0.455\;\text{V}$$

### Step 4 — Percentage Loading Error

$$e_{\%} = \frac{V_{m} - V_{true}}{V_{true}} \times 100 = \frac{-0.455}{5.00} \times 100 = -9.09\,\%$$

### Step 5 — General Loading Error Formula

$$e_{L} = \frac{R_{m}}{R_{s} + R_{m}} - 1 = -\frac{R_{s}}{R_{s} + R_{m}} \times 100\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** To keep loading error below 1 %, choose $R_m \geq 100 \cdot R_s$ (for voltmeters) and $R_m \leq 0.01 \cdot R_s$ (for ammeters). This is a frequently asked KTU 14-mark derivation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What Formula

| Engineering Situation | Primary Characteristic | Governing Formula | Key Caveat |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor giving 0.5 V at 0 kg and 2.5 V at 100 kg | Sensitivity | $K = \Delta y / \Delta x$ | Linear assumption |
| Five readings clustered around a mean | Precision / Repeatability | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sum d_i^2 / (n-1)}$ | Use $n-1$ for sample |
| Thermometer response to sudden bath | Dynamic lag | $y(t) = KA(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | 3τ settling rule |
| Voltmeter on non-ideal source | Loading error | $e_L = -R_s / (R_s + R_m)$ | $R_m$ must $\gg R_s$ |
| Two pressure readings differ at same input | Hysteresis | $e_H = \Delta H_{max} / \text{FS} \times 100$ | Check upscale vs downscale |
| Calibration certificate required | Uncertainty | $U = k \cdot u_c$ | $k = 2$ for 95 % confidence |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generalized Measurement System (Neubert's Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Measurand<br/>x input"] --> B["Primary Sensing<br/>Element Sensor"]
    B --> C["Variable Conversion<br/>Element Transducer"]
    C --> D["Variable Manipulation<br/>Signal Conditioning"]
    D --> E["Data Transmission<br/>Element"]
    E --> F["Data Presentation<br/>Element Display"]
    F --> G["Observer or<br/>Controller Output y"]
    H["Disturbance Noise<br/>Temperature Humidity"] -.-> B
    H -.-> C
    H -.-> D
```

**Interpretation Guide:** Each block contributes its own gain $K_i$ and error $E_i$. The cumulative error at the output is the sum (or RSS combination) of all stage errors. The dashed arrows represent environmental disturbances (temperature, EMI, vibration) that inject noise into the chain.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Calibration Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Reference Standard<br/>High Accuracy"] --> Q1["Apply Known<br/>Input Stimulus"]
    Q1 --> R1["Instrument Under<br/>Test IUT"]
    R1 --> S1["Measure Output<br/>of IUT"]
    S1 --> T1["Compute Deviation<br/>and Calibration Curve"]
    T1 --> U1["Generate Calibration<br/>Certificate"]
    U1 --> V1{"Deviation Within<br/>Tolerance"}
    V1 -->|Yes| W1["Instrument Approved<br/>for Use"]
    V1 -->|No| X1["Adjust or Reject<br/>Instrument"]
    X1 --> R1
```

**Interpretation:** A reference standard provides a known input to the IUT; the IUT's response is recorded; the deviation from the standard's true value is computed. If the deviation exceeds allowable tolerance, the instrument is adjusted (or rejected), and the cycle restarts.

## 4.3 Error Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Measurement<br/>Errors"] --> B["Systematic<br/>Errors"]
    A --> C["Random<br/>Errors"]
    A --> D["Gross<br/>Errors"]
    B --> B1["Instrument Error<br/>Mis-calibration"]
    B --> B2["Environmental Error<br/>Temperature Drift"]
    B --> B3["Loading Error<br/>Source-Sink Interaction"]
    C --> C1["Statistical Fluctuations<br/>Gaussian Noise"]
    C --> C2["Quantization Error<br/>ADC Resolution"]
    C --> C3["Interference EMI<br/>Stray Fields"]
    D --> D1["Human Blunder<br/>Misreading"]
    D --> D2["Recording Mistakes<br/>Transcription"]
    D --> D3["Instrument Misuse<br/>Wrong Range"]
```

**Interpretation:** Always classify an error before deciding how to handle it. Systematic errors require calibration. Random errors require statistical analysis. Gross errors require process improvement and training.

## 4.4 Static Characteristics — Master Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Static Characteristics<br/>of Measurement System"] --> B["Input-Output<br/>Relations"]
    A --> C["Error-Based<br/>Relations"]
    B --> B1["Sensitivity K"]
    B --> B2["Linearity"]
    B --> B3["Span and Range"]
    B --> B4["Resolution"]
    C --> C1["Accuracy"]
    C --> C2["Precision"]
    C --> C3["Hysteresis"]
    C --> C4["Drift and Dead Zone"]
    C --> C5["Repeatability and<br/>Reproducibility"]
```

**Interpretation:** Two super-categories: (1) characteristics that describe the *input-output mapping* (sensitivity, linearity, range, resolution), and (2) characteristics that describe the *error behaviour* (accuracy, precision, hysteresis, drift, repeatability). KTU exams often ask students to differentiate between these two categories.

## 4.5 Calibration Process Flow — Standard Industry Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1 Receive<br/>Instrument with<br/>Calibration Request"] --> S2["Step 2 Visual Inspection<br/>and Function Check"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 Select Reference<br/>Standard of Required<br/>Accuracy Class"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4 Calibrate Reference<br/>Against Higher Class<br/>Verify Traceability"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5 Perform Comparison<br/>at 5 to 10 Calibration<br/>Points Across Span"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6 Record Readings<br/>As Found and As Left"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7 Compute Deviation<br/>Uncertainty and<br/>Linearity"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8 Issue Calibration<br/>Certificate with<br/>Uncertainty Budget"]
    S8 --> S9["Step 9 Apply Calibration<br/>Sticker with Due Date<br/>and Signature"]
```

**Interpretation:** This is the ISO/IEC 17025 accredited laboratory workflow. For KTU lab viva questions, expect to be asked about *as-found vs as-left data* (As-Found: the data when the instrument first arrives; As-Left: data after any adjustment).
