```mermaid
    nodeC["ELECTROMAGNETIC FIELD: B = mu0 N I"]
    nodeD["ARMATURE: Movable ferromagnetic contact lever"]
    nodeE["SPRING: Provides restoring force Fs"]
    nodeF["NO CONTACT: Normally Open Load Terminal"]
    nodeG["COMMON CONTACT: Movable Pole Terminal"]
    nodeH["LOAD CIRCUIT: 230V AC Motor or Lamp"]
    nodeI["FLYBACK DIODE: Suppresses back-EMF spike"]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeE -. opposes motion .-> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeF --- nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeI --- nodeB
```

### 4.2 Solenoid Activation Sequence — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STEP 1: Switch Closed by Controller"]
    nodeB["STEP 2: Current I flows through N-turn coil"]
    nodeC["STEP 3: Magnetic Field B generated along axis"]
    nodeD["STEP 4: Plunger polarized and pulled into coil"]
    nodeE["STEP 5: Mechanical Work W = F times x done on load"]
    nodeF["STEP 6: Spring compressed and energy stored"]
    nodeG["STEP 7: Switch Opened by Controller"]
    nodeH["STEP 8: Magnetic field collapses"]
    nodeI["STEP 9: Spring returns plunger to rest position"]
    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF --> nodeG --> nodeH --> nodeI
```

### 4.3 Relay Switching Configurations — Functional Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSPST["SPST: Single Pole Single Throw - ON OFF switch only"]
    nodeSPDT["SPDT: Single Pole Double Throw - selects between two loads"]
    nodeDPST["DPST: Double Pole Single Throw - two simultaneous ON OFF circuits"]
    nodeDPDT["DPDT: Double Pole Double Throw - used in H-bridge motor reversal"]
    nodeNO["NO Contact: Open when coil de-energized"]
    nodeNC["NC Contact: Closed when coil de-energized"]
    nodeSPST --> nodeNO
    nodeSPDT --> nodeNO
    nodeSPDT --> nodeNC
    nodeDPDT --> nodeNO
    nodeDPDT --> nodeNC
```

### 4.4 Magnetic Circuit Equivalent of Relay + Solenoid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeMMF["MMF Source: NI Ampere turns"]
    nodeCore["Core Reluctance: Rcore"]
    nodeGap["Air Gap Reluctance: Rgap dominant term"]
    nodeFlux["Total Flux Phi in Webers"]
    nodeMMF --> nodeCore
    nodeMMF --> nodeGap
    nodeCore --> nodeFlux
    nodeGap --> nodeFlux
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Hopkinson's Law Analogy:** This magnetic circuit is mathematically identical to Ohm's Law, where $\text{MMF} = \Phi \times \mathcal{R}$. The **air gap reluctance** $\mathcal{R}_{gap}$ dominates because $\mu_0 \ll \mu_{iron}$, which is why most of the magnetic energy is stored in the gap — and *this* is precisely the energy converted to mechanical work.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define a relay and distinguish clearly between an **Electromechanical Relay (EMR)** and a **Solid State Relay (SSR)**. Mention any two advantages of SSR over EMR.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A relay is an **electromagnetic or solid-state switching device** in which a low-power control signal is used to switch a high-power load circuit, providing **electrical isolation** between the control and load sides.

**Distinction:**

| Parameter | Electromechanical Relay (EMR) | Solid State Relay (SSR) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|-------------------------|
| Switching Element | Physical moving contacts | TRIAC / MOSFET / IGBT |
| Moving Parts | Yes (armature + contacts) | None |
| Switching Speed | $10\text{–}100\text{ ms}$ | $1\text{–}10\text{ μs}$ |

**Two Advantages of SSR over EMR:**
1. **No moving parts** → silent operation, no contact bounce, no mechanical wear.
2. **Much longer operational lifetime** ($>10^9$ cycles vs. $10^5\text{–}10^7$ for EMR) due to absence of contact erosion.
3. *(Bonus)* Faster switching speed enables precise PWM control of heaters and AC loads.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
State **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** as applied to a relay coil. Why is a **flyback diode** used across the coil?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Faraday's Law Statement:**
$$\varepsilon = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -L \frac{di}{dt}$$

When the magnetic flux $\Phi$ linking the relay coil changes (during switch-OFF), an EMF $\varepsilon$ is induced that **opposes the change** in current (Lenz's Law).

**Purpose of Flyback Diode:**
When the control transistor switches OFF, the inductor tries to maintain current flow (since $V_L = -L \frac{di}{dt}$ can spike to hundreds of volts). This **back-EMF spike** can destroy the driving transistor/MOSFET.

A **flyback diode** placed in reverse-bias across the coil provides a safe path for the decaying inductor current to recirculate, **clamping the voltage** to approximately $0.7\text{ V}$ (one diode drop) and protecting the driver circuit.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat functional diagram, explain the **construction and working of an electromagnetic relay**. Discuss the role of the **flyback diode** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A relay coil has a resistance of $R = 500\text{ Ω}$ and inductance $L = 2\text{ H}$. It is energized by a $V_c = 24\text{ V DC}$ source. Calculate:
- (i) The steady-state current $I_{max}$
- (ii) The time constant $\tau$
- (iii) The time $t_{op}$ required for the current to reach **63.2%** of its maximum value
- (iv) The energy stored in the coil at steady state. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Construction and Working of Electromagnetic Relay (7 Marks):**

**Construction:**
An electromagnetic relay consists of the following components:
1. **Coil** — A bobbin wound with many turns of fine enameled copper wire (typically $1000\text{–}10{,}000$ turns).
2. **Core** — A soft iron yoke inside the coil to concentrate magnetic flux.
3. **Armature** — A pivoted soft iron lever that moves when the coil is energized.
4. **Contacts** — Stationary (fixed) and movable contacts forming **NO** (Normally Open) and **NC** (Normally Closed) terminals.
5. **Spring** — A return spring that holds the armature in the de-energized position.
6. **Yoke** — Magnetic flux return path connecting the core and armature.

**Working:**
- When control voltage $V_c$ is applied to the coil, current $I_c$ flows and produces a magnetic flux $\Phi$ in the core.
- This flux magnetizes the armature, which is **attracted toward the core** against the spring force.
- The moving contact shifts from the NC terminal to the NO terminal, **closing the load circuit** and energizing the high-power load.
- When $V_c$ is removed, the spring restores the armature, returning the contact to its initial NC position.

**Flyback Diode Explanation:**
During switch-OFF, the collapsing magnetic field induces a large reverse EMF $V_L = -L \frac{di}{dt}$. A **flyback diode** (e.g., 1N4007) connected in reverse-bias parallel to the coil provides a freewheeling path. The inductor current circulates through the diode and decays as $i(t) = I_{max} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$, with $\tau = L/R_{coil}$. This protects the driving transistor from voltage breakdown.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $R = 500\text{ Ω}$, $L = 2\text{ H}$, $V_c = 24\text{ V}$.

**(i) Steady-State Current $I_{max}$:**
At steady state, $\frac{di}{dt} = 0$, so the inductor behaves as a short circuit:

$$
I_{max} = \frac{V_c}{R} = \frac{24}{500} = 0.048 \text{ A} = 48 \text{ mA}
$$

**(ii) Time Constant $\tau$:**

$$
\tau = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2}{500} = 0.004 \text{ s} = 4 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iii) Time to Reach 63.2% of $I_{max}$:**
By definition of the time constant, $i(\tau) = I_{max}(1 - e^{-1}) = 0.632 \cdot I_{max}$.

$$
\therefore t_{op} = \tau = 4 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iv) Energy Stored at Steady State:**

$$
W_m = \frac{1}{2} L I_{max}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times (0.048)^2
$$

$$
W_m = 1 \times 0.002304 = 2.304 \text{ mJ} = 2.304 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}
$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **magnetic pull force** of a solenoid. State clearly all assumptions made during the derivation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A solenoid has $N = 1000$ turns, length $\ell = 0.1\text{ m}$, and pole area $A = 4 \times 10^{-4}\text{ m}^2$. If a current of $I = 2\text{ A}$ flows through the coil, calculate:
- (i) The magnetic flux density $B$ inside the coil.
- (ii) The axial pull force $F$ on the plunger.
- (iii) The mechanical work done if the plunger moves $5\text{ mm}$ against the load.
- (iv) Comment on how the force would change if the air gap were filled with soft iron ($\mu_r = 5000$). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Derivation of Solenoid Pull Force (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The solenoid is infinitely long (end effects neglected).
2. The magnetic field is uniform inside the coil and zero outside.
3. The air gap permeability is $\mu_0$ (no fringing).
4. The plunger is a perfect ferromagnetic material with $\mu_r \to \infty$.

**Derivation:**

Starting with the Maxwell magnetic pressure on the air gap:

$$
P = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}
$$

The solenoid produces an axial field:

$$
B = \mu_0 \frac{N}{\ell} I
$$

Multiplying pressure by pole-face area $A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= P \cdot A = \frac{B^2 \cdot A}{2\mu_0} \\
&= \frac{1}{2\mu_0} \cdot A \cdot \left( \mu_0 \frac{N}{\ell} I \right)^2 \\
&= \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2 \ell^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{F = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2\ell^2}}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $N = 1000$, $\ell = 0.1\text{ m}$, $A = 4 \times 10^{-4}\text{ m}^2$, $I = 2\text{ A}$.

**(i) Magnetic Flux Density $B$:**

$$
B = \mu_0 \frac{N}{\ell} I = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot \frac{1000}{0.1} \cdot 2
$$

$$
B = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (20{,}000) = 8\pi \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}
$$

$$
B \approx 0.02513 \text{ T} = 25.13 \text{ mT}
$$

**(ii) Axial Pull Force $F$:**

$$
F = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2\ell^2}
$$

$$
F = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (1000)^2 \cdot (2)^2 \cdot (4 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \cdot (0.1)^2}
$$

$$
F = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 10^6 \cdot 4 \cdot (4 \times 10^{-4})}{0.02}
$$

$$
F = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 1.6}{0.02} = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 80 = 320\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ N}
$$

$$
F \approx 1.005 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N} = 0.1005 \text{ mN}
$$

**(iii) Mechanical Work Done for $x = 5\text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\text{ m}$:**

$$
W = F \cdot x = 1.005 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 5.027 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J} \approx 0.503 \text{ μJ}
$$

**(iv) Effect of Filling Air Gap with Soft Iron ($\mu_r = 5000$):**

If the air gap is filled with soft iron, the effective permeability becomes $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$. However, the Maxwell force equation for an *iron-filled* gap uses the field inside the iron path. For an **iron-core solenoid**, the field $B$ becomes:

$$
B_{iron} = \mu_0 \mu_r \frac{N}{\ell} I = 5000 \cdot B_{air}
$$

The force scales as $F \propto B^2$, so:

$$
F_{iron} = \mu_r^2 \cdot F_{air} = (5000)^2 \cdot 0.1005 \text{ mN}
$$

$$
F_{iron} = 2.5 \times 10^7 \cdot 0.1005 \text{ mN} \approx 2512.5 \text{ N}
$$

**Conclusion:** Introducing a soft iron core dramatically increases the pull force by a factor of **$\mu_r^2$** (approximately $25 \times 10^6$ times). This is why all practical solenoids use ferromagnetic cores.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Units in the Solenoid Formula:** Many students write $F = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2\ell^2}$ but compute numerically without unit consistency. Always verify that $A$ is in m², $\ell$ in m, $I$ in A, and $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m. **Loss of marks: up to 2.**
> 2. **Mixing Up Time Constant Definition:** Students confuse $\tau = L/R$ with $\tau = RC$. For relay coils (inductive), $\tau = L/R$ is correct. **Loss of marks: up to 1.**
> 3. **Not Drawing Flyback Diode in Reverse Bias:** When asked to draw the relay driver circuit, students often place the diode in forward bias — this shorts the supply voltage and is **functionally wrong**. **Loss of marks: up to 2.**
> 4. **Confusing EMR with SSR Comparison:** Writing "SSR is faster than EMR" without quantifying ($1\text{ μs}$ vs $10\text{ ms}$) loses marks. Always provide numerical justification.
> 5. **Skipping Assumptions in Derivations:** The KTU Board strictly requires stating assumptions (e.g., "uniform field," "no fringing," "ideal soft iron"). Omitting them costs **1 full mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Relay = Electromagnetic Switch** (low-power control → high-power load).
- **Solenoid = Linear Electromagnetic Actuator** (electrical → linear mechanical motion).
- **Solenoid Field Equation:** $B = \mu_0 \frac{N}{\ell} I$ — Ampere's Law result for long solenoid.
- **Solenoid Force Equation:** $F = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2\ell^2}$ — quadratic in $N$ and $I$, linear in $A$, inverse-square in $\ell$.
- **Energy Stored in Coil:** $W_m = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$.
- **Relay RL Transient:** $i(t) = I_{max}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$, where $\tau = L/R$.
- **Time to reach 63.2% of $I_{max}$ = one time constant $\tau$.**
- **Flyback Diode:** Mandatory across DC relay coils to suppress back-EMF $V_L = -L \frac{di}{dt}$.
- **SPDT vs DPDT:** SPDT has 1 pole, 2 throws; DPDT has 2 poles, 2 throws (used in H-bridges).
- **EMR vs SSR:** EMR = mechanical contacts, slower, lower life; SSR = semiconductor, faster, longer life.
- **Force Scaling with Iron Core:** $F \propto \mu_r^2$ — explains why real solenoids always use ferromagnetic plungers.
- **Right-Hand Rule:** Determines solenoid North Pole direction (KTU favorite for diagrams).
- **Magnetic Reluctance Dominance:** Air gap reluctance $\mathcal{R}_{gap} = \frac{g}{\mu_0 A}$ dominates because $\mu_0$ is small.
- **Hopkinson's Law:** $\text{MMF} = \Phi \cdot \mathcal{R}$ — analogous to $V = IR$ in electrical circuits.
- **Pull-Type vs Push-Type Solenoids:** Pull-type retracts plunger inward; push-type extends it outward.
- **Latching Relays:** Bistable relays retain state without continuous coil current — used for memory circuits.
- **Practical Note:** A relay coil draws **holding current** (less) and **inrush current** (more) due to initial low impedance of unsaturated core.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proximity Pickups
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT521 - MECHATRONICS AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Proximity Pickups

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Proximity Pickups — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proximity Pickup (Proximity Sensor):** A non-contact transduction device that detects the presence, absence, position, or distance of a target object within a defined sensing range, without any mechanical contact between the sensor and the target. It converts a physical change (electromagnetic field, capacitance, light intensity, or acoustic reflection) caused by the approaching target into a usable electrical output signal.

In the context of **Mechatronics and Control Systems**, proximity pickups form the *primary sensing layer* of any automated system. They act as the "eyes and skin" of a machine, providing binary or analog feedback to the controller (PLC, microcontroller, or embedded processor) about the state of the operating environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Note:** KTU examiners use the terms **"Proximity Pickup"** and **"Proximity Sensor"** interchangeably. A "pickup" emphasizes its role as a *signal-source* device that "picks up" information from the surroundings.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Bumper"

Imagine you are blindfolded and walking in a dark room. You cannot touch the walls, but as you approach one, you feel the air resistance (a change in pressure field) and you know the wall is near. You have detected an object **without touching it**.

A proximity pickup works exactly like this — but instead of air pressure, it uses:
- **Electromagnetic fields** (Inductive type)
- **Electric fields** (Capacitive type)
- **Light beams** (Optical/Photoelectric type)
- **Sound waves** (Ultrasonic type)
- **Magnetic fields** (Hall-effect / Reed-switch type)

The moment the invisible "field" is disturbed by an object, the sensor *trips* and gives a signal.

## 1.3 Classification Overview

| Type | Detects | Sensing Medium | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inductive** | Metallic targets only | Electromagnetic field | 1–50 mm |
| **Capacitive** | Metal, plastic, liquid, wood | Electric field (capacitance) | 1–50 mm |
| **Magnetic (Hall/Reed)** | Magnets | Magnetic flux | 2–20 mm |
| **Optical (Photoelectric)** | Any opaque/translucent object | Light (IR, visible, laser) | Up to 100 m |
| **Ultrasonic** | Any solid/liquid | Sound waves (>20 kHz) | 50 mm – 10 m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **standard sensing distance** (Sn) for any proximity sensor is measured using a **standard target** — a square piece of mild steel (Fe 360) of 1 mm thickness, with side length equal to the sensor's sensing face diameter, placed in air at **23 °C ± 5 °C**.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light (vacuum):** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Speed of sound (air, 20 °C):** $v_s \approx 343 \text{ m/s}$
- **Standard target material:** Mild Steel (Fe 360), **1 mm** thick
- **Standard operating temperature:** **−25 °C to +70 °C** (industrial grade)
- **Supply voltage (typical):** **10–30 V DC** (3-wire type) or **20–250 V AC** (2-wire type)
- **Switching frequency:** Typically **100 Hz – 5 kHz** depending on type
- **Repeatability:** Usually **≤ 10% of Sn**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Type 1 — Inductive Proximity Sensor

### 2.1.1 Working Principle
An inductive proximity sensor contains an **LC oscillator** (a coil of wire wound around a ferrite core connected to a capacitor). When **no target** is present, the oscillator produces a stable high-frequency electromagnetic field (typically **100 kHz – 1 MHz**).

When a **ferrous or non-ferrous metal target** enters this field, **eddy currents** are induced on the target's surface (per Lenz's Law). These eddy currents draw energy from the oscillator, causing the **oscillation amplitude to drop**. A detector circuit senses this amplitude drop and triggers the output stage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why only metals?** Eddy currents require free electrons. Metals have abundant free electrons; plastics, wood, and liquids do not — so inductive sensors ignore them.

### 2.1.2 Operating Logic Steps
1. Oscillator generates HF alternating current in the sensing coil.
2. Coil produces a high-frequency electromagnetic field at the active face.
3. Metal target enters the field → induces eddy currents in target.
4. Eddy currents cause energy loss → oscillator amplitude decays.
5. Trigger circuit compares amplitude to a threshold.
6. If amplitude < threshold → output transistor switches ON/OFF.
7. Output is fed to a PLC/microcontroller.

### 2.1.3 Key Equations

The inductance of the sensing coil is given by:

$$L = \frac{N^2 \mu A}{l}$$

where $N$ = number of turns, $\mu$ = permeability of the core, $A$ = cross-sectional area, and $l$ = magnetic path length.

The energy dissipated as eddy currents in the target is:

$$P_{eddy} = \frac{B^2 f^2 t^2 k}{6 \rho}$$

where $B$ = magnetic flux density, $f$ = frequency, $t$ = target thickness, $k$ = constant, and $\rho$ = resistivity of target.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction factor (K-factor):** Because eddy current efficiency depends on target material, real sensing range $S_r = K \times S_n$, where $K$ is a material-specific multiplier. For **mild steel = 1.0**, **stainless steel ≈ 0.6–0.85**, **aluminum ≈ 0.3–0.45**, **copper ≈ 0.25–0.4**.

### 2.1.4 Real-World Utility
Used in **CNC machines** for tool-position verification, **conveyor systems** for part counting, **automotive assembly** for piston-position detection, and **packaging lines** for metal-cap detection.

---

## 2.2 Type 2 — Capacitive Proximity Sensor

### 2.2.1 Working Principle
A capacitive sensor has **two concentric electrodes** on its active face, which together with the target form a **capacitor**. The target acts as the third plate (or dielectric) of the capacitor.

The capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

where $\varepsilon_0$ = permittivity of free space ($8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$), $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of target, $A$ = effective plate area, and $d$ = distance to target.

When the target approaches, **$d$ decreases**, and since $C \propto 1/d$, the capacitance **increases**. A tuned oscillator detects this change and switches the output.

> [!NOTE]
> Capacitive sensors can detect **both conductive and non-conductive targets** (plastics, glass, wood, paper, liquids) because even non-conductive materials change the effective dielectric between the plates.

### 2.2.2 Operating Logic Steps
1. Internal oscillator circuit energizes the two electrodes.
2. Target approaches the active face.
3. Capacitance between the electrodes changes (increases).
4. Oscillator amplitude/frequency shifts due to the load change.
5. Schmitt trigger detects threshold crossing.
6. Output transistor switches.

### 2.2.3 Key Equations

Oscillator frequency shift (approximate):

$$\Delta f \approx -\frac{f_0 \cdot \Delta C}{2 C_0}$$

where $f_0$ is the nominal oscillator frequency, $C_0$ is the baseline capacitance, and $\Delta C$ is the change due to the target.

---

## 2.3 Type 3 — Optical (Photoelectric) Proximity Sensor

### 2.3.1 Modes of Operation
1. **Diffuse Reflective:** Emitter and receiver in the same housing. Light reflects off the target back to the receiver. Range: **mm to ~1 m**.
2. **Retro-Reflective:** Emitter-receiver in one housing; light reflects off a separate reflector. Target breaks the beam. Range: **up to 10 m**.
3. **Through-Beam:** Separate emitter and receiver housings. Target breaks the beam. Range: **up to 100 m**.

### 2.3.2 Key Equation — Time-of-Flight (for long-range laser sensors)

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

where $c$ = speed of light ($3 \times 10^8$ m/s) and $t$ = round-trip time of the light pulse. The factor of **2** appears because light travels to the target and back.

---

## 2.4 Type 4 — Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor

### 2.4.1 Working Principle
A piezoelectric transducer emits a **high-frequency sound pulse** (typically **25–500 kHz**, well above human hearing). The pulse travels through air, hits a target, and reflects back. The sensor measures the echo time.

### 2.4.2 Key Equation — Distance from Echo Time

$$d = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$$

where $v_s$ = speed of sound in air (≈ 343 m/s at 20 °C) and $t$ = round-trip echo time.

The speed of sound varies with air temperature as:

$$v_s \approx 331 + 0.6 \cdot T \quad \text{(m/s, where } T \text{ is in °C)}$$

---

## 2.5 Type 5 — Magnetic (Hall-Effect & Reed) Proximity Sensor

### 2.5.1 Hall-Effect Working Principle
When a current-carrying semiconductor is placed in a magnetic field perpendicular to the current, a voltage is generated across the sides of the conductor (Hall voltage):

$$V_H = \frac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot e \cdot t}$$

where $I$ = current through the conductor, $B$ = magnetic flux density, $n$ = charge carrier density, $e$ = electron charge, and $t$ = thickness of the conductor.

### 2.5.2 Reed-Switch Working Principle
Two ferromagnetic reeds inside a sealed glass tube. An approaching magnet magnetizes the reeds, which attract and close the circuit mechanically (no actual contact with the target — only the reeds touch each other inside the tube).

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = N^2 \mu A / l$ | Sensing coil inductance | Henry (H) |
| 2 | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | Capacitance of capacitive sensor | Farad (F) |
| 3 | $d = c \cdot t / 2$ | Distance via light time-of-flight | meter (m) |
| 4 | $d = v_s \cdot t / 2$ | Distance via ultrasonic echo | meter (m) |
| 5 | $v_s = 331 + 0.6 T$ | Speed of sound vs temperature | m/s |
| 6 | $V_H = IB/(net)$ | Hall voltage | Volt (V) |
| 7 | $S_r = K \times S_n$ | Real sensing range with correction | mm |
| 8 | $f_{sw,max}$ (typ. 100 Hz–5 kHz) | Switching frequency | Hz |

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** ABS wheel-speed sensing, gear-tooth detection.
- **Factory automation:** Part counting on conveyors, end-of-travel limit detection on pneumatic cylinders.
- **Robotics:** Obstacle avoidance, docking alignment.
- **Process control:** Liquid level sensing in tanks (capacitive).
- **CNC & machine tools:** Tool-length measurement, broken-tool detection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time-of-Flight Distance Equation for an Ultrasonic Sensor

**Problem Statement:** An ultrasonic proximity sensor emits a 40 kHz pulse towards a target. The echo is received **6 ms** later. Assuming ambient air temperature of **20 °C**, calculate the distance to the target.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing physical law.**

Sound travels from the sensor to the target, reflects, and returns. The total measured time $t$ is therefore the **round-trip time**. We must use:

$$d = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2}$$

The factor of 2 accounts for the to-and-fro travel of the sound pulse.

**Step 2 — Calculate the speed of sound at 20 °C.**

Using the temperature-dependent formula:

$$v_s = 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$$

Substituting $T = 20$ °C:

$$v_s = 331 + 0.6 \times 20 = 331 + 12 = 343 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert the measured echo time into SI units.**

$$t = 6 \text{ ms} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the distance equation.**

$$d = \frac{343 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the numerator.**

$$343 \times 6 \times 10^{-3} = 2058 \times 10^{-3} = 2.058 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Divide by 2 to account for round-trip.**

$$d = \frac{2.058}{2} = 1.029 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$d \approx 1.029 \text{ m}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capacitance Change in a Capacitive Proximity Sensor

**Problem Statement:** A capacitive proximity sensor has two coplanar electrodes, each of effective area $A = 25 \text{ mm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$. The target is a plastic block ($\varepsilon_r = 4$) approaching the sensor. When the target is at distance $d_1 = 8 \text{ mm}$ and then at $d_2 = 3 \text{ mm}$, calculate the change in capacitance $\Delta C$.

**Step 1 — Use the parallel-plate capacitance formula.**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

with $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.

**Step 2 — Compute $C_1$ at distance $d_1 = 8 \text{ mm} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$.**

$$C_1 = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}{8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Simplify the numerator:

$$8.854 \times 4 \times 25 = 885.4$$

$$C_1 = \frac{885.4 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{-6}}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{885.4 \times 10^{-18}}{8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_1 = 110.675 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F} = 110.675 \text{ fF}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_2$ at distance $d_2 = 3 \text{ mm} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$.**

$$C_2 = \frac{885.4 \times 10^{-18}}{3 \times 10^{-3}} = 295.13 \times 10^{-15} \text{ F} = 295.13 \text{ fF}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the change in capacitance.**

$$\Delta C = C_2 - C_1 = 295.13 - 110.675 = 184.46 \text{ fF}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\Delta C \approx 184.5 \text{ fF} = 1.845 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hall Voltage Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A Hall-effect sensor uses a thin silicon strip of thickness $t = 100 \text{ \mu m}$ and charge-carrier density $n = 1.5 \times 10^{21} \text{ m}^{-3}$. A current of $I = 5 \text{ mA}$ flows through the strip, and it is exposed to a magnetic flux density $B = 0.3 \text{ T}$. Calculate the Hall voltage. Take $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C.

**Step 1 — Recall the Hall voltage equation.**

$$V_H = \frac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot e \cdot t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$V_H = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.3}{(1.5 \times 10^{21}) \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times (100 \times 10^{-6})}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator.**

$$\text{Numerator} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.3 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator step-by-step.**

$$1.5 \times 10^{21} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = 2.4 \times 10^{2} = 240$$

$$240 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 240 \times 10^{-4} = 2.4 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 5 — Divide.**

$$V_H = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2.4 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.0625 \text{ V} = 62.5 \text{ mV}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$V_H = 62.5 \text{ mV}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Inductive | Capacitive | Optical | Ultrasonic | Hall/Reed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Detects | Metals only | Metals + non-metals | Opaque objects | Most materials | Magnets only |
| Range | 1–50 mm | 1–50 mm | Up to 100 m | 50 mm–10 m | 2–20 mm |
| Output | Digital (PNP/NPN) | Digital/Analog | Digital/Analog | Analog/Digital | Digital |
| Switching freq. | Up to 5 kHz | Up to 100 Hz | Up to 2 kHz | ~10–50 Hz | ~500 Hz |
| Hysteresis | ~10% of Sn | ~20% of Sn | Minimal | ~5% of range | ~10% of Sn |
| Cost | Low | Low–Medium | Medium | High | Low |
| Susceptible to | Target material | Humidity, dust | Dust, opacity | Temp, soft foam | Stray fields |
| IP rating (typical) | IP67 | IP65 | IP65 | IP67 | IP67 |

---

## 3.5 Output Configurations — PNP vs NPN

A frequently asked KTU concept is the difference between **PNP (sourcing)** and **NPN (sinking)** outputs:

- **NPN output:** Sensor switches the **negative** line. Load is connected between V+ and the sensor's output. Current *sinks* into the sensor's output terminal.
- **PNP output:** Sensor switches the **positive** line. Load is connected between the sensor's output and ground (0V). Current *sources* from the sensor.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** **"PNP Pushes (Positive), NPN Pulls (Negative)"** — PNP pushes current *out* to the load, NPN pulls current *in* from the load.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Proximity Pickup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceA[DC Supply 10 to 30 V] --> regA[Voltage Regulator Block]
    regA --> oscA[Oscillator Circuit]
    oscA --> coilA[Sensing Coil or Active Face]
    coilA -->|electromagnetic field| targA[Target Object]
    targA -->|eddy current feedback| coilA
    coilA --> detA[Detector and Threshold Comparator]
    detA --> trigA[Schmitt Trigger]
    trigA --> outA[Output Stage PNP or NPN Transistor]
    outA --> loadA[Load PLC or Microcontroller]
    regA --> detA
    regA --> trigA
    regA --> outA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Inductive Sensor Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Sensor Operation] --> oscRun[Oscillator Produces HF Field]
    oscRun --> queryA{Target in Range?}
    queryA -->|No| idleA[Oscillator Amplitude Stable HIGH]
    queryA -->|Yes, Metal| eddyA[Eddy Currents Induced in Target]
    eddyA --> dampA[Oscillator Amplitude Decays]
    dampA --> compA[Comparator Checks Amplitude]
    compA --> threshA{Amplitude Below Threshold?}
    threshA -->|Yes| tripA[Output Transistor Switches ON]
    threshA -->|No| idleA
    tripA --> outA[Digital Signal to Controller]
    outA --> resetA[Wait for Target to Leave]
    resetA --> oscRun
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Optical Through-Beam Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    emitterX[Emitter LED IR Source] -->|light beam| targetX[Target Object]
    targetX -->|beam blocked| receiverX[Receiver Photodiode or Phototransistor]
    receiverX --> ampX[Amplifier Stage]
    ampX --> compX[Comparator]
    compX --> outX[Output PNP or NPN Switch]
    outX --> ctrlX[PLC or Microcontroller]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a **through-beam** configuration, the emitter and receiver are physically separated. The sensor output activates when the beam is **broken** (target present) — this is a fail-safe arrangement because a wire cut also breaks the beam and triggers the alarm.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Ultrasonic Time-of-Flight

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ptxA[Piezo Transmitter] -->|pulse 40 kHz| airA[Air Medium]
    airA -->|reflection| targetA[Target Object]
    targetA -->|echo| airA
    airA --> prxA[Piezo Receiver]
    prxA --> tdcA[Time to Digital Converter]
    tdcA --> calcA[Microcontroller Computes d equals v sub s times t divided by 2]
    calcA --> outA[Distance Output mm or m]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Comparison — PNP vs NPN Output

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PNP_Branch [PNP Sourcing Configuration]
        v1[V plus 24V] --> l1[Load Relay or Lamp]
        l1 --> o1[Sensor Output PNP]
        o1 --> g1[0V Ground]
    end
    subgraph NPN_Branch [NPN Sinking Configuration]
        v2[V plus 24V] --> l2[Load Relay or Lamp]
        l2 --> o2[Sensor Output NPN]
        o2 --> g2[0V Ground]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]
**Define a proximity pickup. List any two types of proximity sensors with one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A proximity pickup is a non-contact transducer that detects the presence or distance of an object within a defined range by sensing changes in an electromagnetic, electric, optical, or acoustic field.

- **Inductive proximity sensor** — used for detecting metal parts on a conveyor (e.g., counting steel bolts).
- **Capacitive proximity sensor** — used for liquid level detection inside a non-metallic tank.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is a Hall-effect sensor? State one industrial application.**

**Model Answer:**
A Hall-effect sensor detects magnetic fields by producing a transverse Hall voltage when current-carrying semiconductor material is exposed to a magnetic flux. The Hall voltage is given by $V_H = IB/(net)$.

**Application:** Used as a **crankshaft position sensor** in automotive engines for ignition-timing control.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 14 Marks]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an **inductive proximity sensor**. State two advantages and one limitation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An inductive sensor has a nominal sensing range $S_n = 8 \text{ mm}$ for a mild-steel target. Find the effective range when detecting (i) stainless steel (K = 0.75) and (ii) aluminum (K = 0.40). Comment on why the range varies. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Oscillator] → [Sensing Coil] → [Field] → [Target → Eddy Currents] 
            ↓ (feedback)                  ↓
        [Detector] ← [Amplitude Drop] ← [Energy Loss]
            ↓
        [Schmitt Trigger] → [Output Transistor] → [Load]
```

**Working Principle:**

1. A high-frequency LC oscillator drives a coil wound on a ferrite core, producing an alternating electromagnetic field at the sensor's active face.
2. When a metallic target enters the field, eddy currents are induced on the target's surface (Lenz's law).
3. These eddy currents dissipate energy, lowering the oscillator's amplitude.
4. A detector circuit compares the amplitude to an internal reference.
5. When the amplitude falls below the threshold, the Schmitt trigger fires the output transistor (PNP or NPN).
6. The output goes HIGH/LOW depending on the configuration.

**Advantages:**
- No mechanical contact → long operational life (≥ 10⁸ switching cycles).
- Insensitive to dirt, oil, and water → ideal for harsh industrial environments (IP67).

**Limitations:**
- Detects only metallic targets; cannot sense plastics, wood, or liquids.

#### Model Solution for (b)

Given $S_n = 8$ mm; $S_r = K \times S_n$.

**(i) Stainless steel (K = 0.75):**

$$S_r = 0.75 \times 8 = 6.0 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Aluminum (K = 0.40):**

$$S_r = 0.40 \times 8 = 3.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Reason for variation:** The eddy-current efficiency depends on the target's **electrical conductivity and magnetic permeability**. Mild steel has both high conductivity and high permeability, producing strong eddy currents. Stainless steel has lower conductivity and permeability, while aluminum — although highly conductive — is non-magnetic, leading to weaker eddy current coupling with the sensor's field.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Sample, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain the working of a **capacitive proximity sensor** with a neat diagram. How does it differ from an inductive type? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An ultrasonic sensor measures a round-trip echo time of **t = 4 ms** at **30 °C** ambient. Calculate the distance to the target. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Working Principle:**

A capacitive proximity sensor consists of two concentric electrodes that form a capacitor with the target acting as the third electrode (or altering the dielectric medium between them).

1. The two electrodes are excited by a high-frequency oscillator.
2. The target forms a capacitor with these electrodes.
3. As the target approaches, the effective distance $d$ decreases, causing capacitance to increase per the relation:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

4. The increased capacitance loads the oscillator, shifting its frequency/amplitude.
5. A detector circuit senses this shift and triggers the output.

**Diagram:**

```
[Oscillator] → [Electrode 1]    [Target]
                  [Electrode 2]    ↑
                       ↓
              [Capacitance C↑ as d↓]
                       ↓
              [Detector/Trigger] → [Output]
```

**Differences from Inductive Sensor:**

| Aspect | Inductive | Capacitive |
|---|---|---|
| Detects | Metals only | Metals + non-metals (plastic, wood, liquid) |
| Operating field | Electromagnetic | Electrostatic |
| Typical range | Up to 50 mm | Up to 50 mm |
| Affected by | Target conductivity | Dielectric constant of target |
| Material correction factor (K) | Yes, varies widely | Yes, but more uniform |

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Find the speed of sound at 30 °C:**

$$v_s = 331 + 0.6 \times 30 = 331 + 18 = 349 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert echo time to seconds:**

$$t = 4 \text{ ms} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the round-trip formula:**

$$d = \frac{v_s \cdot t}{2} = \frac{349 \times 4 \times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$d = \frac{1.396}{2} = 0.698 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$d \approx 0.698 \text{ m} \approx 698 \text{ mm}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in time-of-flight (ultrasonic and laser) distance calculations — always remember the pulse travels *to* and *back from* the target.
> 2. **Confusing PNP and NPN** — students frequently write the wrong polarity. Recall: **PNP = Positive Switching = Sources current; NPN = Negative Switching = Sinks current.**
> 3. **Using wrong units** — $d$ must be in **meters** for SI-based capacitance calculation; convert mm carefully.
> 4. **Ignoring the K-correction factor** in inductive sensors — using $S_n$ directly when the target is *not* mild steel is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Mixing up sensor types** — stating that an inductive sensor detects "all objects" or that a Hall sensor detects "metals" is incorrect and reflects poor conceptual clarity.
> 6. **No block diagram** — KTU examiners expect a labelled functional block diagram in 7-mark questions. Skipping it costs a full 2-mark deduction.
> 7. **Forgetting the speed-of-sound temperature correction** in ultrasonic problems — at 30 °C, $v_s = 349$ m/s, not 343 m/s.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Proximity Pickups (Module 1):**

- ⭐ **Inductive sensors** detect **only metals** via **eddy current damping** of an HF oscillator. Use $S_r = K \cdot S_n$ for non-steel targets.
- ⭐ **Capacitive sensors** detect **any material** (metal or non-metal) by change in $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$.
- ⭐ **Optical sensors** come in **3 modes** — diffuse, retro-reflective, and through-beam. The through-beam is the most reliable (fail-safe).
- ⭐ **Ultrasonic sensors** use the equation $d = v_s \cdot t / 2$ with $v_s = 331 + 0.6 T$. Remember the **factor of 2**.
- ⭐ **Hall-effect sensors** output $V_H = IB/(net)$ and detect **magnetic fields only**.
- ⭐ **Standard target** for inductive sensor specs is **1 mm thick Fe 360 mild steel**.
- ⭐ **Standard conditions** are **23 °C ± 5 °C** in air.
- ⭐ **PNP sources** (pushes current to load); **NPN sinks** (pulls current from load). The phrase "**PNP Pushes, NPN Pulls**" is the standard KTU memory aid.
- ⭐ **Switching frequency** of inductive sensors can be as high as **5 kHz**, while ultrasonic is typically only **10–50 Hz**.
- ⭐ **Repeatability** is usually within **±10% of $S_n$** for industrial-grade sensors.
- ⭐ **Hysteresis** is the difference between the switch-on and switch-off points — typically **5–20% of $S_n$** — preventing output chattering.
- ⭐ **IP67** rating (typical for inductive and ultrasonic) means **dust-tight** and **immersion-proof up to 1 m for 30 minutes**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal Conditioning: Operational Amplifiers, Filtering, and Multiplexers; Input/Output Systems: Ports
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT521 - MECHATRONICS AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Signal Conditioning: Operational Amplifiers, Filtering, and Multiplexers; Input/Output Systems: Ports

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Signal Conditioning — The Formal Definition

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework for Mechatronics and Control Systems (PCAUT521), **Signal Conditioning** is formally defined as the *deliberate manipulation of an analog or digital signal from a transducer/sensor into a form that is suitable for further processing, transmission, or display by an embedded controller (microcontroller/PLC/DSP).*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):** Signal Conditioning is the **bridge layer** between the physical sensing domain (Module 1: Sensors/Transducers) and the digital control domain (Module 3: Microcontrollers/PLCs). Without it, raw millivolt-level sensor outputs cannot be processed by a 0–5 V or 0–3.3 V ADC.

The four canonical operations of a signal conditioning circuit are:

1. **Amplification** — boosting weak signals (mV range) to usable levels (V range).
2. **Filtering** — removing unwanted frequency components (noise).
3. **Isolation** — galvanic separation between high-voltage and low-voltage sides.
4. **Multiplexing / Level Translation** — routing multiple signals onto a single ADC channel and converting bipolar signals to unipolar levels.

---

## 1.2 The Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp) — The Heart of Signal Conditioning

### Formal Definition
An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a *direct-coupled, high-gain, DC electronic voltage amplifier with differential inputs and a single-ended output*. In signal conditioning, it is treated as an **ideal building block** with infinite open-loop gain ($A_{OL} \to \infty$), infinite input impedance ($Z_{in} \to \infty$), zero output impedance ($Z_{out} = 0$), and infinite common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR $\to \infty$).

### The Block Diagram and Pinout
A standard Op-Amp (e.g., **µA741, LM358, TL081**) has **8 pins**:
- **Pin 2:** Inverting input ($V^-$)
- **Pin 3:** Non-inverting input ($V^+$)
- **Pin 6:** Output ($V_{out}$)
- **Pin 7:** Positive supply (+$V_{CC}$, typically +12 V to +15 V)
- **Pin 4:** Negative supply (–$V_{EE}$ or GND)
- **Pins 1, 5, 8:** Offset null and NC (no connection).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Leverage" Idea:** Imagine a tiny microphone that produces only **2 mV** when someone speaks normally. Your microcontroller's ADC expects a signal of at least **2 V**. The Op-Amp acts like a *mechanical lever of ratio 1000×* — it takes the tiny push (input) and produces a giant push (output), without changing the *meaning* (frequency content) of the signal. Just like a lever amplifies force, an Op-Amp amplifies voltage — but only when paired with a *feedback network* (resistors) that tells it "how much to amplify."

---

## 1.3 Filtering — Removing the "Static Noise"

### Formal Definition
A **Filter** is a *frequency-selective two-port network that passes signals within a defined passband with minimal attenuation while rejecting signals in the stopband.* In Mechatronics, filters are essential because sensor outputs are always corrupted by **50/60 Hz mains hum**, **electromagnetic interference (EMI)**, and **thermal noise (Johnson-Nyquist noise)**.

### Real-World Analogy
Think of a coffee filter: water (signal) passes through easily, but coffee grounds (noise) are blocked. An electronic filter does the same thing — but instead of blocking particles, it blocks specific *frequencies*. A **low-pass filter** is like a sieve that lets only "slow-moving" low frequencies through (useful for clean DC measurements from a temperature sensor), while a **high-pass filter** lets only "fast-moving" high frequencies through (useful for removing DC bias before AC signal analysis).

---

## 1.4 Multiplexers — The "Traffic Controller" of Signals

### Formal Definition
A **Multiplexer (MUX)** is a *combinational logic/analog switching device that selects one of $2^n$ input signals and forwards it to a single output line, controlled by an $n$-bit select line*. In mechatronic systems, an **analog MUX** (e.g., CD4051, ADG726) allows a single ADC to read multiple sensors, drastically reducing hardware cost and pin count.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** A MUX is like a *railway track switchyard*. Many trains (sensor signals) arrive at different platforms (input channels), but only one track (the single output line) leaves the yard. The signalman (digital select lines S0, S1, S2) decides which train enters the mainline.

---

## 1.5 Input/Output Systems: Ports — The Communication "Doorways"

### Formal Definition
In the context of embedded control systems, a **Port** is a *physical and logical interface on a microcontroller or computer that enables data exchange between the CPU and external peripheral devices.* Ports are categorized as **I/O ports** (GPIO), **communication ports** (UART, SPI, I²C), and **specialized ports** (USB, CAN, Ethernet).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Context:** A *port* in Module 2 of PCAUT521 refers specifically to the **interface** between the conditioned signal and the processing unit. The key distinction is:
> - **Parallel Port**: 8/16/32 lines transferred simultaneously (high speed, many pins).
> - **Serial Port**: Bits sent one after another over 1–2 lines (low pin count, longer distances).
> - **USB Port**: Modern universal serial bus supporting plug-and-play and power delivery.

### Real-World Analogy
Think of a port as a **doorway** of a building:
- A **parallel port** is like a **wide double-door** — many people can walk in side-by-side (fast but takes up wall space).
- A **serial port** is like a **single revolving door** — only one person at a time (slower, but takes far less space).
- A **USB port** is like a **modern automatic sliding door** — standardized, smart, supports both data and power.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ideal Op-Amp Assumption (Two Golden Rules)

When **negative feedback** is present, two constraints simplify all op-amp analysis:

| # | Golden Rule | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|-------------|--------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **No current into inputs** | $i^+ = i^- = 0$ | Infinite input impedance |
| 2 | **Virtual short** between inputs | $V^+ = V^-$ | Infinite open-loop gain forces differential input to zero |

---

## 2.2 The Five Essential Op-Amp Configurations (KTU High-Yield)

### 2.2.1 Inverting Amplifier

The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the inverting terminal ($V^-$) through resistor $R_1$. The non-inverting terminal is grounded. Feedback resistor $R_f$ connects output to the inverting input.

**Voltage Gain:**
$$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

**Input Impedance:**
$$Z_{in} = R_1$$

**Output Impedance (ideal):**
$$Z_{out} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **negative sign** indicates a **180° phase inversion** — this is the *defining* feature of the inverting amplifier and a frequently asked KTU question.

### 2.2.2 Non-Inverting Amplifier

The input signal $V_{in}$ is applied directly to the **non-inverting terminal ($V^+$)**. The inverting terminal is connected to a voltage divider ($R_1$, $R_f$) forming negative feedback.

**Voltage Gain:**
$$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

**Input Impedance:**
$$Z_{in} \to \infty$$

### 2.2.3 Summing Amplifier (Adder)

Multiple inputs ($V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_n$) are applied to the inverting node through separate input resistors $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$, with a common feedback resistor $R_f$.

**Output Voltage:**
$$V_{out} = -R_f \left( \frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \cdots + \frac{V_n}{R_n} \right)$$

**Special Case (Unity Gain Adder):** When $R_1 = R_2 = \cdots = R_n = R_f$, the output is the negated sum:
$$V_{out} = -(V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_n)$$

### 2.2.4 Difference Amplifier (Subtractor)

A single op-amp configuration that produces an output proportional to the *difference* between two inputs, eliminating common-mode noise — the **most important configuration for sensor signal conditioning** (e.g., reading a Wheatstone bridge output).

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_f}{R_1}\,(V_2 - V_1)$$

provided $R_f / R_1 = R_3 / R_4$ (the matching condition).

### 2.2.5 Integrator and Differentiator

**Ideal Integrator:**
$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{R_1 C_f}\int V_{in}(t)\,dt$$

**Ideal Differentiator:**
$$V_{out}(t) = -R_f C_1 \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pure differentiators are unstable** at high frequencies and amplify noise. In practice, a **"leaky" differentiator** (with a small resistor in series with $C_1$) is used. This is a common KTU 14-mark question.

---

## 2.3 Filter Theory — Frequency Response and Transfer Functions

### 2.3.1 Passive vs Active Filters

| Property | Passive Filter | Active Filter |
|----------|----------------|----------------|
| Components | R, L, C only | R, C + Op-Amp |
| Gain | Always $\leq 1$ (attenuation) | Can provide gain $> 1$ |
| Loading Effect | Output depends on load | Op-Amp buffers the load |
| Inductors | Often required (bulky) | Inductors replaced by RC networks |
| Power | No external supply needed | Requires $\pm V_{CC}$ supply |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 syllabus, **active filters (Sallen-Key topology)** are emphasized over passive filters because they avoid bulky inductors and provide gain.

### 2.3.2 First-Order Filter Transfer Functions

For a **first-order low-pass filter (LPF)**:
$$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

The **cutoff frequency (–3 dB frequency)** is:
$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

At $f = f_c$, output power drops to **half** (voltage drops to $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071$).

For a **first-order high-pass filter (HPF)**:
$$H(s) = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$$

### 2.3.3 Second-Order Sallen-Key Low-Pass Filter (KTU High-Yield)

The Sallen-Key topology uses a single op-amp with two RC sections to realize a second-order response with selectable Q-factor. The general transfer function is:

$$H(s) = \frac{K\,\omega_n^2}{s^2 + \frac{\omega_n}{Q}s + \omega_n^2}$$

where:
- $\omega_n$ = natural (undamped) frequency in rad/s
- $Q$ = quality factor (damping)
- $K$ = DC gain

**Butterworth response (maximally flat):** $Q = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071$

**Cutoff frequency:**
$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$

---

## 2.4 Multiplexer Fundamentals

For an **$n$-to-1 analog MUX** (e.g., 8:1 MUX has $n = 3$ select lines):

| Input Channels | Select Lines ($n$) | Boolean Equation |
|----------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| 2 : 1 | 1 | $Y = \bar{S}_0 I_0 + S_0 I_1$ |
| 4 : 1 | 2 | $Y = \bar{S}_1 \bar{S}_0 I_0 + \bar{S}_1 S_0 I_1 + S_1 \bar{S}_0 I_2 + S_1 S_0 I_3$ |
| 8 : 1 | 3 | $Y = \sum_{k=0}^{7} m_k \cdot I_k$ where $m_k$ is the $k^{th}$ minterm of $(S_2, S_1, S_0)$ |
| 16 : 1 | 4 | General $2^n : 1$ form |

**Key MUX Specifications:**
- **$R_{ON}$** (On-resistance): typically 50–500 Ω for CMOS MUX.
- **$t_{ON}$, $t_{OFF}$** (switching time): 50 ns to 1 µs.
- **Crosstalk** between channels: must be $< -90$ dB for instrumentation-grade MUXes.

---

## 2.5 I/O Ports — Serial vs Parallel Communication

### 2.5.1 Parallel Port (e.g., IEEE 1284, Centronics)

A parallel port transfers $N$ bits simultaneously across $N$ physical lines. The original **PC parallel port** had:
- 8 data lines (D0–D7)
- 4 control lines
- 5 status lines
- 8 ground lines

**Throughput (unidirectional):** up to **150 KB/s** (SPP) to **2 MB/s** (EPP/ECP).

### 2.5.2 Serial Port (RS-232, UART)

A serial port transmits bits sequentially using:
- **Baud rate:** bits per second (e.g., 9600, 115200 baud).
- **Frame format:** Start bit (1) + Data bits (5–9) + Parity (optional) + Stop bits (1–2).

**RS-232 voltage levels:** Logic 1 = –3 V to –25 V (MARK), Logic 0 = +3 V to +25 V (SPACE). These are *inverted* from TTL levels (0–5 V).

### 2.5.3 USB (Universal Serial Bus)

USB uses **differential signaling** on a twisted pair (D+ and D–) with **NRZI encoding** and bit stuffing.

| USB Version | Max Speed | Max Current |
|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| USB 1.1 (Low/Full) | 1.5 / 12 Mbps | 500 mA |
| USB 2.0 (High) | 480 Mbps | 500 mA |
| USB 3.0 (SuperSpeed) | 5 Gbps | 900 mA |
| USB 3.1 (SuperSpeed+) | 10 Gbps | 900 mA–5 A (PD) |
| USB4 | 40 Gbps | Up to 240 W (PD) |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Inverting Gain | $A_v = -R_f \mid R_1$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Non-Inverting Gain | $A_v = 1 + R_f \mid R_1$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Summing Amplifier | $V_{out} = -R_f \sum V_k \mid R_k$ | Volts |
| 4 | Difference Amplifier | $V_{out} = (R_f \mid R_1)(V_2 - V_1)$ | Matched resistors |
| 5 | Integrator Output | $V_{out}(t) = -(1 \mid R_1 C_f) \int V_{in} dt$ | Volts |
| 6 | Differentiator Output | $V_{out}(t) = -R_f C_1\, dV_{in} \mid dt$ | Volts |
| 7 | LPF Cutoff | $f_c = 1 \mid (2\pi R C)$ | Hertz |
| 8 | HPF Cutoff | $f_c = 1 \mid (2\pi R C)$ | Hertz |
| 9 | Sallen-Key $f_c$ | $f_c = 1 \mid (2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2})$ | Hertz |
| 10 | Butterworth Q | $Q = 1 \mid \sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Slew Rate Limit | $SR \geq 2\pi f_{max} V_{peak}$ | V/µs |
| 12 | CMRR | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}(A_d \mid A_{cm})$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 13 | Multiplexer Channels | $N = 2^n$ | $n$ = select lines |
| 14 | Serial Throughput | $\eta = (\text{baud rate} \times \text{data bits}) \mid (\text{frame bits})$ | bps |
| 15 | RS-232 Logic 1 | $-3$ V to $-25$ V | MARK |
| 16 | RS-232 Logic 0 | $+3$ V to $+25$ V | SPACE |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** In a **mechatronic temperature control loop** for a plastic injection molding machine:
> - A **thermocouple** produces ~40 µV/°C → a **difference amplifier** (e.g., AD620) with $A_v = 100$ amplifies it to ~4 mV/°C.
> - A **Sallen-Key LPF** with $f_c = 10$ Hz removes 50 Hz mains hum.
> - An **8:1 analog MUX** routes signals from 8 thermocouples to one ADC.
> - The **microcontroller's serial port** (UART) transmits the conditioned data to a PC at 115200 baud for logging.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Inverting Amplifier Gain (Detailed)

**Goal:** Prove $V_{out} = -\left(\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{in}$.

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the inverting node ($V^-$).**

Since the non-inverting terminal is grounded: $V^+ = 0$.

By the **virtual short** rule (Golden Rule #2):
$$V^- = V^+ = 0$$

This is called a **virtual ground** — the node is at 0 V but is *not* physically connected to ground.

**Step 2 — Write the currents flowing into the inverting node.**

The current through $R_1$ (from $V_{in}$ to $V^-$):
$$i_1 = \frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

The current through $R_f$ (from $V^-$ to $V_{out}$):
$$i_f = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Golden Rule #1 (no current into op-amp input).**

By KCL, the sum of currents entering the inverting node must be zero:
$$i_1 + i_f = 0$$

Substituting:
$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} - \frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{out}$.**

$$\frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}\,V_{in}}$$

The **negative sign** emerges because the current through $R_1$ *must equal* the current through $R_f$, forcing $V_{out}$ to have the opposite polarity of $V_{in}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Non-Inverting Amplifier Gain

**Step 1 — Apply virtual short.**

The non-inverting terminal is at $V_{in}$, so:
$$V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at $V^-$.**

The current through $R_1$ (from $V^-$ to ground):
$$i_1 = \frac{V^- - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}$$

The current through $R_f$ (from $V_{out}$ to $V^-$):
$$i_f = \frac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_f} = \frac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_f}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Golden Rule #1.**

Since no current enters the op-amp, $i_1 = i_f$:
$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = \frac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the gain.**

Cross-multiplying:
$$V_{in} \cdot R_f = R_1 (V_{out} - V_{in})$$

$$V_{in}\,R_f = R_1\,V_{out} - R_1\,V_{in}$$

$$V_{in}\,R_f + V_{in}\,R_1 = R_1\,V_{out}$$

$$V_{in}(R_1 + R_f) = R_1\,V_{out}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Summing Amplifier Output

**Step 1 — Virtual ground at inverting node.**

With $V^+ = 0$ grounded, $V^- = 0$.

**Step 2 — KCL at $V^-$ with $n$ inputs.**

The sum of currents from each input must equal the current through $R_f$:
$$\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \cdots + \frac{V_n}{R_n} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

(The negative sign because $V_{out}$ is *negative* of the sum for negative inputs.)

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_{out}$.**

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -R_f \left(\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \cdots + \frac{V_n}{R_n}\right)}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Difference Amplifier (Subtractor)

**Step 1 — Find $V^+$ using voltage divider.**

Since no current flows into the non-inverting input, $R_3$ and $R_4$ form a simple divider:
$$V^+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

**Step 2 — Express $V^-$ in terms of $V_{out}$ and $V_1$.**

Apply KCL at the inverting node:
$$\frac{V_1 - V^-}{R_1} = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

Solving for $V^-$:
$$V^- = \frac{R_f V_1 + R_1 V_{out}}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 3 — Apply virtual short $V^+ = V^-$.**

$$\frac{R_f V_1 + R_1 V_{out}}{R_1 + R_f} = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{out}$.**

After algebraic rearrangement (assuming the matched condition $R_f \mid R_1 = R_3 \mid R_4$):

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{R_f}{R_1}\,(V_2 - V_1)}$$

This is the most important op-amp configuration for **sensor signal conditioning**, especially for **Wheatstone bridge** outputs from strain gauges and load cells.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: First-Order Low-Pass Filter Cutoff Frequency

**Step 1 — Voltage divider in the frequency domain.**

The LPF consists of $R$ in series and $C$ to ground. Using the impedance of the capacitor $Z_C = 1 \mid (sC)$:

$$H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{Z_C}{R + Z_C} = \frac{1 \mid (sC)}{R + 1 \mid (sC)}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $sC$:**

$$H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude response.**

Substitute $s = j\omega = j2\pi f$:
$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Define cutoff frequency.**

At $f = f_c$, the magnitude drops to $1 \mid \sqrt{2}$ of its DC value (i.e., –3 dB point). Setting $\omega_c RC = 1$:

$$\boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A thermistor produces $V_T = 25$ mV at 25 °C. Design an inverting amplifier to produce $V_{out} = -2.5$ V. If $R_1 = 10$ kΩ, find $R_f$. What is the input impedance?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the required gain.**
$$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{-2.5\text{ V}}{25\text{ mV}} = \frac{-2.5}{0.025} = -100$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $R_f$ using the inverting gain formula.**
$$A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \implies -100 = -\frac{R_f}{10\,\text{k}\Omega}$$

$$\boxed{R_f = 100 \times 10\,\text{k}\Omega = 1\,\text{M}\Omega}$$

**Step 3 — Input impedance.**

For an inverting amplifier, $Z_{in} = R_1$:
$$\boxed{Z_{in} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A 1 MΩ feedback resistor provides a 100× gain. However, because this is the *inverting* configuration, the input impedance is only 10 kΩ — which is acceptable for a low-impedance thermistor bridge but problematic for high-impedance sensors like pH electrodes. For such sensors, a **non-inverting or instrumentation amplifier** must be used instead.

---

## 3.7 Worked Example: Active LPF Design (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** Design a first-order active low-pass filter (LPF) with $f_c = 1$ kHz using $C = 10$ nF. Calculate $R$ and verify attenuation at 5 kHz.

**Step 1 — Solve for $R$.**

Using $f_c = 1 \mid (2\pi R C)$:
$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{10^5}{2\pi} \approx 15{,}915.5\,\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Attenuation at 5 kHz.**

The magnitude response:
$$\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f \mid f_c)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (5 \mid 1)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{26}}$$

$$\vert H \vert \approx \frac{1}{5.099} \approx 0.196 \quad \text{or}\quad -14.15\,\text{dB}$$

This confirms that at $5 f_c$, the signal is attenuated by ~14 dB — consistent with the **–20 dB/decade roll-off** of a first-order filter.
