\frac{d_{CD}}{d_{DVD}} &= \frac{2.11}{1.32} \approx 1.60
\end{aligned}
$$

The DVD spot is roughly **1.6× smaller** in diameter, meaning the **bit area** is $(1.60)^2 \approx 2.56$× smaller. Combined with the track-pitch reduction factor $(1.6/0.74) \approx 2.16$, this yields the empirical ~4.5× capacity increase.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — DVD Capacity (Single Layer, Single Sided)

The disc data area is an annulus between inner radius $r_{in} = 25$ mm and outer radius $r_{out} = 60$ mm.

$$
A_{disc} = \pi \cdot (r_{out}^2 - r_{in}^2)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{disc} &= \pi \times \big((60\,\text{mm})^2 - (25\,\text{mm})^2\big) \\
         &= \pi \times (3600 - 625)\,\text{mm}^2 \\
         &= \pi \times 2975\,\text{mm}^2 \\
         &= 9346.2\,\text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

The **bit cell area** is the product of the track pitch and the minimum pit length (a conservative lower bound):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{bit} &= p_t \times L_{min} \\
        &= 0.74\,\mu\text{m} \times 0.40\,\mu\text{m} \\
        &= 0.296\,\mu\text{m}^2 \\
        &= 0.296 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Theoretical track-length bit count:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{bits,\,theoretical} &= \frac{A_{disc}}{A_{bit}} = \frac{9346.2}{0.296 \times 10^{-6}} \\
                        &= 3.1575 \times 10^{10}\,\text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

After applying the **EFMPlus modulation efficiency** ($\eta \approx 0.844$ — actually slightly higher) and ECC overhead (Reed-Solomon product code), the **user data** is approximately:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{user} &= N_{bits,\,theoretical} \times 0.667 \\
         &= 3.1575 \times 10^{10} \times 0.667 \\
         &= 2.106 \times 10^{10}\,\text{bytes} \\
         &\approx 19.6\,\text{GB}\,\text{(raw)} \;\;\rightarrow\;\; 4.7\,\text{GB}\,\text{(user)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The 4.7 GB user-capacity figure (4.377 GiB) accounts for the **ECC / EFMPlus / sector header overheads** and is the value quoted in the KTU/ECMA standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU students must quote **4.7 GB** (decimal gigabytes = $4.7 \times 10^9$ bytes) and not confuse it with 4.7 GiB. The 4.7 GB SL/SS value is the **standard KTU answer**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Data Transfer Rate at 1× Speed

The disc rotates at **Constant Linear Velocity (CLV)** $v = 3.49$ m/s. The **linear bit density** along the track is fixed:

$$
\rho = 3.174 \times 10^6 \,\text{bits per meter}
$$

Therefore the **1× base data rate** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{1\times} &= v \times \rho \\
            &= 3.49 \,\text{m/s} \times 3.174 \times 10^6 \,\text{bit/m} \\
            &= 1.1077 \times 10^7 \,\text{bit/s} \\
            &\approx 11.08 \,\text{Mbit/s} \\
            &= \frac{11.08}{8} \,\text{MB/s} \approx 1.385 \,\text{MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

For an **8× DVD-ROM drive**, the linear velocity (or equivalent data rate) becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{8\times} &= 8 \times 11.08 \,\text{Mbit/s} \\
            &= 88.64 \,\text{Mbit/s} \\
            &\approx 11.08 \,\text{MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU often asks: *“A 16× DVD drive reads how many MB/s?”* — Multiply 1.385 by 16 → **22.16 MB/s**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Rotational Speed at Inner vs Outer Track

Because DVD uses CLV, the angular velocity changes with radius. For inner track ($r_{in} = 25$ mm):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{in} &= \frac{v}{r_{in}} = \frac{3.49}{0.025} = 139.6 \,\text{rad/s} \\
            &= 139.6 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} \,\text{RPM} \approx 1333 \,\text{RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

For outer track ($r_{out} = 60$ mm):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{out} &= \frac{v}{r_{out}} = \frac{3.49}{0.060} = 58.17 \,\text{rad/s} \\
             &= 58.17 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} \,\text{RPM} \approx 555 \,\text{RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

At higher than 4× speeds, drives switch to **Constant Angular Velocity (CAV)** or **Zoned CLV (ZCLV)** to keep the motor within safe RPM limits.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DVD-ROM vs CD-ROM

| Parameter | CD-ROM | DVD-ROM | Reason for DVD improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Laser $\lambda$ | 780 nm | 650 nm | Shorter $\lambda$ → smaller spot |
| NA | 0.45 | 0.60 | Tighter focus |
| Spot diameter | 2.11 $\mu$m | 1.32 $\mu$m | See Derivation 1 |
| Track pitch | 1.6 $\mu$m | 0.74 $\mu$m | Higher NA + thinner substrate |
| Min pit length | 0.83 $\mu$m | 0.40 $\mu$m | Smaller spot |
| Substrate thickness | 1.2 mm | 0.6 mm × 2 | Reduced spherical aberration |
| Capacity (SL) | 700 MB | 4.7 GB | ~6.7× more |
| Modulation | EFM (8→17) | EFMPlus (8→16) | Higher code efficiency |
| 1× data rate | 1.2288 Mbit/s | 11.08 Mbit/s | Higher density + faster linear velocity |
| Error correction | CIRC | RS-PC (Reed-Solomon product code) | Stronger ECC |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a DVD-ROM Drive (Mechatronic Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **mechatronic functional architecture** of a DVD-ROM drive — showing how mechanical, optical, electronic, and control subsystems interact. This is the standard block-level view expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[DC Power Supply 5V/12V] --> B[Drive Controller MCU]
    A --> C[Spindle Motor Driver]
    A --> D[Sled Motor Driver]
    A --> E[Laser Diode Driver]
    B --> C
    B --> D
    B --> E
    C --> F[Brushless Spindle Motor]
    D --> G[Stepper or Linear Sled Motor]
    E --> H[650 nm Laser Diode]
    F --> I[DVD Disc CLV Rotation]
    G --> J[Optical Pickup Head]
    H --> J
    J --> K[Beam Splitter and Quarter Wave Plate]
    K --> L[Photodiode Array 4 Quadrant]
    L --> M[RF Amplifier and Equalizer]
    M --> N[EFMPlus Decoder and CIRC ECC]
    N --> O[ATA or SATA Interface]
    O --> P[Host System CPU]
    B --> Q[Focus Servo PID]
    B --> R[Tracking Servo PID]
    L --> Q
    L --> R
    Q --> S[Voice Coil Actuator]
    R --> S
    S --> J
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Drive Controller MCU** is the brain coordinating all subsystems (Mechatronics “C” for Control).
- The **Spindle Motor** is the “M” (Mechanical) for rotation.
- The **Laser + Optics + Photodiode** is the “S” (Sensors/Optical).
- The **Focus & Tracking Servo loops** (PID controllers) close the loop on lens positioning.

---

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Layered Structure of a DVD-ROM Disc

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Top Label Print Layer] --> L2[Polycarbonate Substrate 0.6 mm]
    L2 --> L3[Adhesive Bonding Layer]
    L3 --> L4[Inner Polycarbonate Substrate 0.6 mm]
    L4 --> L5[Reflective Aluminum Layer with Pits and Lands]
    L5 --> L6[Bottom Clear Polycarbonate 0.6 mm]
    L6 --> L7[Incoming 650 nm Laser Beam from Pickup]
```

For a **dual-layer DVD**, two reflective data layers are stacked: a **semi-reflective layer (gold/silicon)** at ~0.6 mm depth and a **fully reflective layer (aluminum)** at 1.2 mm depth. The laser refocuses by adjusting the objective lens to read either layer.

---

### 4.3 Focus & Tracking Servo Control Loop (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Track Position] --> B[Subtract]
    C[Photodiode Array] --> D[Error Signal Generator]
    D --> E[Tracking Error TE]
    D --> F[Focus Error FE]
    E --> G[PID Controller Tracking]
    F --> H[PID Controller Focus]
    G --> I[Voice Coil Actuator Driver]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Objective Lens 2 Axis Movement]
    J --> C
    B --> C
```

This closed-loop topology — with **photodiode → error signal → PID → actuator → lens** — is the textbook mechatronics feedback loop that KTU examiners expect you to label in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define DVD-ROM. List any three differences between a CD-ROM and a DVD-ROM.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** DVD-ROM (Digital Versatile Disc – Read-Only Memory) is a non-volatile, factory-pressed optical storage medium read by a 650 nm red laser, offering a standard capacity of **4.7 GB** on a single-layer single-sided 120 mm disc.

**Three Differences:**

| Parameter | CD-ROM | DVD-ROM |
|---|---|---|
| Laser wavelength | 780 nm (infrared) | 650 nm (red) |
| Numerical aperture | 0.45 | 0.60 |
| Capacity (SL/SS) | 700 MB | 4.7 GB |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is the role of the photodiode array in a DVD-ROM drive?

**Model Answer:**
The photodiode array (typically a 4-quadrant photodetector) in a DVD-ROM drive performs three critical functions:
1. **RF Data Detection** — converts the modulated reflected laser light into an electrical RF signal that contains the EFMPlus-modulated pit/land data.
2. **Focus Error (FE) Generation** — by exploiting the astigmatic property of an auxiliary lens, the 4-quadrant detector generates a focus error signal used by the PID servo to keep the laser spot in focus on the data layer.
3. **Tracking Error (TE) Generation** — via the differential push-pull (DPP) method, the detector generates a radial tracking error used to keep the laser on the spiral track.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a DVD-ROM drive as a mechatronic system. Identify the mechanical, electronic, and control subsystems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the storage capacity of a single-layer single-sided DVD-ROM disc given that the inner radius is 25 mm, outer radius is 60 mm, track pitch is 0.74 $\mu$m, and minimum pit length is 0.40 $\mu$m. State the user-data capacity in GB. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Mechatronic Subsystems:**

Refer to the **block diagram in Section 4.1** above. The DVD-ROM drive is partitioned as follows:

- **Mechanical Subsystem (M):** Brushless DC spindle motor (rotates disc), sled/stepper motor (radial positioning of pickup), voice-coil actuator (fine focus & tracking movement of objective lens).
- **Electronic/Electrical Subsystem (E):** 650 nm laser diode + driver, photodiode array + RF amplifier, motor driver ICs, power supply (5 V/12 V).
- **Control Subsystem (C):** Drive controller MCU/DSP, PID-based focus servo loop, PID-based tracking servo loop, EFMPlus decoder + Reed-Solomon ECC block.
- **Optical Subsystem:** Diffraction grating, polarizing beam splitter, quarter-wave plate, objective lens (NA = 0.60).

Working summary: The controller commands the spindle motor to spin the disc at CLV, positions the sled to the correct track, focuses the laser via the voice-coil-actuated lens, and reads reflections through the photodiode — closing two feedback loops (focus & tracking) for accurate data recovery.

**Part (b) — Capacity Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{disc} &= \pi \times (r_{out}^2 - r_{in}^2) \\
         &= \pi \times (60^2 - 25^2)\,\text{mm}^2 \\
         &= \pi \times (3600 - 625)\,\text{mm}^2 \\
         &= \pi \times 2975 \,\text{mm}^2 \\
         &= 9346.2\,\text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{bit} &= p_t \times L_{min} \\
        &= 0.74\,\mu\text{m} \times 0.40\,\mu\text{m} \\
        &= 0.296 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{bits,\,raw} &= \frac{A_{disc}}{A_{bit}} = \frac{9346.2}{0.296 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 3.157 \times 10^{10}\,\text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying EFMPlus + Reed-Solomon ECC efficiency factor $\eta \approx 0.667$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{user} &= 3.157 \times 10^{10} \times 0.667 \\
         &= 2.106 \times 10^{10}\,\text{bytes} \\
         &\approx 4.7\,\text{GB (user data)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The standard user capacity of a single-layer single-sided DVD-ROM is **4.7 GB**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Compare the optical pickup systems of CD-ROM and DVD-ROM. Why can DVD-ROM store ~6.7× more data than a CD-ROM? Show the relevant spot-diameter calculations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 16× DVD-ROM drive is being used in a mechatronic system. Calculate: (i) the linear velocity of the disc, (ii) the data transfer rate in MB/s, and (iii) the rotational speed (in RPM) when reading the innermost track ($r_{in} = 25$ mm). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Comparison & Capacity Justification:**

The optical pickup differences stem from the **laser wavelength** and **numerical aperture**:

| Parameter | CD-ROM | DVD-ROM |
|---|---|---|
| $\lambda$ | 780 nm | 650 nm |
| NA | 0.45 | 0.60 |
| Spot diameter | $\dfrac{1.22 \times 780\,\text{nm}}{0.45} \approx 2.11\,\mu\text{m}$ | $\dfrac{1.22 \times 650\,\text{nm}}{0.60} \approx 1.32\,\mu\text{m}$ |

The **spot-area ratio** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{A_{spot,\,CD}}{A_{spot,\,DVD}} &= \left(\frac{d_{CD}}{d_{DVD}}\right)^2 = (1.60)^2 \approx 2.56
\end{aligned}
$$

This means each DVD pit occupies $\sim 1/2.56$ of the CD pit area. The **track pitch** also shrinks from 1.6 $\mu$m (CD) to 0.74 $\mu$m (DVD), adding another factor of $1.6/0.74 \approx 2.16$.

$$
\text{Capacity gain} \approx 2.56 \times 2.16 \times \text{(ECC efficiency)} \approx 6.7
$$

Hence the 4.7 GB vs 700 MB ratio (~6.7×) is the result of **shorter wavelength + higher NA + thinner substrate + better ECC**.

**Part (b) — 16× DVD-ROM Calculations:**

(i) **Linear velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{16\times} &= 16 \times 3.49\,\text{m/s} = 55.84\,\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

(ii) **Data transfer rate in MB/s:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{16\times} &= 16 \times 1.385\,\text{MB/s} = 22.16\,\text{MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

(iii) **Rotational speed at inner track:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{in} &= \frac{v_{16\times}}{r_{in}} = \frac{55.84}{0.025} = 2233.6\,\text{rad/s} \\
\text{RPM}_{in} &= 2233.6 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} \approx 21{,}330\,\text{RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Practical comment:** At 16× linear-velocity scaling, CLV would demand > 21,000 RPM at the inner track — mechanically infeasible. Hence high-speed DVD drives switch to **Zoned CLV (ZCLV)** or **CAV**, so the actual angular velocity is lower than the simple CLV calculation suggests.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit confusion trap:** Students frequently write 4.7 GB when the question asks in bytes (it is $4.7 \times 10^9$ bytes = 4.377 GiB). Lose 1 mark if units are wrong.
> 2. **Wavelength trap:** Always quote **650 nm** for DVD and **780 nm** for CD. Writing 635 nm (a rare HD-DVD variant) is a common error.
> 3. **CLV assumption:** For 1×–4× DVD drives, CLV is valid. For >4× drives, mention ZCLV/CAV — full marks require this nuance.
> 4. **Numerical aperture:** DVD uses **0.60**, NOT 0.45 (that is CD). Confusing the two is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Block diagram:** KTU expects 4 clearly labeled subsystems (Mechanical, Electronic, Control, Optical) plus the host interface. Drawing a "black box" loses 2–3 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Full form:** Digital Versatile Disc – Read-Only Memory.
- **Standard capacity:** **4.7 GB** (SL/SS), **8.5 GB** (DL/SS), **9.4 GB** (SL/DS), **17.08 GB** (DL/DS).
- **Disc dimensions:** 120 mm diameter, 1.2 mm thick (two 0.6 mm halves).
- **Laser:** **650 nm red**, NA **0.60**.
- **Track pitch:** **0.74 $\mu$m**.
- **Min pit length:** **0.40 $\mu$m**.
- **Spot diameter formula (must memorize):** $d = \dfrac{1.22\lambda}{NA}$.
- **DVD spot size:** ~1.32 $\mu$m. **CD spot size:** ~2.11 $\mu$m.
- **Reading principle:** Pit scatters light → low intensity; land reflects light → high intensity. Transition = binary 1.
- **Rotation:** **CLV at $v = 3.49$ m/s** for 1×; switches to **ZCLV/CAV** at higher speeds.
- **1× data rate:** **11.08 Mbit/s = 1.385 MB/s** (8× faster than CD's 1× = 1.2288 Mbit/s).
- **ECC:** Reed-Solomon product code (RS-PC); **modulation:** EFMPlus (8 → 16).
- **Photodiode:** 4-quadrant detector generates both focus error (astigmatism method) and tracking error (differential push-pull).
- **Mechatronic role:** DVD-ROM is the **passive data-source** in a closed-loop mechatronic system (servo-controlled laser pickup).
- **Capacity formula cheat:** Total capacity = 4.7 × 2^(layers − 1) × 2^(sides − 1) GB.
- **Key KTU buzzwords:** Pit, land, EFMPlus, NA, CLV, REED-Solomon, four-quadrant photodiode, voice-coil actuator, diffraction grating, polarizing beam splitter.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute exam tip:** In any 14-mark question on DVD-ROM, always draw the **mechatronic block diagram** first (1 mark reserve), then state **3 key parameters** (wavelength, NA, capacity) in bullet form, and finally show **one numerical derivation** (spot size or data rate). This structure guarantees 12+ marks even with minor content slips.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Printers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT521 - MECHATRONICS AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Printers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Mechatronics and Control Systems**, a **Printer** is classified as a **computer peripheral output device** (often an *electro-mechanical actuator subsystem*) that receives digital data from a host controller (microcontroller, PC, or PLC) and produces a permanent, tangible (hard-copy) representation of that data on a physical medium such as paper, plastic, or even a three-dimensional substrate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** A printer is an electromechanical output actuator that converts electrical control signals into physical marks on a medium, integrating mechanical motion (paper feed, print head carriage), electronic control (driver circuits, microcontroller interfacing), and computer software (firmware/driver protocols).

From a **mechatronics engineering perspective**, a printer is a *textbook example* of a **closed-loop or open-loop multi-axis motion control system** because it must precisely synchronize:
1. The lateral motion of the print head (often driven by a **stepper motor** or **DC servo motor** with encoder feedback).
2. The linear paper-feed motion (also typically stepper-driven).
3. The thermal/piezoelectric/laser actuation that physically deposits or fuses the image.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Automated Signature Machine"**
> Imagine a robotic arm holding a pen that automatically signs thousands of letters. A printer works exactly like this, but instead of one pen, it uses hundreds of microscopic "stamps" (nozzles or laser pulses) and instead of writing one letter at a time, it stamps entire pages at high speed while two motorized axes (X-axis carriage and Y-axis paper feed) move in perfect synchrony.

A printer can be thought of as a **"pixel-by-pixel manufacturing machine"** — whether it is depositing ink droplets, fusing toner powder, or extruding molten plastic in a 3D printer, the underlying mechatronic principle remains the same: **convert digital bits → coordinated mechanical motion → physical output**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Note |
|---|---|
| **Resolution** | Measured in **Dots Per Inch (DPI)** — typical range **300 DPI** (draft) to **1200+ DPI** (photo). |
| **Print Speed** | Measured in **Pages Per Minute (PPM)** or **Characters Per Second (CPS)**. |
| **Color Depth** | Measured in **bits per pixel (bpp)** — standard 24-bit (16.7 million colors). |
| **Paper Feed Accuracy** | Typically **±0.05 mm** to **±0.01 mm** tolerance, achieved by stepper motor micro-stepping. |
| **Interface Standards** | **USB**, **Parallel (IEEE 1284)**, **Ethernet**, **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to differentiate between **Impact** and **Non-Impact** printing technologies and to identify the **actuator type** (electromagnetic, thermal, piezoelectric, laser) used in each.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Printers

Printers are broadly classified based on the **mechanism of image transfer** to the medium:

### 2.1.1 Impact Printers
Impact printers work by physically striking an inked ribbon against the paper — analogous to a **typewriter mechanism** in automation.

**A. Dot Matrix Printer (DMP)**
- Uses a **print head containing 9, 18, or 24 pins** (solenoid-actuated needles).
- Each pin is driven by a small **solenoid coil**. When energized, the pin fires forward, strikes the inked ribbon, and leaves a dot on the paper.
- Characters are formed as **dot matrix patterns** (e.g., 5×7 or 7×9 dot arrays).
- The print head moves horizontally on a carriage driven by a **stepper motor** via a **toothed belt** or **lead screw**.
- Paper is advanced vertically by a **tractor feed** mechanism (sprocket wheels engaging perforated paper edges).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use solenoids?** Solenoids provide the fast, high-force linear actuation needed to strike a ribbon ~200 times per second per pin.

**B. Daisy Wheel / Line Printer (Historical)**
- Uses a spinning wheel with embossed characters. Selected character strikes the ribbon. Now obsolete.

### 2.1.2 Non-Impact Printers
These do **not** physically strike the paper — they use heat, electricity, light, or pressure to form images.

**A. Inkjet Printer**
- **Thermal Inkjet (Bubble Jet):** Tiny resistors (≈ 100 µm) rapidly heat ink to ~300 °C, forming a vapor bubble that ejects a droplet (~3–10 picolitres) through a nozzle. Used by **HP, Canon**.
- **Piezoelectric Inkjet:** A piezo crystal (PZT — lead zirconate titanate) deforms when a voltage is applied, creating a pressure pulse that ejects the droplet. Used by **Epson**.
- Resolution: typically **300–9600 DPI** with multi-pass and dithering.

**B. Laser Printer**
- Operates on the **xerography principle** (Greek *xeros* = dry, *graphein* = to write).
- Steps:
  1. **Charging:** A **corona wire** or **charge roller** gives the **Organic Photoconductor (OPC) drum** a uniform negative charge (~ −600 V).
  2. **Exposure:** A **laser diode** (or LED array) scans the drum, selectively discharging spots to form a **latent electrostatic image** (~ −100 V at exposed areas).
  3. **Developing:** **Toner** (fine plastic powder, ~5–15 µm) carrying opposite charge is attracted to discharged areas.
  4. **Transfer:** Paper is given a stronger opposite charge; toner jumps to paper.
  5. **Fusing:** Paper passes through **fuser rollers** (heated to ~180 °C and pressurized) that melt and bond toner permanently.
  6. **Cleaning:** Residual toner is scraped off the drum by a **wiper blade**.

**C. Thermal Printer**
- Uses **thermochromic paper** that darkens when heated.
- Print head contains a linear array of heating elements (~200–300 DPI).
- Used in receipts (POS), labels, ECG machines.

**D. 3D Printer (Additive Manufacturing)**
- A true **mechatronic system**: combines **CNC motion control**, **thermal control**, and **material extrusion**.
- Types: **FDM** (Fused Deposition Modeling), **SLA** (Stereolithography), **SLS** (Selective Laser Sintering).
- For FDM: A **stepper motor** drives a **lead screw** or **Belt** to position the extruder; the **hot end** melts **PLA/ABS filament** at ~200–250 °C; layers of **0.1–0.3 mm** thickness are deposited.

---

## 2.2 Mechatronic Subsystem Breakdown

A modern printer contains the following mechatronic subsystems:

| Subsystem | Components | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Controller** | Microcontroller / ASIC / SoC | Interprets print data (PCL, PostScript, G-code) |
| **Driver Electronics** | H-bridge, MOSFET/IGBT drivers, stepper drivers (e.g., A4988, TMC2209) | Power amplification for motors |
| **Mechanical Actuators** | Stepper motors, DC motors, solenoids, piezo actuators | Produce motion |
| **Sensors** | Optical encoders, paper sensors, temperature sensors, thermistors (in hot end / fuser) | Feedback for closed-loop control |
| **Power Supply** | SMPS, DC-DC converters | Provide regulated voltages (3.3V, 5V, 12V, 24V, high-voltage AC for fuser/corona) |
| **User Interface** | LCD/LED panel, buttons, buzzers | HMI |
| **Firmware** | Embedded C / Assembly | Control algorithms |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_{\text{step}} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N}$ | Step angle of a stepper motor (N = total steps per revolution) | Degrees |
| 2 | $\theta_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{\theta_{\text{step}}}{\mu}$ | Microstep resolution where μ = microstepping factor (1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32) | Degrees |
| 3 | $L_{\text{feed}} = \theta_{\text{res}} \times \dfrac{P_{\text{lead}}}{360^\circ}$ | Linear paper feed per step (P_lead = lead screw pitch) | mm/step |
| 4 | $v_{\text{print}} = f_{\text{step}} \times L_{\text{feed}}$ | Paper feed velocity | mm/s |
| 5 | $D_{\text{drop}} \propto \sqrt[3]{E_{\text{thermal}}}$ | Thermal inkjet droplet volume scales with cube root of thermal energy | pL (picolitres) |
| 6 | $f_{\text{dot}} = \dfrac{v_{\text{head}} \times R_{\text{DPI}}}{25.4}$ | Required dot firing frequency for given print speed & DPI | Hz |
| 7 | $R_{\text{DPI}} = \dfrac{1}{p_{\text{dot}}}$ | DPI in terms of dot pitch | dots/inch |
| 8 | $V_{\text{3D-layer}} = L \times W \times h$ | Volume of a single 3D printed layer (L=length, W=width, h=layer height) | mm³ |
| 9 | $t_{\text{print}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{3D-layer}} \times N_{\text{layers}}}{Q_{\text{ext}}}$ | Total 3D print time (Q_ext = extrusion flow rate) | seconds |
| 10 | $P_{\text{fuser}} = m_{\text{toner}} \times c_p \times \Delta T \,/\, t_{\text{fuse}}$ | Power required by laser printer fuser | Watts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on table syntax:** All absolute-value and division bars use `\vert`, `\mid`, or `\dfrac` to avoid breaking markdown table formatting.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Printing:** PCB printing, textile printing, food decoration (uses piezoelectric inkjet).
- **Embedded Systems:** Receipt printers in ATMs, POS systems, logistics label printers (thermal).
- **Prototyping:** 3D printers (FDM/SLA) for rapid prototyping in automotive, aerospace, biomedical industries.
- **Medical:** 3D bioprinting of tissues, organ scaffolds; thermal printers in ECG, ultrasound.
- **Automation:** Pick-and-place machines with solder paste printers (stencil printers) use the same xerographic/precision motion principles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stepper Motor Step Angle for Carriage Resolution

**Problem Statement:** A dot matrix printer uses a stepper motor coupled to the print head carriage via a **timing belt with a 20-tooth pulley driving a 60-tooth pulley**, and a **lead screw of pitch 4 mm** for paper feed. The stepper motor has **200 full steps per revolution**. Find the **linear paper feed per step** in mm.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Step angle of the stepper motor (full step mode).**
The stepper motor has 200 full steps per revolution.

$$
\theta_{\text{step}} \;=\; \frac{360^\circ}{N} \;=\; \frac{360^\circ}{200} \;=\; 1.8^\circ \text{ per full step}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to radians (for clarity in subsequent derivations).**

$$
\theta_{\text{step, rad}} \;=\; 1.8^\circ \times \frac{\pi}{180^\circ} \;=\; 0.0314159 \text{ rad} \approx \frac{\pi}{100}
$$

**Step 3 — Linear displacement per step on the lead screw.**
The lead screw has a pitch $P_{\text{lead}} = 4 \text{ mm}$, meaning one full revolution of the lead screw advances the nut by 4 mm. Therefore, the linear displacement per step is:

$$
L_{\text{feed}} \;=\; \frac{\theta_{\text{step}}}{360^\circ} \times P_{\text{lead}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values.**

$$
L_{\text{feed}} \;=\; \frac{1.8^\circ}{360^\circ} \times 4 \text{ mm} \;=\; 0.005 \times 4 \text{ mm} \;=\; 0.02 \text{ mm per step}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply microstepping.**
If the driver uses **1/8 microstepping** (μ = 8), the effective linear feed per microstep is:

$$
L_{\text{micro}} \;=\; \frac{L_{\text{feed}}}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{0.02 \text{ mm}}{8} \;=\; 0.0025 \text{ mm} \;=\; 2.5 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Such sub-micron resolution per microstep enables the **600 DPI** class resolution observed in modern receipt and label thermal printers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Dot Firing Frequency for a Thermal Printer

**Problem Statement:** A thermal line printer has a print head with **R = 203 DPI** (standard for receipt printers) and a print speed of **v = 100 mm/s**. Find the required dot-firing frequency $f_{\text{dot}}$ for each heating element.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Relate DPI to dot pitch.**

$$
p_{\text{dot}} \;=\; \frac{1 \text{ inch}}{R} \;=\; \frac{25.4 \text{ mm}}{203} \;\approx\; 0.1251 \text{ mm/dot}
$$

**Step 2 — Time available between successive dots at print speed v.**

$$
t_{\text{dot}} \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{dot}}}{v} \;=\; \frac{0.1251 \text{ mm}}{100 \text{ mm/s}} \;=\; 0.001251 \text{ s} \;=\; 1.251 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute dot firing frequency.**

$$
f_{\text{dot}} \;=\; \frac{1}{t_{\text{dot}}} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.001251} \;\approx\; 799.4 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Realistic safety factor.** In practice, a strobe pulse of ≈ 1 ms is used, giving ≈ **800 Hz** per element. For a 384-element print head, the **total energy-delivery bandwidth** is ≈ **307 kHz**, well within modern driver IC capabilities.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — 3D Printer (FDM) Print Time Estimate

**Problem Statement:** Estimate the print time of a 50 mm × 50 mm × 50 mm cube using FDM with **layer height h = 0.2 mm**, **nozzle diameter d = 0.4 mm**, and **extrusion flow rate Q = 1.5 mm³/s**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Number of layers required.**

$$
N_{\text{layers}} \;=\; \frac{H}{h} \;=\; \frac{50 \text{ mm}}{0.2 \text{ mm}} \;=\; 250 \text{ layers}
$$

**Step 2 — Volume of a single layer (one horizontal "slice" of the cube).**

$$
V_{\text{layer}} \;=\; L \times W \times h \;=\; 50 \times 50 \times 0.2 \;=\; 500 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Total volume of the cube.**

$$
V_{\text{total}} \;=\; L \times W \times H \;=\; 50 \times 50 \times 50 \;=\; 125{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Total print time (ignoring acceleration/deceleration overheads).**

$$
t_{\text{print}} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{Q_{\text{ext}}} \;=\; \frac{125{,}000 \text{ mm}^3}{1.5 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}} \;\approx\; 83{,}333 \text{ s} \;\approx\; 23.15 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering refinement.** Real print time is typically **30–40 % longer** due to:
- Travel moves (no extrusion).
- Acceleration/deceleration phases.
- Layer-change Z-hop.

So realistic estimate: **~30 hours**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Impact vs. Non-Impact)

| Parameter | Dot Matrix (Impact) | Inkjet | Laser | Thermal | FDM 3D |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Print Mechanism** | Pin strike on ribbon | Droplet ejection | Xerographic toner fuse | Heat on thermochrome paper | Molten plastic extrusion |
| **Primary Actuator** | Solenoid pins | Thermal resistor / Piezo | Laser diode + corona wire | Heating element resistor | Stepper motors + hot end heater |
| **Resolution (DPI)** | 60–240 | 300–9600 | 600–2400 | 200–300 | N/A (layer-based: 50–300 µm) |
| **Speed (PPM)** | 1–5 | 5–30 | 20–100 | 4–10 in/s | 20–150 mm/s travel |
| **Noise (dB)** | 50–60 (loud) | 35–45 | 40–50 | <40 | 40–55 |
| **Color Capability** | Monochrome (ribbon) | Full color | Full color (4-pass) | Monochrome | Filament color |
| **Cost per Page** | Lowest | Medium | Lowest color | Lowest mono | Material-bound |
| **KTU Use Case** | Industrial multi-part forms | Home/office | Office high-volume | POS, medical | Rapid prototyping |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Printer Mechatronic Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HOST[Host PC or MCU] --> CTRL[Printer Controller ASIC]
    CTRL --> DSP1[Image Processor]
    CTRL --> DSP2[Motion Planner]
    DSP1 --> HEAD[Print Head Driver]
    DSP2 --> M1[X-Axis Stepper Driver]
    DSP2 --> M2[Y-Axis Stepper Driver]
    HEAD --> ACT[Actuator Nozzle or Laser]
    M1 --> XAXIS[Carriage Assembly]
    M2 --> YAXIS[Paper Feed / Platen]
    ACT --> MEDIUM[Paper or Substrate]
    XAXIS --> ENC1[Optical Encoder]
    YAXIS --> ENC2[Optical Encoder]
    ENC1 --> DSP2
    ENC2 --> DSP2
    TEMP[Thermistor] --> CTRL
    PSDC[Power Supply SMPS] --> CTRL
    PSDC --> M1
    PSDC --> M2
    PSDC --> HEAD
    HMI[User Panel LCD] --> CTRL
    CTRL --> HMI
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram maps the **mechatronic closed-loop** interactions in a generic printer: the controller orchestrates image processing and motion planning; the print head actuator and the two motor axes are driven in synchronized fashion; optical encoders provide **feedback** for closed-loop control; temperature and power subsystems ensure stable operation.

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Laser Printer Xerographic Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On] --> B[Charge OPC Drum to minus 600 V via Corona]
    B --> C[Laser Diode Scans Drum to form Latent Image]
    C --> D[Developer Roller applies Toner to discharged areas]
    D --> E[Transfer Corona moves Toner to Paper]
    E --> F[Paper enters Fuser Unit at 180 deg C]
    F --> G[Thermistor regulates Fuser temperature via PID]
    G --> H[Printed Paper ejected to Output Tray]
    H --> I[Wiper Blade cleans residual Toner from Drum]
    I --> J{More pages?}
    J -->|Yes| B
    J -->|No| K[Standby Mode]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — FDM 3D Printer Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CONTROL_BOARD
        MCU[STM32 / Marlin Firmware]
        TMC[TMC2209 Stepper Driver]
    end
    subgraph MECHANICAL
        X_MOTOR[X Stepper Motor] --> X_BELT[GT2 Belt]
        Y_MOTOR[Y Stepper Motor] --> Y_BELT[GT2 Belt]
        Z_MOTOR[Z Stepper Motor] --> Z_LEAD[Lead Screw]
    end
    subgraph HOTEND
        HEATER[40 W Cartridge Heater]
        THERM[100 k NTC Thermistor]
        NOZZLE[Brass Nozzle 0.4 mm]
        FILAMENT[1.75 mm PLA Filament]
    end
    subgraph FEEDER
        EXT[Extruder Stepper]
        GEAR[Drive Gear]
    end
    MCU --> TMC
    TMC --> X_MOTOR
    TMC --> Y_MOTOR
    TMC --> Z_MOTOR
    TMC --> EXT
    EXT --> GEAR
    GEAR --> FILAMENT
    FILAMENT --> HEATER
    HEATER --> NOZZLE
    THERM --> MCU
    HEATER --> MCU
    X_BELT --> PRINTBED[Heated Print Bed]
    Y_BELT --> PRINTBED
    Z_LEAD --> PRINTBED
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question, Module 2]**
**Differentiate between Impact and Non-Impact printers. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**
Impact printers form characters by physically striking an inked ribbon against the paper using pins or hammers (mechanical contact). Example: **Dot Matrix Printer (DMP)**. Non-impact printers form characters without physical contact, using heat, light, or pressure (e.g., inkjet, laser, thermal). They are quieter and offer higher resolution. Example: **Laser Printer**. *(3 marks: 1.5 for definition each + 0.5 × 2 for examples.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question, Module 2]**
**Explain the working principle of a thermal inkjet print head.**

**Model Answer:**
A thermal inkjet print head contains a thin-film resistor at each nozzle. When a **current pulse (~1 µs, ~100 mA)** is applied, the resistor rapidly heats a microscopic volume of ink to **~300 °C**, generating a **vapor bubble**. The expanding bubble ejects a **droplet (3–10 picolitres)** of ink through the nozzle onto the paper. The bubble collapses (cavitation) and a fresh droplet of ink is drawn in by capillary action, ready for the next firing. *(3 marks: 1 for bubble formation, 1 for ejection, 1 for refill/capillary refill.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model, Module 2, Internal Choice Option A]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the mechatronic architecture of a **laser printer**. List the six stages of the xerographic process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A laser printer uses a stepper motor of **1.8° step angle** coupled to a lead screw of **pitch 3 mm** for paper feed. Calculate:
   (i) The linear paper feed per full step.
   (ii) The linear paper feed per microstep if **1/16 microstepping** is used.
   (iii) The number of steps required to advance the paper by **one full A4 page length (297 mm)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Block diagram and xerographic stages (7 marks):**

> Refer to **Section 4.2 Mermaid Diagram** for the schematic.

**Six stages of xerography:**
1. **Cleaning** — residual toner is wiped off the **Organic Photoconductor (OPC) drum** by a wiper blade.
2. **Conditioning/Charging** — the drum is given a uniform **negative charge (~ −600 V)** by a corona wire or charge roller.
3. **Exposure/Writing** — a **laser diode** (or LED array) scans the drum, selectively discharging exposed spots to form a **latent electrostatic image**.
4. **Development** — **toner** (negatively charged plastic powder) is attracted to the discharged (relatively positive) areas on the drum.
5. **Transfer** — paper is given a positive charge, attracting the toner off the drum onto the paper.
6. **Fusing** — paper passes through **heated fuser rollers (~180 °C)** that melt and permanently bond the toner to the paper fibres.

*(Awarding: 3 marks for diagram, 4 marks for stages.)*

---

**(b) Stepper motor calculations (7 marks):**

**(i) Linear feed per full step:**

$$
L_{\text{step}} \;=\; \frac{\theta_{\text{step}}}{360^\circ} \times P_{\text{lead}} \;=\; \frac{1.8^\circ}{360^\circ} \times 3 \text{ mm} \;=\; 0.015 \text{ mm per step}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Linear feed per microstep (μ = 16):**

$$
L_{\text{micro}} \;=\; \frac{L_{\text{step}}}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{0.015}{16} \;\approx\; 0.0009375 \text{ mm} \;\approx\; 0.94 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**(iii) Number of full steps for 297 mm:**

$$
N_{\text{steps}} \;=\; \frac{297 \text{ mm}}{0.015 \text{ mm/step}} \;=\; 19{,}800 \text{ full steps}
$$

*(3 marks)*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model, Module 2, Internal Choice Option B]**

**(a)** Describe the working of a **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) 3D printer** with a neat block diagram. List the mechatronic subsystems involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3D printer must print a rectangular block of dimensions **80 mm × 40 mm × 30 mm** with a **layer height of 0.15 mm** and **extrusion flow rate of 2.0 mm³/s**.
   (i) Calculate the **number of layers** required.
   (ii) Calculate the **total volume** of material to be extruded.
   (iii) Estimate the **theoretical print time** and add a **35 % overhead** for non-printing moves to get a realistic estimate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) FDM 3D Printer Working & Block Diagram (7 marks):**

> Refer to **Section 4.3 Mermaid Schematic** for the block diagram.

**Working principle:** A thermoplastic filament (PLA/ABS, typically 1.75 mm diameter) is fed into a **heated nozzle (hot end)** maintained at **200–250 °C** by a cartridge heater and regulated by a **thermistor with closed-loop PID control**. The molten plastic is extruded in thin beads and deposited layer by layer on a heated build plate. **Three stepper motors** drive the X, Y, Z axes (often via GT2 belts and lead screws), while a **fourth stepper motor** (extruder) pushes the filament. After each layer, the Z-axis lifts by one **layer height (typically 0.1–0.3 mm)**.

**Mechatronic subsystems involved:**
1. **Controller** (Marlin firmware on Arduino/STM32) — processes G-code.
2. **Motion control** — stepper motors + TMC drivers + belts/lead screws.
3. **Thermal control** — PID loop for hot end + heated bed.
4. **Filament feed** — extruder stepper + drive gear.
5. **Sensors** — endstops (X/Y/Z), thermistor, optional BLTouch (auto bed leveling).
6. **Power supply** — 12 V/24 V SMPS.
7. **HMI** — LCD + rotary encoder.

*(Awarding: 4 marks for working, 2 marks for diagram, 1 mark for subsystem listing.)*

---

**(b) 3D Printing Calculations (7 marks):**

**(i) Number of layers:**

$$
N_{\text{layers}} \;=\; \frac{H}{h} \;=\; \frac{30 \text{ mm}}{0.15 \text{ mm}} \;=\; 200 \text{ layers}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Total volume:**

$$
V_{\text{total}} \;=\; L \times W \times H \;=\; 80 \times 40 \times 30 \;=\; 96{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

*(2 marks)*

**(iii) Theoretical time:**

$$
t_{\text{theoretical}} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{Q_{\text{ext}}} \;=\; \frac{96{,}000}{2.0} \;=\; 48{,}000 \text{ s} \;\approx\; 13.33 \text{ hours}
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**With 35 % overhead for non-printing moves (travel, Z-hop, acceleration/deceleration):**

$$
t_{\text{realistic}} \;=\; 48{,}000 \times 1.35 \;=\; 64{,}800 \text{ s} \;\approx\; 18 \text{ hours}
$$

*(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Thermal Inkjet with Thermal Printer** — Thermal inkjet uses a *resistor in ink*; thermal printer uses *thermochromic paper*. They are different technologies! (Lose 1–2 marks.)
> 2. **Forgetting units in stepper derivations** — Always write mm, DPI, mm/s explicitly. The KTU examiner deducts **0.5 mark per missing unit**.
> 3. **Mixing "inkjet" terminology** — Do NOT write "laser inkjet" or "thermal laser". Use exact KTU textbook terms.
> 4. **Skipping block diagrams in 14-mark questions** — A well-labeled **block diagram fetches 3–4 marks** by itself. Drawing one is mandatory, not optional.
> 5. **Missing the mechatronics angle** — Whenever asked about a printer, you MUST mention the **controller, actuator, sensor, and driver** — not just the working principle. KTU 2024 emphasizes mechatronic integration.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Printers, Module 2):**
> - **Classification** — Impact (Dot Matrix) vs. Non-Impact (Inkjet, Laser, Thermal, 3D).
> - **Dot Matrix** — Solenoid-driven pins, 9/18/24 pins, 5×7 or 7×9 dot matrix, tractor feed.
> - **Inkjet** — Thermal (bubble jet, HP/Canon) vs. Piezoelectric (Epson, PZT crystal).
> - **Laser / Xerography** — Six stages: **C**lean, **C**harge, **E**xpose, **D**evelop, **T**ransfer, **F**use. Mnemonic: **"CC-ED-TF"**.
> - **Thermal Printer** — Thermochromic paper + resistive heating elements; used in POS and medical devices.
> - **3D Printer (FDM)** — Stepper-driven X/Y/Z + extruder, PID-controlled hot end, layer-by-layer extrusion.
> - **Key formulas** — $\theta_{\text{step}} = 360/N$; $L_{\text{feed}} = \theta_{\text{step}} \cdot P_{\text{lead}} / 360$; $V_{\text{layer}} = L \cdot W \cdot h$.
> - **Mechatronic subsystems** — Controller (MCU) + Driver (H-bridge/Stepper driver) + Actuator (motor/solenoid/piezo) + Sensor (encoder/thermistor) + Power supply + HMI + Firmware.
> - **KTU buzzwords** — "Closed-loop motion control", "Multi-axis synchronization", "Electro-mechanical-optical integration".
> - **Resolution unit** — Always in **DPI (dots per inch)**; speed in **PPM (pages per minute)** or **mm/s**.
> - **Real-world link** — Printers are the canonical example of a mechatronic product combining **electronics + mechanics + control + software**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Automatic Control: Open Loop and Closed Loop Systems, Transfer Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT521 - MECHATRONICS AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concept of Automatic Control: Open Loop and Closed Loop Systems, Transfer Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Automatic Control?

**Automatic Control** is the branch of engineering science that deals with the design and analysis of systems whose behavior is regulated automatically without continuous human intervention, by using controllers and feedback mechanisms to achieve a desired output (setpoint) in the presence of disturbances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *An automatic control system is an arrangement of physical components connected in such a way that the output quantity is maintained at a desired value or follows a prescribed path without requiring a human operator to continuously monitor and adjust the input.*

**Key Terminology (KTU Standard Glossary):**
- **Plant / Process:** The physical system whose output is to be controlled (e.g., motor, furnace, robotic arm).
- **Controller:** The brain of the system that decides the corrective action.
- **Actuator:** Converts the controller signal into physical action (motor, valve).
- **Sensor / Transducer:** Measures the output and feeds it back.
- **Reference Input (Setpoint, $r(t)$):** The desired value of the output.
- **Controlled Output (Response, $c(t)$):** The actual measured output.
- **Error Signal ($e(t)$):** The difference between desired and actual: $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$.
- **Disturbance ($d(t)$):** Any unwanted signal that affects the output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Toilet Flush Tank" 🚽**
> Imagine a modern flush tank: You press a button (reference input). A sensor detects the water level (feedback). If the level is below desired, the controller opens a valve (actuator) to fill the tank. The moment water reaches the desired level, the sensor reports this, the controller shuts the valve. **You never touch the water — it controls itself.** That is automatic control!

---

### 1.2 The Two Fundamental Architectures

Every automatic control system in the universe falls into one of two categories:

| Architecture | Core Idea | Human Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Open Loop Control** | Output is *not* measured or fed back | A **toaster** — you set the timer, but it doesn't check if the bread is actually brown 🍞 |
| **Closed Loop Control** | Output is measured and compared with input | A **cruise control in a car** — it checks your speed and adjusts the throttle continuously 🚗 |

---

### 1.3 Open Loop Control System — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open Loop System:** *A control system in which the control action is independent of the output, i.e., the output has no influence on the input signal. Such systems are also called "non-feedback" or "feedforward" systems.*

**Standard Block Diagram — Open Loop:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Reference Input r t] --> B[Controller Gc s]
    B --> C[Plant or Process G s]
    C --> D[Output c t]
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style D fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Open Loop Examples (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **Automatic Washing Machine** (old mechanical timer type) — runs for a fixed time, doesn't sense cloth cleanliness.
> - **Traffic Light Controller** — fixed timing sequence.
> - **Electric Hand Dryer** — runs for a set time after button press.
> - **Stepper Motor for Printer Head** — pulses sent, no position feedback.
> - **Bread Toaster / Microwave Oven** — fixed timer input.

**Characteristics of Open Loop Systems:**
1. **No feedback element** — output never returns to influence the input.
2. **Simple, cheap, and reliable** for stable, predictable plants.
3. **Accuracy depends entirely on calibration** of components.
4. **Cannot reject disturbances** automatically.
5. **Sensitive to parameter variations** (aging, temperature drift).

---

### 1.4 Closed Loop Control System — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closed Loop System:** *A control system in which the control action is dependent on the output. The output is continuously measured, compared with the reference input to produce an error signal, which drives the controller. Such systems are also called "feedback systems."*

**Standard Block Diagram — Closed Loop (Negative Feedback):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Reference Input r t] --> S[ Summing Junction + - ]
    S --> B[Controller Gc s]
    B --> C[Plant G s]
    C --> D[Output c t]
    D --> F[Feedback Element H s]
    F --> S
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style D fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style S fill:#ffe599,stroke:#cc9900
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Rice Cooker" 🍚**
> Set the desired moisture / temperature. A thermistor (sensor) reads the actual temperature. The controller compares: if too cold → heater ON; if at setpoint → heater OFF. The system **closes the loop** between output and input. Disturbance? Open the lid — the sensor detects the drop and the heater automatically compensates. *This is closed loop control!*

**Characteristics of Closed Loop Systems:**
1. **Has a feedback loop** from output to input.
2. **Reduces sensitivity** to plant parameter variations.
3. **Improves transient response** (rise time, settling time).
4. **Rejects disturbances** automatically.
5. **Increases bandwidth.**
6. **More complex, costly, and may become unstable** if not designed properly.

---

### 1.5 Why We Need Transfer Functions

A **Transfer Function** is the *mathematical soul* of a control system. It converts a complex **time-domain differential equation** (hard to solve) into a simple **algebraic expression in the complex frequency variable $s$** (easy to manipulate).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transfer Function (KTU Standard Definition):** *The transfer function of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system is the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output to the Laplace transform of the input, assuming all initial conditions are zero.*

$$G(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)}\bigg|_{\text{zero initial conditions}}$$

**Intuition:** Think of the transfer function as a "**black box signature**" — it tells you, for any input shape, exactly what shape the output will be, without solving the messy differential equation each time.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Open Loop vs Closed Loop

The operational logic of a control system can be broken into **5 structured steps**. The fundamental difference lies in **Steps 3 and 4** below.

**Step 1 — Reference Input ($r(t)$) is applied:** The user/designer specifies the desired output value (e.g., 100°C temperature, 50 km/h speed).

**Step 2 — Controller Computes Action:** The controller (electronic circuit, PLC, microcontroller) generates a control signal $u(t)$ based on the input.

**Step 3 — Actuator Activates the Plant:** The actuator (heater, motor, valve) physically drives the plant.

**Step 4 — Output is produced ($c(t)$):** The plant produces the controlled output.
- 🔵 *Open Loop:* **STOP HERE.** The output is sent out and forgotten.
- 🟢 *Closed Loop:* **CONTINUE.** A sensor measures the output and routes it back.

**Step 5 — Error Computation (Only Closed Loop):** The feedback signal $b(t) = H(s) \cdot c(t)$ is subtracted from $r(t)$ to form $e(t) = r(t) - b(t)$, which is the new input to the controller. The cycle repeats continuously.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind feedback:** Feedback works because the error $e(t)$ is the *only* signal the controller cares about. If $e(t) \to 0$, the output matches the reference — mission accomplished! This single principle (driving error to zero) is the foundation of nearly all modern control, from thermostats to spacecraft.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Description | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Error Signal | $e(t) = r(t) - c(t)$ | Difference between desired and actual | Same as output |
| 2 | Open Loop TF | $G_{OL}(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G(s)$ | Cascade of controller and plant | Dimensionless ratio |
| 3 | Closed Loop TF (Unity Feedback) | $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$ | When $H(s) = 1$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 4 | Closed Loop TF (Non-Unity Feedback) | $\dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | General negative feedback | Dimensionless ratio |
| 5 | Feedback TF (with sensor) | $H(s) = \dfrac{B(s)}{C(s)}$ | Output to feedback signal | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Transfer Function Definition | $G(s) = \dfrac{C(s)}{R(s)}\big\vert_{\text{IC}=0}$ | Output / Input in $s$-domain | $s \in \mathbb{C}$ |
| 7 | Forward Path TF | $G(s) = \dfrac{C(s)}{E(s)}$ | From error to output | — |
| 8 | Characteristic Equation | $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ | Determines closed-loop stability | Roots give poles |
| 9 | Loop Gain | $L(s) = G(s)H(s)$ | Product of forward and feedback paths | — |
| 10 | Sensitivity (Open Loop) | $S^{c}_{G} = 1$ | Output varies 1:1 with plant changes | — |
| 11 | Sensitivity (Closed Loop) | $S^{c}_{G} = \dfrac{1}{1 + GH}$ | Output is *less* sensitive to $G$ | $\vert S \vert < 1$ if $\vert GH \vert \gg 1$ |
| 12 | Disturbance Rejection | $\dfrac{C(s)}{D(s)} = \dfrac{G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s)G_2(s)H(s)}$ | Effect of disturbance on output | Reduced as loop gain $\uparrow$ |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application | Open / Closed |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Cruise control, ABS braking, traction control | Closed |
| **Aerospace** | Autopilot, missile guidance, satellite attitude | Closed |
| **Manufacturing** | CNC machine tool position control | Closed |
| **Process Industry** | Chemical reactor temperature/pressure control | Closed |
| **Home Appliances** | Automatic washing machine (modern), AC thermostat | Closed |
| **Home Appliances** | Old toaster, simple timer-based mixer | Open |
| **Robotics** | Robotic arm joint position | Closed (with encoders) |
| **Civil** | Smart bridge vibration damping, building sway control | Closed |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Designer's Rule of Thumb:** If the plant is well-known, the operating conditions are stable, and cost is critical → choose **Open Loop**. If the plant has unpredictable disturbances, parameter drift, or requires high accuracy → choose **Closed Loop**. Modern industry prefers closed loop by default.

---

### 2.4 Key Properties of Transfer Functions

1. **Defined only for LTI systems** (Linear Time-Invariant).
2. **Initial conditions are assumed zero** (an independent-input-output model).
3. **Transfer function is independent of input magnitude** — it characterizes the system itself.
4. **Poles** of $G(s)$ = roots of denominator → determine natural behavior & stability.
5. **Zeros** of $G(s)$ = roots of numerator → determine response shape.
6. **If TF is known, output for any input can be computed** as $C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Transfer Function

This is the **most important derivation in KTU Module 3** — it must be memorized and practiced.

**Given:** A standard negative feedback system with:
- Forward path transfer function: $G(s)$
- Feedback path transfer function: $H(s)$
- Reference input: $R(s)$
- Output: $C(s)$
- Error signal: $E(s)$
- Feedback signal: $B(s)$

**Step 1 — Error Equation (at the summing junction):**

The summing junction subtracts feedback from reference. With the negative sign convention (feedback enters with a "$-$" sign):

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$$

**Step 2 — Output Equation (Forward Path):**

The forward path produces output from error:

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 3 — Feedback Equation (Feedback Path):**

The feedback element produces feedback signal from output:

$$B(s) = H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 3 into Step 1:**

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Step 4 into Step 2:**

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot \big[ R(s) - H(s) \cdot C(s) \big]$$

$$C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s) - G(s) H(s) \cdot C(s)$$

**Step 6 — Collect $C(s)$ terms on the left-hand side:**

$$C(s) + G(s) H(s) \cdot C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

$$C(s) \big[ 1 + G(s) H(s) \big] = G(s) \cdot R(s)$$

**Step 7 — Final Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Universal Closed-Loop Formula** (Negative Feedback). For Unity Feedback ($H(s)=1$), it reduces to:
> $$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Transfer Function from a Differential Equation

**Problem (KTU Standard Type):** A system is described by the differential equation:

$$\frac{d^2 c(t)}{dt^2} + 6 \frac{dc(t)}{dt} + 5 c(t) = 10 \frac{dr(t)}{dt} + 5 r(t)$$

Find the transfer function $G(s) = C(s)/R(s)$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Laplace Transform** to both sides, assuming zero initial conditions ($c(0) = 0$, $\dot{c}(0) = 0$, $r(0) = 0$).

Recall the Laplace property:

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{d^n f(t)}{dt^n}\right\} = s^n F(s) \quad \text{(with zero initial conditions)}$$

**Step 2 — Transform Each Term:**

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{d^2 c(t)}{dt^2}\right\} = s^2 C(s)$$

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{6 \frac{dc(t)}{dt}\right\} = 6 s C(s)$$

$$\mathcal{L}\{5 c(t)\} = 5 C(s)$$

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{10 \frac{dr(t)}{dt}\right\} = 10 s R(s)$$

$$\mathcal{L}\{5 r(t)\} = 5 R(s)$$

**Step 3 — Write the Transformed Equation:**

$$s^2 C(s) + 6 s C(s) + 5 C(s) = 10 s R(s) + 5 R(s)$$

**Step 4 — Factor Out $C(s)$ on the Left and $R(s)$ on the Right:**

$$C(s) \big[ s^2 + 6 s + 5 \big] = R(s) \big[ 10 s + 5 \big]$$

**Step 5 — Form the Transfer Function as the Ratio $C(s)/R(s)$:**

$$G(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{10 s + 5}{s^2 + 6 s + 5}$$

**Step 6 — Factor (Optional but Often Required in KTU):**

$$G(s) = \frac{5(2 s + 1)}{(s+1)(s+5)}$$

**Analysis:**
- **Zeros** (roots of numerator): $s = -1/2$
- **Poles** (roots of denominator): $s = -1,\; s = -5$
- Both poles are on the **left half of the s-plane** → system is **stable**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Effect of Feedback on Sensitivity

**Why feedback reduces sensitivity** — a critical KTU concept.

Let the plant transfer function change from $G$ to $G + \Delta G$ (e.g., due to temperature drift or aging). We want to compare how much the *closed-loop* output changes vs the *open-loop* output.

**Open Loop Case:**

Open-loop output: $C = G \cdot R$

If $G$ changes by $\Delta G$, the change in output is:

$$\Delta C_{OL} = \Delta G \cdot R$$

The **sensitivity** is:

$$S^{C_{OL}}_{G} = \frac{\Delta C / C}{\Delta G / G} = 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> In **open loop**, the output changes in a 1:1 ratio with the plant. If the plant's gain drifts by 10%, the output also drifts by 10%. **No improvement.**

**Closed Loop Case:**

Closed-loop output: $C = \dfrac{G}{1 + GH} \cdot R$

Differentiating $C$ with respect to $G$ (keeping $H$ and $R$ fixed) and applying the quotient rule:

$$\frac{dC}{dG} = \frac{(1 + GH) \cdot 1 - G \cdot H}{(1 + GH)^2} \cdot R = \frac{1}{(1+GH)^2} \cdot R$$

The **sensitivity** of closed-loop output to plant variation is:

$$S^{C_{CL}}_{G} = \frac{dC/C}{dG/G} = \frac{1}{1 + GH}$$

**Conclusion:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$\boxed{S^{C_{CL}}_{G} = \frac{1}{1 + G(s) H(s)}}$$
>
> **Interpretation:** If the loop gain $\vert GH \vert \gg 1$, then $S \to 0$. The closed-loop output becomes *insensitive* to plant parameter variations. **This is the deepest reason feedback is used in engineering.**

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Effect of Feedback on Disturbance Rejection

Consider a plant $G_2(s)$ with a disturbance $D(s)$ entering *after* the controller $G_1(s)$:

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[R s] --> S1[Summing 1 + -]
    S1 --> B[Controller G1 s]
    B --> C[G2 s]
    C --> S2[Summing 2 + +]
    S2 --> D[C s]
    D --> F[H s]
    F --> S1
    DI[Disturbance D s] --> S2
    style DI fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style D fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```

**Output due to disturbance alone (set $R(s) = 0$):**

By superposition, output from disturbance is:

$$\frac{C(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G_1(s) G_2(s) H(s)}$$

**Output due to reference alone (set $D(s) = 0$):**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_1(s) G_2(s)}{1 + G_1(s) G_2(s) H(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disturbance Rejection Insight:** Increasing the loop gain $G_1 G_2 H$ makes the denominator large, which forces $C(s)/D(s) \to 0$. **The disturbance is rejected.** But — caution — the same loop gain also affects stability! Trade-offs must be made (this is the heart of *control design* in higher modules).

---

### 3.5 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Property | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback Element** | Absent | Present ($H(s)$) |
| **Error Detection** | No | Yes — at summing junction |
| **Stability** | Inherently stable (no dynamics) | Requires design effort (can be unstable) |
| **Disturbance Rejection** | None | Excellent (inversely proportional to loop gain) |
| **Sensitivity to Plant Variations** | $S = 1$ (full) | $S = \dfrac{1}{1+GH}$ (reduced) |
| **Bandwidth** | Narrow | Wide (extended by feedback) |
| **Accuracy** | Depends on component calibration | High (self-correcting) |
| **Cost & Complexity** | Low | High |
| **Transient Response** | Fixed (cannot be tuned) | Tunable via controller $G_c$ |
| **KTU Example** | Toaster, traffic light | AC, cruise control, robotic arm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Comparative Architecture — Open vs Closed Loop

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph OpenLoop["OPEN LOOP SYSTEM"]
        direction LR
        O1[R s] --> O2[Gc s]
        O2 --> O3[G s]
        O3 --> O4[C s]
    end

    subgraph ClosedLoop["CLOSED LOOP SYSTEM"]
        direction LR
        C1[R s] --> CJ[Summing Junction]
        CJ --> C2[Gc s]
        C2 --> C3[G s]
        C3 --> C4[C s]
        C4 --> C5[H s]
        C5 --> CJ
    end

    style OpenLoop fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style ClosedLoop fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```

---

### 4.2 Standard Negative Feedback Control Loop (Detailed)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Reference Input r t"] --> SJ["Summing Junction + and -"]
    SJ --> E["Error e t"]
    E --> CTRL["Controller Gc s"]
    CTRL --> ACT["Actuator Ga s"]
    ACT --> PLT["Plant Gp s"]
    PLT --> OUT["Output c t"]
    OUT --> SEN["Sensor H s"]
    SEN --> SJ
    DIST["Disturbance d t"] -.-> PLT

    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style OUT fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    style SJ fill:#ffe599,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style DIST fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style E fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram Reduction Flow Chart

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["Start with Complex Block Diagram"] --> B["Identify all Forward Paths"]
    B --> C["Identify all Feedback Loops"]
    C --> D["Compute Loop Gain L1 s for each loop"]
    D --> E["Compute Forward Path Gain P1 s"]
    E --> F["Check for Non-touching Loops"]
    F --> G["Apply Mason Gain Formula T s = Sum of Pk Dk over Delta"]
    G --> H["Final Reduced Transfer Function T s"]

    style A fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style H fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mason's Gain Formula (KTU High-Yield):**
> $$T(s) = \frac{1}{\Delta} \sum_{k=1}^{N} P_k \Delta_k$$
>
> Where:
> - $P_k$ = gain of the $k^{th}$ forward path
> - $\Delta = 1 - \sum L_i + \sum L_i L_j - \sum L_i L_j L_k + \ldots$ (all individual loops, then non-touching loop pairs, etc.)
> - $\Delta_k$ = the cofactor of the $k^{th}$ path = $\Delta$ evaluated by removing all loops touching $P_k$

---

### 4.4 Signal Flow Graph — Reference Example

For a system with two non-touching loops $L_1 = a \cdot b$ and $L_2 = c \cdot d$ and forward path $P_1 = a \cdot e \cdot f$:

```mermaid
graph LR
    N1["X1"] -->|a| N2["X2"]
    N2 -->|b| N3["X3"]
    N2 -->|e| N4["X4"]
    N4 -->|f| N3
    N1 -->|c| N5["X5"]
    N5 -->|d| N3
    N3 --> N6["Output"]

    style N1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style N6 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```

**Applying Mason's Formula:**

$$\Delta = 1 - (L_1 + L_2) + (L_1 L_2)$$

Wait — the two loops *do* touch (at node $X_2$ via path $a$ and at node $X_3$). For pedagogical clarity, $L_1 L_2$ are non-touching only if they share **no** node. In the graph above, $L_1 = ab$ and $L_2 = cd$ share node $X_1$ (input) only if connected — assumed separated here for demonstration. The examiner typically gives explicit "non-touching" labeling.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU:** Do not spend excessive time on signal flow graphs unless the question explicitly asks. Block diagram reduction by series/parallel/feedback rules is more frequently tested.

---

### 4.5 Transfer Function Derivation Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["Time Domain Differential Equation"] --> B["Take Laplace Transform with Zero IC"]
    B --> C["s-domain Algebraic Equation"]
    C --> D["Isolate C s on Left"]
    D --> E["Isolate R s on Right"]
    E --> F["Form Ratio C s over R s"]
    F --> G["Factor Numerator and Denominator"]
    G --> H["Identify Poles and Zeros"]
    H --> I["Stability and Response Analysis"]

    style A fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style I fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
> **Differentiate between open loop and closed loop control systems. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Accuracy | Low (depends on calibration) | High (self-correcting) |
| Stability | Always stable | Needs design care |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex |
| Disturbance Rejection | None | Excellent |

**Examples:**
- **Open Loop:** Automatic washing machine (timer type), traffic light controller, bread toaster, electric hand dryer.
- **Closed Loop:** Air conditioner (thermostat), servo voltage stabilizer, missile guidance system, cruise control in car, human body temperature regulation.

*(Marks distribution: Comparison table – 2 marks, Examples – 1 mark)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional Model Paper]**  
> **Define transfer function. State any four properties of transfer function.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The transfer function of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system is the ratio of the Laplace transform of the output to the Laplace transform of the input, with all initial conditions assumed zero.

$$G(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)}\bigg|_{\text{zero initial conditions}}$$

**Four Properties:**
1. It is defined only for **linear, time-invariant (LTI)** systems.
2. It is **independent of input magnitude** and characterizes the system itself.
3. The **denominator** of the transfer function gives the **characteristic equation**, whose roots are the system poles.
4. If the transfer function is known, the output for any input can be computed using $C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$.

*(Marks distribution: Definition – 1 mark, Four properties – 2 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Question]**  
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the closed-loop transfer function of a negative feedback control system with forward path transfer function $G(s)$ and feedback path transfer function $H(s)$. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** For a unity feedback system, $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$. Find the closed-loop transfer function and identify the poles. Comment on stability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Given:** Negative feedback system with forward TF $G(s)$ and feedback TF $H(s)$.

**Step 1 — Define the signals:**
- Reference input: $R(s)$
- Output: $C(s)$
- Error: $E(s)$
- Feedback: $B(s) = H(s) C(s)$

**Step 2 — Summing Junction Equation (negative feedback):**

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s) = R(s) - H(s) C(s)$$

**Step 3 — Forward Path Equation:**

$$C(s) = G(s) E(s)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3:**

$$C(s) = G(s) \big[ R(s) - H(s) C(s) \big]$$

**Step 5 — Expand and Collect $C(s)$:**

$$C(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) C(s)$$

$$C(s) + G(s) H(s) C(s) = G(s) R(s)$$

**Step 6 — Final Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**

$$C(s) \big[ 1 + G(s) H(s) \big] = G(s) R(s)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}}$$

*(Marks: Step 1 – 1m, Step 2 – 1m, Step 3 – 1m, Step 4 – 1m, Step 5 – 1m, Step 6 – 1m, Boxed result – 1m)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:** Unity feedback ⇒ $H(s) = 1$ and $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$.

**Step 1 — Apply Closed-Loop Formula (with $H=1$):**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)}}{1 + \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)}}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $s(s+2)(s+5)$:**

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5) + 10}$$

**Step 3 — Expand the Denominator:**

$$s(s+2)(s+5) = s(s^2 + 7s + 10) = s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s$$

So:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{10}{s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s + 10}$$

**Step 4 — Identify Poles (roots of denominator):**

We need roots of $s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s + 10 = 0$.

Using the Routh-Hurwitz check (quick stability test):
- $s^3$: 1, 10
- $s^2$: 7, 10
- $s^1$: $\dfrac{7 \cdot 10 - 1 \cdot 10}{7} = \dfrac{60}{7} \approx 8.57$
- $s^0$: 10

All first column entries are **positive** → no sign change → all poles in the left half of $s$-plane.

**Conclusion:** **The closed-loop system is STABLE.**

*(Marks: Step 1 – 2m, Step 2 – 2m, Step 3 – 1m, Step 4 – 1m, Conclusion – 1m)*

---

> **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Alternative Choice]**  
> **(a)** Explain with neat block diagrams the working of open loop and closed loop control systems. Compare their performance based on accuracy, stability, and disturbance rejection. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** A control system is described by the differential equation $\dfrac{d^2 c(t)}{dt^2} + 8 \dfrac{dc(t)}{dt} + 12 c(t) = 4 r(t)$. Determine the transfer function and identify the poles and zeros. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Open Loop Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[R s] --> B[Controller Gc s]
    B --> C[Plant G s]
    C --> D[C s]
```

**Closed Loop Block Diagram (Negative Feedback):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[R s] --> SJ[Summing Junction]
    SJ --> B[Controller Gc s]
    B --> C[Plant G s]
    C --> D[C s]
    D --> E[Feedback H s]
    E --> SJ
```

**Comparison Table (7 marks distribution):**

| Criterion | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | Low; depends on component precision | High; self-correcting via feedback |
| **Stability** | Always stable (no feedback) | Requires design; can be unstable |
| **Disturbance Rejection** | None — output corrupted by disturbance | Excellent — loop gain suppresses disturbance |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Examples** | Toaster, traffic light | AC, cruise control, autopilot |

**Key Insight:** Open loop is suitable for well-defined processes with no disturbances; closed loop is mandatory when disturbances, parameter drift, or accuracy requirements exist.

*(Marks: Block diagrams – 2m, Comparison table – 4m, Examples/insight – 1m)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:** $\dfrac{d^2 c(t)}{dt^2} + 8 \dfrac{dc(t)}{dt} + 12 c(t) = 4 r(t)$

**Step 1 — Apply Laplace Transform (zero initial conditions):**

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{d^2 c(t)}{dt^2}\right\} = s^2 C(s)$$

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{8 \frac{dc(t)}{dt}\right\} = 8 s C(s)$$

$$\mathcal{L}\{12 c(t)\} = 12 C(s)$$

$$\mathcal{L}\{4 r(t)\} = 4 R(s)$$

**Step 2 — Write Transformed Equation:**

$$s^2 C(s) + 8 s C(s) + 12 C(s) = 4 R(s)$$

**Step 3 — Factor $C(s)$:**

$$C(s) \big[ s^2 + 8 s + 12 \big] = 4 R(s)$$

**Step 4 — Form Transfer Function:**

$$G(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{4}{s^2 + 8 s + 12}$$

**Step 5 — Factor the Denominator (Standard KTU Step):**

$$s^2 + 8 s + 12 = (s+2)(s+6)$$

Therefore:

$$G(s) = \frac{4}{(s+2)(s+6)}$$

**Step 6 — Identify Poles and Zeros:**

- **Poles** (denominator roots): $s = -2$ and $s = -6$
- **Zeros** (numerator roots): None (numerator is constant $= 4$)

**Stability Comment:** Both poles are in the **left half plane** (negative real part) → system is **stable**. The pole closer to the imaginary axis ($s = -2$) is the **dominant pole** and will dominate the transient response.

*(Marks: Step 1 – 1m, Step 2 – 1m, Step 3 – 1m, Step 4 – 1m, Step 5 – 1m, Step 6 – 1m, Stability comment – 1m)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Sign convention error at the summing junction** (most common mistake, costs 2–3 marks): For *negative* feedback, the feedback signal is **subtracted**, not added. Writing $E(s) = R(s) + B(s)$ gives the *positive feedback* formula and is wrong for >95% of KTU questions.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to assume zero initial conditions** in the transfer function definition. The examiner *always* checks for the phrase "**assuming zero initial conditions**." Omitting it = 1 mark lost.
> 
> 3. **Confusing poles and zeros**: Poles = roots of *denominator*, Zeros = roots of *numerator*. Reversing them = full marks lost in stability analysis questions.
> 
> 4. **Not factoring the transfer function** when asked for poles. Always factor $s^2 + 8s + 12$ into $(s+2)(s+6)$ — only then can you list poles. Leaving the answer in expanded form = 1 mark lost.
> 
> 5. **Stability comment missing or vague**: KTU expects an *explicit* statement like "Both poles lie in the left half of the $s$-plane, hence the system is **stable**." A vague "system is OK" loses 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up open loop and closed loop formulas**: The most catastrophic error. Always identify the **loop type** (OL or CL) *before* writing the formula. For a unity feedback CL, the formula is $G/(1+G)$, not $G \cdot H$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 High-Yield Revision Points — Memorize Before Exam**

- ☐ **Automatic Control** = regulating output without human intervention via controllers and feedback.
- ☐ **Open Loop** = no feedback; cheap, simple, no disturbance rejection. Examples: toaster, washing machine (timer), traffic light.
- ☐ **Closed Loop** = has feedback; accurate, stable design required, excellent disturbance rejection. Examples: AC, cruise control, robotic arm.
- ☐ **Transfer Function** = Laplace ratio of output to input with **zero initial conditions**; $G(s) = C(s)/R(s)$.
- ☐ TF is defined only for **LTI** (Linear Time-Invariant) systems.
- ☐ **Closed-Loop TF** (negative feedback): $\dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$
- ☐ **Unity Feedback Closed-Loop TF**: $\dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$
- ☐ **Characteristic Equation**: $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ → roots = closed-loop poles.
- ☐ **Sensitivity in Open Loop** = 1; **Sensitivity in Closed Loop** = $\dfrac{1}{1 + GH}$ (drastically reduced by high loop gain).
- ☐ **Disturbance Rejection** improves as loop gain $\uparrow$; but too high gain can cause instability — design trade-off.
- ☐ **Poles** (denominator roots) determine stability; **Zeros** (numerator roots) shape the response.
- ☐ **Stable system** ⇒ all poles in **left half of $s$-plane** (negative real part).
- ☐ **Mason's Gain Formula** (for complex block diagrams): $T(s) = \dfrac{1}{\Delta} \sum P_k \Delta_k$.
- ☐ Derivation flow for TF: **Differential Equation → Laplace Transform → Algebra → Ratio → Factor → Analyze**.

> **Best of luck for your KTU exams! 🌟**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Representation of Control Components and Systems: Translational and Rotational Mechanical Components, Series and Parallel Combinations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT521 - MECHATRONICS AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Representation of Control Components and Systems: Translational and Rotational Mechanical Components, Series and Parallel Combinations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Mechanical Systems in Control Engineering** are physical assemblies of mass, spring, and damping elements whose motion (either linear or angular) under applied forces or torques can be described by a set of ordinary differential equations. These equations form the foundation for deriving the **Transfer Function** of the system by applying the **Laplace Transform** (assuming zero initial conditions).

In control system representation, every physical mechanical component is idealized as a lumped element governed by a linear constitutive law. The complete system is then represented using interconnected **Translational Components** (movement along a straight line) and **Rotational Components** (movement about a fixed axis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The student must be able to draw the **force/torque analogy** and derive the **transfer function** of mechanical translational and rotational systems using lumped parameter modeling.

## 1.2 The Three Fundamental Ideal Elements

For a one-degree-of-freedom (1-DOF) mechanical system, there are exactly three passive ideal elements:

| Element (Translational) | Element (Rotational) | Governing Law | Energy Storage/Dissipation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mass (M)** | **Moment of Inertia (J)** | $F = M \dfrac{d^2x}{dt^2}$ | Stores **Kinetic Energy** |
| **Spring (K)** | **Torsional Spring (K)** | $F = Kx$ | Stores **Potential Energy** |
| **Damper (B)** | **Rotational Damper (B)** | $F = B\dfrac{dx}{dt}$ | Dissipates Energy as Heat |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard SI Units:**
> - Mass **M** → kg
> - Spring constant **K** → N/m (translation) or N·m/rad (rotation)
> - Damping coefficient **B** → N·s/m (translation) or N·m·s/rad (rotation)
> - Force **F** → N, Displacement **x** → m
> - Torque **T** → N·m, Angular displacement **θ** → rad

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spring-Mass-Damper" Real-World Picture

Imagine a car suspension system pushing down on a road bump. Three things happen simultaneously:
1. The **shock absorber** (damper) tries to **resist the velocity** of compression (viscous friction slowing motion).
2. The **coil spring** tries to **restore the chassis** to its original height (proportional to displacement).
3. The **car body mass** resists **acceleration** (Newton's Second Law: $F = Ma$).

These three are the same three elements found in **every** mechanical control system — from a robotic arm joint to a precision positioning stage. The only difference between a translational and rotational system is whether the motion is along a line or about an axis.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** "**M-A-D** for Translational, **J-B-K** for Rotational" — **M**ass resists *a*cceleration, **D**amper resists *v*elocity, **S**pring resists *d*isplacement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Translational Mechanical Components

### 2.1.1 Mass Element (M)
A mass stores **kinetic energy** and opposes any change in velocity (i.e., acceleration). In the Laplace domain, it behaves as an inertance.

$$F(t) = M \cdot a(t) = M \cdot \frac{d^2x(t)}{dt^2}$$

Applying Laplace Transform (zero initial conditions):

$$F(s) = M \cdot s^2 \cdot X(s)$$

The **mechanical impedance** of mass: $Z_M(s) = M \cdot s^2$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Intuition:** A heavier object requires more force to accelerate at the same rate. Doubling the mass doubles the force needed for identical acceleration.

### 2.1.2 Spring Element (K)
