- ✅ **NTC thermistor:** $R_T = R_0 \exp\!\left[ B\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right) \right]$.
- ✅ **63.2% rule:** After one time constant $\tau$, the response reaches 63.2% of final value; after $4\tau$, it settles to within 2%.
- ✅ **Steady-state error** for a step setpoint change is **non-zero** for pure P-control; **PI/PID** is required to eliminate it.
- ✅ Typical KTU problem values: $R$ ≈ 0.01–0.1 °C/W, $C$ ≈ 1000–500,000 J/°C, $\tau$ ≈ 100 s to several hours.
- ✅ Always **define the deviation variable** $\theta = T - T_a$ before applying the Laplace transform.
- ✅ Always **mention units** in the final answer (°C, s, W) to score full marks in KTU 2024 ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed Control Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT521 - MECHATRONICS AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Speed Control Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Speed Control System** is a closed-loop (or open-loop) electromechanical feedback arrangement designed to maintain the rotational velocity of a motor shaft at a desired reference value, regardless of external load disturbances, supply voltage fluctuations, or parametric variations. In the KTU 2024 Mechatronics framework, speed control is treated as a classical *single-input single-output (SISO)* regulation problem, where the controlled variable is angular velocity (rad/s or rpm) and the manipulated variable is typically armature voltage, field flux, or stator frequency depending on the actuator class.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 / Module 3):** Speed control systems bridge the gap between **actuators** (DC/AC motors) and **controllers** (PID, PI, ON-OFF). The system is mathematically modeled using the transfer-function approach developed in Module 2 and benchmarked against transient specifications (rise time, settling time, steady-state error).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cruise Control" Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway and engaging **cruise control** at 80 km/h:

- The **reference input** is the 80 km/h setting on your dashboard.
- Your **eyes and speedometer** act as the *speed sensor (tachogenerator)*.
- The car's **ECU (Electronic Control Unit)** is the *controller* (PID).
- The **accelerator pedal** is the *final control element* feeding the *engine (plant)*.
- If the road inclines, the car slows — the sensor detects the drop, the controller commands more throttle, and speed is restored.

This is **exactly** how an industrial speed control loop works — the only difference is that an electric motor replaces the engine, a tachogenerator replaces your eyes, and a PID controller running on a PLC/microcontroller replaces the human reflex.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Speed Control Matters in Mechatronics**
> - **CNC machines** demand constant spindle speed for surface finish.
> - **Conveyor belts** require RPM regulation under varying payload.
> - **EVs (Electric Vehicles)** rely on precise motor speed for traction.
> - **Robotic joints** use speed-controlled servos for trajectory tracking.
> - The standard engineering metric used is **steady-state speed regulation**:
>   **% Regulation = (N₀ − N_fl) / N_fl × 100%**
>   where N₀ = no-load speed, N_fl = full-load speed.

## 1.3 Classification of Speed Control Systems

| **Type** | **Sensing Required?** | **Accuracy** | **KTU Typical Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Open-Loop (No Feedback) | No | Poor (5–15% error) | Field-rheostat control of DC shunt motor |
| Closed-Loop (With Feedback) | Yes (Tachogenerator/Encoder) | High (<1% error) | PID-controlled armature voltage system |
| Adaptive / Intelligent | Yes + Parameter Estimator | Very High | Fuzzy-logic or NN-based EV drive |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The DC Shunt Motor — The Workhorse of KTU Problems

The **separately excited / shunt DC motor** is the canonical plant in KTU Module 3 derivations. Its behavior is governed by two coupled first-order differential equations derived from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (armature) and Newton's Second Law (rotor dynamics).

### 2.1.1 The Two Governing Equations

**Electrical (Armature) Sub-system:**

$$V_a(t) = R_a \cdot i_a(t) + L_a \cdot \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + e_b(t)$$

**Mechanical (Rotor) Sub-system:**

$$J \cdot \frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} + B \cdot \omega(t) = T_m(t) - T_L(t)$$

**Electromechanical Coupling Constants:**

$$T_m(t) = K_t \cdot i_a(t) \quad \text{(Motor Torque)}$$

$$e_b(t) = K_b \cdot \omega(t) \quad \text{(Back-EMF)}$$

### 2.1.2 The Speed Equation (Steady-State)

At steady state (all derivatives = 0) and no load (T_L = 0):

$$\boxed{\omega_{ss} = \frac{V_a}{K_b} - \frac{R_a \cdot T_L}{K_b \cdot K_t}}$$

This equation is the **foundation of all DC motor speed control strategies** — every control action manipulates either V_a, R_a, or the flux term to maintain ω constant.

## 2.2 Two Classical Speed Control Methods for DC Motors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 examiners expect students to *compare* these two methods in 7–14 mark questions.

### Method 1: Armature Voltage Control (Preferred)

- Flux φ is held **constant** (field current held at rated value).
- Speed is varied by changing V_a (using a chopper / PWM converter / SCR bridge).
- **Wide range of speed control** (from zero to above base speed possible).
- **Excellent transient response** — preferred for closed-loop PID.
- Torque capability remains constant across the speed range.

### Method 2: Field Flux Control (Above Base Speed)

- Armature voltage V_a is held at **rated value**.
- Speed is varied by weakening the field flux φ.
- Allows speed increase *above* base speed (constant power region).
- Limited range (typically 1.5× to 3× base speed) and field winding heating issues.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly examinable** in the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the variable meanings alongside each equation.

| **#** | **Formula / Expression** | **Meaning / Use** | **Standard Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \phi}$ | Steady-state speed of DC motor | rpm |
| 2 | $\omega_{ss} = \dfrac{V_a}{K_b}$ | No-load angular speed | rad/s |
| 3 | $T_m = K_t \cdot I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque | N·m |
| 4 | $e_b = K_b \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF induced | Volts |
| 5 | $G_m(s) = \dfrac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(Js + B) + K_t K_b}$ | DC motor transfer function | rad/s per V |
| 6 | $G_{PID}(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | PID controller transfer function | Dimensionless ratio |
| 7 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | PID control law (time domain) | Volts |
| 8 | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{s \cdot R(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Final value steady-state error | Depends on input |
| 9 | $\% \text{Reg} = \dfrac{N_{nl} - N_{fl}}{N_{fl}} \times 100$ | Speed regulation percentage | % |
| 10 | $f_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{T_{sw}} = \dfrac{V_{DC}}{V_{ref}} \cdot f_{carrier}$ | PWM switching relation (AC drives) | Hz |
| 11 | $\dfrac{V}{f} = \text{constant}$ | V/f scalar control law for induction motor | Volts/Hz |
| 12 | $K_b = K_t = \dfrac{V_{rated} - I_a R_a}{\omega_{rated}}$ | Back-EMF constant (SI units) | V·s/rad |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Pitfall:** $K_t$ (torque constant) has SI unit **N·m/A**, and $K_b$ (back-EMF constant) has SI unit **V·s/rad**. Numerically they are equal in SI units but they are *physically different quantities*. Do not interchange their names.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Application** | **Controlled System** |
|---|---|---|
| Robotics | Joint velocity regulation | DC servo + PID |
| Electric Vehicles | Traction motor RPM | BLDC/PMSM with Field-Oriented Control |
| CNC Machining | Spindle speed | Induction motor with V/f drive |
| Hard Disk Drives | Platter rotation | Brushless DC with closed-loop |
| HVAC | Fan / blower RPM | AC motor with variable frequency drive |
| Paper Mills | Web speed synchronization | Multi-motor line with master-slave control |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Transfer Function of a Separately Excited DC Motor

This is the **single most-asked derivation** in KTU Module 3 across previous university exam papers. We derive the relation $\omega(s) / V_a(s)$ step-by-step.

### Given

- Armature circuit: $R_a$, $L_a$
- Mechanical load: moment of inertia $J$, viscous friction $B$
- Coupling constants: $K_t$ (torque), $K_b$ (back-EMF)
- Load torque $T_L$ (assumed zero for transfer function derivation)

### Step 1 — Write the Armature Loop Equation in the Time Domain

Starting from KVL around the armature circuit:

$$V_a(t) = R_a \cdot i_a(t) + L_a \cdot \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + e_b(t)$$

Substitute the back-EMF coupling $e_b = K_b \omega$:

$$V_a(t) = R_a \cdot i_a(t) + L_a \cdot \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + K_b \cdot \omega(t)$$

### Step 2 — Write the Mechanical (Rotor) Equation from Newton's 2nd Law

$$J \cdot \frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} + B \cdot \omega(t) = T_m(t) - T_L(t)$$

Substitute the electromagnetic torque $T_m = K_t \cdot i_a$ and set $T_L = 0$:

$$J \cdot \frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} + B \cdot \omega(t) = K_t \cdot i_a(t)$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Laplace Transform (Assume Zero Initial Conditions)

The Laplace transform of a derivative is $sX(s)$ when initial conditions are zero.

Armature equation in s-domain:

$$V_a(s) = R_a \cdot I_a(s) + L_a \cdot s \cdot I_a(s) + K_b \cdot \Omega(s)$$

$$V_a(s) = (L_a s + R_a) \cdot I_a(s) + K_b \cdot \Omega(s) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

Mechanical equation in s-domain:

$$J \cdot s \cdot \Omega(s) + B \cdot \Omega(s) = K_t \cdot I_a(s)$$

$$I_a(s) = \frac{(Js + B)}{K_t} \cdot \Omega(s) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute (ii) into (i) to Eliminate I_a(s)

$$V_a(s) = (L_a s + R_a) \cdot \left[ \frac{(Js + B)}{K_t} \cdot \Omega(s) \right] + K_b \cdot \Omega(s)$$

$$V_a(s) = \left[ \frac{(L_a s + R_a)(Js + B)}{K_t} + K_b \right] \cdot \Omega(s)$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Transfer Function $\Omega(s) / V_a(s)$

$$\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(Js + B) + K_t K_b}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result — Memorize This:**
> $$\boxed{G_m(s) = \frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(L_a J) s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + (R_a B + K_t K_b)}}$$
> This is a **2nd-order system** with two electrical/mechanical time constants. The denominator roots are the closed-loop eigenvalues that govern the speed response dynamics.

## 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Error of a Unity-Feedback Speed Loop

Consider the closed-loop system with plant $G_m(s)$ and PI controller $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$. The open-loop transfer function is:

$$G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_m(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}\right) \cdot \frac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(Js + B) + K_t K_b}$$

### Step 1 — Apply the Final Value Theorem

For a step input $R(s) = \dfrac{\omega_{ref}}{s}$, the steady-state error is:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute and Evaluate

With $H(s) = 1$ (unity feedback) and $R(s) = \omega_{ref}/s$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{\omega_{ref}/s}{1 + G(s)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{\omega_{ref}}{1 + G(s)}$$

Compute $G(0)$:

$$G(0) = \left(K_p + \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K_i}{s}\right) \cdot \frac{K_t}{R_a B + K_t K_b}$$

As $s \to 0$, $\dfrac{K_i}{s} \to \infty$, hence $G(0) \to \infty$. Therefore:

$$e_{ss} = \frac{\omega_{ref}}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A PI controller (or any controller with an integrator) makes the **steady-state error to a step input exactly zero**. This is the *Type-1* system property taught in Module 2. PD alone cannot achieve this — that is why KTU problems almost always use PI or PID for speed loops.

## 3.3 Derivation: V/f Control Law for an Induction Motor

For a 3-phase induction motor, the synchronous speed is:

$$N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} \quad \text{rpm}$$

The electromagnetic torque (simplified steady-state model) is:

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2 \cdot (R_2 / s)}{(R_2/s)^2 + (X_2)^2}$$

To keep flux $\phi$ constant (so torque capability is maintained across the speed range), we need $V/f = \text{constant}$. Derive this:

### Step 1 — Air-Gap Flux Equation

The flux is proportional to the ratio of induced EMF to supply frequency:

$$\phi \propto \frac{E}{f}$$

### Step 2 — Approximate E ≈ V (Neglecting Stator Drop at Low Slip)

$$\phi \approx \frac{V}{f}$$

### Step 3 — Enforce Constant Flux

To keep $\phi$ constant as frequency $f$ is varied, the voltage $V$ must be varied **proportionally**:

$$\boxed{\frac{V}{f} = \text{constant} = k}$$

This is the celebrated **V/f (scalar) control law** used in nearly every industrial Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) from manufacturers like ABB, Siemens, and Delta.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Open vs Closed Loop

| **Performance Metric** | **Open-Loop Armature Control** | **Closed-Loop PID Armature Control** |
|---|---|---|
| Steady-State Error | 5–15% (unacceptable for precision) | < 1% (typically 0.1%) |
| Load Disturbance Rejection | None (speed sags under load) | Excellent (corrects in <100 ms) |
| Sensitivity to Parameter Variation | High | Low (feedback desensitizes) |
| Implementation Cost | Low (rheostat only) | Higher (tacho + controller) |
| Stability Issues | None (no feedback) | Must analyze using Routh-Hurwitz |
| KTU 2024 Marks Weightage | 3–4 marks (definition/comparison) | 10–14 marks (full design problem) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop DC Motor Speed Control System

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the full **functional architecture** of a PID-based speed control loop, including all signal flow paths and disturbance injection points.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Rref["R Reference Speed omega_ref"] --> SumA["Summing Junction"]
    Td["T Load Disturbance T_L"] --> SumA
    SumA --> Err["E Error Signal e t"]
    Err --> PIDc["C PID Controller Gc of s"]
    PIDc --> Vaout["V_a Armature Voltage Command"]
    Vaout --> Motor["M DC Motor Plant Gm of s"]
    Motor --> Omega["omega Actual Shaft Speed"]
    Td2["T_L Disturbance at Shaft"] --> Motor
    Omega --> Tap["Tachogenerator Feedback H of s"]
    Tap --> Fb["Feedback Signal y t"]
    Fb --> SumA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The reference ω_ref enters the summing junction, where the negative feedback from the tachogenerator is subtracted. The error e(t) drives the PID controller, which outputs the armature voltage V_a that powers the DC motor. The load disturbance T_L enters at the summing junction (electrical side) and at the motor block (mechanical side).

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — PID Controller Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    In["E Error Input e t"] --> Pgain["P Proportional Branch Kp times e"]
    In --> Ibranch["I Integral Branch"]
    Ibranch --> Int["Integrator 1 over s"]
    Int --> Igain["Ki times integral"]
    In --> Dbranch["D Derivative Branch"]
    Dbranch --> Diff["Differentiator s"]
    Diff --> Dgain["Kd times derivative"]
    Pgain --> Add["Summing Junction"]
    Igain --> Add
    Dgain --> Add
    Add --> Out["U Control Output u t to Motor"]
```

## 4.3 Sensor Subsystem — Tachogenerator Equivalent Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Shaft["Rotating Motor Shaft omega rad per s"] --> Tach["Tachogenerator DC Generator"]
    TachConst["Gain K_tach V per rad per s"] --> Tach
    Tach --> Vfb["V_fb Feedback Voltage Signal to Controller"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A tachogenerator is a small permanent-magnet DC generator coupled to the motor shaft. Its output voltage is **directly proportional to the shaft speed** with a gain of typically 5–50 V per 1000 rpm. This voltage is subtracted from the reference (also in volts) to form the error signal — hence the entire loop is implemented in the *analog voltage domain*, eliminating the need for an ADC in classical designs.

## 4.4 State-Space Block — Motor and Load Combined

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Va["V_a Input Voltage"] --> E1["EMF Equation La s plus Ra"]
    E1 --> Ia["Armature Current i_a"]
    Ia --> Tm["Torque Gen Kt times i_a"]
    Tm --> E2["Mechanical Eq J s plus B"]
    E2 --> Omega["omega Output Shaft Speed"]
    Omega --> E3["Back EMF Kb times omega"]
    E3 --> E1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Coupling:** This diagram visually captures the *electromechanical coupling* that leads to the 2nd-order transfer function derived in Section 3.1. The back-EMF feedback loop (E3 → E1) is the reason the motor exhibits an inherent "damping" effect — the faster the shaft spins, the more voltage is "stolen" by the back-EMF, naturally limiting the armature current and torque.

## 4.5 V/f Control Block Diagram for Induction Motor Drive

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Fref["f_ref Speed Reference Frequency Hz"] --> Vf["V over f Block Constant Ratio"]
    Vf --> Vcalc["V_ref Required Voltage"]
    Fref --> PWM["PWM Modulator"]
    Vcalc --> PWM
    PWM --> Inv["3 Phase Inverter IGBT Bridge"]
    Inv --> IM["Induction Motor Stator"]
    IM --> Wout["omega Motor Shaft Speed"]
    Wout --> Enc["Encoder Speed Sensor"]
    Enc --> Ctrl["PI Speed Controller"]
    Ctrl --> Vf
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Speed Control System. Mention its classification. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

A speed control system is an arrangement (open-loop or closed-loop) used to govern the rotational speed of a motor to a desired reference value.

**Classification:**

- **Open-Loop Systems** — No feedback; accuracy is low. Example: Field rheostat control of DC shunt motor.
- **Closed-Loop Systems** — Uses speed feedback (tachogenerator/encoder) with a controller (PID). Example: PID-based armature voltage control.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention the **feedback element** in closed-loop systems. Always explicitly state that a *tachogenerator* or *rotary encoder* is mandatory in the closed-loop category.

---

### Question 2: Write the steady-state speed equation of a DC shunt motor and explain how it leads to two methods of speed control. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The steady-state speed equation is:

$$N = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \phi}$$

**Two methods emerge directly from this equation:**

1. **Armature Control:** Vary $V_a$ (voltage) while keeping $\phi$ constant.
2. **Field Control:** Vary $\phi$ (flux) while keeping $V_a$ constant at rated value.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not skip the $I_a R_a$ drop term. Writing $N = V_a / (K \phi)$ is only valid for the no-load case and will be marked wrong in a "steady-state" question where load is implied.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1): Full-Design Speed Control Problem

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Internal Choice Q1]**

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a separately excited DC motor relating armature voltage to shaft speed. State all assumptions clearly. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A DC motor has the following parameters: $R_a = 1 \, \Omega$, $L_a = 0.5$ H, $K_b = K_t = 0.05$ N·m/A, $J = 0.01$ kg·m², $B = 0.001$ N·m·s/rad. Design a PI controller so that the closed-loop step response has zero steady-state error and a settling time of approximately 0.5 seconds. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]

We begin with the two governing equations. From KVL of the armature:

$$V_a(t) = R_a i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + K_b \omega(t) \quad \text{...(1)}$$

From Newton's second law for the rotor (load torque assumed zero):

$$J \frac{d\omega}{dt} + B \omega(t) = K_t i_a(t) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

Apply the Laplace transform to both equations assuming zero initial conditions:

$$V_a(s) = (L_a s + R_a) I_a(s) + K_b \Omega(s) \quad \text{...(3)}$$

$$(Js + B) \Omega(s) = K_t I_a(s) \Rightarrow I_a(s) = \frac{(Js + B) \Omega(s)}{K_t} \quad \text{...(4)}$$

Substitute (4) into (3):

$$V_a(s) = (L_a s + R_a) \cdot \frac{(Js + B) \Omega(s)}{K_t} + K_b \Omega(s)$$

$$V_a(s) = \left[ \frac{(L_a s + R_a)(Js + B) + K_t K_b}{K_t} \right] \Omega(s)$$

Therefore the transfer function is:

$$\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(Js + B) + K_t K_b}$$

Expanding the denominator:

$$\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{L_a J s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + (R_a B + K_t K_b)}$$

This is a second-order system. [7 marks]

#### Part (b) — PI Controller Design [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute the plant time constant.**

Substitute the numerical values into the denominator coefficients:

- $L_a J = 0.5 \times 0.01 = 0.005$
- $L_a B + R_a J = (0.5)(0.001) + (1)(0.01) = 0.0005 + 0.01 = 0.0105$
- $R_a B + K_t K_b = (1)(0.001) + (0.05)(0.05) = 0.001 + 0.0025 = 0.0035$

So:

$$G_m(s) = \frac{0.05}{0.005 s^2 + 0.0105 s + 0.0035}$$

**Step 2 — Normalize the transfer function.**

Divide numerator and denominator by 0.0035:

$$G_m(s) = \frac{0.05/0.0035}{(0.005/0.0035) s^2 + (0.0105/0.0035) s + 1} = \frac{14.286}{1.4286 s^2 + 3 s + 1}$$

This is of the standard form $\dfrac{K}{a s^2 + b s + 1}$ where $K = 14.286$, $a = 1.4286$, $b = 3$.

**Step 3 — Identify natural frequency and damping.**

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{1}{a} = \frac{1}{1.4286} = 0.7 \Rightarrow \omega_n = 0.8367 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$2 \zeta \omega_n = \frac{b}{a} = \frac{3}{1.4286} = 2.1 \Rightarrow \zeta = \frac{2.1}{2 \times 0.8367} = 1.255$$

The plant is **overdamped** ($\zeta > 1$) with two real poles. The dominant time constant is:

$$\tau = \frac{a}{b} = \frac{1.4286}{3} = 0.476 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Ziegler-Nichols tuning heuristic for PI.**

Using the Z-N reaction-curve method for an overdamped plant, set $K_p$ such that the desired closed-loop time constant is around 0.5 s. A PI controller $G_c(s) = K_p + K_i/s$ is selected to remove the steady-state error and accelerate the response.

Choose $K_p = 0.1$ and $K_i = 0.5$ (pole-placement / Z-N derived):

$$G_c(s) = 0.1 + \frac{0.5}{s} = \frac{0.1 s + 0.5}{s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the open-loop transfer function.**

$$G(s) = G_c(s) G_m(s) = \frac{0.1 s + 0.5}{s} \cdot \frac{0.05}{0.005 s^2 + 0.0105 s + 0.0035}$$

$$G(s) = \frac{0.005 s + 0.025}{s (0.005 s^2 + 0.0105 s + 0.0035)}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the steady-state error.**

For a step input, since $G(s)$ contains a free integrator ($1/s$ in the controller), the system is **Type 1**, and:

$$e_{ss} = 0 \quad \text{(Zero steady-state error — design objective met ✓)}$$

**Step 7 — Check settling time.**

The dominant closed-loop pole is approximately at $s = -1/0.5 = -2$, giving a settling time of approximately $4\tau = 4 \times 0.5 = 2$ s in worst case. Adjusting $K_p$ upward to 0.15 brings this closer to the 0.5 s target.

Final design: $\boxed{K_p = 0.15, \quad K_i = 0.5}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to:
> 1. **Substitute the numerical values** — earning only partial credit if symbols are left as is.
> 2. **Mention the Type of the system** when answering the steady-state error sub-question.
> 3. **State the final $K_p$ and $K_i$ values** with proper units.

---

### Question B (Option 2): Comparison & AC Drive Problem

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Internal Choice Q2]**

**(a)** Compare armature voltage control and field flux control methods of DC motor speed control. List two advantages and two disadvantages of each. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the V/f control of a 3-phase induction motor. Derive the relation $V/f = \text{constant}$ and state why this is essential for constant torque operation. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Comparison [7 marks]

| **Parameter** | **Armature Voltage Control** | **Field Flux Control** |
|---|---|---|
| Variable Quantity | $V_a$ (Armature Voltage) | $\phi$ (Field Flux) |
| Held Constant | $\phi$ = rated flux | $V_a$ = rated voltage |
| Speed Range | 0 to base speed (and above if V_a > rated) | Above base speed (1.5× to 3×) |
| Torque Capability | Constant (full torque at all speeds) | Reduces as speed increases (P = T·ω = const) |
| Power | Constant (for below base speed) | Constant (for above base speed) |
| **Advantage 1** | Wide speed range from zero | Simple implementation with field rheostat |
| **Advantage 2** | Excellent transient response, good regulation | Allows field weakening above base speed |
| **Disadvantage 1** | Requires a controllable power converter (chopper) | Limited speed range; poor transient response |
| **Disadvantage 2** | Converter cost is high for high-power motors | Field winding heating; commutation issues at very low flux |

[7 marks awarded for: 4 marks table + 2 marks for 2 advantages + 1 mark for 1 disadvantage set]

#### Part (b) — V/f Control Derivation [7 marks]

The V/f control scheme is a **scalar control method** for induction motors where the stator voltage is varied in proportion to the supply frequency to keep the air-gap flux constant.

**Derivation of V/f = constant:**

For an induction motor, the per-phase RMS induced EMF in the stator is:

$$E = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \phi \cdot k_w$$

where $N$ is the number of turns, $k_w$ is the winding factor, and $\phi$ is the air-gap flux per pole.

Rearranging for flux:

$$\phi = \frac{E}{4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot k_w}$$

Since $E \approx V$ (stator voltage drop is small at low slip), the flux is approximately:

$$\phi \approx \frac{V}{4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot k_w}$$

To keep $\phi$ constant, $V$ must be **proportional to $f$**:

$$\boxed{\frac{V}{f} = \text{constant} = 4.44 \cdot N \cdot k_w \cdot \phi_{rated}}$$

**Why this is essential for constant torque operation:**

The electromagnetic torque of an induction motor is proportional to the product of flux and rotor current:

$$T \propto \phi \cdot I_2$$

If the flux $\phi$ is maintained constant by enforcing $V/f = $ const, then the torque capability remains constant across the entire speed range (from low speeds to base speed). This is the **constant-torque region** of the VFD torque-speed curve. Above base speed, both V and f reach their rated values; further speed increase is only possible by weakening the flux, which moves the drive into the **constant-power region** where torque reduces inversely with speed.

[7 marks awarded for: 3 marks derivation + 2 marks block diagram explanation + 2 marks constant-torque justification]

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall for V/f:** Students often forget the **low-frequency voltage boost** required. At very low frequencies, the stator $I R$ drop becomes a significant fraction of $V$, so a small additional voltage must be injected (called **voltage boost** or **torque boost**) to maintain flux. Failing to mention this loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final-minute revision for KTU 2024 ESE — go through this list 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Speed Equation:** $N = (V_a - I_a R_a) / (K \phi)$ — the *master equation* of DC motor control.
- [x] **Two Control Methods:** Armature control (preferred, below base speed) and Field control (above base speed, constant power region).
- [x] **Transfer Function:** DC motor is a **2nd-order** system with two poles derived from electrical and mechanical time constants. Memorize:
      $\dfrac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{(L_a J)s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J)s + (R_a B + K_t K_b)}$
- [x] **Back-EMF Coupling:** $e_b = K_b \omega$ — this is the *internal feedback* that gives the motor its self-regulating property and natural damping.
- [x] **PID Role:** **P** reduces rise time, **I** eliminates steady-state error, **D** improves transient response and damps overshoot.
- [x] **Type-1 System:** A PI or PID controller makes the speed loop **Type-1**, giving $e_{ss} = 0$ for step inputs and $e_{ss} = \text{constant}$ for ramp inputs.
- [x] **V/f Control Law:** $V/f = $ constant is the foundation of every industrial VFD. Constant flux → constant torque capability.
- [x] **Sensors:** Tachogenerator (analog, V per rpm) and Rotary Encoder (digital, pulses per revolution).
- [x] **Speed Regulation Metric:** $\% \text{Reg} = (N_{nl} - N_{fl})/N_{fl} \times 100$. A well-designed closed-loop system achieves < 1%.
- [x] **Units Vigilance:** $K_t$ in N·m/A, $K_b$ in V·s/rad. They are numerically equal in SI but dimensionally distinct.
- [x] **Industrial Drive Manufacturers:** ABB, Siemens, Allen-Bradley, Schneider, Delta, Yaskawa — expect KTU to ask a 3-mark question on identifying manufacturers or drive types.
- [x] **Common Disturbances:** Load torque change $T_L$, supply voltage sag, ambient temperature (affects $R_a$), and field flux drift.
- [x] **Modern Trend:** Field-Oriented Control (FOC) and Direct Torque Control (DTC) are replacing scalar V/f control in high-performance applications — be aware of their names even if not deeply examinable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Word Mnemonic for KTU Viva/Exam:**  
> **"A-F-P-I-D"** → **A**rmature, **F**ield, **P**roportional, **I**ntegral, **D**erivative — the five pillars of any DC motor speed control answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: First and Second Order Systems: Response to Step, Pulse, Ramp, and Sinusoidal Input
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT521 - MECHATRONICS AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** First and Second Order Systems: Response to Step, Pulse, Ramp, and Sinusoidal Input

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: FIRST AND SECOND ORDER SYSTEMS — INPUT RESPONSE ANALYSIS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In control theory, the **order of a system** refers to the highest power of the differential operator (or the complex variable $s$) appearing in the denominator of its transfer function. Systems are classified based on this order, and their dynamic behavior is fundamentally governed by the location of poles in the complex $s$-plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition — First Order System:** A system described by a first-order linear differential equation of the form $\tau \dfrac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$, where $\tau$ is the **time constant** (seconds) and $K$ is the **steady-state (DC) gain** (dimensionless).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition — Second Order System:** A system described by a second-order linear differential equation of the form $\dfrac{d^{2}y(t)}{dt^{2}} + 2\zeta \omega_{n} \dfrac{dy(t)}{dt} + \omega_{n}^{2} y(t) = K \omega_{n}^{2} x(t)$, characterized by the **natural frequency** $\omega_{n}$ (rad/s) and the **damping ratio** $\zeta$ (dimensionless).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**First Order System — "The Hot Coffee Cup" Analogy:**
Imagine pouring hot coffee ($\approx 90^\circ\text{C}$) into a ceramic cup sitting on a room-temperature table ($\approx 25^\circ\text{C}$). The coffee does not cool instantly; it cools *exponentially*. The temperature difference $\Delta T(t)$ follows the rule:
$$\Delta T(t) = 65 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$
This is precisely the impulse response of a first-order system. The parameter $\tau$ represents how "stubborn" the system is — a thick ceramic cup has a *large* $\tau$ (slow to cool), while a thin metallic cup has a *small* $\tau$ (rapid cooling).

**Second Order System — "The Car Suspension Analogy":**
Press down hard on the front of a parked car and release it. The car body will bob up and down several times before settling. The behavior depends on the **shock absorbers** (damping):
- **No shock absorbers** (undamped, $\zeta = 0$): The car oscillates forever.
- **Soft shock absorbers** (underdamped, $0 < \zeta < 1$): The car oscillates a few times and settles.
- **Perfectly tuned absorbers** (critically damped, $\zeta = 1$): Returns to rest in the shortest time without oscillating.
- **Stiff, sticky absorbers** (overdamped, $\zeta > 1$): Slowly creeps back to rest without oscillating.

### 1.3 Standard Test Inputs

For KTU board examinations, four canonical inputs are used to characterize any LTI system:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Test Inputs (must be memorized verbatim):**
> 1. **Step Input** $r(t) = A \cdot u(t)$ → sudden, sustained change (e.g., turning on a switch).
> 2. **Impulse (Pulse) Input** $r(t) = A \cdot \delta(t)$ → instantaneous energy burst (e.g., hammer strike).
> 3. **Ramp Input** $r(t) = A \cdot t \cdot u(t)$ → linearly increasing change (e.g., accelerator pedal being steadily pressed).
> 4. **Sinusoidal Input** $r(t) = A \sin(\omega t)$ → oscillating reference (e.g., AC voltage, vibration shaker).

### 1.4 Why These Inputs Matter in Engineering

| Engineering Field | Practical Use of Input Response |
|---|---|
| **Robotics** | Step response → motor positioning accuracy & settling time |
| **Automotive (ABS/ESP)** | Pulse response → brake actuator response to pedal tap |
| **Aerospace** | Ramp input → tracking a linearly moving target (radar) |
| **Audio/Hi-Fi** | Sinusoidal response → frequency response (Bode plot) of speakers |
| **Process Control** | Step response → tuning PID controllers (Ziegler-Nichols) |
| **Vibration Engineering** | Impulse response → modal analysis of mechanical structures |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 First Order System — Anatomy

The canonical first-order transfer function is:
$$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Two physical parameters fully describe the system:**
- **Steady-state gain** $K$ → final output value per unit input
- **Time constant** $\tau$ → "memory" of the system; how quickly it reaches $\approx 63.2\%$ of its final value

The pole is located at $s = -\dfrac{1}{\tau}$ — a **single real, negative** pole on the left half $s$-plane, ensuring stability.

## 2.2 Second Order System — Anatomy

The canonical second-order transfer function is:
$$G(s) = \frac{K \,\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta \omega_{n} s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$$

**Two poles** are obtained from the characteristic equation $s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2} = 0$:
$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_{n} \pm \omega_{n}\sqrt{\zeta^{2} - 1}$$

The **damping ratio** $\zeta$ dictates the pole geometry and hence the response behavior:

| Damping Case | Range of $\zeta$ | Pole Location | Physical Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Undamped | $\zeta = 0$ | Purely imaginary $\pm j\omega_{n}$ | Sustained oscillation forever |
| Underdamped | $0 < \zeta < 1$ | Complex conjugate in LHP | Oscillation that decays |
| Critically damped | $\zeta = 1$ | Repeated real pole at $-\omega_{n}$ | Fastest return without overshoot |
| Overdamped | $\zeta > 1$ | Two distinct real poles in LHP | Slow, sluggish return |

## 2.3 Time-Domain Performance Specifications (Underdamped Step Response)

These four quantities are **favorites of KTU examiners** for second-order underdamped systems:

- **Rise time** $t_{r} = \dfrac{\pi - \phi}{\omega_{d}}$, where $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}{\zeta}\right)$
- **Peak time** $t_{p} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_{d}}$, where $\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$
- **Maximum percent overshoot** $M_{p} = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \times 100\%$
- **Settling time** $t_{s} = \dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$ (2% criterion) or $\dfrac{3}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$ (5% criterion)

## 2.4 Sinusoidal (Frequency) Response Highlights

For sinusoidal input $x(t) = A\sin(\omega t)$, the **steady-state output** is:
$$y_{ss}(t) = A \vert G(j\omega) \vert \sin(\omega t + \angle G(j\omega))$$

The function $G(j\omega)$ is the **frequency response function**, and its plot is the **Bode plot** — essential for stability and bandwidth analysis.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table — it is the single most important reference for KTU Module 4 problems.**

| # | System / Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | First-order transfer function | $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ |
| 2 | First-order step response | $y(t) = K\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ |
| 3 | First-order ramp response | $y(t) = K\left[t - \tau\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)\right]$ |
| 4 | First-order impulse response | $y(t) = \dfrac{K}{\tau} e^{-t/\tau}$ |
| 5 | First-order steady-state error (ramp) | $e_{ss} = K\tau$ |
| 6 | Second-order transfer function | $G(s) = \dfrac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$ |
| 7 | Second-order poles | $s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_{n} \pm \omega_{n}\sqrt{\zeta^{2}-1}$ |
| 8 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$ |
| 9 | Underdamped step response | $y(t) = K\left[1 - \dfrac{e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\sin(\omega_{d}t + \phi)\right]$ |
| 10 | Phase angle $\phi$ | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}{\zeta}\right)$ |
| 11 | Rise time $t_{r}$ | $t_{r} = \dfrac{\pi - \phi}{\omega_{d}}$ |
| 12 | Peak time $t_{p}$ | $t_{p} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_{d}}$ |
| 13 | Percent overshoot $M_{p}$ | $M_{p} = 100 \cdot e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$ |
| 14 | Settling time $t_{s}$ (2%) | $t_{s} = \dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$ |
| 15 | Settling time $t_{s}$ (5%) | $t_{s} = \dfrac{3}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$ |
| 16 | Critically damped step response | $y(t) = K\left[1 - e^{-\omega_{n}t}(1 + \omega_{n}t)\right]$ |
| 17 | Overdamped step response | $y(t) = K\left[1 - \dfrac{\omega_{n}}{2\sqrt{\zeta^{2}-1}}\left(\dfrac{e^{s_{1}t}}{-s_{1}} - \dfrac{e^{s_{2}t}}{-s_{2}}\right)\right]$ |
| 18 | Frequency response (1st order) | $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{K}{1 + j\omega\tau}$ |
| 19 | Magnitude (1st order) | $\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{1 + \omega^{2}\tau^{2}}}$ |
| 20 | Phase (1st order) | $\angle G(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega\tau)$ |
| 21 | Frequency response (2nd order) | $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{-\omega^{2} + j2\zeta\omega_{n}\omega + \omega_{n}^{2}}$ |
| 22 | Magnitude (2nd order) | $\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{\sqrt{(\omega_{n}^{2}-\omega^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta\omega_{n}\omega)^{2}}}$ |
| 23 | Phase (2nd order) | $\angle G(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\zeta\omega_{n}\omega}{\omega_{n}^{2}-\omega^{2}}\right)$ |
| 24 | Resonant frequency $\omega_{r}$ | $\omega_{r} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-2\zeta^{2}}$ (valid for $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$) |
| 25 | Resonant peak $M_{r}$ | $M_{r} = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$ |
| 26 | Bandwidth (approx) | $\omega_{B} \approx \omega_{n}\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^{2} + \sqrt{4\zeta^{4} - 4\zeta^{2} + 2}}$ |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Step Response

**Given:** $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$, input $X(s) = \dfrac{A}{s}$ (step of magnitude $A$).

**Step 1 — Form the output in the $s$-domain:**

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot X(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} \cdot \frac{A}{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply partial fraction expansion (key KTU algebraic step):**

$$\frac{KA}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \frac{C_{1}}{s} + \frac{C_{2}}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the constants:**

Multiplying both sides by $s$:
$$C_{1} = \left.\frac{KA}{\tau s + 1}\right|_{s=0} = KA$$

Multiplying both sides by $(\tau s + 1)$ and setting $s = -1/\tau$:
$$C_{2} = \left.\frac{KA}{s}\right|_{s=-1/\tau} = -KA\tau$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite and apply inverse Laplace transform:**

$$Y(s) = \frac{KA}{s} - \frac{KA\tau}{\tau s + 1} = \frac{KA}{s} - \frac{KA}{s + 1/\tau}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the standard inverse Laplace pairs** $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{1/s\} = u(t)$ and $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{1/(s+a)\} = e^{-at}$:

$$y(t) = KA\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) \quad \text{for } t \geq 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** At $t = \tau$, the output reaches $63.2\%$ of its final value $KA$. At $t = 4\tau$, it reaches $98.2\%$ — this is the engineering rule-of-thumb for "settled."

---

## 3.2 Derivation: First-Order Ramp Response

**Given:** $X(s) = \dfrac{A}{s^{2}}$.

**Step 1 — Form the $s$-domain output:**

$$Y(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1} \cdot \frac{A}{s^{2}} = \frac{KA}{s^{2}(\tau s + 1)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply partial fraction expansion:**

$$\frac{KA}{s^{2}(\tau s + 1)} = \frac{C_{1}}{s} + \frac{C_{2}}{s^{2}} + \frac{C_{3}}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the three constants** (multiply through by $s^{2}(\tau s + 1)$ first):

$$KA = C_{1}s(\tau s + 1) + C_{2}(\tau s + 1) + C_{3}s^{2}$$

Setting $s = 0$: $C_{2} = KA$.

Setting $s = -1/\tau$: $C_{3} = \dfrac{KA}{1/\tau^{2}} = KA\tau^{2}$.

Comparing the $s^{2}$ coefficients on both sides: $0 = C_{1}\tau + C_{3}$ → $C_{1} = -C_{3}/\tau = -KA\tau$.

**Step 4 — Reconstruct $Y(s)$ and take the inverse Laplace transform:**

$$Y(s) = \frac{-KA\tau}{s} + \frac{KA}{s^{2}} + \frac{KA\tau^{2}}{\tau s + 1}$$

$$Y(s) = \frac{KA}{s^{2}} - \frac{KA\tau}{s} + \frac{KA\tau}{s + 1/\tau}$$

**Step 5 — Apply inverse Laplace transform term by term:**

$$y(t) = KAt - KA\tau + KA\tau e^{-t/\tau}$$

$$\boxed{y(t) = KA\left[t - \tau\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)\right]}$$

**Step 6 — Steady-state error computation:**

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{t\to\infty}\left[r(t) - y(t)\right] = \lim_{t\to\infty}\left[At - KAt + KA\tau(1 - e^{-t/\tau})\right]$$

$$e_{ss} = A\,t\,(1-K) + KA\tau \quad \text{(assuming unity gain K=1)} \implies e_{ss} = A\tau$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A first-order system cannot perfectly track a ramp — it always lags by an amount proportional to the time constant $\tau$. This is a major KTU board question theme.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Second-Order Underdamped Step Response (Most Examined)

**Given:** $G(s) = \dfrac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$, input $X(s) = \dfrac{A}{s}$, $0 < \zeta < 1$.

**Step 1 — Form the $s$-domain output:**

$$Y(s) = \frac{KA\omega_{n}^{2}}{s\left(s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}\right)}$$

**Step 2 — Complete the square in the denominator:**

$$s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2} = (s + \zeta\omega_{n})^{2} + \omega_{n}^{2}(1 - \zeta^{2}) = (s + \zeta\omega_{n})^{2} + \omega_{d}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Set up partial fraction expansion:**

$$\frac{KA\omega_{n}^{2}}{s\left[(s + \zeta\omega_{n})^{2} + \omega_{d}^{2}\right]} = \frac{C_{1}}{s} + \frac{C_{2}(s + \zeta\omega_{n}) + C_{3}\omega_{d}}{(s + \zeta\omega_{n})^{2} + \omega_{d}^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $C_{1}$ (set $s = 0$):**

$$C_{1} = \frac{KA\omega_{n}^{2}}{\zeta^{2}\omega_{n}^{2} + \omega_{d}^{2}} = \frac{KA\omega_{n}^{2}}{\omega_{n}^{2}(\zeta^{2} + 1 - \zeta^{2})} = KA$$

**Step 5 — Equate numerators and solve for $C_{2}$ and $C_{3}$:**

Equating the $s^{2}$ coefficients: $0 = C_{2}$ → wait, we must equate the full polynomial expansion. Multiplying both sides by the common denominator:

$$KA\omega_{n}^{2} = C_{1}\left[(s + \zeta\omega_{n})^{2} + \omega_{d}^{2}\right] + s\left[C_{2}(s + \zeta\omega_{n}) + C_{3}\omega_{d}\right]$$

Expanding and matching coefficients of $s^{0}$:

$$KA\omega_{n}^{2} = C_{1}\omega_{n}^{2} + C_{3}\omega_{d}\cdot 0 = C_{1}\omega_{n}^{2} \quad \checkmark$$

Matching coefficients of $s^{1}$: $0 = 2C_{1}\zeta\omega_{n} + C_{2}\cdot 0 + C_{3}\omega_{d}$ → $C_{3} = -\dfrac{2C_{1}\zeta\omega_{n}}{\omega_{d}} = -\dfrac{2KA\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$.

Matching coefficients of $s^{2}$: $0 = C_{1} + C_{2}$ → $C_{2} = -C_{1} = -KA$.

**Step 6 — Reconstruct $Y(s)$:**

$$Y(s) = \frac{KA}{s} - \frac{KA(s + \zeta\omega_{n}) - \dfrac{2KA\zeta\omega_{n}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\omega_{d}}{(s + \zeta\omega_{n})^{2} + \omega_{d}^{2}}$$

After careful rearrangement and recognizing $\sin$ and $\cos$ Laplace pairs, we obtain:

$$Y(s) = \frac{KA}{s} - \frac{KA}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}} \cdot \left[\frac{\zeta\omega_{n}}{\omega_{d}}\cdot \frac{\omega_{d}}{(s+\zeta\omega_{n})^{2}+\omega_{d}^{2}} + \frac{s+\zeta\omega_{n}}{(s+\zeta\omega_{n})^{2}+\omega_{d}^{2}}\right]$$

**Step 7 — Apply inverse Laplace transform** using the damped sine and cosine pairs:

$$\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{\omega_{d}}{(s+\zeta\omega_{n})^{2}+\omega_{d}^{2}}\right\} = e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t}\sin(\omega_{d}t)$$

$$\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{s+\zeta\omega_{n}}{(s+\zeta\omega_{n})^{2}+\omega_{d}^{2}}\right\} = e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t}\cos(\omega_{d}t)$$

Combining with the identity $\zeta\sin\phi + \cos\phi\cdot\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}\cdot...$, the final result is:

$$\boxed{y(t) = KA\left[1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\sin\!\left(\omega_{d}t + \phi\right)\right], \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}{\zeta}\right)}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Pulse (Rectangular) Response via Superposition

A **rectangular pulse** of width $T_{p}$ and amplitude $A$ is mathematically:
$$x(t) = A\,u(t) - A\,u(t - T_{p})$$

By **linearity and time-invariance (LTI property)**:
$$y_{\text{pulse}}(t) = y_{\text{step}}(t) - y_{\text{step}}(t - T_{p})$$

**For a first-order system** (using $y_{\text{step}}(t) = KA(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$):

$$y_{\text{pulse}}(t) = \begin{cases} KA\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right), & 0 \leq t < T_{p} \\[6pt] KA\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) - KA\left(1 - e^{-(t-T_{p})/\tau}\right), & t \geq T_{p} \end{cases}$$

Simplifying for $t \geq T_{p}$:

$$y_{\text{pulse}}(t) = KA\left(e^{-(t-T_{p})/\tau} - e^{-t/\tau}\right) = KA\,e^{-t/\tau}\left(e^{T_{p}/\tau} - 1\right)$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Examples

### Example A: First-Order Step Response Numerics

**Problem:** A temperature sensor has transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{2}{3s + 1}$. Find (a) steady-state value, (b) value at $t = 3$ s for a unit step input.

**Solution:**

(a) Using final value theorem: $\lim_{s \to 0} sG(s) \cdot \dfrac{1}{s} = G(0) = 2$.

(b) Time constant $\tau = 3$ s, $K = 2$:
$$y(3) = 2\left(1 - e^{-3/3}\right) = 2(1 - e^{-1}) = 2(1 - 0.3679) = 2(0.6321) = 1.2642$$

### Example B: Second-Order Specifications Numerics

**Problem:** $G(s) = \dfrac{100}{s^{2} + 6s + 100}$. Find $\zeta$, $\omega_{n}$, $t_{p}$, $M_{p}$, $t_{s}$.

**Solution:** Comparing with $s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}$:

$$\omega_{n}^{2} = 100 \implies \omega_{n} = 10 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$2\zeta\omega_{n} = 6 \implies \zeta = 0.3$$

$$\omega_{d} = 10\sqrt{1 - 0.09} = 10\sqrt{0.91} = 9.539 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$t_{p} = \frac{\pi}{9.539} = 0.3293 \text{ s}$$
$$M_{p} = 100 \cdot e^{-0.3\pi/\sqrt{0.91}} = 100 \cdot e^{-0.9885} = 37.2\%$$
$$t_{s} = \frac{4}{0.3 \times 10} = 1.333 \text{ s}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "time constant" of a first-order system. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer:**
The **time constant $\tau$** of a first-order system is defined as the time required for the step response to reach **63.2%** of its final steady-state value. Mathematically, for a first-order system $\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = Kx$, the step response is $y(t) = K(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$, and $y(\tau) = 0.632\,K$.

**Physical significance:** $\tau$ represents the "memory" or "inertia" of the system. A small $\tau$ indicates a fast-responding system, while a large $\tau$ indicates a sluggish one. After $t = 4\tau$, the system reaches $\approx 98.2\%$ of its final value (engineering settling rule).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is meant by damping ratio? List the four cases of damping for a second-order system.**

**Model Answer:**
The **damping ratio $\zeta$** is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the degree to which oscillations in a second-order system decay. It is defined as the ratio of actual damping to critical damping:
$$\zeta = \frac{\text{actual damping}}{2\sqrt{km}}$$

The four cases are:
1. **Undamped** ($\zeta = 0$): No damping; sustained oscillation.
2. **Underdamped** ($0 < \zeta < 1$): Oscillatory response that decays.
3. **Critically damped** ($\zeta = 1$): Fastest non-oscillatory return.
4. **Overdamped** ($\zeta > 1$): Slow non-oscillatory return.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** For a unity feedback control system with open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$, determine the damping ratio $\zeta$ and natural frequency $\omega_{n}$ of the dominant closed-loop poles. Comment on the system's stability.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A second-order underdamped system has $\omega_{n} = 4$ rad/s and $\zeta = 0.3$. Compute the rise time, peak time, maximum percent overshoot, and settling time (2% criterion) for a unit step input.

#### **Solution A(a):**

**Step 1 — Write the closed-loop transfer function:**

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{10}{s(s+2)(s+5) + 10} = \frac{10}{s^{3} + 7s^{2} + 10s + 10}$$

**Step 2 — Identify dominant poles** (this is a 3rd-order system; KTU expects approximation to a 2nd-order system by neglecting the farthest pole).

Using approximate analysis, the dominant closed-loop poles of the original system are typically obtained via Routh-Hurwitz or numerical root-finding. The dominant poles approximately equal the roots of the quadratic factor obtained by neglecting the non-dominant pole contribution. For this problem, applying the dominant-pole approximation, the dominant poles are approximately:
$$s_{1,2} \approx -1.0 \pm j1.95$$

**Step 3 — Relate to standard second-order form:**

$$s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2} = (s + 1.0)^{2} + (1.95)^{2} = s^{2} + 2.0s + 4.80$$

Therefore:
$$\omega_{n}^{2} = 4.80 \implies \omega_{n} = 2.19 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$2\zeta\omega_{n} = 2.0 \implies \zeta = \frac{1.0}{2.19} = 0.456$$

**Step 4 — Stability comment:** Since both dominant poles lie in the **left half $s$-plane** (negative real part), the system is **stable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write the entire 3rd-order characteristic equation and forget to reduce to 2nd-order. KTU examiners expect you to **explicitly state the dominant-pole approximation** when comparing to standard 2nd-order form. State: *"Neglecting the non-dominant pole, the system behaves as..."*

#### **Solution A(b):**

**Given:** $\omega_{n} = 4$ rad/s, $\zeta = 0.3$.

**Step 1 — Compute damped natural frequency:**
$$\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}} = 4\sqrt{1 - 0.09} = 4\sqrt{0.91} = 3.815 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the phase angle $\phi$:**
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}{\zeta}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{0.91}}{0.3}\right) = \tan^{-1}(3.185) = 1.257 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Compute rise time:**
$$t_{r} = \frac{\pi - \phi}{\omega_{d}} = \frac{3.1416 - 1.257}{3.815} = \frac{1.8846}{3.815} = 0.494 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute peak time:**
$$t_{p} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_{d}} = \frac{3.1416}{3.815} = 0.823 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute maximum percent overshoot:**
$$M_{p} = 100 \cdot e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} = 100 \cdot e^{-0.3\pi/\sqrt{0.91}}$$
$$M_{p} = 100 \cdot e^{-0.9885} = 100 \times 0.3724 = 37.24\%$$

**Step 6 — Compute settling time (2% criterion):**
$$t_{s} = \frac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}} = \frac{4}{0.3 \times 4} = \frac{4}{1.2} = 3.333 \text{ s}$$

**Summary of answers:**
- Rise time $t_{r} = 0.494$ s
- Peak time $t_{p} = 0.823$ s
- Maximum overshoot $M_{p} = 37.24\%$
- Settling time $t_{s} = 3.333$ s

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternative]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the step response of a first-order system $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$. Sketch the response and indicate the steady-state value, time constant location, and the value of the response at $t = \tau$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a unity feedback system with $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}$, find the steady-state error for step, ramp, and parabolic inputs. Comment on the system type.

#### **Solution B(a):**

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Form the output in $s$-domain** for unit step input $R(s) = 1/s$:

$$C(s) = G(s) R(s) = \frac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply partial fraction expansion:**

$$\frac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{\tau s + 1}$$

Solving: $A = K$ (set $s=0$), $B = -K\tau$ (set $s = -1/\tau$).

**Step 3 — Inverse Laplace transform:**

$$C(s) = \frac{K}{s} - \frac{K}{s + 1/\tau} \implies c(t) = K\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) \quad t \geq 0$$

**Step 4 — Key observations for the sketch:**
- Initial value $c(0) = 0$
- Final value $c(\infty) = K$ (steady-state value)
- At $t = \tau$: $c(\tau) = K(1 - e^{-1}) = 0.632K$
- Tangent at $t = 0$ has slope $K/\tau$ and reaches $K$ at $t = \tau$

**Sketch (drawn on script):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time t in seconds on x axis] --> B[Response c t on y axis]
    B --> C[Exponential rise curve]
    C --> D[Starts at origin 0 0]
    D --> E[Reaches 0.632 K at t equals tau]
    E --> F[Asymptotes to steady state value K at t equals 4 tau]
    F --> G[Horizontal asymptote at y equals K]
```

**Numerical values for sketch labels:** $\tau$, $0.632K$, $0.865K$ (at $2\tau$), $0.95K$ (at $3\tau$), $0.982K$ (at $4\tau$).

#### **Solution B(b):**

**Given:** Open-loop $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}$, unity feedback $H(s) = 1$.

**System Type Identification:** The number of pure integrators (poles at origin) in the open-loop transfer function determines the **system type**. Here there is one $1/s$ factor, so the system is **Type 1**.

**Step 1 — Error constants:**

- **Position error constant** $K_{p} = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)} = \infty$
- **Velocity error constant** $K_{v} = \lim_{s \to 0} s G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1} = K$
- **Acceleration error constant** $K_{a} = \lim_{s \to 0} s^{2} G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{Ks}{\tau s + 1} = 0$

**Step 2 — Steady-state errors:**

- **Step input** $R(s) = 1/s$: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_{p}} = \dfrac{1}{\infty} = 0$
- **Ramp input** $R(s) = 1/s^{2}$: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_{v}} = \dfrac{1}{K}$
- **Parabolic input** $R(s) = 1/s^{3}$: $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{K_{a}} = \dfrac{1}{0} = \infty$

**Step 3 — Comment:**
A Type 1 system can track a step input with **zero** steady-state error, track a ramp input with a **finite** steady-state error of $1/K$, and **cannot** track a parabolic input (infinite error). The DC gain $K$ directly determines the ramp tracking accuracy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (3 marks lost per KTU past papers):**
> 1. **Confusing system type with order.** A Type 2 system is *not* a 2nd-order system — "type" refers to the number of integrators.
> 2. **Forgetting to use $K_{p}$, $K_{v}$, $K_{a}$ in series** when the system type is greater than 0.
> 3. **Writing units in error:** $e_{ss}$ for ramp input has the same unit as input, not a dimensionless number.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places KTU students lose marks in Module 4:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the underdamped step response — the standard form has a MINUS sign before the damped sine term. Writing $+ \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\sin(\omega_{d}t + \phi)$ is **wrong**.
> 2. **Using degrees instead of radians** for $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\ldots)$ and $\omega_{d}$. All KTU answers must be in **radians**.
> 3. **Settling time formula confusion** — KTU accepts both 2% and 5% criteria, but the examiner wants the criterion **explicitly stated** in your answer.
> 4. **Forgetting to compute $\omega_{d}$** when $t_{p}$, $t_{r}$, or $M_{p}$ is asked. Many students use $\omega_{n}$ instead of $\omega_{d}$ in $t_{p} = \pi/\omega_{d}$.
> 5. **Ramp response:** Writing $y(t) = K(1 - e^{-t/\tau}) \cdot t$ instead of the correct $y(t) = K[t - \tau(1 - e^{-t/\tau})]$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4, First & Second Order Systems**

### A. First-Order Systems
- ✅ Canonical form: $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$
- ✅ Pole location: $s = -1/\tau$ (always stable for $\tau > 0$)
- ✅ Step response: $y(t) = K(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ → reaches $63.2\%$ at $t = \tau$
- ✅ Ramp response: $y(t) = K[t - \tau(1 - e^{-t/\tau})]$ with steady-state error $e_{ss} = \tau$ (for unity $K$)
- ✅ Impulse response: $y(t) = \dfrac{K}{\tau} e^{-t/\tau}$
- ✅ Sinusoidal: $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{K}{1 + j\omega\tau}$, magnitude $\dfrac{K}{\sqrt{1+\omega^{2}\tau^{2}}}$, phase $-\tan^{-1}(\omega\tau)$

### B. Second-Order Systems
- ✅ Canonical form: $G(s) = \dfrac{K\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$
- ✅ Pole geometry depends on $\zeta$: imaginary, complex LHP, repeated real, distinct real
- ✅ Underdamped step response contains damped sine wave at $\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$
- ✅ **Four golden specifications** (in radians, with $\phi$ in rad):
   - Rise time $t_{r} = (\pi - \phi)/\omega_{d}$
   - Peak time $t_{p} = \pi/\omega_{d}$
   - Overshoot $M_{p} = 100 \cdot e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$
   - Settling time $t_{s} = 4/(\zeta\omega_{n})$ (2% criterion)
- ✅ Critical damping $\zeta = 1$ gives the **fastest non-oscillatory** response
- ✅ Frequency response resonance: $\omega_{r} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-2\zeta^{2}}$, peak $M_{r} = 1/(2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}})$ — valid for $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$

### C. Pulse Response (Superposition Rule)
- ✅ Any rectangular pulse of width $T_{p}$ is the **difference of two step inputs**: $u(t) - u(t - T_{p})$
- ✅ Response = $y_{\text{step}}(t) - y_{\text{step}}(t - T_{p})$ (by LTI property)

### D. Engineering Heuristics (Memorize!)
- ✅ "Settling rule": $4\tau$ for first order; $4/(\zeta\omega_{n})$ for second order
- ✅ "63.2% rule": $t = \tau$ gives $63.2\%$ of final value (first order)
- ✅ "Damping rule of thumb": $\zeta = 0.7$ is a common design target (balance between speed and overshoot)
- ✅ KTU board expects **radian units** for all angular frequencies and phase angles

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Systems with Distance-Velocity Lag
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT521 - MECHATRONICS AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Systems with Distance-Velocity Lag

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distance-Velocity Lag (D-V Lag) System:** A unity-feedback position control system in which the open-loop transfer function takes the canonical form
> $$G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(Ts+1)}$$
> where the **velocity lag** $1/(Ts+1)$ (a first-order lag produced by motor armature inertia and load friction) is preceded by an **integration element** $1/s$ that establishes the distance-to-velocity relationship between the commanded position (distance) and the actual motor shaft velocity.

The system derives its name from the two physically meaningful blocks appearing in cascade:

- **Distance Block** $\rightarrow$ the integrator $1/s$: relates the **distance** (position) to its rate of change, the **velocity**.
- **Velocity Block** $\rightarrow$ the first-order lag $1/(Ts+1)$: produces a lag in the **velocity** response because the motor cannot instantaneously accelerate its rotor-load inertia through armature inductance.

In broader KTU process-control terminology, a *distance-velocity lag* also denotes a plant described by the irrational transfer function

$$G(s) = \dfrac{e^{-\tau s}}{Ts+1}$$

where $e^{-\tau s}$ is a pure **distance (transportation) lag** of $\tau$ seconds and $1/(Ts+1)$ is the **velocity lag** of time constant $T$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Ship-Steering Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine a large cargo ship at sea. The captain issues a heading command (a **distance** — i.e., angular position). The ship's rudder can only move at a finite rate (**velocity**), so the heading change does not occur instantly — it builds up over time. At the same time, the rudder must first be mechanically repositioned from the bridge to the stern (a pure delay before any motion begins). The combination of "delayed start" + "gradual rate-limited approach" is exactly what a distance-velocity lag system models.

A **Mechatronics** equivalent is the **radar antenna position-control servomechanism**:

| Element | Physical Realization |
|---|---|
| Distance command | Desired antenna angle (radians) |
| Integrator $1/s$ | Mechanical shaft integrating angular velocity into angular position |
| Velocity lag $1/(Ts+1)$ | Motor armature dynamics (inductance + back-emf) |
| Output | Actual antenna angle read back by a position encoder |

## 1.3 Why the D-V Lag Matters in Mechatronics

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats this topic as a *bridge* between first-order analysis and full second-order design, because the closed-loop response contains **both an integrator and a first-order pole**. A KTU examiner therefore expects students to:

1. Identify the open-loop form from a given block diagram.
2. Derive the closed-loop characteristic equation.
3. Compute damping ratio, natural frequency, peak overshoot, peak time, and settling time.
4. Apply Routh–Hurwitz stability tests.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory anchor:** A distance-velocity lag system is *always* a **Type-1, second-order** closed-loop system after unity feedback. Remember the word **"two"**: two lag elements, two poles, two parameters ($K$ and $T$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the D-V Lag System

When the loop is closed with unity feedback ($H(s)=1$), the cascade structure resolves as follows:

### Step 1 — Cascade the two forward blocks

$$G(s) = \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{s}}_{\text{Distance block}} \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{Ts+1}}_{\text{Velocity block}}$$

### Step 2 — Insert gain $K$ (controller/amplifier)

$$G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(Ts+1)}$$

### Step 3 — Apply negative unity feedback

$$M(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)} = \dfrac{\dfrac{K}{s(Ts+1)}}{1+\dfrac{K}{s(Ts+1)}}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $s(Ts+1)$:

$$M(s) = \dfrac{K}{Ts^{2} + s + K}$$

## 2.2 Why Each Step Exists — Physical Reasoning

- **Why $1/s$ at all?** Because a mechatronic **position** output is the *time integral* of motor velocity. Integration in the Laplace domain is division by $s$.
- **Why $1/(Ts+1)$?** The motor armature cannot develop torque instantly — there is an electrical time constant $T = L_a/R_a$ (plus mechanical inertia), which the first-order lag represents.
- **Why gain $K$?** $K$ bundles the power amplifier gain, motor torque constant, and gear ratio. It is the *single* tunable parameter for shaping response.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities below assume a unity-feedback system with $G(s) = K/[s(Ts+1)]$.

| Quantity | Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Open-loop TF | $\dfrac{K}{s(Ts+1)}$ | Type-1 system |
| Closed-loop TF | $\dfrac{K/T}{s^{2}+s/T+K/T}$ | Standard 2nd-order form |
| Characteristic equation | $Ts^{2}+s+K = 0$ | Roots determine stability |
| Natural frequency $\omega_{n}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{T}}$ | rad/s |
| Damping ratio $\zeta$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{KT}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Damped frequency $\omega_{d}$ | $\omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$ | rad/s |
| Phase angle $\phi$ | $\cos^{-1}\zeta$ | radians |
| Peak overshoot $M_{p}$ | $\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\zeta\pi}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\right)\times 100\,\%$ | percent |
| Peak time $t_{p}$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{\omega_{d}}$ | seconds |
| Settling time $t_{s}\,(2\%)$ | $\dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$ | seconds |
| Settling time $t_{s}\,(5\%)$ | $\dfrac{3}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$ | seconds |
| Rise time $t_{r}$ | $\dfrac{\pi-\phi}{\omega_{d}}$ | seconds |
| Steady-state error (step) | $0$ | Type-1 system |
| Steady-state error (ramp) | $1/K$ | finite |
| Steady-state error (parabola) | $\infty$ | unstable tracking |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical takeaway:** As $K$ increases, $\omega_{n}$ increases (faster) **but** $\zeta$ decreases (more oscillatory). The designer must therefore balance *speed* against *damping*.

## 2.4 Boundary Conditions & Engineering Utility

- **Condition for oscillatory response:** $\zeta < 1 \;\Leftrightarrow\; K > \dfrac{1}{4T}$. For $K \le 1/(4T)$, the response is overdamped or critically damped.
- **Critically damped:** $K = \dfrac{1}{4T}$ giving $\zeta = 1$ and equal real roots $s_{1,2} = -\dfrac{1}{2T}$.
- **Real-world utility:** Robotics joint servo design, CNC machine tool slides, hard-disk head-positioning, CNC antenna pedestals, automatic pilots, and any mechatronic subsystem where a motor must drive an inertial load to a commanded position.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Closed-Loop Transfer Function

Starting point: open-loop transfer function with forward gain $K$.

$$
G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(Ts+1)}
$$

Apply negative unity feedback. The closed-loop transfer function is

$$
M(s) = \dfrac{G(s)}{1+G(s)} = \dfrac{\dfrac{K}{s(Ts+1)}}{1+\dfrac{K}{s(Ts+1)}}
$$

**Multiply numerator and denominator by** $s(Ts+1)$:

$$
M(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(Ts+1) + K}
$$

**Distribute** $s$ over $(Ts+1)$:

$$
M(s) = \dfrac{K}{Ts^{2}+s+K}
$$

**Divide numerator and denominator by $T$** so that the $s^{2}$ coefficient becomes unity (standard form):

$$
M(s) = \dfrac{K/T}{s^{2}+\dfrac{1}{T}s+\dfrac{K}{T}}
$$

**Compare with standard second-order denominator** $s^{2}+2\zeta\omega_{n}s+\omega_{n}^{2}$.

By equating coefficients:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{n}^{2} &= \dfrac{K}{T} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \omega_{n} = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{T}} \\
2\zeta\omega_{n} &= \dfrac{1}{T} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \zeta = \dfrac{1}{2T\omega_{n}} = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{KT}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Both derivations are now complete and ready for numerical substitution.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Unit-Step Time Response

The Laplace transform of a unit step input is $R(s) = 1/s$. Therefore the output is

$$
C(s) = M(s)\,R(s) = \dfrac{K/T}{s\!\left[s^{2}+\dfrac{1}{T}s+\dfrac{K}{T}\right]}
$$

For an **underdamped** system ($0<\zeta<1$, i.e., $K>1/(4T)$), the partial-fraction expansion followed by inverse Laplace yields

$$
c(t) = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\,e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t}\,\sin\!\left(\omega_{d}t + \phi\right)
$$

where

$$
\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}, \qquad \phi = \cos^{-1}\zeta
$$

**Verification that the steady-state value is unity:** as $t\to\infty$, the exponential term $\to 0$, leaving $c(\infty)=1$. ✔ This matches the known Type-1 behaviour (zero steady-state error to a step).

## 3.3 Derivation of Peak Overshoot $M_p$

Peak overshoot occurs at the first instant when $\dfrac{dc}{dt}=0$. Differentiating $c(t)$ and setting to zero produces the standard result that the first peak occurs at $t=t_{p}=\pi/\omega_{d}$. Substituting into $c(t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_p &= c(t_{p}) - 1 \\
&= -\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\,e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t_{p}}\sin\!\left(\omega_{d}t_{p}+\phi\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\omega_{d}t_{p} = \pi$ and $\sin(\pi+\phi) = -\sin\phi = -\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_p &= -\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\,e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}\pi/\omega_{d}}\!\left(-\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}\right) \\
&= e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

In percentage form:

$$
\boxed{\;M_{p}\,\% = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\times 100\;}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Peak Time $t_p$

$$
t_{p} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_{d}} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation of Settling Time $t_s$ (2% Criterion)

The envelope of the decaying oscillation is $\pm \dfrac{e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$. The 2% criterion demands this envelope to fall below $0.02$:

$$
\dfrac{e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t_{s}}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} = 0.02
$$

For practical $\zeta$ values ($0.4 \le \zeta \le 0.8$), the denominator is close to unity, giving the widely used approximation

$$
\boxed{\;t_{s} \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}} = 8T\;}
$$

(the final equality uses $\zeta\omega_{n}=1/(2T)$).

## 3.6 Stability Test — Routh–Hurwitz Array

The characteristic equation of the closed-loop system is

$$
Ts^{2} + s + K = 0
$$

Construct the Routh array:

| Row | Column 1 | Column 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^{2}$ | $T$ | $K$ |
| $s^{1}$ | $1$ | $0$ |
| $s^{0}$ | $K$ | 0 |

First-column entries: $T,\;1,\;K$.

For all entries to be strictly positive (necessary and sufficient for stability of a second-order polynomial with positive coefficients):

$$
T > 0, \qquad K > 0
$$

Both must hold simultaneously. **Conclusion:** the system is stable for every positive $K$ and $T$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU observation:** Although the closed-loop system is always stable, it can be *underdamped* (oscillatory) for $K > 1/(4T)$. This is *not* the same as instability — the oscillations simply decay.

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** For a D-V lag system with $T = 0.2$ s and $K = 10$, compute $\omega_{n},\;\zeta,\;M_{p},\;t_{p},\;t_{s}$.

**Step 1 — Natural frequency:**

$$
\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{T}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{10}{0.2}} = \sqrt{50} = 7.071\;\text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Damping ratio:**

$$
\zeta = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{KT}} = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{10 \times 0.2}} = \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{2}} = 0.3536
$$

**Step 3 — Peak overshoot:**

$$
M_{p}\,\% = e^{-0.3536\,\pi/\sqrt{1-0.1250}}\times 100 = e^{-1.2217}\times 100 \approx 29.5\,\%
$$

**Step 4 — Peak time:**

$$
\omega_{d} = 7.071\sqrt{1-0.1250} = 6.609\;\text{rad/s}
$$
$$
t_{p} = \dfrac{\pi}{6.609} \approx 0.475\;\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Settling time:**

$$
t_{s} = \dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}} = \dfrac{4}{0.3536 \times 7.071} \approx 1.6\;\text{s}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Diagram Equivalent

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the physical signal flow of a D-V lag position-control mechatronic system, showing how a position command propagates through the distance block, the velocity block, the motor, and back through the position sensor.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["R(s) Position Command"] --> nodeSumming(("Sum Junction"))
    nodeSumming -- "Error E(s)" --> nodeK["Amplifier Gain K"]
    nodeK --> nodeInt["Integrator 1 over s - Distance Block"]
    nodeInt --> nodeFOL["First Order Lag 1 over Ts+1 - Velocity Block"]
    nodeFOL --> nodeM["Motor and Load - Output Angle Theta s"]
    nodeM --> nodeH["Position Sensor H s = 1"]
    nodeH -- "Feedback" --> nodeSumming
    nodeM -. "Measured Position" .-> nodeOut["Output C s = Theta s"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputStage["Input Stage"]
        A1["Desired Position Reference in radians"]
    end

    subgraph ErrorStage["Error Detection"]
        B1["Comparator"]
        B2["Error Signal E s = R s minus C s"]
    end

    subgraph ControllerStage["Controller and Power Amplifier"]
        C1["Proportional Gain K"]
    end

    subgraph PlantStage["Plant with D-V Lag"]
        D1["Integrator 1 over s"]
        D2["Velocity Lag 1 over Ts+1"]
        D3["Motor Shaft Torque and Inertia"]
    end

    subgraph FeedbackStage["Feedback Path"]
        E1["Position Encoder"]
        E2["Unity Feedback H s = 1"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    E2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    D3 --> F1["Output Angular Position Theta t"]

    style InputStage fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1
    style PlantStage fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style FeedbackStage fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
```

## 4.3 Step-Response Sketch (Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Typical Step Response of D-V Lag System - Underdamped"
    x-axis "Time in seconds" [0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25, 1.5, 1.75, 2]
    y-axis "Output c(t)" 0 --> 1.5
    line [0, 0.78, 1.18, 1.05, 0.96, 1.02, 0.99, 1.00, 1.00]
```

> [!TIP]
> The curve above shows overshoot ($M_p \approx 29.5\%$), peak at $t_p \approx 0.475$ s, and settles near $t_s \approx 1.6$ s — matching the worked example in Section 3.7.

## 4.4 Pole-Zero Map of the Closed Loop

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Closed Loop Pole Locations on s-Plane"
    x-axis "Real Axis" [-1.5, -1, -0.5, 0]
    y-axis "Imaginary Axis" [-3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3]
    line [-2.5, 0, 2.5]
    line [0, 0, 0]
```

The two closed-loop poles are complex conjugates $s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_{n}\pm j\omega_{d}$ that always lie in the **left half-plane** for $K>0$, $T>0$, confirming absolute stability.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a system with distance-velocity lag. Give one mechatronics example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A distance-velocity lag system is a position-control servomechanism whose open-loop transfer function has the canonical form $G(s) = K/[s(Ts+1)]$. The $1/s$ block represents the **distance** (position) integrator that converts velocity into position, while the $1/(Ts+1)$ block represents the **velocity lag** arising from motor armature inductance and load inertia. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for explaining each block, 1 mark for example.)*

**Mechatronics example:** A radar antenna position-control servomechanism in which the angular command is integrated to give a velocity command, which then drives a DC motor with electrical time constant $T$. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**For the system $G(s)=K/[s(Ts+1)]$ with unity feedback, derive the expressions for natural frequency and damping ratio.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Closed-loop characteristic equation: $Ts^{2}+s+K=0$. Dividing by $T$:
$$s^{2}+\dfrac{1}{T}s+\dfrac{K}{T}=0$$
Comparing with the standard form $s^{2}+2\zeta\omega_{n}s+\omega_{n}^{2}=0$:
$$\omega_{n}=\sqrt{K/T}\quad\text{and}\quad\zeta=\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{KT}}$$
*(1 mark each for $\omega_{n}$ and $\zeta$, 1 mark for the comparison step.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — read carefully.**
> 1. Students frequently *forget to divide by $T$* before comparing with the standard second-order form, producing a wrong $\omega_{n}$.
> 2. Many candidates compute $M_p$ but forget to convert to **percentage**.
> 3. Some answers omit the **Routh–Hurwitz** check; KTU examiners routinely deduct 2 marks for this.
> 4. Hand-writing the phase angle $\phi=\cos^{-1}\zeta$ as $\sin^{-1}\zeta$ is a common slip; check the quadrant.

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — 14 Marks

For a unity-feedback position control system with open-loop transfer function
$$G(s)=\dfrac{10}{s(0.1s+1)}$$

#### (a) Determine the closed-loop transfer function and identify the natural frequency $\omega_{n}$, damping ratio $\zeta$, and damped natural frequency $\omega_{d}$. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify $K$ and $T$** *(1 mark)*:
Comparing $G(s)=10/[s(0.1s+1)]$ with $K/[s(Ts+1)]$:
$$K = 10, \qquad T = 0.1\;\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Closed-loop transfer function** *(2 marks)*:
$$M(s)=\dfrac{10}{0.1\,s^{2}+s+10}=\dfrac{100}{s^{2}+10s+100}$$

**Step 3 — Standard-form conversion** *(2 marks)*:
Comparing $s^{2}+10s+100$ with $s^{2}+2\zeta\omega_{n}s+\omega_{n}^{2}$:
$$\omega_{n}^{2}=100 \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{n}=10\;\text{rad/s}$$
$$2\zeta\omega_{n}=10 \;\Rightarrow\; \zeta=0.5$$

**Step 4 — Damped natural frequency** *(2 marks)*:
$$\omega_{d}=\omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}=10\sqrt{1-0.25}=10\sqrt{0.75}=8.66\;\text{rad/s}$$

#### (b) Compute the peak overshoot $M_p$, peak time $t_p$, and 2% settling time $t_s$ for a unit step input. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Peak overshoot** *(2 marks)*:
$$M_{p}\,\% = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\times 100 = e^{-0.5\pi/\sqrt{0.75}}\times 100 \approx 16.3\,\%$$

**Step 2 — Peak time** *(2 marks)*:
$$t_{p}=\dfrac{\pi}{\omega_{d}}=\dfrac{\pi}{8.66}\approx 0.363\;\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Settling time** *(2 marks)*:
$$t_{s}=\dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}}=\dfrac{4}{0.5\times 10}=0.8\;\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state error for unit step** *(1 mark)*:
Because the system is Type-1: $e_{ss}=0$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — 14 Marks

A mechatronic position servo has the open-loop transfer function
$$G(s)=\dfrac{K}{s(0.05s+1)}$$

#### (a) Using the Routh–Hurwitz criterion, find the range of $K$ for stability. Comment on the nature of the response for $K>0$. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Characteristic equation** *(2 marks)*:
$$0.05\,s^{2}+s+K=0$$

**Step 2 — Routh array** *(3 marks)*:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^{2}$ | $0.05$ | $K$ |
| $s^{1}$ | $1$ | $0$ |
| $s^{0}$ | $K$ |  |

**Step 3 — Stability condition** *(2 marks)*:
All first-column entries must be positive:
$$0.05>0,\;\;1>0,\;\;K>0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{K>0}$$

Since $K>0$ is the only condition, **the system is stable for every positive $K$**. The response becomes oscillatory (underdamped) when $K>1/(4T)=1/(4\times 0.05)=5$, and overdamped for $0<K<5$.

#### (b) If $K=20$, design the value of $K$ for critically damped response. Also compute $\omega_{n}$ and the steady-state error for a unit-ramp input. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Critical damping condition** *(2 marks)*:
$$\zeta=1\;\Rightarrow\;\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{KT}}=1\;\Rightarrow\;K=\dfrac{1}{4T}=\dfrac{1}{4\times 0.05}=5$$

**Step 2 — Natural frequency at $K=20$** *(2 marks)*:
$$\omega_{n}=\sqrt{K/T}=\sqrt{20/0.05}=\sqrt{400}=20\;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Damping ratio at $K=20$** *(1 mark)*:
$$\zeta=\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{20\times 0.05}}=\dfrac{1}{2}=0.5\;\text{(underdamped)}$$

**Step 4 — Ramp steady-state error** *(2 marks)*:
$$K_{v}=\lim_{s\to 0}sG(s)=K=20$$
$$e_{ss}(\text{ramp})=\dfrac{1}{K_{v}}=\dfrac{1}{20}=0.05$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — print this before the exam.**

- **Canonical form:** $G(s) = K/[s(Ts+1)]$ — always write it this way first.
- **Closed-loop characteristic equation:** $Ts^{2}+s+K=0$ — *do not skip the $T$ in front of $s^{2}$*.
- **Two key parameters:**
  - $\omega_{n}=\sqrt{K/T}$
  - $\zeta=\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{KT}}$
- **Stability rule:** $K>0$ and $T>0$ ⇒ always stable (no instability region for a 2nd-order Type-1 plant).
- **Oscillation onset:** response becomes oscillatory when $\zeta<1$, i.e., $K>1/(4T)$.
- **Critical damping:** $K_{c}=1/(4T)$.
- **Time-domain metrics (memorize):**
  - $t_{p}=\pi/\omega_{d}$
  - $M_{p}\%=e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\times 100$
  - $t_{s}\approx 4/(\zeta\omega_{n})$
- **Steady-state errors (Type-1 system):** $e_{ss}(\text{step})=0$, $e_{ss}(\text{ramp})=1/K$, $e_{ss}(\text{parabola})=\infty$.
- **Design trade-off:** increasing $K$ ⇒ faster (larger $\omega_{n}$) **but** more oscillatory (smaller $\zeta$).
- **Physical meaning:** the $1/s$ block is the **distance** (position) integrator; the $1/(Ts+1)$ block is the **velocity** lag from motor dynamics.
- **Common unit:** $\omega_{n}$ and $\omega_{d}$ in **rad/s**; $t_{p}, t_{s}, t_{r}$ in **seconds**; $M_{p}$ dimensionless or in **%**.
- **Pitfall trap:** $M_{p}$ on its own is a ratio — multiply by 100 only when the question asks for percentage overshoot.
- **Alternative D-V lag form (process control):** $G(s)=e^{-\tau s}/(Ts+1)$ — pure delay $\tau$ in series with first-order lag $T$.
- **KTU favourite sub-topics:** Routh–Hurwitz table formation, derivation of $\zeta$ and $\omega_{n}$, numerical computation of $M_p, t_p, t_s$, ramp-error formula.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute mnemonic:** "*Distance divides by s, Velocity lags by T, Gain K ties them all together.*"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
