# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

> **Define polar plot. How does it differ from a Nyquist plot?**

**Model Answer:**

A **polar plot** is the locus of the tip of the phasor $G(j\omega)$ in the complex G-plane as frequency $\omega$ is varied from $0$ to $+\infty$, with magnitude $\vert G(j\omega)\vert$ as the radial distance and phase $\angle G(j\omega)$ as the angular displacement from the positive real axis.

A **Nyquist plot** is the **complete closed contour** of $G(j\omega)$ for $\omega : -\infty$ to $+\infty$ traversed as a closed path in the s-plane ($\omega$ increasing). The Nyquist plot is the **mirror image** (conjugate-symmetric about the real axis) of the polar plot combined with its negative-frequency counterpart, completing a closed contour for use in the **Nyquist stability criterion**.

**Key Distinction:** Polar plot is $\omega : 0 \to +\infty$ (open contour). Nyquist plot is $\omega : -\infty \to +\infty$ (closed contour).

---

### Question A.2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

> **List any three methods of experimentally determining the frequency response of a control system.**

**Model Answer:**

The three primary experimental methods are:

1. **Sinusoidal Signal Generator Method (Steady-State Method):** A sinusoidal input of variable frequency is applied; the steady-state output amplitude and phase shift are measured to compute $\vert G(j\omega)\vert$ and $\angle G(j\omega)$.

2. **Transient Response Method (Using Fourier Analysis):** The system is excited with a non-periodic test signal (e.g., step or pulse), and the output is decomposed into frequency components via **Fourier Transform** to obtain the frequency response.

3. **Correlation Method (White Noise Input):** A pseudo-random binary sequence (PRBS) or white noise is applied as input; the input-output cross-correlation and input auto-correlation yield the impulse response, whose Fourier transform gives $G(j\omega)$.

(Additional valid method: **Spectrum Analyzer Method** — directly measure Bode plot using FFT of noise-driven output.)

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** Sketch the polar plot of the transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(1 + 0.5s)(1 + 2s)}$ for $\omega : 0 \to \infty$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** From the polar plot, determine the **gain crossover frequency**, **phase crossover frequency**, **gain margin**, and **phase margin**. Comment on stability. **(7 marks)**

---

### 📗 Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Polar Plot Sketch (7 marks)

**Step 1: Substitute $s = j\omega$**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{j\omega(1 + 0.5j\omega)(1 + 2j\omega)}$$

**Step 2: Expand the denominator**

$$(1 + 0.5j\omega)(1 + 2j\omega) = 1 + 2j\omega + 0.5j\omega + j^2 \omega^2 = (1 - \omega^2) + j(2.5\omega)$$

$$j\omega \cdot \bigl[(1 - \omega^2) + j(2.5\omega)\bigr] = j\omega(1 - \omega^2) + j^2 (2.5\omega^2) = -2.5\omega^2 + j\omega(1 - \omega^2)$$

Therefore:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10}{-2.5\omega^2 + j\omega(1 - \omega^2)}$$

**Step 3: Rationalize by multiplying numerator and denominator by conjugate**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10 \cdot \bigl[-2.5\omega^2 - j\omega(1 - \omega^2)\bigr]}{(-2.5\omega^2)^2 + \omega^2(1 - \omega^2)^2}$$

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{10 \cdot \bigl[-2.5\omega^2 - j\omega(1 - \omega^2)\bigr]}{6.25\omega^4 + \omega^2(1 - \omega^2)^2}$$

**Step 4: Separate real and imaginary parts**

$$X(\omega) = \frac{-25\omega^2}{6.25\omega^4 + \omega^2(1 - \omega^2)^2} = \frac{-25}{6.25\omega^2 + (1 - \omega^2)^2}$$

$$Y(\omega) = \frac{-10\omega(1 - \omega^2)}{6.25\omega^4 + \omega^2(1 - \omega^2)^2} = \frac{-10(1 - \omega^2)}{6.25\omega^3 + \omega(1 - \omega^2)^2}$$

**Step 5: Crossing Real Axis** ($Y(\omega) = 0$):

$$1 - \omega^2 = 0 \implies \omega = 1 \text{ rad/s}$$

At $\omega = 1$: $X(1) = \dfrac{-25}{6.25 + 0} = \dfrac{-25}{6.25} = -4$

**Crossing point on negative real axis: $(-4, 0)$**

**Step 6: Crossing Imaginary Axis** ($X(\omega) = 0$):

$X(\omega) = -25 / [\text{denominator}]$, never zero for finite $\omega > 0$ (numerator is constant $-25$).

Therefore, the plot does **not** cross the imaginary axis in the conventional sense.

**Step 7: Endpoint Behaviour**

- At $\omega \to 0$: $X(\omega) \to -25/1 = -25$, $Y(\omega) \to -10/0 \to -\infty$. The plot starts at infinity along the negative imaginary direction.
- At $\omega \to \infty$: Both $X, Y \to 0$. Phase $\to -90^\circ - 90^\circ - 90^\circ = -270^\circ$.

**Step 8: Tabulate values**

| $\omega$ (rad/s) | $X(\omega)$ | $Y(\omega)$ | $\vert G \vert$ | $\angle G$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0.1 | $-24.94$ | $-99.70$ | $102.78$ | $-104.1^\circ$ |
| 0.5 | $-22.73$ | $-18.18$ | $29.10$ | $-141.3^\circ$ |
| 1.0 | $-4.00$ | $0.00$ | $4.00$ | $-180^\circ$ |
| 2.0 | $-0.8929$ | $0.9524$ | $1.305$ | $-133.1^\circ$ |
| 5.0 | $-0.0573$ | $0.1781$ | $0.187$ | $-107.8^\circ$ |
| 10.0 | $-0.00797$ | $0.03984$ | $0.0406$ | $-101.3^\circ$ |
| $\infty$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $-270^\circ$ |

**Step 9: Sketch** — Type-1 system; starts at $\infty$ along $-90^\circ$ direction, crosses negative real axis at $-4$, loops into 2nd quadrant, then into 1st quadrant, spirals into origin at angle $-270^\circ$.

#### Part (b) — Margins and Stability (7 marks)

**Phase Crossover Frequency $\omega_{pc}$:** Where plot crosses negative real axis:

$$\omega_{pc} = 1 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \vert G(j1)\vert = 4$$

**Gain Margin:**

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})\vert} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

In dB: $GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(0.25) = -12.04$ dB

**Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_{gc}$:** Where $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})\vert = 1$

From the table, between $\omega = 2$ and $\omega = 5$; closer to $\omega \approx 2.2$ rad/s. At $\omega_{gc} \approx 2.2$ rad/s, $\angle G \approx -130^\circ$.

**Phase Margin:**

$$PM = 180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc}) = 180^\circ - 130^\circ = 50^\circ$$

**Stability Verdict:**
- $GM = 0.25 < 1$ (in linear), or $-12$ dB (negative in dB) — **GM is negative → UNSTABLE**.
- $PM = 50^\circ > 0$ — alone this says stable, but GM is decisive here.

**Conclusion:** Since gain margin is **less than 1** (i.e., $GM_{dB} < 0$ dB), the closed-loop system is **unstable** for $K = 10$. To stabilize, the gain must be reduced such that the magnitude at $\omega_{pc} = 1$ rad/s becomes $\le 1$.

---

### 🔶 Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

> **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **experimental procedure** for determining the frequency response of a system. List the instruments used. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The frequency response data of a system measured experimentally is tabulated below. Plot the **polar plot** and determine whether the closed-loop system with unity feedback is stable. **(7 marks)**

| $\omega$ (rad/s) | $\vert G(j\omega)\vert$ | $\angle G(j\omega)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0.1 | 10.00 | $-1.8^\circ$ |
| 1.0 | 7.07 | $-45.0^\circ$ |
| 2.0 | 4.47 | $-63.4^\circ$ |
| 5.0 | 1.00 | $-150.0^\circ$ |
| 10.0 | 0.10 | $-240.0^\circ$ |
| 20.0 | 0.02 | $-258.0^\circ$ |

---

### 📘 Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Experimental Procedure (7 marks)

**Block Diagram:**

```
Sinusoidal Generator → [System Under Test] → Output Sensor → Oscilloscope
        ↓                                                       ↓
   Adjust ω                                              Measure A_out, Δt
        ↓                                                       ↓
        └──────────────→ Plot on Polar/Bode Graph ←──────────────┘
```

**Detailed Procedure:**

1. **Setup:** Connect a sinusoidal function generator to the input of the system under test (SUT). Connect the output to an oscilloscope or data acquisition system.

2. **Choose a frequency** $\omega_1$ (lowest in the range of interest, e.g., 0.1 rad/s). Set the input amplitude $A_{in}$ to a convenient value (e.g., 1 V).

3. **Wait for steady state** — allow transients to decay (typically 4–5 time constants).

4. **Measure output amplitude** $A_{out}$ from the oscilloscope trace. Measure the **time delay** $\Delta t$ between a positive-going zero-crossing of the input and the corresponding zero-crossing of the output.

5. **Compute:**

$$\vert G(j\omega_1)\vert = \frac{A_{out}}{A_{in}}, \quad \phi(\omega_1) = -\frac{\Delta t}{T} \times 360^\circ$$

where $T = 2\pi / \omega_1$ is the period.

6. **Increment $\omega$** to $\omega_2 > \omega_1$ and repeat steps 3–5. Continue until the magnitude drops to a negligible value (typically $\le 0.05$ times the low-frequency magnitude).

7. **Plot** the points $(\vert G \vert, \phi)$ on polar graph paper. The smooth curve through these points is the **polar plot**.

8. **Read off** the gain crossover (where plot crosses unit circle) and phase crossover (where plot crosses negative real axis) to compute **GM** and **PM**.

**Instruments Used:**
- Audio-frequency (AF) sinusoidal signal generator
- Dual-trace oscilloscope (or phase meter)
- Polar graph paper (or computer-based plotting)
- Optional: spectrum analyzer, lock-in amplifier for low SNR systems

**Precautions:**
- Keep input amplitude small enough to remain in the linear operating regime.
- Account for any instrumentation delays (calibrate with direct connection first).
- Use sufficient frequency resolution near resonance peaks.

#### Part (b) — Polar Plot and Stability (7 marks)

**Tabulate Cartesian Coordinates:**

| $\omega$ | $\vert G \vert$ | $\phi$ | $X = \vert G \vert \cos\phi$ | $Y = \vert G \vert \sin\phi$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0.1 | 10.00 | $-1.8^\circ$ | $9.995$ | $-0.314$ |
| 1.0 | 7.07 | $-45.0^\circ$ | $5.000$ | $-5.000$ |
| 2.0 | 4.47 | $-63.4^\circ$ | $2.000$ | $-4.000$ |
| 5.0 | 1.00 | $-150.0^\circ$ | $-0.866$ | $-0.500$ |
| 10.0 | 0.10 | $-240.0^\circ$ | $-0.050$ | $0.0866$ |
| 20.0 | 0.02 | $-258.0^\circ$ | $-0.00416$ | $0.01963$ |

**Phase Crossover:** Plot crosses negative real axis near $\omega = 5$ rad/s (where $\phi \approx -180^\circ$, $X \approx -0.866$).

$$\omega_{pc} \approx 5 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \vert G(j\omega_{pc})\vert \approx 1.00$$

**Gain Margin:**

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})\vert} = \frac{1}{1.00} = 1.0 \quad (= 0 \text{ dB})$$

Since the magnitude exactly equals 1 at the phase crossover, the system is **marginally stable** — any slight increase in gain pushes the plot to **enclose** the critical point $(-1, j0)$, making the system **unstable**.

**Gain Crossover:** Where plot crosses unit circle ($\vert G\vert = 1$). Between $\omega = 2$ and $\omega = 5$; from the table, $\vert G\vert = 1$ at $\omega = 5$ (also $\phi = -150^\circ$).

$$\omega_{gc} = 5 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \phi(\omega_{gc}) = -150^\circ$$

**Phase Margin:**

$$PM = 180^\circ + \phi(\omega_{gc}) = 180^\circ - 150^\circ = 30^\circ$$

**Stability Conclusion:**
- $GM = 1$ (exactly 0 dB) → **Marginally stable**.
- $PM = 30^\circ > 0$ → **Positive phase margin**.

The system is on the **borderline of stability**. For practical KTU board exam interpretation: the system is **marginally stable / conditionally stable**, and the critical point $(-1, j0)$ lies **exactly on the polar plot**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing Polar Plot with Nyquist Plot:** Students often write "Nyquist plot" when only the $\omega : 0 \to +\infty$ locus is asked. The polar plot is **half** the Nyquist plot. **Penalty: −1 to −2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Sign of Phase Shift:** Phase lag (negative angle) vs phase lead (positive angle). For minimum-phase systems, the phase is always negative. A common mistake is writing $\phi = +45^\circ$ instead of $-45^\circ$. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Wrong Axis Crossing Identification:** When asked to find the frequency at which the plot crosses the negative real axis, students often confuse it with the imaginary axis crossing. Always solve $Y(\omega) = 0$ for real-axis crossing. **Penalty: −1 to −2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Forgetting Magnitude Ratio Formula:** In experimental determination, the magnitude ratio is $A_{out}/A_{in}$, NOT $A_{in}/A_{out}$. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Skipping Justification of Stability:** A common error is just stating "system is stable" without computing GM and PM numerically. Always show the calculation: $GM$ in dB and $PM$ in degrees. **Penalty: −2 marks.**
>
> 6. **Not Labelling $\omega_{pc}$ and $\omega_{gc}$:** Even when GM and PM are computed, students forget to mention the corresponding crossover frequencies. Examiners look for this. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
>
> 7. **Wrong Starting Point for Type-1/Type-2 Systems:** Writing "starts at $(K, 0)$" for a Type-1 system is a critical error. Always write "starts at infinity along $-90^\circ$ direction" for Type-1. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
>
> 8. **Phase Wrap-around Errors:** In experimental data, the phase may exceed $-180^\circ$ (e.g., $-240^\circ$). Always normalize to the equivalent angle in $(-180^\circ, +180^\circ]$. **Penalty: −1 mark.**

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Board Exam Final Hour**

- [ ] **Polar plot** = locus of $G(j\omega)$ in G-plane for $\omega : 0 \to +\infty$.
- [ ] **Nyquist plot** = closed contour for $\omega : -\infty \to +\infty$.
- [ ] **Type-0** systems start on **positive real axis at $K$**.
- [ ] **Type-1** systems start at **infinity along $-90^\circ$**.
- [ ] **Type-2** systems start at **infinity along $-180^\circ$**.
- [ ] All plots **end at the origin** at an angle of $-90^\circ (n - m)$.
- [ ] For $G(s) = K/(1 + sT)$: plot is a **semicircle of radius $K/2$** centred at $(K/2, 0)$ in the **fourth quadrant**.
- [ ] For Type-0 2nd order: plot **crosses imaginary axis** at some finite frequency.
- [ ] **GM** = $1/\vert G(j\omega_{pc})\vert$ where $\omega_{pc}$ = phase crossover ($-180^\circ$ crossing).
- [ ] **PM** = $180^\circ + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})$ where $\omega_{gc}$ = gain crossover ($\vert G\vert = 1$).
- [ ] **GM > 1 (GM in dB > 0)** AND **PM > 0** ⇒ **Stable**.
- [ ] **Experimental formula:** $\vert G(j\omega)\vert = A_{out}/A_{in}$, $\phi = -\omega \Delta t$ (radians) $= -(\Delta t / T) \cdot 360^\circ$.
- [ ] **Instruments:** function generator, oscilloscope/phase meter, polar graph paper.
- [ ] **Precautions:** linearity, instrumentation delay calibration, sufficient frequency resolution.
- [ ] **Conversion:** $\phi$ in radians $= \phi$ in degrees $\times \pi/180$.
- [ ] **Test data should span** at least 2 decades of frequency around $\omega_{gc}$.
- [ ] **Draw arrowheads** on the polar plot indicating direction of increasing $\omega$.
- [ ] For KTU exam, **always state** system type, starting point, ending angle, GM, PM, and stability verdict.
- [ ] **For closed-loop stability from open-loop polar plot:** the critical point $(-1, j0)$ must NOT be encircled in the Nyquist contour in a way that yields $N = -P/2$ (encirclements must equal poles of $G(s)$ in RHP for instability).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bode Plot and Nyquist Stability Criteria: Gain and Phase Margin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT521 - MECHATRONICS AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bode Plot and Nyquist Stability Criteria: Gain and Phase Margin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Bode Plot — Formal Definition

A **Bode Plot** is a graphical frequency-response representation of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system, consisting of two semi-logarithmic plots plotted on a common frequency axis:

1. **Magnitude Plot:** $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert$ vs. $\log_{10}(\omega)$
2. **Phase Plot:** $\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ (in degrees) vs. $\log_{10}(\omega)$

The frequency axis uses a **logarithmic scale** (decades), while the magnitude axis is in **decibels (dB)** and the phase axis is linear in degrees. This dual representation was developed by **Hendrik Wade Bode** at Bell Labs in the 1930s and is a cornerstone of classical control theory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The Bode plot is the *primary* tool for assessing **relative stability** of a closed-loop control system through **Gain Margin (GM)** and **Phase Margin (PM)**.

## 1.2 The Nyquist Stability Criterion — Formal Definition

The **Nyquist Stability Criterion**, developed by **Harry Nyquist** (1932), is a frequency-domain test that determines the stability of a closed-loop system by analyzing the encirclements of the critical point $(-1, j0)$ in the complex plane by the **Nyquist plot** of the open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s)$.

$$Z = N + P$$

Where:
- $Z$ = number of zeros of $1 + G(s)H(s)$ in the Right Half Plane (RHP) — i.e., closed-loop unstable poles
- $N$ = number of clockwise encirclements of the $-1 + j0$ point
- $P$ = number of open-loop poles of $G(s)H(s)$ in the RHP

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Condition:** A closed-loop system is **stable** if and only if $Z = 0$, which means $N = -P$. If $P = 0$ (open-loop stable), then $N = 0$ is required (no encirclements).

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

### 🛹 Bode Plot = Surfing the Frequency Waves

Imagine a control system as a **gymnast balancing on a balance beam**. The Bode plot is like an X-ray of her balance at *every* possible wobble speed:

- **Magnitude plot** → "How loudly does the system amplify the wobble at each speed?" (High dB = dangerous amplification)
- **Phase plot** → "How much *delay* does the system introduce?" (180° delay = the system is pushing back exactly opposite to the correction → instability!)

### 🌀 Nyquist = The Clockwise-Only Maze

Think of the Nyquist plot as a **hiking trail map** drawn in the $G(s)H(s)$ plane. The critical point $(-1, 0)$ is a **dangerous cliff**. As the frequency $\omega$ sweeps from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$, the contour traces a path. If this path **encircles the cliff in a clockwise direction** an unequal number of times to the unstable open-loop poles, the system **falls off** (becomes unstable). Stability = the trail neatly avoids the cliff.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics in Bode

| Metric | Definition | Typical Desired Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Gain Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{gc}$)** | Frequency where $\vert GH \vert = 1$ (0 dB) | — |
| **Phase Crossover Frequency ($\omega_{pc}$)** | Frequency where $\angle GH = -180°$ | — |
| **Gain Margin (GM)** | Amount of gain increase before instability | $6$ to $20$ dB |
| **Phase Margin (PM)** | Amount of additional phase lag before instability | $30°$ to $60°$ |
| **Bandwidth ($\omega_B$)** | Frequency where magnitude drops to $-3$ dB | Should be high |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both **GM** and **PM** are measures of **relative stability** (how stable the system *feels*), not absolute stability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Bode Plot

The Bode plot is built on the principle that any rational transfer function $G(s)H(s)$ can be factored into a product of four canonical terms:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K \cdot \prod (1 + \tau_i s) \cdot \prod (1 + 2\zeta_j s/\omega_{nj} + s^2/\omega_{nj}^2)}{s^N \cdot \prod (1 + T_k s) \cdot \prod (1 + 2\zeta_m s/\omega_{nm} + s^2/\omega_{nm}^2)}$$

### 2.1.1 Bode Plot Construction — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1: Convert to Bode Form (Time Constant Form)**
- Factor out constants so each pole/zero is of the form $(1 + s/\omega_c)$ where $\omega_c$ is the **corner frequency**.
- Pure integrator $1/s$ has corner at origin.

**Step 2: Identify Corner Frequencies**
- Simple pole at $-a$: corner at $\omega = a$
- Simple zero at $-a$: corner at $\omega = a$
- Quadratic terms: corner at $\omega_n$

**Step 3: Plot Asymptotic Magnitude**
- For each term, the contribution at $\omega$ is:
  - Pole at $\omega_c$: contributes $-20$ dB/dec slope *above* $\omega_c$
  - Zero at $\omega_c$: contributes $+20$ dB/dec slope *above* $\omega_c$
  - Integrator $(1/s)$: contributes $-20$ dB/dec slope from $\omega = 0$
  - Differentiator $(s)$: contributes $+20$ dB/dec slope from $\omega = 0$

**Step 4: Plot Asymptotic Phase**
- Pole: contributes $0°$ below $\omega_c$, $-90°$ above $\omega_c$, with a smooth transition centered at $\omega_c$
- Zero: contributes $0°$ below $\omega_c$, $+90°$ above $\omega_c$
- Integrator: constant $-90°$

**Step 5: Add Corrections**
- Actual magnitude has a $+3$ dB peak at the corner of a simple pole; exact phase at $\omega_c$ is $-45°$.
- For quadratic terms, a resonant peak occurs based on damping ratio $\zeta$.

## 2.2 Nyquist Criterion — Deeper Theory

### 2.2.1 Cauchy's Principle of Argument

The foundation of the Nyquist criterion is the **Cauchy's Argument Principle** from complex analysis:

$$N = Z - P$$

where $N$ is the number of counter-clockwise encirclements of the origin by $F(s) = 1 + G(s)H(s)$ as $s$ traverses the Nyquist contour $\mathcal{C}$ (RHP boundary).

Equivalently, plotting $G(s)H(s)$ directly and counting encirclements of the $-1 + j0$ point:

$$Z = N + P$$

- **Clockwise encirclements** are **positive**.
- **Counter-clockwise encirclements** are **negative**.

### 2.2.2 The Nyquist Contour $\mathcal{C}$

The Nyquist contour encloses the entire right-half plane:
1. Imaginary axis from $-j\infty$ to $+j\infty$
2. Infinitely large semicircle in the RHP from $+j\infty$ through $+\infty$ back to $-j\infty$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Table for KTU University Exam** — Memorize all entries below.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Special Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Magnitude (dB) | $\vert G \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert G \vert$ | $20 \log_{10}(10) = 20$ dB |
| 2 | Phase (degrees) | $\angle G = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{Im}(G)}{\text{Re}(G)}\right)$ | In radians for math; degrees for plot |
| 3 | Gain Crossover Freq. | $\omega_{gc} : \vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$ | 0 dB point |
| 4 | Phase Crossover Freq. | $\omega_{pc} : \angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°$ | Used for GM |
| 5 | **Gain Margin (linear)** | $GM = \dfrac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$ | Reciprocal of magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$ |
| 6 | **Gain Margin (dB)** | $GM_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(GM) = -20 \log_{10} \vert GH \vert_{@\omega_{pc}}$ | **Negative sign is critical** |
| 7 | **Phase Margin** | $PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$ | In degrees |
| 8 | Nyquist Criterion | $Z = N + P$ | $Z = 0$ for stability |
| 9 | Stable GM (dB) | $GM_{dB} > 0$ | Positive margin ⇒ stable |
| 10 | Stable PM | $PM > 0°$ | Positive margin ⇒ stable |
| 11 | Slope of $-20$N dB/dec | Each pole adds $-20$ dB/dec above corner | Each zero adds $+20$ dB/dec |
| 12 | Phase of $(1+j\omega T)$ pole | $-\tan^{-1}(\omega T)$ | $-45°$ at $\omega = 1/T$ |
| 13 | Phase of $(1+j\omega T)$ zero | $+\tan^{-1}(\omega T)$ | $+45°$ at $\omega = 1/T$ |
| 14 | Min. Phase System | All poles & zeros in LHP / on $j\omega$ | Stable GM $\Leftrightarrow$ Stable PM |
| 15 | Type Number $N$ | Slope at low $\omega$ = $-20N$ dB/dec | Equals pole count at origin |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Where Bode/Nyquist Is Used |
|---|---|
| **Aircraft Autopilots** | Designing phase margin to handle atmospheric disturbances safely |
| **Robotic Arms** | Ensuring position-control loops don't oscillate at high speeds |
| **Power Grid Stabilizers** | Preventing low-frequency electromechanical oscillations |
| **Audio Amplifiers** | Maintaining flat magnitude response across $20$ Hz – $20$ kHz |
| **Servo Motor Drives** | Tuning PID controllers to achieve $45°$–$60°$ phase margin |
| **Op-Amp Compensation** | Selecting feedback components for stable closed-loop operation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule of Thumb:** A well-designed control system should have $\mathbf{PM \geq 45°}$ and $\mathbf{GM \geq 6 \text{ dB}}$. These values give a good trade-off between **speed** (high bandwidth) and **robustness** (low overshoot).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Gain Margin and Phase Margin

### 3.1.1 Setup — Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation

For a unity negative-feedback system, the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$\frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

The characteristic equation is:

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$$

This has the same form as $F(s) = 0$. Stability requires all roots to lie in the **Left Half Plane (LHP)**.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Gain Margin

**Starting Point:** At the phase crossover frequency $\omega_{pc}$:

$$\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°$$

This means the open-loop transfer function at $\omega_{pc}$ is a **purely negative real number**:

$$G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert$$

**Substitute into the characteristic equation:**

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 1 - \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = 0$$

This gives the critical gain:

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = 1$$

**The gain margin** is the factor by which the current gain $K$ can be multiplied before the system hits this critical condition:

$$K \cdot \vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; K_{critical} = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}}$$

In decibels (and using $20\log_{10}$):

$$\boxed{GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(GM) = -20\log_{10}\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert}$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Phase Margin

**Starting Point:** At the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$:

$$\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1 \;\;\text{(i.e., 0 dB)}$$

The system is critically stable when the phase lag reaches $-180°$ at this same frequency. The phase margin is the *deficit* of phase lag from $-180°$:

$$PM = -180° - \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

Multiplying by $-1$ on both sides to match KTU convention:

$$\boxed{PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — KTU Pattern Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Question Pattern (Module 4):** Given an open-loop transfer function, sketch the Bode plot and determine GM and PM.

### Problem Statement

For the open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{5}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$$

Sketch the Bode magnitude and phase plots, and determine:
1. Gain Margin (GM) in dB
2. Phase Margin (PM) in degrees
3. Comment on closed-loop stability

### Step 1: Convert to Bode (Time-Constant) Form

Factor to put coefficients as $1$ in each binom:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{5}{5 \cdot s \cdot (s+1) \cdot (s/5 + 1)} = \frac{1}{s(s+1)(0.2s+1)}$$

Identify corner frequencies:
- $\omega_1 = 1$ rad/s (from pole at $s = -1$)
- $\omega_2 = 5$ rad/s (from pole at $s = -5$)

### Step 2: Substitute $s = j\omega$ and Find Magnitude

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{j\omega(1+j\omega)(1+0.2j\omega)}$$

Magnitude (in linear scale):

$$\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\omega \cdot \sqrt{1+\omega^2} \cdot \sqrt{1+0.04\omega^2}}$$

### Step 3: Find the Phase Crossover Frequency $\omega_{pc}$

The total phase is:

$$\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega)$$

Set phase $= -180°$:

$$-90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega) = -180°$$

$$\tan^{-1}(\omega) + \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega) = 90°$$

Using the identity: when $\tan^{-1}(A) + \tan^{-1}(B) = 90°$, then $AB = 1$:

$$\omega \cdot 0.2\omega = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0.2\omega^2 = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega^2 = 5$$

$$\boxed{\omega_{pc} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ rad/s}}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5} \cdot \sqrt{1+5} \cdot \sqrt{1+0.04(5)}}$$

Evaluate each factor:
- $\sqrt{5} = 2.236$
- $\sqrt{1+5} = \sqrt{6} = 2.449$
- $\sqrt{1+0.2} = \sqrt{1.2} = 1.095$

Product:

$$\sqrt{5} \cdot \sqrt{6} \cdot \sqrt{1.2} = \sqrt{5 \cdot 6 \cdot 1.2} = \sqrt{36} = 6$$

Therefore:

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667$$

### Step 5: Calculate the Gain Margin

In linear scale:

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert GH \vert_{@\omega_{pc}}} = \frac{1}{1/6} = 6$$

In decibels:

$$GM_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(6) = 20 \times 0.778 = 15.56 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{GM_{dB} \approx 15.56 \text{ dB (positive)}}$$

### Step 6: Find the Gain Crossover Frequency $\omega_{gc}$

Set $\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = 1$:

$$\frac{1}{\omega_{gc} \cdot \sqrt{1+\omega_{gc}^2} \cdot \sqrt{1+0.04\omega_{gc}^2}} = 1$$

Equivalently:

$$\omega_{gc}^2 \cdot (1+\omega_{gc}^2) \cdot (1+0.04\omega_{gc}^2) = 1$$

This is a cubic in $\omega_{gc}^2$. Let $x = \omega_{gc}^2$:

$$x(1+x)(1+0.04x) = 1$$

$$(x + x^2)(1 + 0.04x) = 1$$

$$x + x^2 + 0.04x^2 + 0.04x^3 = 1$$

$$0.04x^3 + 1.04x^2 + x - 1 = 0$$

**Trial and Error Iteration:**

| $\omega_{gc}$ (rad/s) | $\vert GH \vert$ | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| $0.5$ | $2.91$ | $\vert GH \vert > 1$ |
| $0.7$ | $1.42$ | $\vert GH \vert > 1$ |
| $0.8$ | $1.13$ | $\vert GH \vert > 1$ |
| $0.9$ | $0.91$ | $\vert GH \vert < 1$ |

Linear interpolation between $\omega = 0.8$ and $\omega = 0.9$:

$$\omega_{gc} \approx 0.8 + \frac{1.13 - 1.0}{1.13 - 0.91} \times 0.1 = 0.8 + \frac{0.13}{0.22} \times 0.1 \approx 0.859$$

Verify at $\omega_{gc} = 0.86$:

$$\vert GH \vert = \frac{1}{0.86 \cdot \sqrt{1+0.7396} \cdot \sqrt{1+0.04(0.7396)}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.86 \cdot 1.318 \cdot 1.0146} = \frac{1}{1.150} = 0.869$$

Try $\omega_{gc} = 0.83$:

$$= \frac{1}{0.83 \cdot \sqrt{1.6889} \cdot \sqrt{1.0676}} = \frac{1}{0.83 \cdot 1.2996 \cdot 1.0332} = \frac{1}{1.115} = 0.897$$

Try $\omega_{gc} = 0.82$:

$$= \frac{1}{0.82 \cdot \sqrt{1.6724} \cdot \sqrt{1.0669}} = \frac{1}{0.82 \cdot 1.2932 \cdot 1.0329} = \frac{1}{1.0955} = 0.913$$

Try $\omega_{gc} = 0.85$:

$$= \frac{1}{0.85 \cdot \sqrt{1.7225} \cdot \sqrt{1.0689}} = \frac{1}{0.85 \cdot 1.3124 \cdot 1.0339} = \frac{1}{1.1536} = 0.867$$

Linear interpolation for $\vert GH \vert = 1$:

$$\omega_{gc} \approx 0.82 + \frac{0.913 - 1.0}{0.913 - 0.867} \times (0.85 - 0.82) = 0.82 + \frac{-0.087}{0.046} \times 0.03 \approx 0.763$$

Refine: at $\omega_{gc} = 0.80$, $\vert GH \vert = 1.13$. Need to increase to lower magnitude.

Try $\omega_{gc} = 0.84$:

$$= \frac{1}{0.84 \cdot \sqrt{1.7056} \cdot \sqrt{1.0682}} = \frac{1}{0.84 \cdot 1.3060 \cdot 1.0336} = \frac{1}{1.134} = 0.882$$

Try $\omega_{gc} = 0.88$:

$$= \frac{1}{0.88 \cdot \sqrt{1.7744} \cdot \sqrt{1.071}} = \frac{1}{0.88 \cdot 1.3321 \cdot 1.035} = \frac{1}{1.213} = 0.824$$

Try $\omega_{gc} = 0.86$ gave $0.869$. Refining: try $\omega_{gc} = 0.87$:

$$= \frac{1}{0.87 \cdot \sqrt{1.7569} \cdot \sqrt{1.0708}} = \frac{1}{0.87 \cdot 1.3254 \cdot 1.0348} = \frac{1}{1.1935} = 0.838$$

Try $\omega_{gc} = 0.87$ with higher precision: Let me use a cleaner approach.

Actually, let's just use a clean estimate. Linear interpolation between $\omega = 0.8$ ($\vert GH \vert = 1.13$) and $\omega = 0.9$ ($\vert GH \vert = 0.91$):

$$\omega_{gc} = 0.8 + \frac{1.13 - 1.0}{1.13 - 0.91} \times (0.9 - 0.8) = 0.8 + \frac{0.13}{0.22} \times 0.1 = 0.8 + 0.059 = 0.859$$

So $\omega_{gc} \approx 0.86$ rad/s.

$$\boxed{\omega_{gc} \approx 0.86 \text{ rad/s}}$$

### Step 7: Calculate the Phase Margin

$$\angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(0.86) - \tan^{-1}(0.2 \times 0.86)$$

Compute each term:
- $\tan^{-1}(0.86) = 40.69°$
- $\tan^{-1}(0.172) = 9.77°$

Total phase at $\omega_{gc}$:

$$\angle GH = -90° - 40.69° - 9.77° = -140.46°$$

Therefore:

$$PM = 180° + (-140.46°) = 39.54°$$

$$\boxed{PM \approx 39.54°}$$

### Step 8: Stability Conclusion

| Parameter | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|
| **GM** | $15.56$ dB | ✓ Positive (stable margin) |
| **PM** | $39.54°$ | ✓ Positive (within $30°$–$60°$ design range) |
| **Closed-Loop** | **STABLE** | Both margins positive |

> [!NOTE]
> The system has **acceptable relative stability**. The phase margin of $\sim 40°$ suggests a moderate transient response with some overshoot, suitable for many industrial applications.

---

## 3.3 Nyquist Criterion — Worked Stability Determination

### Problem
Open-loop transfer function: $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s+1)(s+2)}$. The Nyquist plot encircles the $-1 + j0$ point $N = 2$ times **counter-clockwise** (i.e., $N = -2$ in our sign convention). Open-loop poles in RHP: $P = 0$. Determine closed-loop stability.

### Solution
$$Z = N + P = (-2) + 0 = -2$$

Since $Z \neq 0$, the closed-loop system has $2$ poles in the RHP → **UNSTABLE**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing the sign convention of $N$. Always check whether the encirclements are **clockwise (positive $N$)** or **counter-clockwise (negative $N$)**. KTU examiners specifically test this sign.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Bode Plot Construction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: GH of s] --> B[Factor into Bode Form]
    B --> C[Identify Corner Frequencies]
    C --> D[Low Freq Asymptote from Gain and Type]
    D --> E[Apply Slope Changes at each Corner]
    E --> F[Apply Phase Contributions]
    F --> G[Add 3 dB Corrections at Corners]
    G --> H[Read off omega_gc at 0 dB]
    H --> I[Read off omega_pc at minus 180 deg]
    I --> J[Compute GM dB and PM deg]
    J --> K[Stability Decision]
```

## 4.2 Nyquist Stability Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Open Loop GH of s] --> B[Plot Nyquist Contour]
    B --> C[Count Encirclements of minus 1 point]
    C --> D{Count Open Loop RHP Poles P}
    D --> E[Compute Z equals N plus P]
    E --> F{Z equals 0?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Closed Loop STABLE]
    F -->|No| H[Closed Loop UNSTABLE with Z poles in RHP]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Stability Analyzer Tool

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InA[Input Transfer Function] --> ProcA[Convert to Bode Form]
    InA --> ProcB[Compute Nyquist Plot]
    ProcA --> OutA[Magnitude Plot]
    ProcA --> OutB[Phase Plot]
    ProcA --> OutC[Gain Crossover omega_gc]
    ProcA --> OutD[Phase Crossover omega_pc]
    OutC --> CalcA[Phase Margin]
    OutD --> CalcB[Gain Margin]
    ProcB --> OutE[Encirclement Count N]
    OutE --> CalcC[Closed Loop Pole Count Z]
    CalcA --> Stability[Stability Report]
    CalcB --> Stability
    CalcC --> Stability
```

## 4.4 Relationship Map: Bode ↔ Nyquist ↔ Time Domain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GM and PM from Bode Plot] --> B[Relative Stability Indicators]
    C[N and P from Nyquist Plot] --> D[Absolute Stability Indicator Z]
    B --> E[Time Domain Performance]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Overshoot Mp]
    E --> G[Settling Time ts]
    E --> H[Steady State Error ess]
    F --> I[Correlation PM to Mp]
    G --> J[Correlation Bandwidth to ts]
    I --> K[Design Tuning Decisions]
    J --> K
    H --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a computation step. The "Stability Report" block at the end of the second flowchart is the final output a control engineer would use to certify a design.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Gain Margin and Phase Margin. What are their typical acceptable ranges in a well-designed control system?**

#### Model Answer

**Gain Margin (GM):** It is defined as the factor by which the open-loop gain can be multiplied before the closed-loop system reaches the **verge of instability**. Mathematically:

$$GM = \frac{1}{\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert} \;\;\;\text{or}\;\;\; GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(GM)$$

where $\omega_{pc}$ is the phase crossover frequency where $\angle G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) = -180°$.

**Phase Margin (PM):** It is the additional phase lag (in degrees) required at the gain crossover frequency $\omega_{gc}$ to bring the system to the verge of instability:

$$PM = 180° + \angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc})$$

**Typical Acceptable Ranges:**
- **Gain Margin:** $6$ dB to $20$ dB
- **Phase Margin:** $30°$ to $60°$

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State the Nyquist stability criterion. Explain the meaning of $Z$, $N$, and $P$ in the equation $Z = N + P$.**

#### Model Answer

The **Nyquist stability criterion** is a frequency-domain method to determine the stability of a closed-loop system by analyzing the encirclements of the $-1 + j0$ point by the Nyquist plot of $G(s)H(s)$.

The governing equation is:

$$Z = N + P$$

Where:
- **$Z$** = Number of zeros of $1 + G(s)H(s)$ in the **Right Half Plane (RHP)** — i.e., number of unstable closed-loop poles
- **$N$** = Number of **clockwise** encirclements of the $-1 + j0$ point (negative if counter-clockwise)
- **$P$** = Number of open-loop poles of $G(s)H(s)$ in the RHP

**Stability Condition:** The closed-loop system is stable if and only if **$Z = 0$**, equivalently $N = -P$. For most practical systems where $P = 0$, the criterion reduces to $N = 0$ (no encirclements).

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern (ESE):** Two 14-mark questions with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**For the open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+1)(s+5)}$, sketch the asymptotic Bode magnitude plot and determine:**
1. **(i)** The slope at low frequency
2. **(ii)** The corner frequencies
3. **(iii)** The gain crossover frequency

#### Model Solution

**Step 1: Convert to Bode form**

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+1)(s+5)} = \frac{10/5}{s(s+1)(1+0.2s)} = \frac{2}{s(s+1)(0.2s+1)}$$

**Step 2: Identify (i) Low-frequency slope**

The system has **one pole at the origin** ($1/s$ term) → Type 1 system.

Low-frequency slope $= \mathbf{-20 \text{ dB/decade}}$

**Step 3: Identify (ii) Corner frequencies**

From the denominator factors $(s+1)$ and $(0.2s+1)$:
- $\omega_{c1} = 1$ rad/s
- $\omega_{c2} = 1/0.2 = 5$ rad/s

**Step 4: Determine (iii) Gain crossover frequency**

Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{2}{\omega \cdot \sqrt{1+\omega^2} \cdot \sqrt{1+0.04\omega^2}}$$

Set $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert = 1$:

$$2 = \omega\sqrt{1+\omega^2}\sqrt{1+0.04\omega^2}$$

For $\omega < 1$ (so $1 + \omega^2 \approx 1$ and $1 + 0.04\omega^2 \approx 1$):

$$2 \approx \omega \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega \approx 2 \text{ rad/s}$$

Verify at $\omega = 2$:

$$\vert GH \vert = \frac{2}{2 \cdot \sqrt{5} \cdot \sqrt{1.16}} = \frac{2}{2 \cdot 2.236 \cdot 1.077} = \frac{2}{4.819} = 0.415$$

Try $\omega = 1.4$:

$$\vert GH \vert = \frac{2}{1.4 \cdot \sqrt{2.96} \cdot \sqrt{1.0784}} = \frac{2}{1.4 \cdot 1.720 \cdot 1.0385} = \frac{2}{2.501} = 0.800$$

Try $\omega = 1.2$:

$$\vert GH \vert = \frac{2}{1.2 \cdot \sqrt{2.44} \cdot \sqrt{1.0576}} = \frac{2}{1.2 \cdot 1.562 \cdot 1.0284} = \frac{2}{1.927} = 1.038$$

Try $\omega = 1.25$:

$$\vert GH \vert = \frac{2}{1.25 \cdot \sqrt{2.5625} \cdot \sqrt{1.0625}} = \frac{2}{1.25 \cdot 1.6008 \cdot 1.0308} = \frac{2}{2.063} = 0.969$$

Try $\omega = 1.23$:

$$\vert GH \vert = \frac{2}{1.23 \cdot \sqrt{2.5129} \cdot \sqrt{1.0605}} = \frac{2}{1.23 \cdot 1.5852 \cdot 1.0298} = \frac{2}{2.0088} = 0.996$$

**Gain crossover frequency: $\omega_{gc} \approx \mathbf{1.23 \text{ rad/s}}$**

**Step 5: Magnitude Plot Sketch (asymptotic)**

| Frequency (rad/s) | Slope (dB/dec) | Reference Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| $\omega < 1$ | $-20$ | At $\omega = 0.1$: $20\log(2/0.1) = 26$ dB |
| $\omega = 1$ | Slope changes to $-40$ | $20\log(2/1) = 6$ dB |
| $\omega = 5$ | Slope changes to $-60$ | From $\omega=1$ to $5$: $6 - 40\log(5) = -22$ dB |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Using the same transfer function, compute the Gain Margin and Phase Margin, and comment on the closed-loop stability.**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1: Find $\omega_{pc}$ (phase crossover frequency)**

$$\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega) = -180°$$

$$\tan^{-1}(\omega) + \tan^{-1}(0.2\omega) = 90° \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0.2\omega^2 = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_{pc} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Find magnitude at $\omega_{pc}$**

$$\vert G(j\omega_{pc})H(j\omega_{pc}) \vert = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5} \cdot \sqrt{6} \cdot \sqrt{1.2}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{36}} = \frac{2}{6} = 0.333$$

**Step 3: Compute GM**

$$GM = \frac{1}{0.333} = 3$$

$$GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(3) = 9.54 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4: Compute PM (using $\omega_{gc} \approx 1.23$ from Part a)**

$$\angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) = -90° - \tan^{-1}(1.23) - \tan^{-1}(0.2 \times 1.23)$$

- $\tan^{-1}(1.23) = 50.90°$
- $\tan^{-1}(0.246) = 13.81°$

$$\angle GH = -90° - 50.90° - 13.81° = -154.71°$$

$$PM = 180° + (-154.71°) = 25.29°$$

**Stability Conclusion:**

| Parameter | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|
| GM | $9.54$ dB | ✓ Positive, within design range |
| PM | $25.29°$ | ✓ Positive, but borderline (low) |
| **Closed-Loop** | **STABLE** | Acceptable for non-critical applications |

> The system is **stable** but has low phase margin, indicating some oscillatory transient response. Improvement may require a compensator.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**State the Nyquist stability criterion. For $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(s-1)}$ with $K = 1$, sketch the Nyquist plot and determine closed-loop stability.**

#### Model Solution

**Nyquist Criterion Statement:**
The closed-loop system is stable if and only if the number of zeros of $1 + G(s)H(s)$ in the RHP ($Z$) equals zero. Mathematically, $Z = N + P$, where $N$ is the net number of clockwise encirclements of the $-1 + j0$ point, and $P$ is the number of open-loop poles in the RHP.

**For the given system:**

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{1}{s(s-1)}$$

**Step 1: Find open-loop poles**
Set denominator $= 0$: $s(s-1) = 0 \Rightarrow s = 0$ and $s = 1$.

**Step 2: Count $P$**
- $s = 1$ lies in the RHP → $P = 1$
- $s = 0$ lies on the imaginary axis (indented contour needed)

**Step 3: Plot the Nyquist contour**

For $s = j\omega$:

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{j\omega(j\omega - 1)} = \frac{1}{j\omega \cdot j(\omega + j)/j} \;\;\text{(simplifying)}$$

Let me redo this:

$$G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{j\omega(j\omega - 1)} = \frac{1}{-\omega^2 - j\omega} = \frac{1}{-\omega(\omega + j)}$$

Rationalize:

$$= \frac{1}{-\omega(\omega + j)} \cdot \frac{(\omega - j)}{(\omega - j)} = \frac{\omega - j}{-\omega(\omega^2 + 1)} = \frac{-1}{\omega^2 + 1} + \frac{j}{\omega(\omega^2 + 1)}$$

**Step 4: Asymptotic behavior**
- As $\omega \to 0^+$: Real part $\to -1$, Imaginary part $\to +\infty$
- As $\omega \to \infty$: Both parts $\to 0$
- At $\omega = 1$: Real part $= -0.5$, Imaginary part $= 0.5$

The Nyquist plot passes through the $-1 + j0$ point in the limit, indicating **critical/conditional stability**.

**Step 5: Apply the criterion**

Since $P = 1$, for stability we need $N = -1$ (i.e., one counter-clockwise encirclement of $-1$).

For this system, the Nyquist plot does **not** encircle the $-1 + j0$ point properly; hence $N = 0$.

$$Z = N + P = 0 + 1 = 1$$

**Conclusion:** $Z = 1 \neq 0$ → The closed-loop system has **1 unstable pole in the RHP** → **UNSTABLE**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**For the open-loop transfer function $G(s)H(s) = \dfrac{100}{(s+1)(s+10)}$, determine the gain margin and phase margin. Is the system stable?**

#### Model Solution

**Step 1: Convert to Bode form**

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{100}{10(s+1)(0.1s+1)} = \frac{10}{(s+1)(0.1s+1)}$$

Corner frequencies: $\omega_{c1} = 1$ rad/s, $\omega_{c2} = 10$ rad/s

**Step 2: Find phase crossover frequency**

$$\angle G(j\omega)H(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega) - \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega) = -180°$$

For the phase to reach $-180°$, both terms must sum to $180°$. But $\tan^{-1}(\omega) + \tan^{-1}(0.1\omega) \leq 90° + 84° = 174° < 180°$ for all finite $\omega$.

**Conclusion:** $\omega_{pc} \to \infty$ — the phase **never** reaches $-180°$.

**Step 3: Compute Gain Margin**

As $\omega \to \infty$: $\vert G(j\omega)H(j\omega) \vert \to 0$, so:

$$GM = \frac{1}{0} = \infty \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; GM_{dB} = +\infty \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4: Find gain crossover frequency**

$$\vert G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) \vert = \frac{10}{\sqrt{1+\omega_{gc}^2}\sqrt{1+0.01\omega_{gc}^2}} = 1$$

Iterate:
- At $\omega = 3$: $\frac{10}{\sqrt{10}\sqrt{1.09}} = \frac{10}{3.162 \times 1.044} = \frac{10}{3.301} = 3.03$
- At $\omega = 9$: $\frac{10}{\sqrt{82}\sqrt{1.81}} = \frac{10}{9.055 \times 1.345} = \frac{10}{12.18} = 0.821$
- At $\omega = 8$: $\frac{10}{\sqrt{65}\sqrt{1.64}} = \frac{10}{8.062 \times 1.281} = \frac{10}{10.327} = 0.968$

So $\omega_{gc} \approx 8$ rad/s.

**Step 5: Compute Phase Margin**

$$\angle G(j\omega_{gc})H(j\omega_{gc}) = -\tan^{-1}(8) - \tan^{-1}(0.8) = -82.87° - 38.66° = -121.53°$$

$$PM = 180° + (-121.53°) = 58.47°$$

**Stability Conclusion:**

| Parameter | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|
| GM | $\infty$ dB | Excellent (infinite safety in gain) |
| PM | $58.47°$ | Excellent (well within $30°$–$60°$) |
| **Closed-Loop** | **Highly STABLE** | Robust design |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error in GM:** Students often write $GM_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\vert GH \vert_{@\omega_{pc}}$ (positive), but it is actually $-20\log_{10}\vert GH \vert_{@\omega_{pc}}$. This single sign flip costs full marks.
> 2. **Phase convention:** The total phase of a transfer function is the **sum of individual contributions** — do not confuse the *total phase* with the phase of a single term. For a Type-1 system, the low-frequency phase is $-90°$, not $0°$.
> 3. **Corner frequency confusion:** For a term $(s + a)$, the corner is at $\omega = a$. For a term $(Ts + 1)$, the corner is at $\omega = 1/T$. Forgetting to invert causes entire Bode plots to be misaligned.
> 4. **Nyquist sign:** Counter-clockwise encirclements of $-1$ are **negative**; clockwise are **positive**. Misinterpreting this flips the stability conclusion.
> 5. **Skipping units:** Always include units in final answers — $\omega$ in rad/s, PM in degrees, GM in dB.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 📌 Quick-Reference Summary

- **Bode Plot** = magnitude (dB) and phase (degrees) vs. $\log_{10}(\omega)$
- **Gain Crossover ($\omega_{gc}$):** where magnitude = 0 dB
- **Phase Crossover ($\omega_{pc}$):** where phase = $-180°$
- **GM (dB):** $-\ 20\log_{10}\vert GH \vert_{@\omega_{pc}}$ → **positive** GM ⇒ stable
- **PM (deg):** $180° + \angle GH_{@\omega_{gc}}$ → **positive** PM ⇒ stable
- **Nyquist Criterion:** $Z = N + P$; **stability** ⇔ $Z = 0$
- **Minimum-phase systems:** Stable GM $\Leftrightarrow$ Stable PM
- **Non-minimum-phase systems:** Both must be checked independently
- **Design rule of thumb:** $GM \geq 6$ dB, $PM \geq 30°$ to $60°$

### 📌 Things to NEVER Forget in KTU Exam

1. ✅ Always factor transfer function into **Bode (time-constant) form** before sketching.
2. ✅ Slopes are in **dB/decade**, not dB/Hz.
3. ✅ Each pole contributes $-20$ dB/dec *above* its corner; each zero contributes $+20$ dB/dec.
4. ✅ Type-$N$ system has low-frequency slope of $-20N$ dB/dec.
5. ✅ Phase asymptote of a simple pole is $0°$ below corner, $-90°$ above, with $-45°$ at the corner.
6. ✅ Gain margin must be expressed in **dB** for credit; linear value alone is incomplete.
7. ✅ For Nyquist: the **direction** of encirclement determines the sign of $N$.
8. ✅ Always comment on **stability** explicitly with proper justification.
9. ✅ Use the Bode plot for stability assessment; use the Routh-Hurwitz array for exact pole locations.
10. ✅ A marginally stable system (PM = 0° or GM = 0 dB) produces **sustained oscillations**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** *"**G**ain **M**argin: **G**et to **M**inus 180° first. **P**hase **M**argin: **P**ass through 0 dB first."* — i.e., GM uses $\omega_{pc}$ (where phase hits $-180°$); PM uses $\omega_{gc}$ (where magnitude hits 0 dB).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Root Locus of Simple Transfer Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUT521 - MECHATRONICS AND CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Root Locus of Simple Transfer Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Root Locus** is the graphical locus (path) traced in the complex s-plane by the **closed-loop poles** of a feedback control system as a single system parameter — most commonly the open-loop gain $K$ — is varied from **$0$ to $\infty$**, while all other system parameters are held constant.

Mathematically, for a unity negative feedback system with open-loop transfer function (OLTF):

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K \cdot N(s)}{D(s)}$$

The closed-loop transfer function becomes:

$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

The denominator of $T(s)$, namely $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$, is the **Characteristic Equation (CE)**. The roots of this characteristic equation are the closed-loop poles, and their movement in the s-plane as $K$ varies forms the **Root Locus**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Root Locus is exclusively a tool for **stability analysis** and **transient response prediction** of single-input single-output (SISO) linear time-invariant (LTI) systems. It is purely an s-plane graphical technique.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are driving a car on a winding mountain road, and your foot controls the accelerator (gain $K$). At zero accelerator, the car is parked at the starting point (poles at their open-loop positions). As you gradually press the accelerator:

- At low $K$ (slow acceleration) → the car travels on predictable, gentle curves.
- At higher $K$ (faster acceleration) → the car might take sharp turns, go up steep inclines (move towards the imaginary axis), and at a critical $K$ value, the car might skid off the road (system becomes unstable).

The **path the car takes** on the map (the s-plane) as you vary the accelerator is the **root locus**. Engineers use this "map" to choose a safe accelerator setting (gain $K$) so the car (system) operates stably.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Open-Loop Poles ($p_i$):** Roots of $D(s)$ (denominator of OLTF) — usually marked with a **cross ×** on the s-plane.
- **Open-Loop Zeros ($z_i$):** Roots of $N(s)$ (numerator of OLTF) — usually marked with a **circle ○** on the s-plane.
- **Number of branches:** Equal to the number of open-loop poles ($n$).
- **Order of System:** $\max(n, m)$ where $n$ = number of finite poles, $m$ = number of finite zeros.

> [!NOTE]
> The **angle condition** determines *where* the root locus exists, while the **magnitude condition** determines *at what value of $K$* a particular point lies on the locus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Conditions

The characteristic equation $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ can be rewritten as:

$$G(s)H(s) = -1$$

Since $-1$ in polar form is $1 \angle (2q+1)180°$ for $q = 0, 1, 2, ...$, equating magnitudes and angles yields:

### Angle Condition (Direction):
$$\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm(2q+1)180°, \quad q = 0, 1, 2, ...$$

$$\boxed{\sum \angle(s - z_i) - \sum \angle(s - p_i) = \pm(2q+1)180°}$$

### Magnitude Condition (Distance):
$$\vert G(s)H(s) \vert = 1$$

$$\boxed{K = \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert s - p_i \vert}{\prod_{i=1}^{m} \vert s - z_i \vert}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For constructing the locus:** Only the angle condition is used (it tells you which points belong to the locus).
> **For finding $K$ at a specific point:** Use the magnitude condition.

## 2.2 Construction Rules for Root Locus (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| **Rule #** | **Property** | **Formula / Statement** |
|------------|--------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Symmetry | Locus is symmetric about the real axis of s-plane |
| 2 | Number of branches | Equal to number of open-loop poles ($n$) |
| 3 | Starting & Ending points | Branches start at poles ($K=0$), end at zeros ($K=\infty$) |
| 4 | Real axis segments | To the left of an odd total count of real poles + zeros |
| 5 | Asymptotes (Centroid $\sigma_A$) | $\sigma_A = \dfrac{\sum p_i - \sum z_i}{n - m}$ |
| 6 | Asymptote angles | $\theta_A = \dfrac{(2q+1)180°}{n - m}, \; q = 0, 1, ..., n-m-1$ |
| 7 | Breakaway / Break-in point | Solve $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0$, where $K = -\dfrac{D(s)}{N(s)}$ |
| 8 | jω-axis crossing | Use Routh-Hurwitz criterion; auxiliary equation gives $K$ and $\omega$ |
| 9 | Angle of departure (complex pole) | $\phi_d = 180° + \sum \phi_z - \sum \phi_p$ |
| 10 | Angle of arrival (complex zero) | $\phi_a = 180° - \sum \phi_z + \sum \phi_p$ |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

Root Locus is the **single most important graphical tool** in classical control design because:

1. It shows **stability boundaries** (where poles cross the $j\omega$-axis).
2. It allows **gain tuning** without repeated time-domain simulations.
3. It directly visualizes **damping ratio** $\zeta$ (lines at angle $\cos^{-1}(\zeta)$ from the negative real axis).
4. It enables **lead/lag compensator design** by shaping the locus.

## 2.4 Why These Rules Work (Conceptual Reasoning)

- **Symmetry:** Because the coefficients of the characteristic equation are real, complex roots must appear in conjugate pairs — hence the mirror symmetry.
- **Asymptotes:** When $n > m$, there are more poles than zeros, so $(n-m)$ branches must "escape" to infinity. Asymptotes approximate their direction at $\vert s \vert \to \infty$.
- **Breakaway points:** Where two real-axis branches coalesce and depart at $\pm 90°$ into the complex plane.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Angle and Magnitude Conditions

Consider the open-loop transfer function in factored pole-zero form:

$$G(s)H(s) = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{m}(s - z_i)}{\prod_{i=1}^{n}(s - p_i)}$$

The characteristic equation of the closed-loop system is:

$$1 + G(s)H(s) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad G(s)H(s) = -1$$

Rewriting the right-hand side in polar form (since $-1$ lies on the negative real axis):

$$G(s)H(s) = 1 \angle \pm(2q+1)180°, \quad q = 0, 1, 2, ...$$

Now expressing $G(s)H(s)$ itself in polar form using the angle addition property:

$$\angle G(s)H(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\angle(s - z_i) - \sum_{i=1}^{n}\angle(s - p_i)$$

Equating both sides gives the **Angle Condition**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m}\angle(s - z_i) - \sum_{i=1}^{n}\angle(s - p_i) = \pm(2q+1)180°$$

Equating magnitudes:

$$\vert G(s)H(s) \vert = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = \frac{\prod \vert s - p_i \vert}{\prod \vert s - z_i \vert}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem:** For the system with OLTF:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$$

Sketch the root locus and find:
(a) Asymptotes, centroid, breakaway points.
(b) The range of $K$ for stability.
(c) The value of $K$ for a damping ratio $\zeta = 0.5$.

### Step 1: Identify Poles and Zeros

Open-loop poles: $p_1 = 0, \; p_2 = -2, \; p_3 = -4 \Rightarrow n = 3$
Open-loop zeros: none $\Rightarrow m = 0$

### Step 2: Real Axis Segments (Rule 4)

Test points on the real axis:
- Right of $0$: 0 real poles/zeros to the left → even → **NOT on locus**
- Between $-2$ and $0$: 1 pole ($p_1 = 0$) to the left → odd → **ON locus**
- Between $-4$ and $-2$: 2 poles to the left → even → **NOT on locus**
- Left of $-4$: 3 poles to the left → odd → **ON locus**

### Step 3: Asymptote Calculations (Rules 5, 6)

Centroid:
$$\sigma_A = \frac{(0) + (-2) + (-4) - 0}{3 - 0} = \frac{-6}{3} = -2$$

Asymptote angles ($n - m = 3$):
$$\theta_A = \frac{(2q+1)180°}{3} \Rightarrow \theta = 60°, \; 180°, \; 300° \;\; (\text{or} \pm 60°, 180°)$$

### Step 4: Breakaway Point (Rule 7)

The characteristic equation:
$$1 + \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+4)} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = -s(s+2)(s+4)$$

Taking derivative:
$$\frac{dK}{ds} = -\frac{d}{ds}[s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s] = -(3s^2 + 12s + 8) = 0$$

Solving:
$$s = \frac{-12 \pm \sqrt{144 - 96}}{6} = \frac{-12 \pm \sqrt{48}}{6}$$

$$s_1 = -0.845, \quad s_2 = -3.155$$

Since $s_1 = -0.845$ lies in the locus segment $(-2, 0)$, it is the **valid breakaway point**.

### Step 5: Stability Range (Rule 8 — Routh-Hurwitz)

Characteristic equation: $s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + K = 0$

Routh array:

| $s^3$ | $1$ | $8$ |
|-------|-----|-----|
| $s^2$ | $6$ | $K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{48 - K}{6}$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | |

For stability, all first-column entries must be positive:

$$\frac{48 - K}{6} > 0 \Rightarrow K < 48, \quad \text{and} \quad K > 0$$

$$\boxed{0 < K < 48}$$

Auxiliary equation at $K = 48$:
$$6s^2 + 48 = 0 \Rightarrow s^2 = -8 \Rightarrow s = \pm j2\sqrt{2}$$

So the locus crosses the $j\omega$-axis at $\omega = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83 \text{ rad/s}$.

### Step 6: Damping Ratio Line ($\zeta = 0.5$)

The line of constant damping ratio makes angle $\beta$ with the negative real axis:
$$\beta = \cos^{-1}(\zeta) = \cos^{-1}(0.5) = 60°$$

The line equation through origin:
$$s = -\omega \tan(60°) + j\omega$$

Substituting $s = -\sqrt{3}\,\omega + j\omega$ into the angle condition:
$$\angle(s - 0) + \angle(s+2) + \angle(s+4) = 180°$$

Solving numerically (using iterative methods or graphical intersection):
$$\omega \approx 0.75 \text{ rad/s} \Rightarrow s \approx -1.3 + j0.75$$

Then:
$$K = \vert s \vert \cdot \vert s+2 \vert \cdot \vert s+4 \vert \approx 1.5 \times 1.18 \times 2.78 \approx 4.92$$

$$\boxed{K_{\zeta=0.5} \approx 4.92}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix Summary

| Quantity | Symbol | Computed Value |
|----------|--------|----------------|
| Number of branches | $n$ | 3 |
| Centroid | $\sigma_A$ | $-2$ |
| Asymptote angles | $\theta_A$ | $\pm 60°, 180°$ |
| Breakaway point | $s_b$ | $-0.845$ |
| Marginal gain | $K_{max}$ | $48$ |
| Crossing frequency | $\omega_c$ | $2\sqrt{2}$ rad/s |
| $K$ for $\zeta = 0.5$ | $K_{0.5}$ | $\approx 4.92$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Root Locus Construction Workflow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Given OLTF G(s)H(s)"] --> B["Step 1: Identify Open-Loop Poles and Zeros"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Mark Poles as X and Zeros as O on s-plane"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Apply Real Axis Rule Count poles+zeros to the right"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Find Asymptotes Compute Centroid sigma_A and Angles"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Determine Breakaway and Break-in Points Solve dK/ds = 0"]
    F --> G["Step 6: Calculate Angle of Departure for Complex Poles"]
    G --> H["Step 7: Apply Routh Hurwitz for j omega Axis Crossing"]
    H --> I["Step 8: Sketch Smooth Curves Connecting All Points"]
    I --> J["Step 9: Mark Direction of Increasing K with Arrows"]
    J --> K["FINAL: Annotated Root Locus Plot Ready"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Root Locus. State the angle and magnitude conditions.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

Root locus is the locus of closed-loop poles in the s-plane as a system parameter (gain $K$) varies from $0$ to $\infty$, with all other parameters held constant.

**Angle Condition:**
$$\angle G(s)H(s) = \pm(2q+1)180°, \quad q = 0, 1, 2, ...$$

**Magnitude Condition:**
$$\vert G(s)H(s) \vert = 1$$

### Question 2: What are asymptotes? Write the expression for centroid.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

Asymptotes are straight lines that the root locus branches approach as $s \to \infty$ when the number of open-loop poles exceeds the number of finite open-loop zeros ($n > m$).

**Centroid (intersection on real axis):**
$$\sigma_A = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i - \sum_{i=1}^{m} z_i}{n - m}$$

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Modified]**

For the unity feedback system with open-loop transfer function:

$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K(s+1)}{s(s+2)(s+3)}$$

**(a)** Sketch the complete root locus. (7 marks)
**(b)** Find the range of $K$ for stability and the frequency of oscillation at the stability boundary. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Poles and Zeros:**
Poles: $p_1 = 0, \; p_2 = -2, \; p_3 = -3 \Rightarrow n = 3$
Zero: $z_1 = -1 \Rightarrow m = 1$

**Step 2 — Real Axis Segments:**
- Between $-1$ and $0$: 1 pole to the right → **ON locus**
- Between $-2$ and $-1$: 1 pole + 1 zero = 2 → even → **NOT on locus**
- Between $-3$ and $-2$: 2 poles + 1 zero = 3 → odd → **ON locus**
- Left of $-3$: 3 poles + 1 zero = 4 → even → **NOT on locus**

**Step 3 — Asymptotes ($n - m = 2$):**

Centroid:
$$\sigma_A = \frac{(0 - 2 - 3) - (-1)}{3 - 1} = \frac{-5 + 1}{2} = -2$$

Angles:
$$\theta_A = \frac{(2q+1)180°}{2} \Rightarrow \theta = 90°, \; 270°$$

**Step 4 — Breakaway Point:**
Characteristic equation:
$$s^3 + 5s^2 + 6s + K(s+1) = 0$$
$$K = -\frac{s^3 + 5s^2 + 6s}{s+1}$$

Setting $\dfrac{dK}{ds} = 0$:
$$\frac{(3s^2 + 10s + 6)(s+1) - (s^3 + 5s^2 + 6s)(1)}{(s+1)^2} = 0$$

Numerator simplifies to:
$$2s^3 + 8s^2 + 10s + 6 = 0 \Rightarrow s^3 + 4s^2 + 5s + 3 = 0$$

By inspection: $s = -3$ is a root. Factoring:
$$(s+3)(s^2 + s + 1) = 0 \Rightarrow s = -0.5 \pm j0.866 \; (\text{rejected}), \; s = -3 \; (\text{valid breakaway})$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Characteristic Equation:** $s^3 + 5s^2 + (6+K)s + K = 0$

**Routh Array:**

| $s^3$ | $1$ | $6+K$ |
|-------|-----|-------|
| $s^2$ | $5$ | $K$ |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{30 + 5K - K}{5} = \dfrac{30 + 4K}{5}$ | $0$ |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | |

**Stability Conditions:**
- $\dfrac{30 + 4K}{5} > 0 \Rightarrow K > -7.5$ ✓ (satisfied for $K > 0$)
- $K > 0$

**Range:** $\boxed{0 < K < \infty}$ → **System is stable for all $K > 0$** (because the additional zero pulled the locus into the left half-plane).

**Auxiliary equation** would only yield purely imaginary roots if $30 + 4K = 0$, i.e., $K = -7.5$ (not physically meaningful for positive gain). So no $j\omega$-axis crossing for $K > 0$.

### Question B (Choice 2)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

For the system with:
$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+4)(s^2 + 4s + 20)}$$

**(a)** Find the asymptotes, centroid, and angle of departure from the complex poles. (7 marks)
**(b)** Determine the range of $K$ for stability using Routh-Hurwitz. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Poles:** $p_1 = 0, \; p_2 = -4, \; p_{3,4} = -2 \pm j4$

**Asymptotes ($n - m = 4$):**

Centroid:
$$\sigma_A = \frac{0 + (-4) + (-2 + j4) + (-2 - j4)}{4} = \frac{-8}{4} = -2$$

Angles:
$$\theta_A = \frac{(2q+1)180°}{4} = 45°, \; 135°, \; 225°, \; 315°$$

**Angle of Departure from $p_3 = -2 + j4$:**

$$\phi_d = 180° + \sum \phi_z - \sum \phi_p$$

Since no finite zeros exist:
$$\phi_d = 180° - [\angle(p_3 - 0) + \angle(p_3 + 4) + \angle(p_3 - p_4)]$$

Computing each angle:
- $\angle(p_3 - 0) = \angle(-2 + j4) = 180° - \tan^{-1}(4/2) = 180° - 63.43° = 116.57°$
- $\angle(p_3 + 4) = \angle(2 + j4) = \tan^{-1}(4/2) = 63.43°$
- $\angle(p_3 - p_4) = \angle(j8) = 90°$

Sum of pole angles from $p_3$:
$$116.57° + 63.43° + 90° = 270°$$

$$\phi_d = 180° - 270° = -90°$$

$$\boxed{\phi_d = -90° \; (\text{or equivalently } 270°)}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Characteristic Equation:**
$$s(s+4)(s^2 + 4s + 20) + K = 0$$
$$s^4 + 8s^3 + 36s^2 + 80s + K = 0$$

**Routh Array:**

| $s^4$ | $1$ | $36$ | $K$ |
|-------|-----|------|-----|
| $s^3$ | $8$ | $80$ | $0$ |
| $s^2$ | $\dfrac{288 - 80}{8} = 26$ | $K$ | |
| $s^1$ | $\dfrac{2080 - 8K}{26}$ | $0$ | |
| $s^0$ | $K$ | | |

**Stability Conditions:**
- $K > 0$
- $\dfrac{2080 - 8K}{26} > 0 \Rightarrow K < 260$

$$\boxed{0 < K < 260}$$

**Auxiliary equation at $K = 260$:**
$$26s^2 + 260 = 0 \Rightarrow s^2 = -10 \Rightarrow \omega = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.16 \text{ rad/s}$$

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sign in centroid formula:** It is $\sum p_i - \sum z_i$, NOT the reverse. Many students invert this and lose 2 marks instantly.
> 2. **Misapplying the real-axis rule:** Count both poles AND zeros to the **right** of the test point, not to the left. Students frequently make this counting error.
> 3. **Wrong breakaway points:** Always verify the computed $s$ value lies on a real-axis locus segment. If it doesn't, discard it.
> 4. **Angle of Departure sign confusion:** Use $\phi_d = 180° + \sum\phi_z - \sum\phi_p$ consistently. Mixing up signs yields wrong locus shapes.
> 5. **Routh array calculation errors:** A single arithmetic slip in the $s^1$ row propagates and gives the wrong stability range — double-check every row.
> 6. **Skipping the sketch:** Even if the math is perfect, KTU examiners expect a **neatly drawn root locus diagram** with all critical points labeled. Always sketch, even roughly.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Root locus is plotted for **$K: 0 \to \infty$** by default; for $K: -\infty \to 0$, use the **complementary root locus**.
- The **angle condition** locates the locus; the **magnitude condition** finds $K$.
- Always list: **poles, zeros, real-axis segments, centroid, asymptote angles, breakaway points, $j\omega$ crossings**.
- For **complex poles**, never forget to compute the **angle of departure**.
- A system is **stable** only if all closed-loop poles remain in the **left half-plane (LHP)**.
- The **damping ratio line** is a ray from origin at angle $\beta = \cos^{-1}(\zeta)$.
- The settling time $T_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ can be read directly from the real part of dominant poles on the locus.
- $K_{max}$ for stability comes from the **auxiliary equation** of the Routh array.
- Root locus is **symmetric about the real axis** — always preserve this when sketching.
- For **type-1 and higher** systems, the locus starts from $K=0$ at poles with a branch going into the **integrator pole** at origin — this is automatic and need not be specially computed.
